topics.title: General Characteristics of Catalysis - Classification of Catalyst
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Characteristics of Catalysis - Classification of Catalyst

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Catalysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Berzelius (1835)** first coined the term **Catalysis** (Greek: *kata* = down, *lyein* = loosen) to describe substances that alter the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed in the overall process.
> **Ostwald (1895)** later refined this definition: *"A catalyst is a substance that changes the rate of a reaction without itself appearing in the overall stoichiometric equation of the reaction."*

A **catalyst** is therefore a substance that:
- Increases the **rate of approach to equilibrium** of a reaction (kinetic effect).
- Does **NOT** alter the position of thermodynamic equilibrium ($\Delta G$).
- Is **regenerated** at the end of the catalytic cycle (true catalyst) — though it may undergo temporary chemical change.

The phenomenon of acceleration (or retardation) by such a substance is called **catalysis**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tunnel Through The Mountain

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine** you are a ball trying to roll from valley A to valley B, separated by a massive mountain. The mountain's peak represents the **Activation Energy ($E_a$)**.
> - **Without a catalyst** — the ball must be pushed to the very top. A tiny fraction makes it, slowly.
> - **With a catalyst** — workers drill a **tunnel** through the mountain. The ball rolls through easily and quickly.
> - **Important**: The tunnel doesn't change the *height* of either valley (thermodynamics). It only changes the *path* (kinetics). The tunnel is unchanged at the end — just like a catalyst.

## 1.3 Catalytic Activity — Key Quantitative Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Turnover Number | **TON** | Total number of reactant molecules converted by **one active site** before deactivation | dimensionless |
| Turnover Frequency | **TOF** | Number of catalytic cycles per unit time per active site | $\text{s}^{-1}$ (or $\text{h}^{-1}$) |
| Conversion (%) | $X$ | Fraction of reactant consumed | % |
| Selectivity (%) | $S$ | Fraction of converted reactant that forms the *desired* product | % |
| Space Velocity | **GHSV / LHSV** | Volume of feed processed per volume of catalyst per hour | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |

## 1.4 Salient Features of a Catalyst (KTU 2024 Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Characteristics of Catalysis (KTU Board Favourite — asked repeatedly):**
> 1. The catalyst remains **chemically unchanged** in mass and chemical composition at the end of reaction.
> 2. Only a **small quantity** of catalyst is generally required.
> 3. A catalyst cannot initiate a reaction that is **thermodynamically non-spontaneous** ($\Delta G > 0$).
> 4. It does **not alter the equilibrium constant** $K$ or $\Delta G^0$.
> 5. The catalyst provides an **alternative pathway** with a lower $E_a$ (lowering the **energy of activation**).
> 6. The efficiency is expressed via **activity, selectivity, and stability**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How Does a Catalyst Work? — Stepwise Logic

The catalytic cycle generally proceeds in 5 logical steps (valid for both homogeneous and heterogeneous):

1. **Diffusion/Adsorption of reactant(s)** onto the catalyst surface (or into a coordination sphere).
2. **Activation** — bonds of the reactant are weakened/cleaved on the active site.
3. **Rearrangement / Surface Reaction** — new bonds form; transition state is stabilized.
4. **Desorption of product(s)** from the active site.
5. **Regeneration of the active site** — the catalyst returns to its original form.

> [!NOTE]
> Each step has its own rate constant; the **rate-determining step (RDS)** governs the overall rate. This is why catalyst design focuses on the *slowest elementary step*.

## 2.2 Effect of Catalyst on the Arrhenius Equation

The Arrhenius equation governs the rate constant:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

When a catalyst provides an alternative pathway, the activation energy drops from $E_a$ (uncatalysed) to $E_a^{\text{cat}}$ (catalysed), where $E_a^{\text{cat}} < E_a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** Catalysts do **NOT** change the pre-exponential factor $A$ in a *significant* way for most cases — the dominant effect is the **exponential** drop in $E_a$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Arrhenius Rate | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Predicts how temperature affects rate |
| Catalytic rate ratio | $\dfrac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{-(E_a^{\text{cat}} - E_a)/RT}$ | Quantifies catalyst "power" |
| Turnover Number | $\text{TON} = \dfrac{\text{moles of product}}{\text{moles of catalyst (active sites)}}$ | Catalyst lifetime metric |
| Turnover Frequency | $\text{TOF} = \dfrac{\text{TON}}{t}$ | Activity metric in $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{\text{moles of desired product}}{\text{moles of total product}}$ | Purity / yield metric |
| Site Time Yield | $\text{STY} = \dfrac{\text{mass of product}}{\text{mass of catalyst} \cdot t}$ | Industrial reactor sizing |
| Deactivation rate | $-\dfrac{dA}{dt} = k_d \cdot a^n$ | Predicts catalyst regeneration schedule |

> [!NOTE]
> Note: Use $\vert$ or `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol $\vert x \vert$ in any text version to prevent table breaks.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Industrial Application | Catalyst Used |
|---|---|---|
| Petroleum refining | Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) | **Zeolite Y** |
| Ammonia synthesis (Haber–Bosch) | $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \to 2\text{NH}_3$ | Fe / K–Al$_2$O$_3$ |
| Sulfuric acid (Contact process) | $2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{SO}_3$ | V$_2$O$_5$/SiO$_2$ |
| Automobile exhaust | CO, NO$_x$ oxidation | Pt–Pd–Rh three-way catalyst |
| Polymerisation | Polyethylene (Ziegler–Natta) | TiCl$_4$/MgCl$_2$ + AlR$_3$ |
| Pharmaceutical | Chiral resolution | Lipase enzyme |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Triad (KTU favourite):** A *good industrial catalyst* must be **active, selective, and stable** — the "three pillars" of catalytic engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Catalyst on Rate Constant

Starting with the Arrhenius expression for both uncatalysed and catalysed paths.

**Step 1:** Write the rate constant for the uncatalysed pathway.

$$k_{\text{uncat}} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Step 2:** Write the rate constant for the catalysed pathway.

$$k_{\text{cat}} = A' \cdot e^{-E_a^{\text{cat}} / RT}$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio of the two rate constants. Assuming the pre-exponential factors are of comparable order, $A \approx A'$:

$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = \frac{A'}{A} \cdot e^{-(E_a^{\text{cat}} - E_a)/RT}$$

**Step 4:** For surface/adsorption-limited heterogeneous reactions, take the natural logarithm to linearize.

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{A'}{A}\right) + \frac{(E_a - E_a^{\text{cat}})}{RT}$$

**Step 5:** For simplicity, if $A' = A$, the second term dominates. Therefore:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}}\right) \approx \frac{\Delta E_a}{RT}, \quad \text{where } \Delta E_a = E_a - E_a^{\text{cat}}$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate back to get a *practical engineering estimate*:

$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{\Delta E_a / RT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Empirical):** A reduction in $E_a$ by **~10 kJ/mol** typically *doubles* the rate at 300 K. The exponential sensitivity is enormous.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Pattern)

> **Question:** The uncatalysed reaction has $E_a = 80 \text{ kJ/mol}$. A catalyst reduces $E_a$ to $50 \text{ kJ/mol}$. At $T = 500 \text{ K}$, what is the rate enhancement? ($R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$)

**Step 1:** Compute the energy difference.

$$\Delta E_a = (80 - 50) \text{ kJ/mol} = 30{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $RT$ at 500 K.

$$RT = 8.314 \times 500 = 4{,}157 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponent.

$$\frac{\Delta E_a}{RT} = \frac{30{,}000}{4{,}157} \approx 7.22$$

**Step 4:** Apply the enhancement formula.

$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{7.22} \approx 1{,}374$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The catalyst accelerates the reaction by a factor of **~1,374×** at 500 K — explaining why even trace amounts of catalyst drive industrial reactions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Catalysts (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Class | Phase (vs. reactants) | Active Site | Example | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Homogeneous** | Same phase (usually liquid) | Metal complex / acid molecule | H$_2$SO$_4$ in esterification | Hydroformylation (Rh/PPh$_3$) |
| **Heterogeneous** | Different phase (usually solid) | Surface atoms / acid sites | Pt gauze in $\text{NH}_3$ oxidation | FCC, Haber, three-way catalyst |
| **Biocatalyst (Enzyme)** | Usually aqueous / liquid | Amino acid residues | Glucose isomerase | High-fructose corn syrup |
| **Heterogenized Homogeneous** | Solid support + liquid reactants | Anchored complex | SiO$_2$–Rh(CO)(PPh$_3$)$_3$ | Continuous-flow fine chemicals |
| **Phase Transfer (PTC)** | Two immiscible liquid phases | Cation (e.g., R$_4$N$^+$) | TBAB in SN2 reactions | Halide substitution |
| **Shape-selective** | Solid (zeolite) | Micropore window | ZSM-5 | Methanol → gasoline (MTG) |

## 3.4 Sub-Classification of Heterogeneous Catalysts

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiners love asking:** "Classify heterogeneous catalysts with examples." Memorize the four families below.

1. **Metal catalysts** — d-block transition metals (Pt, Pd, Ni, Cu, Ag).
2. **Metal oxide / sulfide catalysts** — V$_2$O$_5$, Cr$_2$O$_3$, MoS$_2$, Co-Mo-S.
3. **Acid–base catalysts** — Zeolites (HZSM-5), clays, sulfated zirconia.
4. **Supported catalysts** — Active phase dispersed on high-surface-area carrier (Al$_2$O$_3$, SiO$_2$, TiO$_2$, carbon).

## 3.5 Sub-Classification by Function

| Function | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive catalyst** | Speeds up reaction | MnO$_2$ in $\text{KClO}_3$ decomposition |
| **Negative catalyst (inhibitor)** | Slows down reaction (anti-oxidant) | Acetone retards H$_2$O$_2$ decomposition |
| **Auto-catalyst** | One of the products catalyses the reaction | Mn$^{2+}$ in $\text{KMnO}_4$ + oxalic acid |
| **Induced catalyst** | Reaction induces catalysis in a second | Na$_2$SO$_3$ oxidation induces As$_2$O$_3$ oxidation |
| **Promoter** | Enhances catalyst activity (not catalytic alone) | K, Al$_2$O$_3$ in Haber iron catalyst |
| **Poison** | Reduces/deactivates catalyst | S, As, Pb poisons Pt/Ni |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Profile Diagram (Mermaid — Energy Path on Y-axis)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the qualitative energy barrier reduction achieved by a catalyst along the reaction coordinate.

```mermaid
%%{init: {"theme": "default", "flowchart": {"htmlLabels": true}}}%%
graph LR
    R0["Reactants Energy Level"]:::reactNode
    P0["Products Energy Level"]:::prodNode
    TS1["Transition State Uncatalysed"]:::peakNode1
    TS2["Transition State Catalysed"]:::peakNode2

    R0 --> TS1
    TS1 --> P0
    R0 --> TS2
    TS2 --> P0

    classDef reactNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef prodNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef peakNode1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef peakNode2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The *red* (higher) curve is the uncatalysed path.
- The *yellow* (lower) curve is the catalysed path.
- Both start at the same reactant level and end at the same product level (thermodynamics unchanged).
- Only the **peak height** drops — kinetics accelerated.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Catalytic Cycle & Auxiliary Functions

```mermaid
%%{init: {"theme": "default", "flowchart": {"htmlLabels": true}}}%%
graph TD
    A1["Feedstock Inlet"]:::feedNode
    A2["Reactor Vessel with Catalyst Bed"]:::reactorNode
    A3["Product Separator"]:::sepNode
    A4["Product Outlet Stream"]:::prodOutNode
    A5["Catalyst Regeneration Loop"]:::regenNode
    A6["Spent Catalyst Return"]:::returnNode

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A3 --> A6
    A6 --> A5
    A5 --> A2

    classDef feedNode fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef reactorNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef sepNode fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef prodOutNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef regenNode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef returnNode fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Functional Mapping:**
- The reactor hosts the **active site** where conversion occurs.
- Spent catalyst is sent to a **regeneration unit** (burn-off of coke, re-reduction of metal).
- A **closed loop** keeps the catalyst in service for years — a hallmark of industrial heterogeneous catalysis.

## 4.3 Classification Tree (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
%%{init: {"theme": "default", "flowchart": {"htmlLabels": true}}}%%
graph TD
    CAT["Catalysts"]:::rootNode
    HOM["Homogeneous"]:::homNode
    HET["Heterogeneous"]:::hetNode
    BIO["Biocatalysts"]:::bioNode
    MET["Metal Catalysts"]:::subNode
    OX["Oxide and Sulfide Catalysts"]:::subNode
    ACD["Acid Base Catalysts"]:::subNode
    SUP["Supported Catalysts"]:::subNode

    CAT --> HOM
    CAT --> HET
    CAT --> BIO
    HET --> MET
    HET --> OX
    HET --> ACD
    HET --> SUP

    classDef rootNode fill:#1e293b,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef homNode fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0c4a6e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef hetNode fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bioNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef subNode fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Define the terms Turnover Number (TON) and Turnover Frequency (TOF). Why are they preferred over simple conversion percentage for evaluating industrial catalysts?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Turnover Number (TON):** It is the total number of reactant molecules (or product molecules) that one mole of active site converts before the catalyst is deactivated.
  $$\text{TON} = \frac{\text{moles of product formed}}{\text{moles of active sites in catalyst}}$$
- **Turnover Frequency (TOF):** It is the number of catalytic cycles completed per unit time per active site.
  $$\text{TOF} = \frac{\text{TON}}{t} \quad \left[\text{units: s}^{-1}\text{ or h}^{-1}\right]$$
- **Why preferred over conversion %:** TON/TOF are **intrinsic** — they normalize the performance to the *number of active sites*, making them independent of catalyst mass, reactor size, and operating conditions. Conversion % depends on contact time, feed rate, and mass of catalyst used.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Distinguish between a promoter and a poison with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Promoter | Poison |
|---|---|---|
| Effect on catalyst | **Enhances** activity, selectivity, or stability | **Suppresses** activity |
| Behaviour alone | Has little or no catalytic activity on its own | Often irreversible |
| Example (Haber) | $\text{K}_2\text{O}$, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ added to Fe | $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, CO adsorbs on Fe |
| Recovery | Generally does not destroy catalyst | Often **irreversible** (sintering/coking) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — *General Characteristics*

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Discuss the general characteristics of a catalyst with reference to the energy of activation, equilibrium constant, and Gibbs free energy. Use the Arrhenius equation in your justification.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks)** *Catalyst and Activation Energy:* A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a *lower* $E_a$. The Arrhenius expression $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ shows that a reduction in $E_a$ produces an **exponential** increase in $k$.

2. **(2 marks)** *Equilibrium Constant:* Since $K = k_{\text{fwd}} / k_{\text{rev}}$ and a catalyst lowers $E_a$ for **both** forward and reverse reactions equally (in the simplest case), the ratio remains unchanged. Therefore, **$K$ is unchanged**.

3. **(2 marks)** *Gibbs Free Energy:* The catalyst does not change $\Delta G^0$ because the *initial* and *final* states of the system are thermodynamically identical. Catalysis is purely a **kinetic** phenomenon.

4. **(1 mark)** *Conclusion:* A catalyst changes the **rate** at which equilibrium is reached, not the *position* of equilibrium.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "catalyst changes equilibrium position." This is **wrong** and costs 1–2 marks. Use the phrase "**approach to equilibrium** is accelerated."

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A first-order reaction has $E_a = 75 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at 600 K without a catalyst. With a catalyst, $E_a$ drops to $50 \text{ kJ/mol}$. Calculate the rate enhancement assuming $A$ is constant. ($R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$.)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the energy difference.

$$\Delta E_a = 75{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 25{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $RT$ at 600 K.

$$RT = 8.314 \times 600 = 4{,}988.4 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Formulate the enhancement ratio using Arrhenius.

$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{-(E_a^{\text{cat}} - E_a)/RT} = e^{\Delta E_a / RT}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute and solve.

$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{25{,}000 / 4{,}988.4} = e^{5.011} \approx 150.4$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude.

> **The reaction rate is enhanced by a factor of ~150× at 600 K.**

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — *Classification of Catalysts*

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Classify catalysts with suitable examples. Discuss homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis, highlighting their advantages and limitations.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** *Definition:* Catalysts are classified based on the **phase** of the catalyst relative to the reactants.

2. **(2 marks)** *Homogeneous Catalysis:* Catalyst and reactants are in the **same phase** (usually liquid). Example: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ catalysed esterification; Wacker oxidation (PdCl$_2$/CuCl$_2$).

3. **(2 marks)** *Heterogeneous Catalysis:* Catalyst (solid) and reactants (gas/liquid) are in **different phases**. Example: Fe in Haber process, Pt in catalytic converters.

4. **(2 marks)** *Comparison:*

| Criterion | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| Activity per site | High (every molecule is a site) | Lower (only surface atoms) |
| Selectivity | Excellent (single-site) | Moderate (multiple site types) |
| Separation from product | **Difficult** (costly) | **Easy** (filtration) |
| Thermal stability | Low | High |
| Recyclability | Poor | Excellent |

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Explain the role of a **promoter** and a **support** in heterogeneous catalysis. With an example, discuss how a **poison** deactivates a catalyst.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks)** *Promoter:* A substance that, when added in small quantity, **enhances** the activity/selectivity/stability of a catalyst but is **not catalytic by itself**.
   - Example: $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ and $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ in the Fe catalyst for the Haber process. K donates electrons to Fe, weakening the N–N bond of adsorbed N$_2$.

2. **(2 marks)** *Support:* A high-surface-area material (Al$_2$O$_3$, SiO$_2$, TiO$_2$, carbon) on which the active phase is dispersed.
   - Functions: Increases surface area, prevents sintering, improves mechanical strength, may contribute to activity (e.g., SMSI — Strong Metal–Support Interaction).

3. **(2 marks)** *Poison:* A substance that **chemisorbs strongly** on active sites, blocking them permanently or semi-permanently.
   - Example: $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ poisons Ni in methanation. Sulfur forms Ni–S bonds that are difficult to remove, reducing active surface area.

4. **(1 mark)** *Conclusion:* Promoters and supports *engineer* the catalyst surface; poisons *destroy* it. Industrial catalyst design is a battle against poisons.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **promoter** and **support**.
> - *Promoter* is added in small amount and **modifies electronic/chemical behaviour** of the active site.
> - *Support* is the **bulk carrier** that *disperses* and *stabilises* the active phase mechanically.
> Using them interchangeably costs a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Catalyst = NOT consumed; changes *rate* only; does NOT change $K$ or $\Delta G$.**
- [ ] **Catalyst lowers $E_a$** along an *alternative* pathway — the dominant effect is **exponential** via Arrhenius.
- [ ] **TON** = lifetime metric (dimensionless); **TOF** = activity metric ($\text{s}^{-1}$).
- [ ] **Classification (KTU main):** Homogeneous, Heterogeneous, Biocatalyst.
- [ ] **Heterogeneous sub-classes:** Metals, Oxides/Sulfides, Acid–Base, Supported.
- [ ] **Functional sub-classes:** Positive, Negative, Auto-, Induced, Promoter, Poison.
- [ ] **Industrial triad:** Activity + Selectivity + Stability = "the three pillars."
- [ ] **Promoter ≠ Support** — promoter modifies chemistry; support provides dispersion.
- [ ] **Phase Transfer Catalysis (PTC)** uses quaternary ammonium salts to ferry anions across immiscible phases.
- [ ] **Shape-selective catalysis** (e.g., ZSM-5) restricts reaction to molecules fitting the pore aperture.
- [ ] **Typical rule of thumb:** A 10 kJ/mol drop in $E_a$ ≈ **2× rate** at 300 K.
- [ ] **Haber catalyst (Fe) is promoted by K$_2$O + Al$_2$O$_3$** and poisoned by $\text{H}_2\text{S}$/CO.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamics of Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamics of Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Thermodynamics of Adsorption** is the branch of surface thermodynamics that quantitatively describes the energetic state, equilibrium distribution, and spontaneity of adsorbate molecules on a solid (or liquid) surface. It applies the three fundamental thermodynamic potentials — **Gibbs free energy change (ΔG°_ads)**, **enthalpy change (ΔH°_ads)**, and **entropy change (ΔS°_ads)** — to the adsorption process, allowing engineers and chemists to predict the feasibility, extent, and temperature-dependence of adsorption-based separation and catalytic processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The thermodynamic treatment of adsorption is foundational to Module 1 because it bridges **bulk-phase thermodynamics** (classical Gibbs theory) with **surface phenomena** (excess surface quantities, Gibbs dividing surface). Every adsorption isotherm used in industrial reactor design (e.g., Langmuir, BET, Temkin, Freundlich) ultimately has its origin in the thermodynamic equilibrium condition: **μ_adsorbate(bulk) = μ_adsorbate(surface)**.

The governing universal relationship is:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} - T \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$$

and equivalently:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln K_{ads}$$

where **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** is the universal gas constant and **K_ads** is the thermodynamic equilibrium constant for adsorption.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — The "Velcro Wall" Analogy**
> Imagine a Velcro-covered wall (the **catalyst surface**) and a room full of thrown tennis balls (the **gas-phase reactant molecules**). When you throw balls at the wall:
> - Some balls **stick firmly** by their hooks (analogous to **chemisorption**, ΔH < −40 kJ/mol, monolayer, activated).
> - Some balls simply **rest loosely** against the wall in stacks (analogous to **physisorption**, ΔH ≈ −5 to −40 kJ/mol, multilayer, non-activated).
> - The wall can only hold a **finite number** of balls before saturation — this is the concept of **monolayer capacity (q_m)**.
> - As the room gets **hotter**, balls vibrate more and many will detach (exothermic adsorption is favored at lower T).
> - The **amount that sticks at equilibrium** is exactly what thermodynamics predicts via the equilibrium constant K.

For a B.Tech student, the key takeaway is: **adsorption is an exothermic surface phenomenon** (ΔH°_ads < 0) that **loses entropy** (ΔS°_ads < 0, because the molecule goes from a 3-D gas to a 2-D confined state). For the process to be **spontaneous**, we need ΔG°_ads < 0, which requires **low-to-moderate temperatures** (Le Chatelier's principle for exothermic processes).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **R** | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| **T** | Absolute temperature | **K** (Kelvin) |
| **ΔG°_ads** | Standard Gibbs free energy of adsorption | **kJ·mol⁻¹** |
| **ΔH°_ads** | Standard enthalpy of adsorption | **kJ·mol⁻¹** |
| **ΔS°_ads** | Standard entropy of adsorption | **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| **K_ads** | Thermodynamic equilibrium constant | **dimensionless** (when referenced to standard state) |
| **θ** | Surface coverage fraction | **0 ≤ θ ≤ 1** (dimensionless) |
| **q_m** | Monolayer adsorption capacity | **mol·g⁻¹** or **mg·g⁻¹** |
| **P** | Partial pressure / equilibrium pressure | **Pa** or **bar** |
| **K_H** | Henry's law constant | **mol·g⁻¹·Pa⁻¹** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Basis of Adsorption Equilibrium

Adsorption reaches **thermodynamic equilibrium** when the chemical potential (μ) of the adsorbate in the bulk (gas/liquid) phase equals the chemical potential of the adsorbate in the adsorbed (surface) state. This single equality generates **every** classical adsorption isotherm.

### Step-by-step logical framework:

1. **Bulk-phase chemical potential (ideal gas):**
   The chemical potential of the i-th species in an ideal gas mixture is given by:
   $$\mu_i^{gas} = \mu_i^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln \left(\frac{P_i}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$
   where **P° = 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa** is the standard-state pressure and **P_i** is the partial pressure of adsorbate.

2. **Surface-phase chemical potential (definition):**
   For a species adsorbed on a uniform surface, the chemical potential in the surface phase (defined via the **Gibbs dividing surface** convention) is:
   $$\mu_i^{surf} = \mu_i^{\circ,\,surf}(T) + RT \ln(\theta_i)$$
   where **θ_i** is the fractional surface coverage by species i.

3. **Equilibrium condition (μ equality):**
   At equilibrium, no net flux occurs between phases, so:
   $$\mu_i^{gas} = \mu_i^{surf}$$
   $$\mu_i^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln\left(\frac{P_i}{P^{\circ}}\right) = \mu_i^{\circ,\,surf}(T) + RT \ln(\theta_i)$$

4. **Rearrangement to the Langmuir form:**
   $$\ln\left(\frac{\theta_i}{P_i/P^{\circ}}\right) = -\frac{\mu_i^{\circ,\,surf}(T) - \mu_i^{\circ}(T)}{RT} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT}$$
   Defining **K = exp(−ΔG°_ads/RT)**:
   $$\theta = \frac{K \, P}{1 + K \, P}$$
   This is the **Langmuir isotherm** — derived purely from thermodynamic equilibrium of chemical potentials.

## 2.2 The Three Principal Thermodynamic Quantities

### (a) Gibbs Free Energy of Adsorption (ΔG°_ads)

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln K_{ads}$$

- **Units:** kJ·mol⁻¹
- **Sign convention:** ΔG°_ads < 0 → **spontaneous** adsorption; ΔG°_ads > 0 → **non-spontaneous**.
- A ΔG°_ads of **−20 kJ·mol⁻¹** corresponds to K ≈ 3.2 × 10³ at 298 K (strongly favorable).

### (b) Enthalpy of Adsorption (ΔH°_ads)

The **isosteric enthalpy (or heat) of adsorption** is the most practically measured thermodynamic quantity. It is obtained from adsorption isosteres (lines of constant θ) plotted as ln P vs 1/T:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \ln P}{\partial (1/T)}\right)_{\theta} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}$$

This is the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation applied to adsorption**, valid when ΔH°_ads is approximately constant over the temperature range of interest.

- **Units:** kJ·mol⁻¹
- **Physical meaning:** Energy released when 1 mole of gas is adsorbed at constant coverage.
- **Typical ranges:** −5 to −40 kJ·mol⁻¹ (physisorption); −40 to −400 kJ·mol⁻¹ (chemisorption).

### (c) Entropy of Adsorption (ΔS°_ads)

$$\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} - \Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}}{T}$$

- **Units:** J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- **Sign convention:** ΔS°_ads < 0 because the molecule loses translational and rotational degrees of freedom upon confinement to a 2-D surface.
- **Typical range:** −50 to −200 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

## 2.3 The Five Classical Adsorption Isotherms (Engineering Toolbox)

| Isotherm | Equation | Form | Best-Use Engineering Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Henry's Law** | $q = K_H \, P$ | Linear | Very low coverage, P → 0 limit |
| **Langmuir** | $q = \dfrac{q_m \, K_L \, P}{1 + K_L \, P}$ | Rectangular hyperbola | Monolayer chemisorption on uniform surfaces (heterogeneous catalysis) |
| **Freundlich** | $q = K_F \, P^{1/n}$ | Power-law | Heterogeneous surfaces, empirical industrial correlations |
| **Temkin** | $q = B_T \ln(K_T \, P)$ | Logarithmic | Chemisorption on energetically heterogeneous surfaces |
| **BET** | $\dfrac{P}{q(P^{\circ}-P)} = \dfrac{1}{q_m C} + \dfrac{(C-1)}{q_m C}\dfrac{P}{P^{\circ}}$ | Two-parameter | Multilayer physisorption, surface area measurement (N₂ at 77 K) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) isotherm, although empirical in form, is itself derived from a **layer-by-layer thermodynamic equilibrium** assuming equal heat of adsorption for all layers above the first. The constant **C = exp[(E₁ − E_L)/RT]** is essentially a **Boltzmann factor** comparing the heat of the first layer (E₁) to the heat of liquefaction (E_L).

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Spontaneity of Adsorption

| Term | Sign | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **ΔH°_ads** | **Negative** | Adsorbate–surface attractive forces (van der Waals or covalent) release energy as heat. |
| **ΔS°_ads** | **Negative** | Gas molecule (3-D translational freedom) → 2-D confined state. |
| **ΔG°_ads** | **Negative** | Requires **\|ΔH°\| > T·\|ΔS°\|**; this is why **low T** is required for high coverage. |
| **ΔG°_ads → 0** at high T | — | The T·ΔS° term grows, eventually offsetting ΔH°. This is **why desorption occurs at high T** — the basis of **TPD (Temperature-Programmed Desorption)** experiments. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heterogeneous Catalysis:** ΔG°_ads of reactants must be **moderately negative** (~ −50 to −100 kJ·mol⁻¹). Too weak → molecules don't stick. Too strong → catalyst poisoning (Sabatier's principle).
- **Gas Separation (PSA / TSA):** Pressure-Swing and Temperature-Swing Adsorption processes exploit the **T-dependence of K_ads** (governed by ΔH°_ads via van't Hoff).
- **Catalyst Characterization:** BET surface area (m²/g) is computed from the N₂ adsorption isotherm at 77 K — a routine ASTM standard.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Activated carbon water treatment uses Langmuir parameters to design contactor columns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Langmuir Isotherm from Thermodynamic Equilibrium

We derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm **from first principles** using chemical potential equality.

**Starting assumptions:**
1. The surface has a **fixed number of identical adsorption sites** (N_total).
2. Each site can hold **at most one adsorbate molecule** (no stacking).
3. Adsorption is **reversible** and reaches dynamic equilibrium: rate of adsorption = rate of desorption.

**Step 1 — Define the equilibrium constant:**

The adsorption reaction is written as:

$$A_{(g)} + S_{\text{(free site)}} \rightleftharpoons A \cdot S_{\text{(occupied site)}}$$

The dimensionless equilibrium constant (referenced to the standard state P°) is:

$$K = \frac{\theta}{(1-\theta)\, (P/P^{\circ})}$$

where θ = fraction of sites occupied, and (1 − θ) = fraction of sites free.

**Step 2 — Solve for θ:**

Cross-multiplying:

$$K \, (1 - \theta) \, (P/P^{\circ}) = \theta$$

Expanding the left side:

$$K (P/P^{\circ}) - K (P/P^{\circ}) \, \theta = \theta$$

Collecting θ terms on one side:

$$K (P/P^{\circ}) = \theta + K (P/P^{\circ}) \, \theta = \theta \, [1 + K (P/P^{\circ})]$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \frac{K (P/P^{\circ})}{1 + K (P/P^{\circ})}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to loading q (mol/g):**

If the monolayer capacity is q_m (mol of sites per gram of solid), then q = q_m · θ:

$$q = \frac{q_m \, K \, P}{1 + K \, P}$$

This is the **Langmuir isotherm** in its standard engineering form (where K implicitly absorbs the P° factor).

**Step 4 — Limiting cases (sanity check):**

- At **very low P** (P → 0): denominator ≈ 1, so **q ≈ q_m K P** → linear (Henry's law limit).
- At **very high P** (P → ∞): denominator ≈ K P, so **q → q_m** → monolayer saturation.
- At **θ = 0.5**: **P = P°/K = 1/K_L** — this is the **half-saturation pressure**, an important catalyst descriptor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Clausius–Clapeyron Equation for Adsorption (Isosteric Heat)

The isosteric heat of adsorption Q_st is the **differential molar enthalpy** released when an infinitesimal amount of gas is adsorbed at constant surface coverage θ.

**Step 1 — Start from the Clausius–Clapeyron equation:**

For any two-phase equilibrium, along a coexistence line:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{trans}}{T \, \Delta V_{trans}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the "phase transition" in adsorption:**

Here, the "transition" is **gas → adsorbed state** at constant θ.

- ΔH_trans = ΔH°_ads (enthalpy of adsorption, negative for exothermic)
- ΔV_trans ≈ V_gas (the adsorbed volume is negligible compared to the gas volume) ≈ **RT/P** (ideal gas)

**Step 3 — Substitute and invert:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{T \cdot (RT/P)} = \frac{P \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R T^2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{P}\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R T^2}$$

Or, equivalently:

$$\frac{d(\ln P)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R T^2} \quad \text{(note: Q_st is positive magnitude, so we write)}$$

$$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial \ln P}{\partial (1/T)}\right)_{\theta} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Integrated form (assuming ΔH°_ads ≈ constant over a small T range):**

$$\ln P = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C$$

A plot of **ln P vs 1/T** at fixed θ is a **straight line** whose slope gives **−ΔH°_ads/R**. This is the **isostere method** used in DSC, TPD, and gravimetric experiments.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The van't Hoff Equation for the Adsorption Constant

The van't Hoff equation relates the **temperature dependence of K_ads** to the **enthalpy of adsorption**, just as for any chemical reaction.

**Step 1 — Combine ΔG° = ΔH° − TΔS° with ΔG° = −RT ln K:**

$$-RT \ln K_{ads} = \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} - T \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$$

**Step 2 — Divide by −RT:**

$$\ln K_{ads} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to T (assuming ΔH°, ΔS° are weakly T-dependent):**

$$\frac{d(\ln K_{ads})}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R T^2}$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{d(\ln K_{ads})}{d(1/T)} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Integrated form between two temperatures:**

$$\ln \left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** This equation is the **design equation for Temperature-Swing Adsorption (TSA)** units. By raising T from T_1 to T_2, K drops exponentially, releasing the adsorbed species (regeneration step).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Standard-State Convention for Surface Thermodynamics

A subtle but **board-exam-critical** point: the choice of standard state for the adsorbed phase is non-trivial.

**Choice (a) — Pure surface standard state (most common in Langmuir):**

The standard state is a **hypothetical state** where **θ = 1** (full monolayer) at the **same T and P as the bulk gas**, but the surface phase behaves ideally. The equilibrium constant is dimensionless.

$$K = \frac{\theta}{1-\theta} \cdot \frac{1}{P/P^{\circ}}$$

**Choice (b) — Unit-fugacity standard state:**

K_ads has units of (pressure)⁻¹. This is convenient when one wishes to use **fugacity** in non-ideal gas treatments.

**Practical implication:** The numerical value of ΔG°_ads depends on the chosen standard state, but **the predicted adsorption isotherm is identical** in both conventions. KTU questions often ask for the **dimensionless K** and the corresponding ΔG° in kJ/mol.

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Calculation of ΔG°, ΔH°, and ΔS°

**Problem:** For the adsorption of CO on a Pd catalyst, the equilibrium constant at 300 K is K = 1.5 × 10⁴, and the isosteric heat of adsorption is −110 kJ/mol. Calculate ΔG°, ΔS° and comment on feasibility.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K = -(8.314 \times 10^{-3}) \times 300 \times \ln(1.5 \times 10^{4})$$

$$\ln(1.5 \times 10^{4}) = \ln(1.5) + 4 \ln(10) = 0.4055 + 9.2103 = 9.6158$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -2.494 \times 9.6158 = -23.99 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$\Delta S^{\circ} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ} - \Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = \frac{(-110) - (-23.99)}{300} = \frac{-86.01}{300} = -0.2867 \; \text{kJ/mol·K}$$

$$\Delta S^{\circ} = -286.7 \; \text{J/mol·K}$$

**Conclusion:** ΔG° < 0 (spontaneous), |ΔH°| ≫ T|ΔS°| (enthalpy-driven), and ΔS° < 0 as expected for adsorption (consistent with physical reasoning).

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Physisorption vs Chemisororption

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| **ΔH°_ads (kJ/mol)** | −5 to −40 | −40 to −400 |
| **Activation energy (E_a)** | ≈ 0 (non-activated) | > 0 (activated) |
| **ΔS°_ads** | Negative, large magnitude | Negative, often smaller |
| **Specificity** | Non-specific (depends on polarizability) | Highly specific (depends on electronic structure) |
| **Layers** | Multilayer possible | Strictly monolayer |
| **Reversibility** | Easily reversible | Often irreversible (or requires high T) |
| **Surface coverage dependence of ΔH** | Roughly constant | Decreases strongly with θ (heterogeneity) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Thermodynamics of Adsorption Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of how a thermodynamic analysis of adsorption is performed in a catalysis laboratory or industrial setting. It captures the entire workflow from raw experimental data to design-relevant thermodynamic parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step A: Experimental Measurement] --> nodeB[Step B: Obtain Adsorption Isotherm q vs P at fixed T]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step C: Fit to Isotherm Model Langmuir / BET / Freundlich]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step D: Extract Model Parameters K_L and q_m]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step E: Repeat at Multiple Temperatures]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step F: Construct Isosteres ln P vs 1 slash T at constant theta]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step G: Slope Gives minus Delta H slash R from Clausius Clapeyron]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step H: Compute Delta G from minus R T ln K]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step I: Compute Delta S from Delta H minus Delta G over T]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step J: Design Decision Catalyst Selection or Adsorber Sizing]

    nodeA:::stageBox
    nodeB:::stageBox
    nodeC:::stageBox
    nodeD:::stageBox
    nodeE:::stageBox
    nodeF:::stageBox
    nodeG:::stageBox
    nodeH:::stageBox
    nodeI:::stageBox
    nodeJ:::finalBox

    classDef stageBox fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#003366
    classDef finalBox fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2.5px,color:#003300
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Energy Profile of Physisorption vs Chemisorption

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart[Reactant A in gas phase] --> nodePhy1[Physisorption path: shallow well]
    nodeStart --> nodeChem1[Chemisorption path: activated barrier]

    nodePhy1 --> nodePhy2[Physi State: weak van der Waals bond]
    nodeChem1 --> nodeChem2[Transition State with E_a]
    nodeChem2 --> nodeChem3[Chemi State: strong covalent bond]

    nodePhy2 --> nodePhyRelease[Desorb easily with small T increase]
    nodeChem3 --> nodeChemRelease[Desorb only at high T or react further]

    nodeStart:::gasBox
    nodePhy1:::phyBox
    nodePhy2:::phyBox
    nodePhyRelease:::phyBox
    nodeChem1:::chemBox
    nodeChem2:::chemBox
    nodeChem3:::chemBox
    nodeChemRelease:::chemBox

    classDef gasBox fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#663300

```

## 4.3 Schematic Description: Standard Isotherm Classification (IUPAC)

Since Mermaid cannot render graphs, we describe the **IUPAC classification of physisorption isotherms** as a textual schematic that students should memorize for the KTU exam:

```
        q (loading)
        ^
        |        ____________________  Type I (Langmuir - microporous)
        |      /
        |    /
        |   /  __________ Type II (BET - non-porous, strong adsorbate)
        |  / /
        | / /
        |/ /   ____________ Type III (weak adsorbate-adsorbent, e.g., N2 on ice)
        |//
        |/_____________ Type IV (mesoporous, hysteresis loop)
        |/   \_________ Type V (weak + mesoporous)
        +---------------------------------> P/P°
        0                                 1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory aid for KTU:** Type I → **M**icroporous (Langmuir); Type II → **M**onolayer-multilayer (BET, non-porous); Type IV → **M**esoporous with hysteresis (industrial catalysts like MCM-41).
