# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the isosteric heat of adsorption. How is it determined experimentally from adsorption isotherms measured at different temperatures?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The isosteric heat of adsorption (Q_st or −ΔH°_ads) is the **differential molar enthalpy change** when a small amount of gas is adsorbed on a solid surface at **constant surface coverage (θ)**. It is a **positive quantity** representing the magnitude of energy released.

**Experimental determination:**
1. Measure adsorption isotherms q(P) at several temperatures T₁, T₂, T₃.
2. For a chosen value of θ (i.e., constant loading q), read off the equilibrium pressure P at each T.
3. Plot **ln P vs 1/T** (the isostere plot) — yields a straight line.
4. Slope of the line equals **−ΔH°_ads/R** (Clausius–Clapeyron for adsorption).
5. Hence:

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} = -R \cdot \text{slope}$$

**Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + procedure (1) + equation (1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*For an adsorption process, ΔH°_ads = −85 kJ/mol and ΔS°_ads = −150 J/mol·K at 298 K. Calculate ΔG°_ads and comment on the spontaneity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} - T \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -85 - (298)(-0.150) = -85 + 44.7 = -40.3 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

Since ΔG°_ads = **−40.3 kJ/mol < 0**, the adsorption process is **spontaneous** at 298 K. The process is **enthalpy-driven** because |ΔH°| (85) > T|ΔS°| (44.7).

**Mark distribution:** Equation (1) + substitution (1) + interpretation (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**Q3(a). Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm starting from the equality of chemical potentials of the adsorbate in the gas phase and the adsorbed phase. State clearly all the assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions of Langmuir model:**
1. The surface has a **fixed, finite number of identical adsorption sites**.
2. Each site can hold **at most one adsorbate molecule** (no multilayer formation).
3. The **heat of adsorption is independent of coverage** (energetically uniform surface).
4. There is **no interaction** between adsorbed molecules on adjacent sites.
5. Adsorption is **reversible**; dynamic equilibrium is established.

**Derivation:**

The chemical potential of an ideal-gas adsorbate at partial pressure P is:

$$\mu_{gas} = \mu^{\circ}_{gas} + RT \ln\left(\frac{P}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$

The chemical potential of the same species in the adsorbed state, using the surface-phase convention, is:

$$\mu_{surf} = \mu^{\circ}_{surf} + RT \ln(\theta)$$

At equilibrium, μ_gas = μ_surf:

$$\mu^{\circ}_{gas} + RT \ln(P/P^{\circ}) = \mu^{\circ}_{surf} + RT \ln(\theta)$$

Rearranging:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\theta}{P/P^{\circ}}\right) = -\frac{\mu^{\circ}_{surf} - \mu^{\circ}_{gas}}{RT} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT}$$

Defining K = exp(−ΔG°_ads/RT):

$$\theta = \frac{K (P/P^{\circ})}{1 + K (P/P^{\circ})}$$

In terms of loading q = q_m θ:

$$q = \frac{q_m K P}{1 + K P}$$

**Mark split:** Assumptions (2) + chemical potentials setup (2) + equilibrium equality (1) + final equation (2).

---

**Q3(b). The following data were obtained for the adsorption of N₂ on a catalyst surface at 77 K. Plot suitable linearized forms and determine the monolayer capacity q_m and the Langmuir constant K_L. (7 marks)**

| P (kPa) | 8 | 16 | 30 | 50 | 75 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| q (cm³ STP/g) | 60 | 100 | 145 | 180 | 210 |

**Model Answer:**

**Linearization:** Langmuir in the form **P/q = 1/(q_m K_L) + P/q_m** is the standard BET/Langmuir linear plot.

Compute P/q for each point:

| P (kPa) | q (cm³/g) | P/q (kPa·g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|
| 8 | 60 | 0.1333 |
| 16 | 100 | 0.1600 |
| 30 | 145 | 0.2069 |
| 50 | 180 | 0.2778 |
| 75 | 210 | 0.3571 |

Linear regression on P/q vs P gives:

- **Slope = 1/q_m ≈ (0.3571 − 0.1333)/(75 − 8) = 0.2238/67 = 0.00334 g/cm³·kPa⁻¹**
- **Intercept = 1/(q_m K_L) ≈ 0.107 kPa·g/cm³**

Therefore:

$$q_m = \frac{1}{\text{slope}} = \frac{1}{0.00334} \approx 299 \; \text{cm}^3\text{ STP/g}$$

$$K_L = \frac{1}{q_m \cdot \text{intercept}} = \frac{1}{299 \times 0.107} \approx 0.0313 \; \text{kPa}^{-1}$$

**Mark split:** Linearization strategy (1) + table of P/q (2) + slope and intercept (2) + q_m and K_L (2).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4(a). Explain the BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) theory of multilayer adsorption. Derive the BET equation and discuss its significance in the determination of surface area of catalysts. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Theory:** BET extends Langmuir's monolayer concept to **multilayer physisorption**. Each adsorbed layer acts as a substrate for the next. The heat of adsorption for the **first layer** is E₁, while that of all subsequent layers is equal to the **heat of liquefaction E_L** (≈ heat of vaporization).

**Derivation (outline):**

The BET equation is obtained by summing the contributions of an infinite number of layers in thermodynamic equilibrium. The standard derivation involves the **ratio of desorption to adsorption rates** at each layer, combined with the definition of a dimensionless constant:

$$C = \frac{(a_1 g_1 / \nu_1) \exp[(E_1 - E_L)/RT]}{(a_i g_i / \nu_i) \exp[E_L/RT]}$$

which simplifies to **C = exp[(E₁ − E_L)/RT]**.

The final linear form of the BET isotherm is:

$$\frac{P}{q(P^{\circ} - P)} = \frac{1}{q_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{q_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P^{\circ}}$$

**Significance for surface area determination:**
1. Plot **P/[q(P° − P)] vs P/P°** — gives a straight line in the range 0.05 < P/P° < 0.30.
2. From slope and intercept, compute **q_m** (monolayer capacity in cm³ STP/g).
3. The **specific surface area (S_BET)** is obtained from:

$$S_{BET} = \frac{q_m \cdot N_A \cdot \sigma}{V_{STP}}$$

where N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹, σ = cross-sectional area of N₂ = **16.2 × 10⁻²⁰ m²/molecule**, and V_STP = 22,414 cm³/mol.

4. Standard test conditions: **N₂ adsorption at 77 K** (ASTM D3663, ISO 9277).

**Mark split:** Theory explanation (2) + assumptions and C (2) + final equation (1) + surface area significance (2).

---

**Q4(b). The following BET data were obtained for N₂ adsorption at 77 K on a catalyst. Determine the monolayer volume V_m and the BET constant C. (7 marks)**

| P/P° | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.20 | 0.25 | 0.30 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| q (cm³ STP/g) | 50 | 60 | 70 | 80 | 90 | 100 |

**Model Answer:**

**Linearized BET form:**

$$\frac{P/P^{\circ}}{q (1 - P/P^{\circ})} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P^{\circ}}$$

Compute y = (P/P°)/[q(1 − P/P°)] for each data point:

| P/P° | q (cm³/g) | 1 − P/P° | y = (P/P°)/[q(1−P/P°)] (g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.05 | 50 | 0.95 | 0.05/(50·0.95) = 0.001053 |
| 0.10 | 60 | 0.90 | 0.10/(60·0.90) = 0.001852 |
| 0.15 | 70 | 0.85 | 0.15/(70·0.85) = 0.002521 |
| 0.20 | 80 | 0.80 | 0.20/(80·0.80) = 0.003125 |
| 0.25 | 90 | 0.75 | 0.25/(90·0.75) = 0.003704 |
| 0.30 | 100 | 0.70 | 0.30/(100·0.70) = 0.004286 |

Linear regression y vs P/P°:

- **Slope (s) = (0.004286 − 0.001053)/(0.30 − 0.05) = 0.003233/0.25 = 0.01293 g/cm³**
- **Intercept (i) ≈ 0.000417 g/cm³** (read from extrapolation or compute mean: y − s·(P/P°))

Solving simultaneously:

$$V_m = \frac{1}{s + i} = \frac{1}{0.01293 + 0.000417} = \frac{1}{0.01335} = 74.9 \; \text{cm}^3\text{ STP/g}$$

$$C = \frac{s}{i} + 1 = \frac{0.01293}{0.000417} + 1 = 31.0 + 1 = 32.0$$

**Interpretation:** C > 2 indicates **Type II isotherm** (strong adsorbate-surface interaction), consistent with N₂ on a non-porous oxide catalyst.

**Mark split:** Linearization (1) + table computation (2) + slope and intercept (2) + V_m and C (2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** ΔH°_ads is **negative** (exothermic). The isosteric heat Q_st is reported as a **positive** number. Mixing up the two costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Missing standard-state declaration:** ΔG° is **only meaningful** when the standard state is specified. If a question asks for ΔG° in kJ/mol from K, you must state "dimensionless K, referenced to P° = 1 bar" or "K in Pa⁻¹". Omitting this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Inverting the Langmuir equation:** Many students write the linear form as **1/q vs 1/P** — this is the Lineweaver–Burk form, **not** Langmuir. The correct linear Langmuir is **P/q vs P**, with slope = 1/q_m and intercept = 1/(q_m K_L).
> 4. **BET range misuse:** The BET equation is valid **only in 0.05 < P/P° < 0.30**. Extrapolating to P/P° → 1 gives nonsense (denominator → 0).
> 5. **Confusing ΔH°_ads with E_activation:** Adsorption may be activated (chemisorption) or non-activated (physisorption). They are **different quantities**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**

- **Adsorption** is a **surface, exothermic (ΔH° < 0), entropy-decreasing (ΔS° < 0)** process. Spontaneity requires ΔG° < 0, which generally implies **low-to-moderate T**.
- **Three master equations** to memorize:
  1. ΔG°_ads = ΔH°_ads − T·ΔS°_ads
  2. ΔG°_ads = −RT ln K_ads
  3. (∂ ln P / ∂(1/T))_θ = −ΔH°_ads / R  ← *Clausius–Clapeyron for adsorption*
- **Langmuir:** monolayer, uniform sites, no interactions. Linear form: P/q = (1/q_m K_L) + P/q_m.
- **BET:** multilayer, E₁ ≠ E_L. Linear form: P/[q(P°−P)] = 1/(q_m C) + [(C−1)/(q_m C)]·(P/P°). Used for **S_BET** with N₂ at 77 K, σ = 16.2 × 10⁻²⁰ m².
- **Freundlich:** q = K_F·P^(1/n); empirical; heterogeneous surfaces; n > 1 for favorable adsorption.
- **Temkin:** q = B_T·ln(K_T·P); accounts for linearly decreasing heat of adsorption with coverage.
- **Sabatier's principle:** Best catalyst = **optimum ΔG°_ads** (neither too strong nor too weak).
- **TSA/PSA design** uses the **van't Hoff equation** d(ln K)/d(1/T) = −ΔH°/R.
- **Physisorption:** ΔH = −5 to −40 kJ/mol, no activation, multilayer, reversible.
- **Chemisorption:** ΔH = −40 to −400 kJ/mol, activation energy exists, monolayer, often irreversible.
- **IUPAC isotherm types:** I (microporous), II (non-porous, BET), III (weak interaction), IV (mesoporous + hysteresis), V (weak + mesoporous).
- **Cross-sectional area of N₂** at 77 K = **16.2 Å²** = 16.2 × 10⁻²⁰ m² — a numerical constant you must remember for S_BET calculations.
- **Henry's law limit:** at θ → 0, all isotherms reduce to q = K_H·P.
- **Isostere method:** plot ln P vs 1/T at fixed θ to extract ΔH°_ads (the most common experimental technique).
- **Units trap:** K is dimensionless only when referenced to P°; otherwise it has units of pressure⁻¹.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Adsorption and Chemisorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Adsorption and Chemisorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Foundational Definition of Adsorption

**Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon in which molecules of a fluid (the *adsorbate*) accumulate on the surface of a solid or liquid (the *adsorbent*) due to intermolecular forces acting at the interface. The process results in a higher concentration of the adsorbate at the surface than in the bulk phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The terms *adsorption* and *absorption* are NOT synonymous. *Adsorption* is a strictly two-dimensional surface accumulation, whereas *absorption* is a bulk phenomenon where molecules penetrate into the interior of a medium. Together, the combined phenomenon is termed *sorption*.

## 1.2 Physical Adsorption (Physisorption)

**Physical Adsorption**, also called **Physisorption**, is a type of adsorption driven by weak, non-specific **van der Waals forces** (also called dispersion forces or London forces) between the adsorbate and the adsorbent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Physisorption is a reversible, multi-layered adsorption process characterized by low heats of adsorption (typically **5–40 kJ/mol**), with no activation energy requirement, and preservation of the chemical identity of both adsorbate and adsorbent.

## 1.3 Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption)

**Chemical Adsorption**, also called **Chemisorption** or **Activated Adsorption**, is a type of adsorption involving the formation of genuine **chemical bonds** (covalent or ionic) between the adsorbate and the surface atoms of the adsorbent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Chemisorption is typically a monolayer, often irreversible adsorption process involving high heats of adsorption (typically **40–400 kJ/mol**), usually requiring an **activation energy**, and may involve electron transfer or bond rearrangement.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Magnet vs The Glue Analogy

Imagine you want to attach a paper notice to a refrigerator door:

| Mechanism | Analogy | Energy Required | Reversibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physisorption** | The notice is held by a **weak refrigerator magnet**. It sticks, but a gentle tug removes it without damaging either surface. | Very low — just a faint attractive force | Fully reversible |
| **Chemisorption** | The notice is attached using **strong industrial glue**. It cannot be removed without tearing the paper or leaving residue on the surface. | High — actual chemical bond formation | Irreversible or partially reversible only at high temperatures |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Insight:** Physisorption is like two strangers standing close because they enjoy each other's company (van der Waals attraction). Chemisorption is like two strangers forming a marriage contract (chemical bond) — far more committed and harder to undo.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Typical Physisorption Enthalpy:** **5–40 kJ/mol** (close to the heat of liquefaction of the adsorbate)
- **Typical Chemisorption Enthalpy:** **40–400 kJ/mol** (comparable to energies of chemical bond formation, often **> 80 kJ/mol**)
- **Standard State Reference Pressure:** **P° = 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**
- **Surface Coverage Symbol:** **θ (theta)**, with **0 ≤ θ ≤ 1** for monolayer systems
- **Avogadro's Number (used in monolayer capacity calculations):** **Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Monolayer capacity unit:** **cm³(STP)/g** or **mol/g**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physisorption — The "Why" and "How"

**Why does it occur?**
- All atoms and molecules exhibit instantaneous dipoles (electron cloud fluctuations) that induce complementary dipoles in nearby species.
- These induced-dipole–induced-dipole (London dispersion) interactions are always attractive and **non-specific** — any molecule can physisorb on essentially any surface.

**How does it occur?**
1. The adsorbate molecule approaches the surface.
2. At a critical distance (a few angstroms, typically **2–5 Å**), attractive van der Waals forces dominate over thermal agitation.
3. The molecule is trapped in a shallow **physical adsorption potential well** (depth = heat of physisorption, $q_p$).
4. **No activation energy** is required: physisorption is therefore fast and dominates at **low temperatures**.
5. After the first layer forms, additional layers can condense on top — leading to **multi-layer adsorption**.

## 2.2 Chemisorption — The "Why" and "How"

**Why does it occur?**
- Certain surface atoms (especially transition metals like Pt, Ni, Fe) have unfilled d-orbitals or dangling bonds that can form true covalent/ionic links with adsorbate molecules.
- The interaction is **chemically specific** — only certain pairs (e.g., O₂ on Pt, H₂ on Ni) will chemisorb strongly.

**How does it occur?**
1. The adsorbate approaches the surface.
2. Electron density redistributes — partial sharing (covalent) or transfer (ionic) occurs.
3. An **activation energy barrier ($E_a$)** must be overcome — hence the name *activated adsorption*.
4. The molecule resides in a deep **chemical potential well** (depth = heat of chemisorption, $q_c$).
5. **Strictly monolayer** in nature — once all surface sites are occupied, no further chemisorption occurs.

## 2.3 Energetic Profile Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The Lennard-Jones Potential Diagram is the most frequently asked visual comparison. It shows how the potential energy of an adsorbate varies with its distance from the surface. Physisorption appears as a shallow well at a larger distance; chemisorption as a deep well at a smaller distance, separated by an activation barrier $E_a$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Use / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Extent of Adsorption** | $\dfrac{x}{m}$ (mass adsorbed per mass of adsorbent) | Used in Freundlich isotherm and lab data analysis |
| 2 | **Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = k \cdot P^{1/n}$ (gas) ; $\dfrac{x}{m} = k \cdot C^{1/n}$ (solution) | Empirical; describes heterogeneous surfaces |
| 3 | **Freundlich Linear Form** | $\log\left(\dfrac{x}{m}\right) = \log k + \dfrac{1}{n}\log P$ | Plot $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log P$ → slope $1/n$, intercept $\log k$ |
| 4 | **Langmuir Isotherm** | $\theta = \dfrac{KP}{1+KP}$ | Monolayer, uniform sites, no interaction between adsorbates |
| 5 | **Langmuir Linear Form** | $\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{1}{V_m K} + \dfrac{P}{V_m}$ | $P/V$ vs $P$ gives slope $1/V_m$, intercept $1/(V_m K)$ |
| 6 | **BET Isotherm** | $\dfrac{P}{V(P^0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C}\cdot\dfrac{P}{P^0}$ | Multi-layer adsorption; **standard for surface area measurement** |
| 7 | **BET Constant** | $C \approx \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q_1 - q_L}{RT}\right)$ | $q_1$ = heat of first-layer adsorption, $q_L$ = heat of liquefaction |
| 8 | **Surface Area from BET** | $S = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot A_{cs}}{V_{STP} \cdot m}$ | $A_{cs}$ = cross-sectional area of adsorbate (e.g., **0.162 nm²** for N₂) |
| 9 | **Polanyi Adsorption Potential** | $\varepsilon = RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P^0}{P}\right)$ | Characteristic curve for vapor adsorption |
| 10 | **Arrhenius for Adsorption Rate** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $E_a$ = activation energy of adsorption |
| 11 | **Isosteric Heat of Adsorption (Clausius–Clapeyron)** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln P}{\partial T}\right)_{\theta} = \dfrac{q_{st}}{RT^2}$ | Used to find $q_{st}$ from isosteres |
| 12 | **Physisorption Heat Range** | $5 \leq q_p \leq 40$ kJ/mol | Distinguishing benchmark |
| 13 | **Chemisorption Heat Range** | $40 \leq q_c \leq 400$ kJ/mol | Distinguishing benchmark |
| 14 | **Adsorption Entropy Change** | $\Delta S_{ads} < 0$ | Adsorbate loses translational freedom on surface |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Safety:** All vertical bars inside the table cells (such as in the BET constant expression and the isostere equation) have been written as plain-text fallback equivalents or are clearly outside any single pipe that would break markdown. The actual formula uses standard mathematical grouping with parentheses.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Heterogeneous Catalysis:** Chemisorption is the *first essential step* in catalytic reactions (e.g., H₂ chemisorbs on Ni in hydrogenation).
2. **Gas Mask / Respirators:** Activated carbon uses physisorption to trap toxic gases in military and industrial safety equipment.
3. **Cryogenic Storage & Gas Purification:** Silica gel and zeolites physically adsorb moisture and impurities.
4. **Catalyst Characterization (BET):** Nitrogen physisorption at **77 K** is the global standard for measuring **specific surface area** of porous catalysts.
5. **Chromatography:** Both modes underlie separation science.
6. **Pollution Control (Scrubbers):** Chemisorption on metal oxides removes SO₂ and NOₓ from flue gases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

### 3.1.1 Statement of Assumptions

Langmuir (1918) proposed the following idealized model:

1. The surface possesses a **fixed number of adsorption sites**, all of which are **energetically equivalent** (uniform surface).
2. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule** (monolayer coverage).
3. There is **no interaction** between molecules adsorbed on neighboring sites.
4. Adsorption is **reversible**: a dynamic equilibrium exists between adsorbed and free molecules.

### 3.1.2 Defining the Equilibrium

Let:
- $\theta$ = fraction of surface sites occupied (surface coverage)
- $1 - \theta$ = fraction of vacant sites
- $P$ = pressure of gas above the surface
- $k_a$ = rate constant for adsorption
- $k_d$ = rate constant for desorption

The elementary steps are:

$$
A(g) + S \xrightleftharpoons[k_d]{k_a} A\text{–}S
$$

where $S$ denotes a vacant surface site and $A\text{–}S$ denotes an occupied site.

### 3.1.3 Rate of Adsorption

Rate of adsorption is proportional to the pressure of gas and the number of vacant sites:

$$
r_a = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)
$$

### 3.1.4 Rate of Desorption

Rate of desorption is proportional only to the number of occupied sites (no $P$ dependence, since the molecule is leaving the surface):

$$
r_d = k_d \cdot \theta
$$

### 3.1.5 Applying Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium, $r_a = r_d$:

$$
k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta
$$

### 3.1.6 Solving for $\theta$

Rearranging — divide both sides by $k_d$:

$$
\dfrac{k_a}{k_d} \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = \theta
$$

Define the **adsorption equilibrium constant** $K = \dfrac{k_a}{k_d}$:

$$
K \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = \theta
$$

Expand the left side:

$$
K P - K P \theta = \theta
$$

Group all $\theta$ terms on the right side:

$$
K P = \theta + K P \theta
$$

$$
K P = \theta \,(1 + K P)
$$

Divide both sides by $(1 + K P)$:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{K P}{1 + K P}
$$

### 3.1.7 Linearized Form (for Experimental Plotting)

Take the reciprocal:

$$
\dfrac{1}{\theta} = \dfrac{1 + K P}{K P} = \dfrac{1}{K P} + 1
$$

Multiply both sides by $P$ (or rewrite in terms of the volume adsorbed $V$, where $\theta = V/V_m$, and $V_m$ is monolayer volume):

$$
\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{1}{V_m K} + \dfrac{P}{V_m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Langmuir linear plot of $P/V$ versus $P$ yields a straight line. The slope gives $1/V_m$ and the intercept gives $1/(V_m K)$. From $V_m$, one can calculate monolayer capacity, and then with the cross-sectional area of the adsorbate molecule, the **specific surface area** of the adsorbent.

## 3.2 Derivation Sketch of the BET Isotherm

The BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, 1938) isotherm extends Langmuir to **multi-layer** physisorption.

### 3.2.1 Key Assumptions

1. The heat of adsorption for the **first layer** is $q_1$ (constant and uniform).
2. The heat of adsorption for the **second and subsequent layers** equals the heat of liquefaction $q_L$.
3. The Langmuir assumptions apply to each layer.
4. At equilibrium, the rate of condensation onto layer $i$ equals the rate of evaporation from layer $i$.

### 3.2.2 Defining the BET Parameter

Define a parameter $x = P / P^0$, where $P^0$ is the saturation vapor pressure. Define:

$$
C = \dfrac{a_1 b_2}{a_2 b_1} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q_1 - q_L}{RT}\right) \approx \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q_1 - q_L}{RT}\right)
$$

where $a_i$ and $b_i$ are the condensation and evaporation rate constants for layer $i$.

### 3.2.3 The Final BET Equation

The total volume adsorbed $V$ at relative pressure $P/P^0$ is given by:

$$
\dfrac{P}{V\,(P^0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m\,C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m\,C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P^0}
$$

### 3.2.4 Linear Plot

A plot of $\dfrac{P}{V(P^0 - P)}$ (y-axis) versus $\dfrac{P}{P^0}$ (x-axis) gives a straight line:

- **Slope** $= \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C}$
- **Intercept** $= \dfrac{1}{V_m C}$
- **Monolayer volume** $V_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}}$

### 3.2.5 Surface Area Calculation

The specific surface area is then:

$$
S = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot A_{cs}}{V_{STP} \cdot m}
$$

where $V_{STP} = 22{,}414$ cm³/mol, and $A_{cs} = 0.162$ nm² for N₂ at 77 K.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Physisorption vs Chemisorption

| # | Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Adsorbing Force** | Van der Waals (London dispersion) | Covalent / ionic chemical bond |
| 2 | **Heat of Adsorption** | Low (5–40 kJ/mol) | High (40–400 kJ/mol) |
| 3 | **Activation Energy** | ~ 0 (none) | Significant (often > 0) |
| 4 | **Temperature Dependence** | Favored at low T (exothermic) | May occur at higher T (after $E_a$ overcome) |
| 5 | **Reversibility** | Highly reversible | Often irreversible |
| 6 | **Layers Formed** | Multi-layer possible | Strictly mono-layer |
| 7 | **Specificity** | Non-specific (any gas on any surface) | Highly specific (depends on chemistry) |
| 8 | **Effect on Catalysis** | Not catalytic by itself | First step in heterogeneous catalysis |
| 9 | **Pressure Dependence** | Strong (multilayers at high P) | Saturates quickly (monolayer) |
| 10 | **Effect of Surface Area** | Strong; pore-filling important | Moderate; only external sites matter |
| 11 | **Surface Coverage vs P** | Continuous, no clear saturation | Sharp saturation at $\theta = 1$ |
| 12 | **Surface Migration** | Easy (molecules glide on surface) | Restricted (locked in place) |
| 13 | **Enthalpy–Liquefaction Comparison** | $q_p \approx$ heat of liquefaction | $q_c \gg$ heat of liquefaction |
| 14 | **Effect on Electronic Properties** | Negligible | Major (work function changes, etc.) |
| 15 | **Desorption Temperature** | Low T (often below boiling point of adsorbate) | High T required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lennard-Jones Potential Energy Diagram (Schematic Representation)

The following Mermaid flowchart represents the energetic pathway of an adsorbate molecule approaching a solid surface, illustrating both the physisorption well and the chemisorption well separated by an activation barrier.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA(["FARAWAY FROM SURFACE: PEAK ENERGY AT INFINITY"]):::freeNode
    nodeB["PHYSICAL ADSORPTION WELL SHALLOW MINIMUM AT DISTANCE 2 TO 5 ANGSTROM HEAT QP APPROX 5 TO 40 KJ PER MOL"]:::phyNode
    nodeC["ACTIVATION BARRIER EA MOLECULE MUST CLIMB THIS HILL"]:::barNode
    nodeD["CHEMICAL ADSORPTION WELL DEEP MINIMUM AT DISTANCE 1 TO 2 ANGSTROM HEAT QC APPROX 40 TO 400 KJ PER MOL"]:::chemNode
    nodeE["MOLECULE LOCKED ON SURFACE FORMING A STRONG CHEMICAL BOND"]:::lockedNode
    nodeA -->|"APPROACHES SURFACE" nodeB
    nodeB -->|"MUST OVERCOME BARRIER" nodeC
    nodeC -->|"DESCENDS INTO WELL" nodeD
    nodeD -->|"STABLE ADSORBED STATE" nodeE
    classDef freeNode fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91
    classDef phyNode fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F4B400,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A4F01
    classDef barNode fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A0E0E
    classDef chemNode fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D5226
    classDef lockedNode fill:#D7CCC8,stroke:#5D4037,stroke-width:2px,color:#3E2723
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The horizontal axis represents the perpendicular distance of the adsorbate from the surface; the vertical axis represents the potential energy. A molecule undergoing **physisorption** is trapped in the shallow well on the right. To reach the **chemisorbed state**, it must climb the central activation barrier.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Adsorption Workflow

The following block diagram maps out the operational sequence of an adsorption experiment or process — from gas contact to surface characterization.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inletA["BULK GAS PHASE ADSORBATE STREAM"]:::inNode
    inletB["ADSORBENT SOLID OR POROUS MATERIAL"]:::adsNode
    procA["MASS TRANSPORT DIFFUSION TO SURFACE"]:::procNode
    procB["SURFACE DIFFUSION SEEKING ACTIVE SITE"]:::procNode
    procC["ADSORPTION STEP EITHER PHYSICAL OR CHEMICAL"]:::coreNode
    outA["OCCUPIED SURFACE SITES MONITORED BY BET OR TGA"]:::monNode
    outB["DESORPTION PRODUCT STREAM FOR REGENERATION"]:::regenNode
    ctrlA["TEMPERATURE AND PRESSURE CONTROL FEEDBACK LOOP"]:::ctrlNode
    inletA --> procA
    inletB --> procA
    procA --> procB
    procB --> procC
    procC --> outA
    procC --> outB
    ctrlA -.->|"ADJUSTS P AND T" procC
    classDef inNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B
    classDef adsNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef procNode fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef coreNode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:3px,color:#7F0000
    classDef monNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef regenNode fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#558B2F,stroke-width:2px,color:#33691E
    classDef ctrlNode fill:#CFD8DC,stroke:#455A64,stroke-width:2px,color:#263238
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Is It Physisorption or Chemisorption?

Use this diagnostic flowchart to identify the type of adsorption from experimental observations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["OBSERVED ADSORPTION EVENT"]):::startNode
    q1["MEASURE HEAT OF ADSORPTION Q"]:::decideNode
    qLow{"Q LESS THAN 40 KJ PER MOL?"}:::decideNode
    qHigh{"Q GREATER THAN 40 KJ PER MOL?"}:::decideNode
    rev{"EASILY DESORBED AT LOW TEMPERATURE?"}:::decideNode
    layer{"MULTIPLE LAYERS OBSERVED?"}:::decideNode
    act{"ACTIVATION ENERGY REQUIRED?"}:::decideNode
    outPhy["CONCLUSION PHYSICAL ADSORPTION"]:::phyRes
    outChem["CONCLUSION CHEMICAL ADSORPTION"]:::chemRes
    start --> q1
    q1 --> qLow
    q1 --> qHigh
    qLow --> rev
    rev -->|"YES" layer
    layer -->|"YES" outPhy
    qHigh --> act
    act -->|"YES" outChem
    classDef startNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef decideNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#F57F17
    classDef phyRes fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:3px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef chemRes fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:3px,color:#7F0000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark and 3-Mark Variants)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between physical adsorption and chemical adsorption with respect to (i) heat of adsorption, (ii) reversibility, and (iii) number of layers formed.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Physical Adsorption | Chemical Adsorption |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Heat of Adsorption** | Low, in the range **5–40 kJ/mol** (comparable to heat of liquefaction) | High, in the range **40–400 kJ/mol** (comparable to chemical bond energies) |
| (ii) **Reversibility** | Highly reversible — the adsorbate can be removed easily by lowering pressure or slightly raising temperature | Generally irreversible — desorption is difficult, requires high temperature and may even change the species |
| (iii) **Number of Layers** | Multi-layer adsorption possible (especially near the saturation pressure $P^0$) | Strictly mono-layer — limited to one molecule per active site |

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the assumptions of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm.**

**Model Answer:**

The Langmuir isotherm is based on the following four assumptions:

1. The adsorbent surface possesses a **fixed and finite number of adsorption sites**.
2. **All sites are energetically equivalent** — the heat of adsorption is constant and independent of surface coverage.
3. **Each site can hold only one adsorbate molecule** — leading to monolayer coverage.
4. **There is no lateral interaction** between molecules adsorbed on adjacent sites, and the process is reversible, reaching dynamic equilibrium.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Module 1, Choice 1]`

**(a) Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and discuss its significance. (7 Marks)**
**(b) The following data were obtained for the adsorption of N₂ on a catalyst surface at 77 K. Calculate the monolayer volume $V_m$ and the Langmuir equilibrium constant $K$:**

| $P$ (torr) | 50 | 100 | 200 | 300 | 400 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V$ (cm³/g) | 32 | 56 | 84 | 102 | 116 |

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The Langmuir isotherm derivation begins by writing the elementary equilibrium for adsorption on a uniform surface:

$$
A(g) + S \xrightleftharpoons[k_d]{k_a} A\text{–}S
$$

The rate of adsorption is proportional to the gas pressure and the fraction of vacant sites $(1 - \theta)$:

$$
r_a = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)
$$

The rate of desorption is proportional to the fraction of occupied sites $\theta$:

$$
r_d = k_d \cdot \theta
$$

At equilibrium, $r_a = r_d$:

$$
k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta
$$

Dividing both sides by $k_d$ and substituting $K = k_a/k_d$:

$$
K P (1 - \theta) = \theta
$$

Expanding and grouping terms:

$$
K P - K P \theta = \theta \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K P = \theta (1 + K P)
$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{K P}{1 + K P}
$$

Since the volume adsorbed is proportional to $\theta$, we have $V = V_m \theta$. Substituting:

$$
V = \dfrac{V_m K P}{1 + K P}
$$

Rearranging into linear form:

$$
\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{1}{V_m K} + \dfrac{P}{V_m}
$$

**Significance:**
- It is the **first theoretical isotherm** in adsorption science.
- Predicts **monolayer saturation** — at high $P$, $\theta \to 1$.
- The **Langmuir constant $K$** reflects the strength of the adsorbate–adsorbent interaction.
- Forms the foundation for the BET multi-layer extension.
- Directly used to calculate **specific surface area** of catalysts and porous solids.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Using the linear form, compute $P/V$ for each data point:

| $P$ (torr) | $V$ (cm³/g) | $P/V$ (torr·g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|
| 50 | 32 | 1.5625 |
| 100 | 56 | 1.7857 |
| 200 | 84 | 2.3810 |
| 300 | 102 | 2.9412 |
| 400 | 116 | 3.4483 |

**Linear regression** of $P/V$ versus $P$:

Let $x = P$, $y = P/V$. For the five data points:

- $\sum x = 50 + 100 + 200 + 300 + 400 = 1050$
- $\sum y = 1.5625 + 1.7857 + 2.3810 + 2.9412 + 3.4483 = 12.1187$
- $\bar{x} = 210$, $\bar{y} = 2.4237$
- $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) \approx 717.86$
- $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 70000$

**Slope** $= 717.86 / 70000 \approx 0.01026$ per cm³
**Intercept** $\approx 2.4237 - 0.01026 \times 210 \approx 0.2692$ torr·g/cm³

Therefore:
- $1/V_m = 0.01026$ $\Rightarrow$ $V_m \approx 97.5$ cm³/g
- $1/(V_m K) = 0.2692$ $\Rightarrow$ $K \approx 0.01026 / 0.2692 \approx 0.0381$ torr⁻¹

**Final Answer:** $V_m \approx 97.5$ cm³/g, $K \approx 0.038$ torr⁻¹

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Module 1, Choice 2]`

**(a) Describe the BET adsorption isotherm. How is it used to determine the specific surface area of a porous solid? (7 Marks)**
**(b) Compare physical adsorption and chemisorption across TEN different properties. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — BET Isotherm Description and Surface Area:**

The BET isotherm (1938) extends the Langmuir theory to **multi-layer** physical adsorption. It assumes that:

- The heat of adsorption for the **first layer** is a constant $q_1$.
- The heat of adsorption for **all subsequent layers** equals the heat of liquefaction $q_L$ of the adsorbate.
- Each layer follows Langmuir-type behavior.
- At equilibrium, the rate of condensation onto any layer equals the rate of evaporation from it.

The BET equation in its standard linear form is:

$$
\dfrac{P}{V(P^0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P^0}
$$

where:
- $P$ = equilibrium pressure
- $P^0$ = saturation vapor pressure of the adsorbate
- $V$ = volume of gas adsorbed at STP
- $V_m$ = monolayer volume
- $C$ = BET constant, $C \approx \exp[(q_1 - q_L)/RT]$

**Procedure for Surface Area Determination:**

1. **Measure** the adsorption of a gas (typically **N₂ at 77 K**, the boiling point of liquid nitrogen) at several relative pressures $P/P^0$ in the range **0.05 to 0.30**.
2. **Plot** $\dfrac{P}{V(P^0 - P)}$ on the y-axis versus $\dfrac{P}{P^0}$ on the x-axis — a straight line is obtained.
3. **Determine slope and intercept:**
   - Slope $= (C-1)/(V_m C)$
   - Intercept $= 1/(V_m C)$
4. **Calculate $V_m$:**
   - $V_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}}$
5. **Compute surface area:**
   - The number of molecules in a monolayer: $n = V_m / V_{STP}$, where $V_{STP} = 22{,}414$ cm³/mol
   - Total area: $A = n \cdot N_A \cdot A_{cs}$
   - Specific surface area: $S = A / m$, where $m$ is the sample mass
   - For N₂ at 77 K, $A_{cs} = 0.162$ nm²

**Part (b) — Ten-Property Comparison Table:**

| # | Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Adsorbing force** | Van der Waals forces | Covalent / ionic chemical bonds |
| 2 | **Heat of adsorption** | 5–40 kJ/mol | 40–400 kJ/mol |
| 3 | **Activation energy** | Essentially zero | Significant positive value |
| 4 | **Number of layers** | Multi-layer possible | Monolayer only |
| 5 | **Reversibility** | Highly reversible | Often irreversible |
| 6 | **Specificity** | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| 7 | **Temperature of occurrence** | Low (below critical T) | Higher (needs to overcome $E_a$) |
| 8 | **Effect on surface properties** | Negligible | Changes work function, conductivity |
| 9 | **Rate of process** | Very fast | May be slow due to activation step |
| 10 | **Role in catalysis** | Generally not catalytic | Critical first step in heterogeneous catalysis |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Mixing up adsorption and absorption:** Students often use these terms interchangeably — this is a direct 1-mark deduction in KTU valuations. Memorize: *adsorption = surface; absorption = bulk.*
> 2. **Heat of adsorption units:** KTU examiners check for **correct units (kJ/mol)** in numerical answers. Writing "J/g" without conversion is penalized.
> 3. **Langmuir vs BET applicability:** Many students apply the **Langmuir isotherm to multi-layer systems** (e.g., N₂ at 77 K near saturation) — this is **incorrect**. The Langmuir equation is for monolayer only; use **BET** for multi-layer physisorption.
> 4. **Forgetting the saturation pressure $P^0$:** In BET problems, students frequently use $P$ directly instead of the **relative pressure $P/P^0$** — this guarantees wrong numerical answers.
> 5. **Sign of activation energy:** Chemisorption does not *always* have an activation energy; some systems (e.g., H₂ on clean Pt) chemisorb with **near-zero $E_a$**. Do not over-generalize.
> 6. **Insufficient diagrams:** In 14-mark derivation questions, the **Lennard-Jones potential diagram** is expected alongside the algebraic derivation. Skipping the diagram costs at least **2 marks**.
> 7. **Confusing monolayer capacity with total volume:** $V_m$ is the volume corresponding to **one complete monolayer**, not the total adsorbed volume.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Adsorption** = surface phenomenon; **Absorption** = bulk phenomenon; **Sorption** = both combined.
- **Physisorption** uses **van der Waals forces**; **Chemisorption** uses **chemical bonds**.
- Physisorption heat: **5–40 kJ/mol**; Chemisorption heat: **40–400 kJ/mol**.
- Physisorption: **multi-layer**, **reversible**, **no activation energy**, **non-specific**, favored at **low temperature**.
- Chemisorption: **mono-layer**, **often irreversible**, **activation energy** present, **highly specific**, may occur at **higher temperature**.
- **Langmuir Isotherm** — monolayer on uniform sites: $\theta = \dfrac{KP}{1+KP}$, linear form $\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{1}{V_m K} + \dfrac{P}{V_m}$.
- **BET Isotherm** — multi-layer physisorption; standard method for **specific surface area** of porous solids.
- **BET linear form** is the only form plotted experimentally — using it gives slope and intercept from which $V_m$ and $C$ are obtained.
- **N₂ at 77 K** with cross-sectional area **$A_{cs} = 0.162$ nm²** is the standard probe molecule for BET.
- **Freundlich isotherm** is empirical — $\dfrac{x}{m} = k \cdot P^{1/n}$ — useful for heterogeneous surfaces and low-pressure data.
- **Lennard-Jones potential** is the standard energy-distance profile; physisorption well is shallow and far, chemisorption well is deep and close, with an **activation barrier** in between.
- **Chemisorption is the obligatory first step in heterogeneous catalysis** — without it, the catalytic cycle cannot begin.
- **Physisorption is the standard probe** for textural characterization (surface area, pore size distribution, pore volume).
- **Polanyi potential** $\varepsilon = RT \ln(P^0/P)$ describes the adsorption potential at the adsorbed phase.
- **Isosteric heat** $q_{st}$ is obtained from the **Clausius–Clapeyron** relation on isosteres.
- **Catalyst regeneration** (desorption) is the final step that frees the active site for another cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalyst Preparative Methods - Precipitation and Co-Precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Catalyst Preparative Methods - Precipitation and Co-Precipitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In heterogeneous catalysis, the **active phase**, **support**, and **promoter** of a solid catalyst are seldom produced in their final usable form directly. They are usually synthesized as finely divided, hydrated, amorphous or poorly crystalline precursor solids that are later transformed (by calcination, reduction, etc.) into the active catalytic material. **Precipitation** and **co-precipitation** are the two most industrially relevant *wet-chemical preparative routes* used to obtain such precursor solids from homogeneous aqueous (or sometimes non-aqueous) solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim spirit):**
> *Precipitation is a process in which a soluble component in a liquid phase is converted into an insoluble solid by a chemical reaction, whereas co-precipitation is the simultaneous precipitation of more than one soluble component from a common solution to form a mixed or composite precursor solid possessing high homogeneity at the molecular/atomic level.*

Concretely, when an aqueous solution of a metal salt (e.g., $\text{Al(NO}_3\text{)}_3$, $\text{Ni(NO}_3\text{)}_2$, $\text{CuSO}_4$) is contacted with a precipitating agent (typically $\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$, $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, $\text{(NH}_4\text{)}_2\text{CO}_3$ or $\text{KOH}$), the metal cation is converted into an insoluble hydroxide, basic salt, or carbonate that comes out of solution as a finely divided solid phase. The resulting **gel** or **amorphous precipitate** is then filtered, washed, dried, calcined and reduced to yield the final catalyst.

- **Precipitation**: only **one** cation is converted into an insoluble solid; the product is a single-component precursor.
- **Co-precipitation**: **two or more** cations are converted *simultaneously* into a mixed, often homogeneously distributed, insoluble precursor. This is the principal route to **multi-component catalysts** such as $\text{CuO–ZnO–Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (methanol synthesis), $\text{NiO–Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (hydrogenation), and $\text{Fe–Cu–K}$ (Fischer–Tropsch).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Sweet Tea Gone Cold":**
> Imagine preparing sweet tea. While the tea is hot, the sugar dissolves completely and the solution is clear (analogue: a homogeneous salt solution). As the tea cools, the solution reaches *supersaturation* — it holds more dissolved material than equilibrium allows. Tiny sugar crystals spontaneously *nucleate* (analogue: primary nucleation of catalyst particles), then existing crystals *grow* by deposition of more sugar (analogue: particle growth), and the smallest crystals slowly dissolve and re-deposit on larger ones (analogue: **Ostwald ripening**, leading to larger, more thermally stable primary particles).
> 
> Now extend the analogy: if you dissolve *both* sugar and a pinch of salt together and cool the tea, both will come out together, but they are *not* perfectly distributed at the molecular level — the sugar-rich and salt-rich micro-regions form a mixed crystal. **Co-precipitation** in catalyst preparation behaves similarly: two or more cations come out together, ideally giving an *atomic-scale mixture* of the metal hydroxides/carbonates so that, after calcination/reduction, the active metal sites are uniformly dispersed on the support.

### 1.3 Why This Method Dominates Industrial Catalyst Manufacture

- It is **cheap, scalable, and uses only aqueous chemistry** (no vacuum, plasma, or exotic atmospheres).
- It yields **high-purity** precursors with controllable **particle size** and **porosity**.
- It enables **atomic-level mixing** of multiple metals (impossible to obtain by simple mechanical mixing).
- It allows precise tuning of **BET surface area** (often $100$–$500\,\text{m}^2/\text{g}$) and **pore volume**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key metric highlighted in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1:**
> A *well-executed* co-precipitation gives a precursor in which the cation of interest is *coprecipitated within the lattice of the host precipitate* rather than merely adsorbed on its outer surface. This is the gold standard for high-dispersion industrial catalysts, because dispersion determines the **turnover frequency (TOF)** and the **metal-time-yield** of the final catalyst.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Precipitation Process — A Five-Stage Sequence

Any precipitation, whether single- or multi-component, proceeds through the following mandatory stages. Mastering this sequence is essential for the *Part A* and *Part B* questions on Module 1.

1. **Selection of precursor salt and precipitant**
   - Salt: usually nitrate, sulfate, chloride or acetate of the active metal.
   - Precipitant: $\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$, $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{KOH}$, $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, $\text{(NH}_4\text{)}_2\text{CO}_3$, urea (for *homogeneous* precipitation).
2. **Generation of supersaturation (ΔC)**
   - Supersaturation is the *driving force* of precipitation.
   - Achieved by: changing pH, mixing two solutions, cooling a hot saturated solution, or solvent change.
3. **Nucleation** (formation of stable nuclei of critical radius $r^*$)
   - Two types: *primary* (homogeneous vs heterogeneous) and *secondary*.
4. **Growth of nuclei** into primary particles by addition of solute species.
5. **Aging / Ostwald ripening** — small particles dissolve, large particles grow; aggregates form.

### 2.2 Nucleation — The "Why and How"

A solute molecule in solution has to overcome an *energy barrier* $\Delta G^{*}$ to form a stable nucleus. According to **Classical Nucleation Theory (CNT)**, the free energy change for the formation of a spherical nucleus of radius $r$ is:

$$\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s k_B T \ln S + 4\pi r^2 \gamma$$

- The first term is the **bulk free-energy gain** (proportional to $r^3$ and to $\ln S$).
- The second term is the **surface-energy cost** (proportional to $r^2$ and to interfacial tension $\gamma$).
- $S$ is the **supersaturation ratio**, $S = C/C_s$, where $C$ is the actual concentration and $C_s$ is the equilibrium solubility at the operating temperature.

Setting $d\Delta G/dr = 0$ gives the **critical nucleus radius**:

$$r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{R T \ln S}$$

and the **activation barrier for nucleation**:

$$\Delta G^{*} = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^3 V_m^{2}}{3 (R T \ln S)^{2}}$$

The **steady-state nucleation rate** is:

$$B = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^{*}}{k_B T}\right) = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{16\pi \gamma^{3}V_{m}^{2}}{3 k_{B}^{3}T^{3}(\ln S)^{2}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering insight (often asked in KTU ESE):**
> A **small increase in $\ln S$ (i.e. high supersaturation)** dramatically *reduces* $\Delta G^{*}$, so the nucleation rate *explodes*. This produces *many small nuclei* → final particles are **fine and uniform**. Conversely, **low supersaturation** favours growth over nucleation → larger, often broader-distribution crystals. Catalyst preparators control particle size principally by *controlling supersaturation*.

### 2.3 Particle Growth

After nucleation, growth proceeds by one or more of:
- **Diffusion-controlled growth** (rate $\propto (C - C_s)$, valid at low supersaturation).
- **Surface-reaction-controlled (mononuclear/polynuclear) growth** (rate $\propto (C - C_s)^n$).
- **Aggregation / coagulation** of primary particles into secondary aggregates (very common in catalyst precipitates — controls pore structure).

A widely used empirical expression for the mean particle size of the precipitate is:

$$\bar{d} \propto \left(\frac{\text{Growth rate}}{\text{Nucleation rate}}\right)^{1/4} = \left(\frac{k_g (C - C_s)}{A \exp(-\Delta G^{*}/k_BT)}\right)^{1/4}$$

### 2.4 Co-Precipitation — Mechanism of Incorporation

When a second cation $\text{M}^{n+}$ is present in solution alongside the host cation $\text{A}^{n+}$, the co-precipitate can form by one (or combination) of the following *incorporation mechanisms* — these are **direct KTU exam favourites**:

| Mechanism | Description | Resulting Homogeneity |
|---|---|---|
| **Adsorption / surface co-precipitation** | Second cation adsorbs on the surface of the growing host precipitate. | Surface-rich, poor bulk homogeneity. |
| **Occlusion** | Second cation is mechanically trapped inside pores of a rapidly growing host crystal. | Moderate; depends on growth rate. |
| **Inclusion (lattice incorporation)** | Second cation is built into the host crystal lattice (e.g. isovalent substitution). | Good bulk homogeneity. |
| **Isomorphous replacement** | Second cation has a similar ionic radius and charge; substitutes directly for the host cation in the lattice. | Excellent — atomic-level dispersion. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial example:** In the co-precipitation of $\text{Cu}^{2+}$, $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ and $\text{Al}^{3+}$ with $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ to form the precursor of the **low-pressure methanol synthesis catalyst** ($\text{CuO/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3$), the $\text{Al}^{3+}$ substitutes isomorphously for $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ in the **hydrotalcite-like** $(\text{Cu,Zn})_6\text{Al}_2(\text{OH})_{16}\text{CO}_3 \cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$ precursor, providing *atomic-level dispersion* of Al in the final catalyst and preventing Cu particle sintering.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbols | Units / Form | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Supersaturation ratio | $S = C/C_s$ | dimensionless | Drive force of pptn. |
| 2 | Critical nucleus radius | $r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{RT \ln S}$ | m | Predicts smallest stable particle. |
| 3 | Nucleation activation barrier | $\Delta G^{*} = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^{3} V_{m}^{2}}{3(RT\ln S)^{2}}$ | J mol$^{-1}$ | Higher $\ln S$ → smaller $\Delta G^{*}$. |
| 4 | Steady-state nucleation rate | $B = A\exp(-\Delta G^{*}/k_BT)$ | nuclei m$^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$ | How many nuclei form. |
| 5 | Solubility product (binary salt) | $K_{sp} = [\text{A}^{a+}][\text{B}^{b-}]$ | mol$^{a+b}$ L$^{-(a+b)}$ | Predicts pptn onset pH. |
| 6 | Ostwald ripening (Gibbs–Thomson) | $\ln\!\left(\frac{C_r}{C_\infty}\right) = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{rRT}$ | dimensionless | Explains aging. |
| 7 | Mean particle size | $\bar{d} \propto (\text{growth}/\text{nucleation})^{1/4}$ | m | Tune final dispersion. |
| 8 | Surface area of spherical particle | $S_{BET} = \frac{6}{\rho_p \bar{d}}$ | m$^2$ g$^{-1}$ | Link particle size ↔ surface area. |
| 9 | Supersaturation generation by mixing | $S = \frac{C_{\text{after mix}}}{C_s(T, pH)}$ | dimensionless | Real-time process control. |
| 10 | Von Weimarn stability ratio | $W = \frac{C_s}{C - C_s}$ | dimensionless | Lower $W$ → finer precipitate. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX RULE (for the student writing the exam):** In KTU answer scripts, absolute value or "vertical bar" should be written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never as $|x|$, which can clash with table cell delimiters in any tabulated answer. This is the *same safeguard* the engine enforces here.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Methanol synthesis** ($\text{CuO/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3$) — co-precipitation of $\text{Cu}$, $\text{Zn}$, $\text{Al}$ carbonates.
- **Water-gas shift** ($\text{CuO/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3$) — same family, optimized by co-precipitation pH.
- **Steam reforming** ($\text{NiO/Al}_2\text{O}_3$) — co-precipitation of Ni and Al hydroxides.
- **Fischer–Tropsch** ($\text{Fe–Cu–K/SiO}_2$) — co-precipitation of Fe–Cu followed by impregnation of K.
- **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** — co-precipitation of $\text{Ni-Mo}$ or $\text{Co-Mo}$ oxo-hydroxides.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Nucleus Radius $r^{*}$

**Starting point:** For a spherical embryo of a new phase of radius $r$ forming in a supersaturated solution, the change in Gibbs free energy of the system is the sum of a *bulk term* (favourable) and a *surface term* (unfavourable):

$$
\Delta G(r) = \Delta G_{\text{bulk}} + \Delta G_{\text{surface}}
$$

**Step 1 — write the bulk term.**
The bulk free energy released when a volume $V$ of solid forms from a solution of supersaturation $S = C/C_s > 1$ is:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{bulk}} = -V \cdot \rho_s \cdot k_B T \ln S
$$

with $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$ and $\rho_s$ the number density of molecules in the solid.

**Step 2 — write the surface term.**

$$
\Delta G_{\text{surface}} = A \cdot \gamma = 4\pi r^{2} \gamma
$$

**Step 3 — combine.**

$$
\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,\rho_s\,k_BT \ln S \;+\; 4\pi r^{2}\gamma \tag{1}
$$

**Step 4 — locate the maximum (critical nucleus).**
Set $d\Delta G/dr = 0$:

$$
\frac{d\Delta G}{dr} = -4\pi r^{2}\,\rho_s\,k_BT \ln S \;+\; 8\pi r\,\gamma = 0
$$

Divide by $4\pi r$ (with $r > 0$):

$$
- r\,\rho_s\,k_BT \ln S \;+\; 2\gamma = 0
$$

Solve for $r$:

$$
\boxed{\,r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma}{\rho_s\,k_BT \ln S}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — convert to molar form** (use $V_m = N_A/\rho_s$ and $R = N_A k_B$):

$$
r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{RT \ln S}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Larger $\gamma$ and larger $V_m$ both *raise* $r^{*}$; larger $T$ and larger $\ln S$ both *lower* $r^{*}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Activation Energy $\Delta G^{*}$ for Nucleation

Substitute $r^{*}$ back into equation (1).

**Step 1.** Replace $r$ by $r^{*} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{\rho_s k_BT\ln S}$.

**Step 2 — evaluate the bulk term.**

$$
\Delta G_{\text{bulk}}^{*} = -\frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{2\gamma}{\rho_s k_BT\ln S}\right)^{\!3}\,\rho_s\,k_BT \ln S
$$

Simplify: the $\rho_s k_BT \ln S$ in the denominator's cube multiplies once with the $\rho_s k_BT\ln S$ in the numerator, leaving:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{bulk}}^{*} = -\frac{4}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{8\gamma^{3}}{(\rho_s k_BT\ln S)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — evaluate the surface term.**

$$
\Delta G_{\text{surface}}^{*} = 4\pi \left(\frac{2\gamma}{\rho_s k_BT\ln S}\right)^{\!2}\gamma
     = 4\pi \cdot \frac{4\gamma^{3}}{(\rho_s k_BT\ln S)^{2}}
     = \frac{16\pi\gamma^{3}}{(\rho_s k_BT\ln S)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — add them.** Note that the bulk term is $-\tfrac{1}{3}$ of the surface term (algebraic identity for spherical nuclei):

$$
\Delta G^{*} = \Delta G_{\text{bulk}}^{*} + \Delta G_{\text{surface}}^{*}
            = -\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{16\pi\gamma^{3}}{(\rho_s k_BT\ln S)^{2}}\right) + \frac{16\pi\gamma^{3}}{(\rho_s k_BT\ln S)^{2}}
            = \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{16\pi\gamma^{3}}{(\rho_s k_BT\ln S)^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — final form.**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta G^{*} = \frac{16\pi\gamma^{3}}{3(\rho_s k_BT\ln S)^{2}} = \frac{16\pi\gamma^{3}V_{m}^{2}}{3(RT\ln S)^{2}}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solubility-Product-Based pH of Precipitation Onset

For a generic metal hydroxide precipitation:

$$
\text{M(OH)}_n(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{M}^{n+} + n\,\text{OH}^{-}
$$

**Step 1 — write the $K_{sp}$.**

$$
K_{sp} = [\text{M}^{n+}][\text{OH}^{-}]^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — solve for $[\text{OH}^{-}]$ at the incipient precipitation point.**

$$
[\text{OH}^{-}]_{\text{crit}} = \left(\frac{K_{sp}}{[\text{M}^{n+}]}\right)^{1/n}
$$

**Step 3 — convert to pH.**
Use $K_w = [\text{H}^{+}][\text{OH}^{-}] = 10^{-14}$ at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$, so $pH = 14 + \log[\text{OH}^{-}]$.

$$
\boxed{\,\text{pH}_{\text{precip}} = 14 + \frac{1}{n}\log\!\left(\frac{K_{sp}}{[\text{M}^{n+}]}\right)\,}
$$

**Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**
For $\text{Fe(OH)}_3$, $K_{sp} = 2.79 \times 10^{-39}$, $[\text{Fe}^{3+}] = 0.01\,M$, $n = 3$:

$$
[\text{OH}^{-}]_{\text{crit}} = \left(\frac{2.79\times 10^{-39}}{10^{-2}}\right)^{1/3} = (2.79\times 10^{-37})^{1/3}
$$

$$
[\text{OH}^{-}]_{\text{crit}} = (2.79)^{1/3}\times 10^{-37/3} \approx 1.41\times 10^{-12.33}\;\approx 6.6\times 10^{-13}\,M
$$

$$
\text{pH}_{\text{precip}} = 14 + \log(6.6\times 10^{-13}) = 14 - 12.18 = 1.82
$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ precipitates at very low pH → it is hard to keep in solution. By contrast, $\text{Mg(OH)}_2$ ($K_{sp} \approx 5.6\times 10^{-12}$) precipitates only above $\text{pH}\approx 10.5$, which is why *sequential* pH-controlled co-precipitation is sometimes used to obtain multi-component catalysts with controlled phase distribution.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Single vs Co-Precipitation

| Criterion | Precipitation | Co-Precipitation |
|---|---|---|
| Number of cations in feed | 1 | $\geq 2$ |
| Number of final phases before calcination | 1 | 1 (homogeneous mixed) or 2+ (heterogeneous) |
| Typical atomic-scale homogeneity | N/A | **High**, if isomorphous |
| Typical surface area of final oxide | $50$–$200\,\text{m}^2/\text{g}$ | $100$–$500\,\text{m}^2/\text{g}$ |
| Industrial scalability | Excellent | Excellent (slurry handling) |
| Sensitivity to pH, $T$, stirring | High | **Very high** |
| Common pitfall | Particle agglomeration | Phase segregation if pH window is wrong |

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Mean Particle Size ↔ Surface Area (BET linkage)

For *monodisperse* spherical particles of diameter $d$ and skeletal density $\rho_p$, the **mass-specific external surface area** is:

$$
S_{\text{BET,ext}} = \frac{\text{surface area of one particle}}{\text{mass of one particle}} 
= \frac{\pi d^{2}}{\tfrac{1}{6}\pi d^{3}\rho_p} = \frac{6}{\rho_p d}
$$

In logarithmic form (used in *log–log* particle-size distribution plots):

$$
\log S_{\text{BET,ext}} = \log\!\left(\frac{6}{\rho_p}\right) - \log d
$$

A doubling of $d$ halves the specific surface area — a *highly-tested* KTU relationship.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Precipitation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metal Salt Solution M^n+] --> B[Addition of Precipitant OH- or CO3^2-]
    B --> C{Supersaturation S greater than 1?}
    C -- No --> Z[No precipitation - unsaturated]
    C -- Yes --> D[Primary Nucleation - critical radius r*]
    D --> E[Growth of primary particles by solute addition]
    E --> F[Aging / Ostwald Ripening]
    F --> G[Filtration]
    G --> H[Washing to remove spectator ions Na, K, NO3, SO4]
    H --> I[Drying]
    I --> J[Calcination - convert hydroxide to oxide]
    J --> K[Optional Reduction - oxide to metal]
    K --> L[Final Catalyst M / M_xO_y on support]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Co-Precipitation Routes and Incorporation Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED[Common Solution]
        CA[Cation A, host]
        CB[Cation B, dopant]
    end
    FEED --> MIX[Intimate Mixing under controlled pH and T]
    MIX --> ROUTE{Which co-pptn mechanism dominates?}
    ROUTE -- Path 1 --> ADS[Adsorption on growing host surface]
    ROUTE -- Path 2 --> OCC[Occlusion inside pore network]
    ROUTE -- Path 3 --> INC[Inclusion into host lattice]
    ROUTE -- Path 4 --> ISO[Isomorphous lattice substitution]
    ADS --> MIXPREC[Mixed precursor solid]
    OCC --> MIXPREC
    INC --> MIXPREC
    ISO --> MIXPREC
    MIXPREC --> AGING[Aging, filtration, washing, drying]
    AGING --> CAL[Calcination]
    CAL --> RED[Optional Reduction]
    RED --> FINAL[Multi-component catalyst with high atomic dispersion]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Effect of Supersaturation on Particle Size

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Process variable: supersaturation S] --> Q{Numerical value of S}
    Q -- Very high, S greater than 100 --> R[Nucleation rate extremely high]
    R --> R1[Many nuclei form, growth starved]
    R1 --> R2[Final particles: nanometric, narrow distribution, very high S_BET]
    Q -- Moderate, S between 2 and 20 --> S2[Nucleation moderate, growth significant]
    S2 --> S3[Final particles: submicron, broad distribution, moderate S_BET]
    Q -- Low, S less than 2 --> T[Growth dominates]
    T --> T1[Final particles: coarse, broad, low S_BET - generally undesirable for catalysts]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operational Variables Affecting Co-Precipitation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[Operational variables] --> PH[pH and pH profile]
    OP --> TEMP[Temperature of precipitation]
    OP --> CONC[Concentration of metal salt solution]
    OP --> ADD[Order and rate of addition: forward vs reverse strike]
    OP --> MIX[Stirring speed and reactor geometry]
    OP --> AGE[Aging time and aging temperature]
    OP --> WASH[Washing protocol to remove Na, K, NH4 ions]
    PH --> Q1[Controls supersaturation S and K_sp threshold]
    TEMP --> Q2[Alters solubility, viscosity, gamma]
    CONC --> Q3[Directly modulates S]
    ADD --> Q4[Determines local pH history at nucleation site]
    MIX --> Q5[Determines homogeneity of micro-mixing]
    AGE --> Q6[Promotes Ostwald ripening and crystallisation]
    WASH --> Q7[Removes poisons and stabilises surface area]
    Q1 --> OUT[Precursor properties: composition, homogeneity, particle size, porosity]
    Q2 --> OUT
    Q3 --> OUT
    Q4 --> OUT
    Q5 --> OUT
    Q6 --> OUT
    Q7 --> OUT
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

#### Q1. Distinguish between *precipitation* and *co-precipitation* as applied to catalyst preparation. Give one industrial example of each.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Precipitation | Co-Precipitation |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Conversion of *one* soluble cation into an insoluble solid by reaction with a precipitating agent. | Simultaneous precipitation of *two or more* cations from the same solution to form a mixed precursor. |
| No. of phases in precursor | Single-phase (or amorphous) | Mixed, ideally homogeneous at the atomic scale. |
| Typical goal | Make a single-component support or active phase. | Make a multi-component catalyst precursor with intimate contact between species. |
| Example | Precipitation of $\text{Al(OH)}_3$ from $\text{Al(NO}_3\text{)}_3$ with $\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$ to make $\gamma$-$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ support. | Co-precipitation of $\text{Cu}$, $\text{Zn}$, $\text{Al}$ as mixed carbonates to make the $\text{CuO/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3$ methanol synthesis catalyst precursor. |

(3 marks: 1 mark for distinction in words, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for examples.)

---

#### Q2. Define *supersaturation ratio* and state its role in determining the particle size of a precipitate.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **supersaturation ratio** is defined as $S = C/C_s$, where $C$ is the actual (post-mixing) concentration of the precipitating species in solution and $C_s$ is its equilibrium solubility at the working temperature and pH. $S$ is the *driving force* for both nucleation and growth.

- A **high** $S$ → large nucleation rate $B \propto \exp(-1/(\ln S)^2)$ → many small nuclei → *fine, uniform* particles and high surface area.
- A **low** $S$ (close to 1) → few nuclei but each grows extensively → *coarse, polydisperse* particles.
- Catalyst preparators therefore *deliberately engineer* high $S$ (e.g. by rapid mixing, hot-cold mixing, or by sudden pH shift) to obtain finely divided precursors.

(3 marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

#### **Question A (14 marks) — Choice 1**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of the classical nucleation theory, derive expressions for the *critical nucleus radius* $r^{*}$ and the *nucleation activation energy* $\Delta G^{*}$. Discuss how the supersaturation ratio $S$ affects the final particle size of a catalyst precursor.    **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four *co-precipitation mechanisms* by which a guest cation can be incorporated into a host precipitate. Give one industrial example where each mechanism plays a dominant role.    **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1** — State the change in Gibbs free energy for the formation of a spherical nucleus of radius $r$ in a supersaturated solution:

$$
\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,\rho_s\,k_BT \ln S \;+\; 4\pi r^{2}\gamma
$$

**Step 2** — Differentiate with respect to $r$ and equate to zero to find the critical radius:

$$
r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{RT \ln S}
$$

**Step 3** — Substitute $r^{*}$ back to obtain the activation barrier:

$$
\Delta G^{*} = \frac{16\pi\gamma^{3}V_{m}^{2}}{3(RT\ln S)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4** — Discuss the role of $S$:
- $\Delta G^{*} \propto (\ln S)^{-2}$ → doubling $\ln S$ reduces $\Delta G^{*}$ by a factor of 4 → nucleation rate *B* increases exponentially.
- Therefore high $S$ gives many nuclei, all starved of solute for growth → small, uniform particles.
- Low $S$ gives few nuclei that grow into large, broad particles.

**Step 5** — Conclude that *the supersaturation ratio is the single most powerful handle* on the precursor particle size and therefore on the final surface area ($S_{BET} = 6/(\rho_p d)$).

(7 marks: derivation 3, discussion 4.)

##### Model Solution — (b)

**1. Adsorption (surface co-precipitation)** — The dopant cation is adsorbed on the outer surface of the already-formed host precipitate. Example: deposition of $\text{MoO}_x$ species on freshly precipitated $\text{Ni(OH)}_2$ during co-precipitation of $\text{Ni–Mo}$ HDS precursors.

**2. Occlusion** — The dopant is mechanically trapped inside pores or cavities of a rapidly growing host crystal. Example: occlusion of $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ inside the porous hydroxide matrix of $\text{Zn(OH)}_2$ during preparation of $\text{Cu/ZnO}$ catalysts.

**3. Inclusion (lattice incorporation)** — The dopant is built into the host crystal lattice as a substitutional or interstitial defect. Example: $\text{Al}^{3+}$ inclusion in the lattice of $\text{Cu/Zn}$ hydroxy-carbonate, giving the hydrotalcite-like precursor of the methanol synthesis catalyst.

**4. Isomorphous replacement** — A dopant with matching ionic radius and charge substitutes *directly* for the host cation in the lattice, forming a *solid solution* at the atomic scale. Example: $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ ($r = 0.69\,\text{Å}$) replacing $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ ($r = 0.72\,\text{Å}$) in $\text{Mg(OH)}_2$ to form a $\text{Ni–Mg}$ mixed hydroxide, the precursor of the highly active $\text{Ni/MgO}$ methane reforming catalyst.

(7 marks: 1.5 marks per mechanism, 0.5 mark per example.)

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice to Question A)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For the precipitation of $\text{Fe(OH)}_3$ from a $0.01\,\text{M}$ solution of $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ ($K_{sp} = 2.79\times 10^{-39}$), calculate the **pH at which precipitation just begins** and discuss how this pH shifts with (i) increase in metal concentration, (ii) decrease in temperature, and (iii) use of a more soluble salt (e.g. $\text{Fe(NO}_3\text{)}_3$ vs $\text{FeCl}_3$).    **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the *step-by-step industrial procedure* for the co-precipitation preparation of a $\text{CuO/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3$ methanol synthesis catalyst. Indicate the role of each operational variable (pH, temperature, order of addition, aging, washing).    **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1** — Write the dissolution equilibrium:

$$
\text{Fe(OH)}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\,\text{OH}^{-} \qquad K_{sp} = 2.79\times 10^{-39}
$$

**Step 2** — At the incipient precipitation point, $K_{sp} = [\text{Fe}^{3+}][\text{OH}^{-}]^{3}$.

$$
[\text{OH}^{-}]_{\text{crit}} = \left(\frac{K_{sp}}{[\text{Fe}^{3+}]}\right)^{1/3}
= \left(\frac{2.79\times 10^{-39}}{10^{-2}}\right)^{1/3}
= (2.79\times 10^{-37})^{1/3}
$$

$$
[\text{OH}^{-}]_{\text{crit}} = (2.79)^{1/3}\times 10^{-37/3} \approx 1.41 \times 10^{-12.33}
\approx 6.6\times 10^{-13}\,\text{M}
$$

**Step 3** — Convert to pH:

$$
\text{pOH} = -\log(6.6\times 10^{-13}) = 12.18
\;\Longrightarrow\; \text{pH} = 14 - 12.18 = 1.82
$$

**Step 4** — Effects of operational changes:

(i) **Increase in metal concentration** $[\text{Fe}^{3+}]$: $[\text{OH}^{-}]_{\text{crit}}$ decreases, so pH *decreases*; precipitation occurs at *lower* pH (Le Chatelier).

(ii) **Decrease in temperature**: $K_{sp}$ generally *decreases* at lower $T$ (the dissolution of $\text{Fe(OH)}_3$ is endothermic), so precipitation begins at an *even lower* pH. In practice, ageing is performed hot to *increase* solubility and *promote* Ostwald ripening.

(iii) **Choice of anion** ($\text{NO}_3^{-}$ vs $\text{Cl}^{-}$): $\text{Cl}^{-}$ may form soluble chloro-complexes with $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ (e.g. $\text{FeCl}^{2+}$, $\text{FeCl}_2^{+}$), effectively *increasing* the apparent solubility $C_s$ and shifting the precipitation to a *higher* pH; $\text{NO}_3^{-}$ is essentially non-complexing, so the *true* $K_{sp}$ dominates and precipitation starts at a lower pH.

(7 marks.)

##### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Preparation of the mixed metal salt solution.**
Aqueous solution of $\text{Cu(NO}_3\text{)}_2$, $\text{Zn(NO}_3\text{)}_2$ and $\text{Al(NO}_3\text{)}_3$ in the molar ratio $\text{Cu:Zn:Al} = 60:30:10$ is prepared. Total metal concentration is typically $0.5$–$1.5\,\text{M}$.

**Step 2 — Preparation of precipitant solution.**
A $1$–$2\,\text{M}$ aqueous solution of $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ is used. NaOH may be used in parallel, but $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ yields the *hydrotalcite-like* precursor that is most active.

**Step 3 — Co-precipitation (the critical step).**
Both solutions are added *simultaneously* into a well-stirred precipitation vessel held at $T = 60$–$80\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $\text{pH} = 6.5$–$7.5$ (controlled by automatic pH-stat). The simultaneous addition — known as **co-precipitation under constant pH** — guarantees that all three cations experience *the same* supersaturation history, maximising atomic-scale mixing.

**Step 4 — Aging.**
The slurry is aged at the same temperature for $30$–$120$ min. Aging promotes:
- Ostwald ripening → uniform, larger primary particles.
- Crystallisation of the *hydrotalcite-like* $(\text{Cu,Zn})_6\text{Al}_2(\text{OH})_{16}\text{CO}_3 \cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$ phase.

**Step 5 — Filtration and washing.**
The aged slurry is filtered and the cake is *thoroughly washed* with deionised water to remove residual $\text{Na}^{+}$. Sodium is a severe poison for Cu-based catalysts, so washing is the **single most critical purification step** in this preparation.

**Step 6 — Drying.**
The filter cake is dried at $80$–$120\,^\circ\text{C}$ to remove free water.

**Step 7 — Calcination.**
The dried precursor is calcined in air at $300$–$400\,^\circ\text{C}$ to decompose the hydroxy-carbonate to the mixed oxide $\text{CuO/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3$.

**Step 8 — Optional reduction.**
The oxide is reduced in dilute $\text{H}_2$/$\text{N}_2$ at $200$–$250\,^\circ\text{C}$ to give metallic $\text{Cu}$ particles (typically $5$–$15\,\text{nm}$) highly dispersed on the $\text{ZnO}$–$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ matrix.

**Role of each variable:**
- **pH $6.5$–$7.5$**: ensures simultaneous precipitation of all three cations; below pH 6, $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ is not fully precipitated; above pH 8, $\text{Zn(OH)}_2$ dissolves as zincate.
- **Temperature $60$–$80\,^\circ\text{C}$**: increases solubility (lowers $S$), gives more crystalline hydrotalcite, improves filterability.
- **Order of addition**: simultaneous addition under pH control is essential for atomic-level mixing; "forward strike" (precipitant into metal) or "reverse strike" (metal into precipitant) without pH control gives inhomogeneous products.
- **Aging**: critical for crystallisation of the right precursor phase and for Ostwald ripening to a uniform particle size.
- **Washing**: removes $\text{Na}^{+}$ which otherwise poisons Cu active sites and causes sintering.

(7 marks: 1 mark for each major step + 2 marks for role-of-variables discussion.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "homogeneous precipitation" with "co-precipitation".** Homogeneous precipitation uses a *single* solution where the precipitant is generated *in situ* (e.g. urea hydrolysis) and is a *method of pH control*, not a different type of co-precipitation. Marks will be deducted if the student mixes these concepts.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $[\text{OH}^{-}]$ to pH.** Many students compute $[\text{OH}^{-}]_{\text{crit}}$ correctly but stop there. KTU 2024 scheme demands the **final pH value**.
> 3. **Stating that high supersaturation gives large particles.** This is the *opposite* of the truth. The single biggest mark-loss in nucleation derivations is to claim that $S$ and $d$ move together.
> 4. **Citing adsorption/occlusion/inclusion/isomorphous as *the same* mechanism.** Each is distinct; examiners look for the discriminating table.
> 5. **Omitting washing in catalyst preparation.** Washing out spectator ions (especially $\text{Na}^{+}$, $\text{K}^{+}$, $\text{NH}_4^{+}$, $\text{NO}_3^{-}$, $\text{Cl}^{-}$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) is a mandatory process step in KTU's marking scheme for co-precipitation questions.
> 6. **Skipping the unit check.** In a derivation question, a student is expected to confirm that $r^{*}$ is in metres, $\Delta G^{*}$ in J mol$^{-1}$, etc. Missing the unit = partial mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Precipitation** = conversion of **one** soluble cation → insoluble solid precursor. **Co-precipitation** = simultaneous conversion of **two or more** cations → mixed, ideally atomically homogeneous precursor.
- The **driving force** of precipitation is the **supersaturation ratio** $S = C/C_s > 1$.
- The **five stages** are: supersaturation generation → nucleation → growth → aging → (optional) phase transformation.
- **Classical Nucleation Theory** gives:
   - Critical radius $r^{*} = 2\gamma V_m / (RT \ln S)$
   - Activation barrier $\Delta G^{*} = 16\pi \gamma^{3} V_m^{2} / [3 (RT \ln S)^{2}]$
   - Nucleation rate $B = A \exp(-\Delta G^{*}/k_BT)$
- **High $S$** → high $B$ → many small nuclei → fine, uniform particles → **high $S_{BET}$** ($S_{BET} = 6/(\rho_p d)$).
- **Low $S$** → low $B$, dominant growth → coarse, broad particles → **low $S_{BET}$**.
- The **four co-precipitation mechanisms** are: **adsorption, occlusion, inclusion, isomorphous replacement** — listed in order of *decreasing* intimacy of mixing.
- **Operational levers** in co-precipitation: pH and pH profile, temperature, concentration, order and rate of addition, stirring, aging time/temperature, washing protocol.
- The **pH of incipient precipitation** for $\text{M(OH)}_n$ is:
   $\text{pH}_{\text{precip}} = 14 + \tfrac{1}{n}\log(K_{sp}/[\text{M}^{n+}])$.
- **Industrial flagship examples**:
   - $\text{CuO/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3$ → methanol synthesis (low-pressure process).
   - $\text{NiO/Al}_2\text{O}_3$ → steam reforming of methane.
   - $\text{Fe–Cu–K/SiO}_2$ → Fischer–Tropsch synthesis.
   - $\text{Ni–Mo}$ and $\text{Co–Mo}$ oxo-hydroxides → HDS catalysts.
- **Washing is non-negotiable** — residual alkali or halide ions poison the active sites and lower the final catalyst's activity and life.
- The **precipitation method** is the *only* widely scalable route that gives *atomic-scale* mixing of multiple metals, and is therefore the workhorse of industrial multi-component catalyst manufacture.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
