Substituting: $$K_P = \frac{\theta/N}{(1-\theta)/N \cdot P} = \frac{\theta}{(1-\theta) P}$$ Rearranging: $$K_P P (1-\theta) = \theta$$ $$\theta (1 + K_P P) = K_P P$$ $$\boxed{\theta = \frac{K_P P}{1 + K_P P}}$$ **Limiting Cases (2 marks):** | Case | Condition | Result | CVD Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Low pressure | $K_P P \ll 1$ | $\theta \approx K_P P$ | Linear regime; rate ∝ precursor pressure | | High pressure | $K_P P \gg 1$ | $\theta \to 1$ | Saturation; deposition rate becomes pressure-independent | **CVD Operation Relevance:** Operate at intermediate $K_P P \approx 1$ for maximum sensitivity and uniform monolayer formation. --- ### Question B1 — Option (B) (14 Marks) **[KTU ESE Pattern — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD)** technique. Derive the expression for **growth per cycle (GPC)** and compute the number of cycles required to deposit 5 nm of Al₂O₃ on SiO₂, given $GPC = 0.12 \text{ nm/cycle}$. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare **CVD, ALD, and Chemical Vapor Condensation (CVC)** with respect to operating principle, particle size, and at least three advantages/limitations. (7 marks) ### Complete Model Solution **(a) ALD Block Diagram + GPC Derivation (7 marks)** **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Use the ALD cycle diagram (Figure 4.2) above showing Pulse A → Purge → Pulse B → Purge. **Explanation of ALD (3 marks):** ALD is a vapor-phase technique based on **two sequential, self-limiting surface reactions**. In each half-cycle, a precursor is pulsed into the reactor; it reacts with all available surface sites until saturation. Excess precursor and by-products are purged with an inert gas. The second half-cycle introduces a co-reactant that reacts with the adsorbed first precursor, regenerating the surface functional groups. The cycle is repeated $N$ times to build the film **one atomic layer at a time**. **GPC Derivation (1.5 marks):** Let $N_{dep}$ = number of molecules deposited per unit area per cycle (m⁻²), $M_{ox}$ = molar mass of the deposited oxide (g/mol), $\rho$ = density (g/m³), $N_A$ = Avogadro's number. The thickness per cycle is: $$GPC = \frac{N_{dep} \cdot M_{ox}}{\rho \cdot N_A}$$ **Numerical Computation (0.5 marks):** $$N_{cycles} = \frac{d}{GPC} = \frac{5 \text{ nm}}{0.12 \text{ nm/cycle}} = 41.67 \approx 42 \text{ cycles}$$ **(b) Comparative Analysis (7 marks)** | Feature | CVD | ALD | CVC | |---|---|---|---| | Operating Principle | Continuous co-flow of precursor + co-reactant; thermal decomposition on support | Sequential self-limiting surface reactions in pulses | High-T vaporization followed by rapid cold-quench condensation | | Particle/Feature Size | 2–50 nm crystallites | 0.5–5 nm, atomic-layer films | 2–10 nm free nanoparticles | | Advantages | (i) High metal loading possible; (ii) Scalable to industrial reactors; (iii) Strong metal–support bonding | (i) Atomic precision; (ii) Excellent uniformity on high-aspect-ratio supports; (iii) Excellent for sub-monolayer precious metals | (i) Produces uncapped nanoparticles; (ii) High throughput; (iii) No support required | | Limitations | (i) Gas-phase nucleation must be avoided; (ii) High-T may sinter the support; (iii) Precursor availability | (i) Slow deposition rate; (ii) High equipment cost; (iii) Limited to ALD-compatible precursors | (i) Free particles must be re-dispersed on support; (ii) High energy input; (iii) Broad size distribution | --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings / Pitfall Callouts > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing "deposition rate" with "growth rate":** Deposition rate is mass per area per time ($g \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}$); growth rate is thickness per time ($nm/s$ or $nm/cycle$). Mixing these units loses 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting the self-limiting nature of ALD:** Many students describe ALD as "slow CVD." ALD is **fundamentally different** because each half-cycle saturates the surface. The Board examiner marks this distinction heavily (up to 2 marks). > > 3. **Skipping the Arrhenius plot analysis:** When asked to compute $E_a$, students forget that the slope of $\ln r_d$ vs. $1/T$ is $-E_a/R$, not $-E_a$. This is a classic 2-mark trap. > > 4. **Not stating the role of surface hydroxyl groups:** In CVD of metal oxides, the support hydroxyls (≡Si–OH, ≡Al–OH) provide the nucleation sites. Omitting this costs 1 mark. > > 5. **Drawing flowcharts without labeling flow streams:** In block diagrams, every arrow must be labeled with the species flowing (e.g., "Mo(CO)₆ + N₂", "H₂", "HCl exhaust"). Unlabeled arrows cost 0.5–1 mark. > > 6. **Numerical errors in GPC calculation:** Students often confuse $\rho$ in $g/cm^3$ with $g/m^3$. A factor-of-10⁶ error leads to wrong $GPC$ and wrong cycle count. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Supported Catalysts from CVD & Related Techniques:** - ☐ **CVD** is a vapor-phase technique using **volatile precursors** (carbonyls, halides, alkoxides, β-diketonates). - ☐ Five stages: **Vapor generation → Transport → Gas-phase reactions (avoid) → Surface reaction (rate-limiting) → By-product desorption**. - ☐ **Arrhenius dependence**: $k_d = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$; plot $\ln r_d$ vs. $1/T$ to extract $E_a$. - ☐ **Langmuir isotherm** governs surface coverage: $\theta = K_P P / (1 + K_P P)$. - ☐ **Langmuir–Hinshelwood** governs bimolecular surface reactions; denominator is **squared**. - ☐ **ALD** is the pulsed, self-limiting variant of CVD with atomic-layer precision. - ☐ **GPC** (growth per cycle) formula: $GPC = N_{dep} M_{ox} / (\rho N_A)$. - ☐ **CVC** uses hot-furnace vaporization + cold-quench condensation to make free nanoparticles. - ☐ **Support hydroxyls (≡Si–OH, ≡Al–OH)** are the nucleation sites for oxide deposition. - ☐ **Thiele modulus** $\phi = L\sqrt{k_s/D_{eff}}$; effectiveness factor $\eta = (\phi \coth \phi - 1)/\phi^2$ quantifies internal diffusion limitations. - ☐ **TOF** (turnover frequency) is the gold-standard activity metric: $\dot{n}_{product}/n_{surface \, metal}$. - ☐ **Advantages of CVD over impregnation**: stronger metal–support bonding, narrower particle-size distribution, better dispersion, lower sintering. - ☐ **Knudsen diffusion** governs transport inside mesopores: $D_K = (2/3) r_p \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$. - ☐ **Supersaturation S** in CVC: high S → many small nuclei; low S → few large particles. - ☐ **Industrial examples**: MoS₂/CVD-Mo/Al₂O₃ (HDS); Pt-ALD/C (PEMFC); Ru-CVD/MgO (NH₃); TiO₂-CVD/glass (photocatalysis). - ☐ **KTU-BoE keyword trigger phrases**: "self-limiting surface reaction," "volatile precursor," "heterogeneous decomposition," "atomic layer precision," "Knudsen regime."

topics.title: Synthesis of Aluminosilicate Zeolites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Synthesis of Aluminosilicate Zeolites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aluminosilicate Zeolites** are crystalline, hydrated tecto-aluminosilicates built from corner-sharing $\text{SiO}_4$ and $\text{AlO}_4$ tetrahedra, forming a rigid, open three-dimensional framework with uniform pores and cavities of molecular dimensions (typically **0.3 – 1.5 nm**).

The general crystallographic unit cell formula is:

$$M_{x/n}^{n+} \cdot \left[\text{Al}_x\text{Si}_{1-x}\text{O}_2\right] \cdot y\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

where:
- $M^{n+}$ = exchangeable extra-framework cation (e.g., $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{K}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$)
- $x$ = atomic fraction of aluminium (always $x \leq 0.5$ per **Löwenstein's Rule**)
- $y$ = moles of intracrystalline water per unit cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Löwenstein's Rule (Al–O–Al Avoidance):** Two $\text{AlO}_4$ tetrahedra cannot share a single oxygen bridge. This is the foundational principle controlling the **Si/Al ratio** in every aluminosilicate zeolite synthesis.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Molecular LEGO Bricks"

Imagine you are building an enormous **LEGO castle**:
- **Silica ($\text{SiO}_4$) and Alumina ($\text{AlO}_4$)** are your LEGO bricks — tetrahedral pieces with four connector pegs.
- Each brick connects to four neighbours at the corners, forming an infinite 3D scaffolding.
- Because aluminium bricks carry a **negative charge**, you must tuck a positive counter-ion (like $\text{Na}^+$ or $\text{H}^+$) into every internal "room" of the castle — this is the **extra-framework cation**.
- Inside the castle rooms, you can host water, gases, or hydrocarbon molecules — this is exactly what makes zeolites extraordinary **molecular sieves and catalysts**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the holes are uniform:** Because the crystal is built from tetrahedra in a periodic pattern, every channel has the *exact same* width — making zeolites **shape-selective catalysts**.

## 1.3 Industrial & Engineering Significance

| Property | Engineering Value |
|---|---|
| Uniform micropores | Selective adsorption of molecules |
| Strong Brønsted acid sites | Petroleum cracking (FCC) |
| Thermal stability up to **800 °C** | Regenerable catalysts |
| Ion-exchange capacity | Water softening, nuclear waste cleanup |
| High internal surface area (**400 – 800 m²/g**) | Ultra-high catalyst dispersion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Zeolite Synthesis

The hydrothermal synthesis of aluminosilicate zeolites is governed by **four interacting pillars**:

### Pillar I — Chemical Composition of the Reaction Gel

The starting "gel" is a homogeneous mixture of:

1. **Silica source** – Sodium silicate ($\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3$), colloidal silica (Ludox), fumed silica, or tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS).
2. **Alumina source** – Sodium aluminate ($\text{NaAlO}_2$), aluminium hydroxide ($\text{Al(OH)}_3$), or aluminium isopropoxide.
3. **Alkali hydroxide (mineralizer)** – $\text{NaOH}$ or $\text{KOH}$ to dissolve silica and raise pH.
4. **Structure-Directing Agent (SDA / template)** – Organic amines or quaternary ammonium cations (e.g., tetrapropylammonium for ZSM-5).
5. **Water** – the reaction medium and hydrolyser.

A typical zeolite-A synthesis gel molar ratio:

$$\text{Na}_2\text{O} : \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 : \text{SiO}_2 : \text{H}_2\text{O} = 2 : 1 : 2 : 120$$

### Pillar II — Physical Conditions

- **Temperature range:** **80 – 200 °C**
- **Pressure range:** **Autogenous** (typically 1 – 15 bar)
- **Time range:** **6 hours – 14 days**
- **pH range:** Strongly alkaline (**pH = 10 – 14**)

### Pillar III — Kinetic Control

Two competitive pathways operate simultaneously during crystallization:

| Pathway | Mechanism | Dominant When |
|---|---|---|
| **Solution-mediated transport** | Species dissolve from gel → nucleate → grow in solution | High alkalinity, dilute gels |
| **Solid-phase transformation** | Gel reorganizes directly into crystalline framework | High supersaturation |

### Pillar IV — The SDA (Template)

The SDA is **not a catalyst** — it is consumed and trapped inside the pore voids. After synthesis, it is removed by **calcination at 500 – 550 °C**, leaving behind the empty, catalytically active framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zeolite Type vs. SDA — High-Yield Match Table for KTU Exams:**
> | Zeolite | Code | SDA Used |
> |---|---|---|
> | Zeolite A | LTA | Sodium (no organic SDA) |
> | Zeolite X / Y | FAU | Sodium (no organic SDA) |
> | ZSM-5 | MFI | Tetrapropylammonium (TPA⁺) |
> | Mordenite | MOR | Sodium / TEA |

## 2.2 Why Each Component Matters — The "WHY-HOW" Logic

- **Why alkalies?** $\text{OH}^-$ attacks Si–O–Si bridges, breaking the silica network into reactive silicate monomers that can reorganize.
- **Why SDA?** The SDA acts as a **molecular mould** — its size and shape pre-determine the pore size and dimensionality of the resulting framework.
- **Why water?** Provides ionic mobility, dissolves precursors, and acts as the pressurizing medium in the autoclave.
- **Why controlled temperature?** Balances the **rate of nucleation** (favours low T) against the **rate of crystal growth** (favours high T).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Si/Al} = \dfrac{n_{\text{SiO}_2}}{n_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3}} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Defines framework composition | Dimensionless, must be $\geq 1$ |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{Z \cdot e}{R}$ | Charge density of framework | Determines acid strength |
| 3 | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | Arrhenius rate of crystallization | $E_a$ in kJ/mol |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(1-\alpha)^n$ | Avrami crystallization kinetics | $\alpha$ = fractional crystallinity |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{L}{D_s}$ | Induction period relationship | $D_s$ = diffusion coefficient |
| 6 | $\Delta G^* = \dfrac{16\pi \gamma^3 v^2}{3(k_BT \ln S)^2}$ | Critical nucleus free energy | $\gamma$ = surface energy, $S$ = supersaturation |
| 7 | $r^* = \dfrac{2\gamma v}{k_BT \ln S}$ | Critical nucleus radius | Metres |
| 8 | $T_{\text{calc}} \geq T_{\text{SDA decomposn}}$ | Calcination temperature limit | Typically **500 – 550 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Equation 1 (Si/Al ratio) and Equation 3 (Arrhenius) appear in **more than 70% of KTU PYQs** on zeolite synthesis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Framework Si/Al Ratio from Synthesis Gel

**Problem:** A zeolite gel contains 10 mol of $\text{SiO}_2$ and 1 mol of $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$. Find the Si/Al ratio.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Express moles of framework atoms.**

The framework is built from Si atoms and Al atoms. From $\text{SiO}_2$, we get Si atoms 1-to-1:

$$n_{\text{Si}} = n_{\text{SiO}_2} = 10 \text{ mol}$$

From $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, each mole of alumina gives **2 moles of Al atoms**:

$$n_{\text{Al}} = 2 \cdot n_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Si/Al atomic ratio.**

$$\frac{\text{Si}}{\text{Al}} = \frac{n_{\text{Si}}}{n_{\text{Al}}} = \frac{10}{2} = 5$$

**Step 3 — Validate against Löwenstein's Rule.**

Löwenstein requires $\dfrac{\text{Si}}{\text{Al}} \geq 1$. Here, $\dfrac{\text{Si}}{\text{Al}} = 5 \geq 1$ ✅

> **Result:** The framework Si/Al = **5** (a moderately siliceous zeolite — typical of ZSM-5).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Crystallization Activation Energy

**Problem:** Zeolite-A crystallization rate constants were measured as $k_1 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{min}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 363 \text{ K}$ and $k_2 = 4.8 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{min}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 383 \text{ K}$. Determine $E_a$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius equation.**

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the left-hand side (LHS).**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.0$$

$$\ln(4.0) = 1.3863$$

**Step 3 — Compute the right-hand side (RHS) bracket.**

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{383} - \frac{1}{363}$$

$$= \frac{363 - 383}{383 \times 363} = \frac{-20}{139029} = -1.4386 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E_a$.**

$$E_a = -\frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$

$$E_a = -\frac{8.314 \times 1.3863}{-1.4386 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = \frac{11.524}{1.4386 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.01 \times 10^{4} \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 80.1 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

This value lies in the typical zeolite crystallization activation range of **60 – 120 kJ/mol**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Critical Nucleus Size

**Step 1 — Classical nucleation theory gives:**

$$\Delta G = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma$$

where $\Delta G_v = \dfrac{k_B T \ln S}{v}$ is the bulk free-energy change per unit volume.

**Step 2 — Differentiate $\Delta G$ w.r.t. $r$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = -4\pi r^2 \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \gamma = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $r^*$.**

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma}{\Delta G_v} = \frac{2\gamma v}{k_B T \ln S}$$

This is the **critical nucleus radius**. Nuclei smaller than $r^*$ dissolve; larger ones grow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Adding more SDA reduces interfacial tension $\gamma$, which lowers $r^*$ and speeds nucleation — a key strategy in industrial zeolite scale-up.

---

## 3.4 Master Analytical Matrix — Synthesis Parameter Effects

| Parameter | Nucleation Rate | Crystal Growth Rate | Final Crystal Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | Smaller (more nuclei) |
| ↑ pH (more $\text{OH}^-$) | ↑ | ↑ | Smaller |
| ↑ Supersaturation $S$ | ↑↑↑ | ↑ | Smaller |
| ↑ SDA concentration | ↑ | Slightly ↓ | Smaller, more uniform |
| ↑ Aging time | ↑ | ↑ | More monodisperse |
| ↑ Si/Al ratio | ↓ | Slow | Larger |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Synthesis Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[silicaSource: Silica Source] --> B[gelMixing: Gel Mixing in NaOH]
    C[aluminaSource: Alumina Source] --> B
    D[sdaAgent: Structure Directing Agent] --> B
    E[water: Distilled Water] --> B
    B --> F[agedGel: Aging Step 25C 24h]
    F --> G[autoclaveSeal: Stainless Steel Autoclave]
    G --> H[crystallization: Hydrothermal Crystallization 100 to 180C 6h to 14d]
    H --> I[filterWash: Vacuum Filtration and DI Wash]
    I --> J[dryOven: Drying at 110C 12h]
    J --> K[calcination: Calcination 550C 6h to remove SDA]
    K --> L[finalZeolite: Crystalline Zeolite Product]
    K --> M[offGas: CO2 and H2O from SDA burnout]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style E fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00838f
    style L fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style M fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

## 4.2 Zeolite Building Hierarchy — From Tetrahedra to Unit Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[primaryUnit: Primary TO4 Tetrahedron]
    T2[secondaryUnit: Secondary Building Unit SBU]
    T3[compositeUnit: Composite Building Unit CBU]
    T4[unitCell: Crystallographic Unit Cell]
    T5[finalCrystal: Macroscopic Crystal Microns]

    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5

    style T1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style T2 fill:#90caf9,stroke:#1565c0
    style T3 fill:#64b5f6,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    style T4 fill:#42a5f5,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    style T5 fill:#1e88e5,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Three-Stage Crystallization Kinetics Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[phase1: Induction Period: Nuclei form, no XRD peaks]
    P2[phase2: Crystal Growth: Sharp XRD peaks appear]
    P3[phase3: Saturation: Plateau reached, equilibrium]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3

    style P1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#ef6c00
    style P2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style P3 fill:#b0bec5,stroke:#37474f,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Insight:** The induction period can be **shortened** by adding seed crystals (≈ 1 wt% of pre-formed zeolite) — a critical industrial trick.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define aluminosilicate zeolites and write their general unit cell formula. Mention Löwenstein's rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Aluminosilicate zeolites are **crystalline, hydrated tecto-aluminosilicates** built from corner-sharing $\text{SiO}_4^{4-}$ and $\text{AlO}_4^{5-}$ tetrahedra, possessing uniform pores of molecular dimensions **(1 mark)**.

General unit cell formula:

$$M_{x/n}^{n+} \cdot \left[\text{Al}_x\text{Si}_{1-x}\text{O}_2\right] \cdot y\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Löwenstein's rule:** Two aluminium atoms cannot occupy tetrahedral sites linked by a common oxygen — i.e., Al–O–Al linkages are forbidden. Hence Si/Al $\geq 1$ always **(1 mark)**.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Structure-Directing Agent (SDA)? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Structure-Directing Agent (SDA)**, also called a template, is an organic molecule (typically an amine or quaternary ammonium cation) added during zeolite synthesis to **direct the formation of a specific pore geometry** around itself **(1.5 marks)**.

Examples:
- **Tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH)** → forms ZSM-5 (MFI)
- **Tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH)** → forms Beta zeolite **(1.5 marks)**

> The SDA is later removed by **calcination at 500–550 °C**, leaving empty pores.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Describe the **hydrothermal synthesis procedure** for the preparation of Zeolite-A (LTA) with a neat flowchart. Mention the role of each reactant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the effect of **(i) Si/Al ratio, (ii) temperature, and (iii) pH** on zeolite synthesis. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — Hydrothermal Synthesis of Zeolite-A

**Step 1 — Gel Preparation:** Dissolve **sodium aluminate** ($\text{NaAlO}_2$) in distilled water and add **sodium hydroxide** pellets to raise the pH. Slowly add **sodium silicate** solution with vigorous stirring at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous gel of molar composition **(2 marks)**:

$$\text{Na}_2\text{O} : \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 : \text{SiO}_2 : \text{H}_2\text{O} = 2 : 1 : 2 : 120$$

**Step 2 — Aging:** Keep the gel at **25 °C for 24 hours**. Aging homogenizes the gel and promotes formation of primary aluminosilicate nuclei **(1 mark)**.

**Step 3 — Hydrothermal Crystallization:** Transfer to a **teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave**, seal, and heat at **100 °C for 4 – 6 hours** under autogenous pressure **(1 mark)**.

**Step 4 — Recovery:** Filter, wash with deionized water until pH ≈ 9, dry at **110 °C overnight** **(1 mark)**.

**Step 5 — Characterization:** Confirm phase purity by **XRD**; observe cubic crystals by **SEM** **(1 mark)**.

**Role of each reactant (1 mark):**
- $\text{NaAlO}_2$ → Al source (framework)
- $\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3$ → Si source (framework)
- $\text{NaOH}$ → Mineralizer; raises pH; dissolves silica
- $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ → Solvent & pressure medium

### Part (b) — Effect of Synthesis Parameters

**(i) Si/Al ratio:**
- Low Si/Al (≈ 1) → more acidic sites → high cation-exchange capacity → e.g., Zeolite-A, X
- High Si/Al (≥ 10) → hydrophobic, thermally stable → e.g., ZSM-5, Silicalite **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Temperature:**
- Low T (80 – 100 °C) → slow crystallization, fewer nuclei, large crystals (e.g., Analcime, Zeolite-A)
- High T (150 – 200 °C) → fast nucleation, smaller crystals (e.g., ZSM-5, Faujasite) **(2.5 marks)**

**(iii) pH:**
- Highly alkaline (pH > 13) → favours dissolution of silica and formation of dense phases (e.g., sodalite)
- Mildly alkaline (pH 10 – 12) → favours open-framework zeolites **(2.5 marks)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **role of Structure-Directing Agents** in zeolite synthesis with suitable examples. How are they removed after synthesis? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a labelled diagram, explain the **mechanism of nucleation and crystal growth** during zeolite formation. Derive the equation for the **critical nucleus radius**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — Role of Structure-Directing Agents

**Definition (1 mark):** SDAs are organic cations or molecules that **template** the geometry of the pores by occupying void space during crystallization.

**Functions (3 marks):**
1. **Pore geometry control:** The shape of the SDA determines pore size and connectivity — e.g., TPA⁺ (linear) → 10-membered ring MFI channels; TEA⁺ (spherical) → 12-membered ring BEA channels.
2. **Framework charge stabilization:** The positive charge on the SDA balances the negative AlO₄⁻ tetrahedra, guiding framework assembly.
3. **Nucleation control:** SDA reduces interfacial energy $\gamma$, lowering the activation barrier $\Delta G^*$ for nucleus formation.

**Examples (2 marks):**
- **TPAOH** → ZSM-5 (MFI, 10-MR)
- **TEAOH** → Beta (BEA, 12-MR)
- **Na⁺ alone** → Zeolite-A, Zeolite-X (no organic SDA)

**Removal (1 mark):** SDA is removed by **calcination at 500 – 550 °C in air for 6 – 8 hours**, burning it off as $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

### Part (b) — Nucleation & Crystal Growth

**Stages (4 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Induction period:** Dissolved silicate and aluminate species form a supersaturated solution. Sub-critical clusters form and redissolve.

**Stage 2 — Nucleation:** Once a cluster exceeds the critical size $r^*$, it becomes thermodynamically stable and acts as a growth nucleus.

**Stage 3 — Crystal growth:** Monomers add to the nucleus surface, releasing the heat of crystallization, until equilibrium is reached.

**Derivation of $r^*$ (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.3:

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma v}{k_B T \ln S}$$

where $\gamma$ is the surface free energy, $v$ is the molecular volume of the framework unit, and $S$ is the supersaturation ratio.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "SDA" with "catalyst."** The SDA is *consumed* and occluded — it is not regenerated. Many students lose 1 mark by writing "SDA acts as a catalyst."
> 2. **Always mention Löwenstein's Rule** whenever discussing the Si/Al ratio. Skipping it costs 1 mark in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Calcination ≠ activation.** Calcination removes SDA; activation (often with $\text{NH}_4^+$ exchange + heating) generates acidic sites. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Units in Arrhenius problems:** Always convert T to Kelvin and report $E_a$ in kJ/mol, not J/mol. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for wrong units.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Aluminosilicate zeolites** are crystalline, hydrated tecto-aluminosilicates with the formula $M_{x/n}^{n+} [\text{Al}_x\text{Si}_{1-x}\text{O}_2] \cdot y\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- ✅ **Löwenstein's Rule:** Al–O–Al linkages are forbidden → Si/Al $\geq 1$.
- ✅ The four pillars of synthesis are: **Silica source + Alumina source + Mineralizer (NaOH/KOH) + SDA**.
- ✅ Synthesis proceeds by **hydrothermal crystallization in autoclaves** at **80 – 200 °C** under autogenous pressure.
- ✅ The **three kinetic stages** are: (1) Induction, (2) Crystal growth, (3) Saturation plateau.
- ✅ The SDA is **removed by calcination at 500 – 550 °C**, not by solvent extraction (for most industrial cases).
- ✅ **Higher Si/Al** → more hydrophobic, thermally stable, less acidic (ZSM-5).
- ✅ **Lower Si/Al** → more hydrophilic, more acidic sites, higher ion-exchange capacity (Zeolite-A, X, Y).
- ✅ Critical nucleus radius: $r^* = \dfrac{2\gamma v}{k_B T \ln S}$.
- ✅ Arrhenius activation energy for crystallization lies in the range **60 – 120 kJ/mol**.
- ✅ Typical zeolite-A gel ratio: $\text{Na}_2\text{O} : \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 : \text{SiO}_2 : \text{H}_2\text{O} = 2 : 1 : 2 : 120$.
- ✅ **Seed crystals** (≈ 1 wt%) dramatically shorten the induction period — important industrial strategy.
- ✅ **High-Yield PYQ Triggers:** Si/Al ratio, SDA role, Hydrothermal synthesis steps, Löwenstein's rule, Calcination purpose.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalyst Characterisation - Surface Area Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Catalyst Characterisation - Surface Area Measurements # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Surface Area Measurement** in heterogeneous catalysis refers to the quantitative determination of the **total accessible surface area** (per unit mass, expressed in **m²/g**) of a porous solid catalyst. It is the single most important textural property of a catalyst, since all heterogeneous reactions occur exclusively at the gas–solid or liquid–solid interface. The standard reference method universally adopted by the **International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)** is the **BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) nitrogen physisorption method**, carried out at **77 K** (the boiling point of liquid nitrogen) using **N₂** as the adsorbate (with a molecular cross-sectional area of **0.162 nm²**). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a Sponge City:** > Think of a solid catalyst as a *sponge city* packed with skyscrapers (catalytically active sites). The "surface area" of a sponge is **not** the outside of the cube you hold — it is the sum of the walls of *every internal channel and pore*. A tiny 1-gram pellet of activated carbon can have a surface area equivalent to a **football field** (~1000 m²/g), because each gram contains millions of sub-nanometer pores whose walls contribute enormous internal area where reactant molecules can adsorb, react, and desorb. ## 1.3 Why Surface Area Matters in Catalysis - **Active site accessibility** – Only the *exposed* surface atoms (≈1–10 % of total atoms) participate in catalysis. - **Dispersion of active phase** – $D = \dfrac{N_s}{N_t}$, where $N_s$ = surface atoms, $N_t$ = total atoms. - **Turnover Frequency (TOF)** – $\text{TOF} = \dfrac{\text{moles of product}}{\text{moles of active sites} \times \text{time}}$. - **Supports with high area** (e.g., γ-Al₂O₃ ≈ 200 m²/g, SiO₂ ≈ 300–800 m²/g, zeolites ≈ 500–1000 m²/g) disperse expensive metals (Pt, Pd, Ni). ## 1.4 Classification of Surface Area | Category | Type | Typical Origin | |---|---|---| | **External (Geometric) Area** | Outer particle surface | Particle size, morphology | | **Internal (Pore) Area** | Walls of pores & channels | Porosity, pore structure | | **Total (BET) Area** | Sum of external + internal | Measured by N₂ physisorption | | **Active Metal Area** | Exposed metal atoms | Measured by chemisorption (e.g., H₂, CO) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** In Module 2, the focus is on **physical adsorption (physisorption)** for total BET area. **Chemisorption** techniques (H₂, CO, O₂ uptake) for **active metal area / metal dispersion** are covered in the subsequent section of the syllabus. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Adsorption (Physisorption) — Foundation Physisorption is governed by **van der Waals (London dispersion) forces**: - **Low enthalpy of adsorption:** $\Delta H_{ads} \approx 5 \text{ to } 40 \text{ kJ/mol}$ - **Non-specific** — occurs on *all* surfaces - **Reversible** — easily desorbed by lowering pressure or raising temperature - **Multilayer** — more than one layer of adsorbate can form - **No activation energy** — instantaneous This contrasts with **chemisorption**, which has $\Delta H_{ads} \approx 40 \text{ to } 400 \text{ kJ/mol}$ and is monolayer + activated + specific. ## 2.2 The Langmuir Isotherm (1918) — The Building Block Langmuir proposed that gas molecules adsorb on a **homogeneous surface** as a **monolayer**, with **no lateral interactions** and **equivalent sites**. $$ \theta = \frac{bP}{1 + bP} $$ The **volume adsorbed** $V$ is proportional to surface coverage $\theta$: $$ V = \frac{V_m \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P} \quad \text{(Langmuir Equation)} $$ Where: - $V$ = volume of gas adsorbed at equilibrium pressure $P$ - $V_m$ = monolayer volume - $b$ = adsorption equilibrium constant, $b = b_0 \, e^{\Delta H_{ads}/RT}$ ## 2.3 The BET Isotherm (1938) — Extension to Multilayers Brunauer, Emmett and Teller extended Langmuir's model to **multilayer adsorption** with these assumptions: 1. The heat of adsorption for the **first layer** is $E_1$ (high). 2. The heat of adsorption for the **second and subsequent layers** equals the **heat of liquefaction** $E_L$ (low). 3. The Langmuir equation applies to each layer individually. 4. At saturation pressure $P = P_0$, the adsorbate condenses into a bulk liquid. The result is the **canonical BET equation**: $$ \frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C - 1}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0} $$ This is a **linear equation** in the form $y = mx + c$ where: $$ y = \frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)}, \quad x = \frac{P}{P_0} $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in EVERY KTU exam on this topic.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0-P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | **Linear BET equation** (the workhorse) | — | | 2 | $C \approx \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right)$ | BET constant, related to enthalpy difference | dimensionless | | 3 | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m \, N_A \, A_{cs}}{V_{molar}}$ | Specific surface area from monolayer volume | m²/g | | 4 | $V_{molar} = 22414 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$ | Molar volume of gas at STP | cm³/mol | | 5 | $A_{cs}(\text{N}_2) = 0.162 \text{ nm}^2$ | Cross-sectional area of N₂ at 77 K | nm² | | 6 | $A_{cs}(\text{Kr}) = 0.210 \text{ nm}^2$ | Cross-sectional area of Kr at 77 K | nm² | | 7 | $A_{cs}(\text{Ar}) = 0.166 \text{ nm}^2$ | Cross-sectional area of Ar at 87 K | nm² | | 8 | $V_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}}$ | Monolayer volume from BET plot | cm³/g (STP) | | 9 | $C = \dfrac{\text{slope}}{\text{intercept}} + 1$ | BET constant from plot | — | | 10 | $\text{valid range: } 0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.30$ | IUPAC-recommended linear range for BET | — | | 11 | $r_K = \dfrac{2\gamma V_m \cos\theta}{RT \ln(P_0/P)}$ | **Kelvin equation** — pore radius | nm | | 12 | $t = \left[\dfrac{13.99}{0.034 - \log(P/P_0)}\right]^{1/2}$ | Harkins–Jura **t-curve** (statistical thickness) | Å | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refinery catalysts** — FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking) zeolites are selected and graded by BET area (typically 300–600 m²/g). - **Emission control** — Three-way catalysts (Pt/Rh/Pd on washcoat) require controlled high-area supports. - **Pharmaceutical industry** — Drug dissolution rate is proportional to surface area. - **Battery electrodes** — High surface area carbon blacks (e.g., Ketjenblack > 1000 m²/g) enable fast ion transport. - **Carbon capture** — Amine-impregnated high-area silicas (SBA-15, MCM-41) for CO₂ sorption. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the BET Equation (Exhaustive) **Starting point:** Consider adsorption of layers 1, 2, 3, ... $\infty$. Let $S_0, S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_i$ represent the surface area covered by $0, 1, 2, \ldots, i$ adsorbed layers. At dynamic equilibrium, the rate of condensation on layer $i$ equals the rate of evaporation from layer $i$: $$ a_i \, P \, S_{i-1} = b_i \, S_i \, e^{-E_i/RT} $$ **Step 1** — For the *first* layer ($i = 1$): $$ a_1 P S_0 = b_1 S_1 e^{-E_1/RT} $$ **Step 2** — For the *second and subsequent* layers ($i \geq 2$), the heat of adsorption equals the heat of liquefaction $E_L$ (since the surface beneath is now the condensed adsorbate, not the solid): $$ a_i P S_{i-1} = b_i S_i e^{-E_L/RT} \quad \text{for } i \geq 2 $$ **Step 3** — Define the ratio $x = \dfrac{P}{P_0} \cdot e^{E_L/RT} \cdot \dfrac{a_i}{b_i}$. By the standard thermodynamic argument, at equilibrium $P = P_0$, the bulk liquid is in equilibrium with its own vapour, forcing $x = P/P_0$. **Step 4** — Solve the recurrence relation $S_i = x S_{i-1}$: $$ S_1 = c x, \quad S_2 = c x^2, \quad \ldots, \quad S_i = c x^i $$ where $c = S_0 \cdot \dfrac{a_1}{b_1} e^{E_1/RT}$. **Step 5** — Total surface area: $$ S_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} S_i = S_0 \left( 1 + c \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} x^i \right) = S_0 \left( 1 + \frac{cx}{1-x} \right) $$ **Step 6** — Total volume adsorbed (proportional to the *number* of adsorbed molecules, which equals $\sum i S_i$): $$ V = V_m \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} i S_i = V_m \cdot S_0 \cdot c \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i x^i $$ Using the standard sum identity $\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i x^{i} = \dfrac{x}{(1-x)^2}$: $$ V = V_m \cdot S_0 \cdot c \cdot \frac{x}{(1-x)^2} $$ **Step 7** — Divide and substitute $c = \dfrac{1}{x} \cdot \dfrac{C - 1}{C}$ where the BET constant $C$ is defined as $C = e^{(E_1 - E_L)/RT}$: $$ \frac{V}{V_m} = \frac{C x}{(1-x)(1-x + Cx)} $$ **Step 8** — Replace $x = P/P_0$ and rearrange to obtain the **linear BET form**: $$ \frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C - 1}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Memory trick:** The slope is always larger than the intercept when $C > 1$ (typical for N₂). If slope $\approx$ intercept, then $C \to \infty$ (Type I isotherm, microporous). ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Standard) **Problem:** A 0.512 g sample of a silica catalyst was analysed by N₂ physisorption at 77 K. The following equilibrium data were obtained: | $P/P_0$ | Volume adsorbed $V$ (cm³/g at STP) | |---|---| | 0.05 | 78.4 | | 0.10 | 92.7 | | 0.15 | 103.5 | | 0.20 | 113.1 | | 0.25 | 121.8 | | 0.30 | 130.2 | **Calculate the BET surface area and the BET constant $C$.** ### Solution — Step 1: Construct the BET linear table | $P/P_0$ | $P_0 - P$ | $V$ | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)}$ (y) | |---|---|---|---| | 0.05 | 0.95 | 78.4 | $\dfrac{0.05}{78.4 \times 0.95} = 6.71 \times 10^{-4}$ | | 0.10 | 0.90 | 92.7 | $\dfrac{0.10}{92.7 \times 0.90} = 1.199 \times 10^{-3}$ | | 0.15 | 0.85 | 103.5 | $\dfrac{0.15}{103.5 \times 0.85} = 1.705 \times 10^{-3}$ | | 0.20 | 0.80 | 113.1 | $\dfrac{0.20}{113.1 \times 0.80} = 2.211 \times 10^{-3}$ | | 0.25 | 0.75 | 121.8 | $\dfrac{0.25}{121.8 \times 0.75} = 2.736 \times 10^{-3}$ | | 0.30 | 0.70 | 130.2 | $\dfrac{0.30}{130.2 \times 0.70} = 3.291 \times 10^{-3}$ | ### Solution — Step 2: Linear regression A least-squares fit of $y$ vs. $x = P/P_0$ gives: $$ \text{slope } m = 1.083 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^{-3}\text{g} $$ $$ \text{intercept } c = 1.59 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^{-3}\text{g} $$ ### Solution — Step 3: Extract $V_m$ and $C$ $$ V_m = \frac{1}{m + c} = \frac{1}{(1.083 + 0.159) \times 10^{-2}} = 80.45 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g (STP)} $$ $$ C = \frac{m}{c} + 1 = \frac{1.083 \times 10^{-2}}{1.59 \times 10^{-4}} + 1 = 69.1 $$ ### Solution — Step 4: Calculate $S_{BET}$ $$ S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \, N_A \, A_{cs}}{V_{molar}} $$ $$ S_{BET} = \frac{80.45 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m}^2}{22414 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}} $$ $$ S_{BET} = \frac{80.45 \times 9.756 \times 10^{4} \text{ m}^2}{22414} = 350.2 \text{ m}^2/\text{g} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check:** Typical high-area silica has $S_{BET} = 300\text{–}800 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$. The value **350.2 m²/g** is internally consistent. ✅ > > The $C$ value of **69.1** indicates **moderate adsorbent–adsorbate interaction** (typical range 50–200 for non-microporous oxides). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Isotherms | Isotherm | Layers | Surface | Equation | Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Henry** | Sub-monolayer | Ideal | $V = K_H P$ | Very low $P/P_0$ | | **Langmuir** | Monolayer | Homogeneous | $V = \dfrac{V_m b P}{1 + b P}$ | Chemisorption, micropores (Type I) | | **Freundlich** | Multilayer, heterogeneous | $V = K P^{1/n}$ | Empirical | Liquid-phase adsorption | | **Temkin** | Monolayer, linear $\Delta H$ decrease | — | $V = B \ln(K P)$ | Heterogeneous chemisorption | | **BET** | Multilayer, homogeneous | $V = \dfrac{V_m C P}{(P_0 - P)[1 + (C-1)P/P_0]}$ | Mesoporous catalysts (Type II, IV) | **Standard for BET area** | | **Kelvin + BJH** | Capillary condensation | Cylindrical pores | $r_K = \dfrac{2\gamma V_m \cos\theta}{RT \ln(P_0/P)}$ | Pore size distribution (mesopores) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 BET Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB GasCyl["N2 Gas Cylinder
High Purity 99.999%"] HeCyl["He Gas Cylinder
For Dead Volume"] GC["Gas Cabinet
Regulators and Valves"] Dosing["Dosing Manifold
Pressure Transducer P1"] SampleT["Sample Tube
Containing Catalyst
at 77 K in LN2 Dewar"] RefT["Reference Tube
Empty Balance Volume"] PC["Personal Computer
Data Acquisition"] Vac["Vacuum Pump
Rotary plus Turbomolecular"] DegasUnit["Outgassing Station
300 C under Vacuum"] GasCyl --> GC HeCyl --> GC GC --> Dosing GC --> RefT Dosing --> SampleT Dosing --> PC SampleT --> PC RefT --> PC SampleT --> DegasUnit Vac --> DegasUnit Vac --> Dosing PC --> Result["Isotherm Plot
V vs P by P0
BET Linear Plot"] ``` ## 4.2 BET Linear Plot — Conceptual Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR A["P by P0 equals 0.05"] --> B["Compute y equals
P divided by V times P0 minus P"] C["P by P0 equals 0.30"] --> B B --> D["Plot y versus P by P0"] D --> E["Linear Region
P by P0 between 0.05 and 0.30"] E --> F["Extract Slope m
and Intercept c"] F --> G["Vm equals 1 over m plus c"] F --> H["C equals m by c plus 1"] G --> I["Compute S BET
Using N2 Cross Section
0.162 nm squared"] H --> I I --> J["Surface Area in m squared per g"] ``` ## 4.3 The Six IUPAC Physisorption Isotherm Types ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Type1["Type I Microporous"] T1["Zeolites, Activated Carbon
Langmuir type, sharp knee"] end subgraph Type2["Type II Non porous Macroporous"] T2["S BET determined easily
Infinite multilayer"] end subgraph Type3["Type III Weak Interaction"] T3["Non porous, low affinity
BET not recommended"] end subgraph Type4["Type IV Mesoporous"] T4["Capillary condensation
Hysteresis loop, BJH applicable"] end subgraph Type5["Type V Weak plus Mesopores"] T5["Similar to Type III plus hysteresis"] end subgraph Type6["Type 6 Layer by Layer"] T6["Non porous step isotherm
Graphite basal plane"] end ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow — From Sample to BET Surface Area ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["Step 1 Sample Weighing
typically 0.1 to 1.0 g"] --> S2["Step 2 Outgassing
150 to 350 C for 4 to 16 hours
Vacuum less than 10 mPa"] S2 --> S3["Step 3 Dead Volume Calibration
He at room temperature"] S3 --> S4["Step 4 Adsorption
Dose known volumes of N2
Record equilibrium P and V"] S4 --> S5["Step 5 Build Isotherm
Plot V versus P by P0"] S5 --> S6["Step 6 Select Linear Range
P by P0 between 0.05 and 0.30"] S6 --> S7["Step 7 Linear Regression
Obtain slope m and intercept c"] S7 --> S8["Step 8 Calculate Vm and C"] S8 --> S9["Step 9 Compute S BET
Report in m squared per g"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the term "BET surface area". What are the standard experimental conditions used for its measurement?** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Definition (1 mark):** The BET surface area is the **total accessible surface area per unit mass** of a porous solid, determined from the **multilayer physical adsorption** of a gas (typically N₂) using the **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) equation**. > > **Standard Conditions (2 marks):** > 1. **Adsorbate:** Nitrogen (N₂) gas, **cross-sectional area 0.162 nm²** > 2. **Temperature:** **77 K** (boiling point of liquid nitrogen) > 3. **Sample preparation:** Outgassing at 150–350 °C under high vacuum (< 10 mPa) for 4–16 h to remove adsorbed moisture/volatiles > 4. **Relative pressure range:** $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.30$ (linear region of BET plot) > 5. **Equation used:** $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption. Which of the two is used in the BET method and why?** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption | > |---|---|---| > | **Forces** | van der Waals (weak) | Covalent / ionic (strong) | > | **Enthalpy** | 5–40 kJ/mol | 40–400 kJ/mol | > | **Specificity** | Non-specific (all surfaces) | Specific (surface dependent) | > | **Layers** | Multilayer | Monolayer only | > | **Reversibility** | Reversible | Often irreversible | > | **Activation energy** | None | Finite (activated) | > > **BET uses physisorption (1 mark)** because: (i) it is **non-specific**, giving the *total* surface area rather than active sites; (ii) **multilayer formation** is essential for the BET model; (iii) **reversibility** allows desorption for re-use; (iv) **low heat** avoids altering the catalyst structure during measurement. --- ## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. The split is typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### ✅ **OPTION A: QUESTION A (14 MARKS)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]** **Q.A(a) [7 marks]** — State the assumptions of the BET theory. Derive the linear form of the BET equation starting from the Langmuir concept of monolayer adsorption. **Q.A(b) [7 marks]** — A catalyst sample weighing 0.725 g gives a monolayer volume of 95.5 cm³/g (STP) of N₂ at 77 K. Calculate the BET surface area. (Given: cross-sectional area of N₂ = 0.162 nm², molar volume at STP = 22414 cm³/mol.) --- ### ✅ **OPTION B: QUESTION B (14 MARKS)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q.B(a) [7 marks]** — Describe with a neat labelled diagram the **volumetric static method** for the measurement of BET surface area. Explain the role of the **dead volume** and **outgassing**. **Q.B(b) [7 marks]** — From the following adsorption data of N₂ at 77 K on a zeolite catalyst, calculate the BET surface area and the constant $C$: | $P/P_0$ | $V$ (cm³/g STP) | |---|---| | 0.06 | 105.3 | | 0.12 | 122.0 | | 0.18 | 135.2 | | 0.24 | 146.7 | | 0.30 | 156.8 | --- ## 📕 Model Solutions for Part B (14-Mark) ### **Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks** **Assumptions of BET theory (3 marks):** 1. Gas molecules physically adsorb on the surface in **infinitely many layers**. 2. The **first layer** has heat of adsorption $E_1$; **subsequent layers** have heat of adsorption equal to the **heat of liquefaction** $E_L$. 3. Langmuir's equation applies to each individual layer. 4. At saturation pressure $P = P_0$, the adsorbate condenses to a **bulk liquid**. 5. **No lateral interactions** between adsorbed molecules. **Derivation (4 marks):** [As shown in §3.1 above — give concise steps] - Rate of condensation = Rate of evaporation for each layer. - Recurrence: $S_i = x S_{i-1}$ where $x = P/P_0$. - Sum geometric series. - Define $C = e^{(E_1 - E_L)/RT}$. - Final linear form: $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ ✓ --- ### **Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks** $$ S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \, N_A \, A_{cs}}{V_{molar}} $$ Substitute the values: - $V_m = 95.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$ - $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$ - $A_{cs} = 0.162 \text{ nm}^2 = 0.162 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m}^2$ - $V_{molar} = 22414 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$ $$ S_{BET} = \frac{95.5 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162 \times 10^{-18}}{22414} $$ $$ S_{BET} = \frac{95.5 \times 9.756 \times 10^{4}}{22414} = \frac{9.317 \times 10^{6}}{22414} = 415.6 \text{ m}^2/\text{g} $$ > **Answer:** $S_{BET} \approx 416 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$ ✓ --- ### **Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks** **Volumetric Static Method (5 marks):** 1. **Sample preparation:** Weigh accurately $m$ grams of catalyst. Place in a clean, dry sample tube. 2. **Outgassing (1 mark):** Connect to high-vacuum line. Heat to 150–350 °C for several hours to remove physisorbed water and impurities. Cool to room temperature. 3. **Dead-volume calibration (1 mark):** Introduce Helium (non-adsorbing gas) at room temperature into the sample side. By measuring $P, V, T$ using the gas law, compute the **dead volume** $V_d$ (free space around the sample). 4. **Adsorption measurement (1 mark):** Immerse sample tube in liquid N₂ dewar at 77 K. Dose known volumes of N₂ incrementally. After each dose, record equilibrium pressure $P$. 5. **Data reduction:** Apply dead-volume correction to obtain **true volume adsorbed** $V_{ads}$ at each $P/P_0$. **Role of dead volume:** It is the gas in the *free space* around the sample (not adsorbed). It must be subtracted because only the *adsorbed* gas contributes to surface area calculation. **Role of outgassing:** Removes pre-adsorbed contaminants (water, CO₂) that would block adsorption sites and lead to **erroneously low** BET areas. **Diagram:** Refer to §4.1 Mermaid block. --- ### **Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Build BET table (3 marks):** | $P/P_0$ | $V$ | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0-P)} \times 10^{3}$ (cm³/g)⁻¹ | |---|---|---| | 0.06 | 105.3 | $\dfrac{0.06}{105.3 \times 0.94} = 0.606$ | | 0.12 | 122.0 | $\dfrac{0.12}{122.0 \times 0.88} = 1.118$ | | 0.18 | 135.2 | $\dfrac{0.18}{135.2 \times 0.82} = 1.622$ | | 0.24 | 146.7 | $\dfrac{0.24}{146.7 \times 0.76} = 2.152$ | | 0.30 | 156.8 | $\dfrac{0.30}{156.8 \times 0.70} = 2.733$ | **Step 2 — Linear regression (2 marks):** Slope $m = 9.13 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/cm}^3$ Intercept $c = 0.06 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/cm}^3 = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g/cm}^3$ (Use a calculator to confirm exact values in the exam.) **Step 3 — Extract parameters (1 mark):** $$ V_m = \frac{1}{m + c} = \frac{1}{(9.13 + 0.06) \times 10^{-3}} = 108.7 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g} $$ $$ C = \frac{m}{c} + 1 = \frac{9.13 \times 10^{-3}}{6.0 \times 10^{-5}} + 1 = 153.2 $$ **Step 4 — Surface area (1 mark):** $$ S_{BET} = \frac{108.7 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162 \times 10^{-18}}{22414} = 473.4 \text{ m}^2/\text{g} $$ > **Answer:** $S_{BET} \approx 473 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$, $C \approx 153$ (indicates strong adsorbent–adsorbate interaction, consistent with zeolite microporosity). ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks > > 1. **Using wrong cross-sectional area (–2 marks):** Students frequently use $A_{cs} = 0.195$ or $0.215$ nm² instead of the IUPAC-recommended **0.162 nm² for N₂ at 77 K**. Memorise the table. > 2. **Wrong units of $V_m$ (–1 mark):** Always convert to **cm³/g at STP** before substituting. > 3. **Forgetting $N_A$ (Avogadro's number) (–1 mark):** The formula needs $N_A$ to convert volume → number of molecules → area. > 4. **Selecting wrong $P/P_0$ range (–1 mark):** Points below 0.05 are not reliable; above 0.30, multilayer physics deviates from BET. Use **0.05–0.30**. > 5. **Skipping unit conversion of $A_{cs}$ from nm² to m² (–1 mark):** $1 \text{ nm}^2 = 10^{-18} \text{ m}^2$. > 6. **Not mentioning sample outgassing (–1 mark):** A standard BET answer should always include degassing. > 7. **Confusing BET area with active metal area (–1 mark):** BET = total area (physisorption); active metal area = chemisorption (H₂, CO uptake). --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2 Surface Area Measurements** - ✅ **BET** stands for **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (1938)** — multilayer adsorption theory. - ✅ **Standard adsorbate:** N₂ at **77 K**; **A_cs = 0.162 nm²**. - ✅ **Linear BET equation:** $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0-P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$. - ✅ **Valid range:** $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.30$ (IUPAC). - ✅ **Surface area formula:** $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m N_A A_{cs}}{V_{molar}}$. - ✅ **Constants to memorise:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$, $V_{molar}(\text{STP}) = 22414 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$. - ✅ **Physisorption** is **multilayer, weak, non-specific, reversible**; **Chemisorption** is **monolayer, strong, specific, often irreversible**. - ✅ **Three steps in BET measurement:** (1) **Outgassing** (2) **Dead volume calibration with He** (3) **N₂ adsorption at 77 K**. - ✅ **High $C$ value (>100)** = strong interaction = microporous; **low $C$ (2–50)** = weak interaction = mesoporous. - ✅ **Six IUPAC isotherms:** Type I (microporous), II (non-porous), III (weak), IV (mesoporous with hysteresis), V (weak + mesoporous), VI (layer-by-layer). - ✅ **Volumetric (static) method** uses calibrated dosing volumes; **Gravimetric method** uses a microbalance. - ✅ **Kelvin equation** for pore size; **t-plot** and **α-plot** for micropore analysis (advanced). - ✅ **Ar at 87 K** and **Kr at 77 K** are used for **low-area samples** (< 1 m²/g) to amplify the signal. --- > 🎯 **End of Notes — Catalyst Characterisation: Surface Area Measurements (Module 2)** > *You are now equipped to score full marks on any KTU question from this topic!*

topics.title: BET Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** BET Theory # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of BET Theory ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) Theory** is a generalized extension of the **Langmuir monolayer adsorption isotherm** to **multilayer physical adsorption (physisorption)**. Formulated in **1938** by Stephen Brunauer, Paul Hugh Emmett, and Edward Teller, the theory mathematically describes the relationship between the volume of gas adsorbed on a solid surface and the **relative pressure (P/P₀)** of the adsorbing gas at a constant temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT412 Module 2):** > BET theory is the *primary* quantitative method used in heterogeneous catalysis to determine the **specific surface area (S_BET)** of porous solids such as supported metal oxides, zeolites, activated carbons, and industrial catalysts. It is the *only* universally accepted standard for surface area measurement in catalyst characterization. Mathematically, the linear (canonical) form of the BET equation is expressed as: $$\frac{P}{V \, (P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m \, C} + \left(\frac{C - 1}{V_m \, C}\right) \frac{P}{P_0}$$ where every symbol has a strict physical meaning defined in the formula sheet of Section 2. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Parking Lot & The Mall" Imagine a parking lot outside a busy shopping mall. The **Langmuir theory** says every visitor parks in *one* specific spot, and once that spot is filled, no one else can park — a **single-layer** situation. Now imagine the **BET reality**: a *multi-storey* parking complex. Cars can park on the **ground floor** (the actual catalyst surface), but they can *also* stack up on **second, third, and fourth floors** (additional physisorbed layers). The ground floor is the strongest (analogous to the first layer with high heat of adsorption), and each subsequent floor is slightly weaker (layers with heat approaching the heat of liquefaction of the gas). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** > BET does *not* assume all layers are equivalent. The **first layer** has a unique adsorption energy (related to the **BET constant C**), while **all subsequent layers** behave like the bulk liquid of the adsorbate (heat of adsorption ≈ heat of liquefaction, $\Delta H_{ads} \approx \Delta H_{liq}$). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | |---|---|---| | Cross-sectional area of N₂ molecule | $a_m$ | **0.162 nm²** (16.2 × 10⁻²⁰ m²) at **77 K** | | Molar volume of gas (STP) | $V_{STP}$ | **22,414 cm³/mol** | | Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** | | Cross-sectional area of Kr atom | $a_m$ | **0.195 nm²** (low surface area samples) | | Cross-sectional area of Ar atom | $a_m$ | **0.142 nm²** (at 87 K) | | Valid relative pressure range | $P/P_0$ | **0.05 to 0.30** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why N₂ at 77 K?** > Nitrogen is the standard adsorbate because its molecular size is well-characterized and **77 K** (boiling point of liquid nitrogen) provides a stable, easily accessible cryogenic temperature. For low-surface-area samples (< 1 m²/g), **Krypton (Kr)** is preferred due to its low saturation vapor pressure, which minimizes instrumental error. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Assumptions of BET Theory The BET model rests on **five rigorous postulates**. Every derivation in Section 3 flows from these: 1. **Layer-by-layer adsorption:** Gas molecules adsorb on the surface in **infinite layers**; there is no limit to the number of layers that can form at saturation. 2. **Identical adsorption sites:** The surface of the adsorbent contains a **uniform array of equivalent sites** (homogeneous surface assumption, inherited from Langmuir). 3. **Distinct first layer:** The heat of adsorption for the **first layer** ($\Delta H_1$) is a constant, characteristic of the gas–solid interaction. All **subsequent layers** (2nd, 3rd, …, nth) have an identical heat of adsorption equal to the **heat of liquefaction** ($\Delta H_L$) of the bulk adsorbate. 4. **No lateral interactions:** Adsorbed molecules in any given layer do *not* interact with each other sideways — this is the same mean-field assumption as Langmuir. 5. **Dynamic equilibrium at each layer:** At equilibrium, the **rate of condensation onto a layer equals the rate of evaporation from that layer**, layer by layer. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the BET Equation — A Layer-by-Layer Rate Balance | Step | Physical Event | Rate Equation | |---|---|---| | 1 | Bare surface (layer 0) → layer 1 | Condensation: $a_1 P \, \theta_0$; Evaporation: $b_1 \theta_1 \, e^{-E_1/RT}$ | | 2 | Layer 1 ↔ layer 2 | Condensation: $a_2 P \, \theta_1$; Evaporation: $b_2 \theta_2 \, e^{-E_L/RT}$ | | 3 | Layer $i-1$ ↔ layer $i$ | $a_i P \, \theta_{i-1} = b_i \theta_i \, e^{-E_L/RT}$ | | 4 | Total volume adsorbed = sum of all layer coverages | $V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \, \theta_i$ | The clever trick of BET is recognizing that for **all layers above the first**, the ratio $a_i / b_i$ is essentially the same as the equilibrium constant for **bulk liquefaction**. Defining: $$x = \frac{P}{P_0}, \quad c = \frac{a_1 b_2}{a_2 b_1} \, e^{(E_1 - E_L)/RT}$$ the derivation yields a **geometric series** that can be summed in closed form (see Section 3). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **BET linear form** | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0-P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | Plot yields straight line in range 0.05–0.30 | | 2 | **Non-linear BET form** | $V = \dfrac{V_m C x}{(1-x)\left[1 + (C-1)x\right]}$ | Where $x = P/P_0$ | | 3 | **Monolayer volume** | $V_m = \dfrac{1}{(\text{slope} + \text{intercept})}$ | Read from BET plot | | 4 | **BET constant** | $C = \dfrac{\text{slope}}{\text{intercept}} + 1$ | Indicates strength of adsorbate–adsorbent interaction | | 5 | **Specific surface area** | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m \, N_A \, a_m}{m \, V_{STP}}$ | Units: m²/g | | 6 | **Total surface area** | $S_{tot} = S_{BET} \times m$ | Units: m² | | 7 | **Relative pressure** | $x = P/P_0$ | $P_0$ = saturation vapor pressure at $T$ | | 8 | **Heat of first layer** | $E_1 = E_L + RT \ln C$ | From BET constant $C$ | | 9 | **Mean pore radius** (cylindrical) | $r_p = \dfrac{2 V_p}{S_{BET}}$ | Used with pore volume from $t$-plot or BJH | | 10 | **Point B method** | $V_m$ at the "knee" of Type II isotherm | Independent cross-check of BET $V_m$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip — The "C" Constant:** > A **large C value (>> 1)** means a strong interaction in the first layer, and the BET plot shows a sharp "knee" (Type II isotherm). A **low C value (≈ 2)** indicates a weak interaction and the isotherm is Type III (convex). For meaningful surface area determination, $C$ should be positive; for microporous materials the BET method is **inapplicable** because the assumption of infinite layers fails in narrow pores. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Catalyst Selection & Ranking:** Industrial catalyst manufacturers (e.g., for fluid catalytic cracking, hydrodesulfurization) routinely report $S_{BET}$ as a primary quality-control metric. - **Support Material Design:** High surface area supports like **γ-Al₂O₃** (~150–250 m²/g), **SBA-15** (~600–800 m²/g), and **activated carbon** (~1000–3000 m²/g) are benchmarked via BET. - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** BET is the official method (USP <846>) for characterizing the surface of drug powders, affecting dissolution and bioavailability. - **Battery & Fuel Cell R&D:** Electrode surface area in **Li-ion batteries** and **PEM fuel cells** is determined by BET on carbon blacks and Pt/C catalysts. - **Heterogeneous Catalysis Kinetics:** The rate of a surface reaction is proportional to active site density, which is often correlated with $S_{BET}$ when the surface composition is known. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the BET Equation We start by defining the **fractional coverage** of the bare surface as $\theta_0$ and the coverage of the $i$-th layer as $\theta_i$. The total volume of gas adsorbed is the sum over all layers: $$V = V_m \, \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \, \theta_i$$ The total surface is normalized: $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \theta_i = 1$. ### Step 1 — Equilibrium between bare surface and the first layer At equilibrium, the rate of condensation onto bare sites equals the rate of evaporation from layer 1: $$a_1 \, P \, \theta_0 = b_1 \, \theta_1 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_1}{RT}\right)$$ Solving for $\theta_1$: $$\theta_1 = \frac{a_1}{b_1} \, P \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_1}{RT}\right) \, \theta_0$$ Define a constant $y \equiv \frac{a_1 P}{b_1} \exp(E_1/RT)$ such that $\theta_1 = y \, \theta_0$. ### Step 2 — Equilibrium between consecutive upper layers For any $i \geq 2$, the heat of adsorption is the heat of liquefaction $E_L$, and the pre-exponential factors become those of the bulk liquid ($a_2 = a_3 = \cdots = a$, $b_2 = b_3 = \cdots = b$). The equilibrium condition is: $$a \, P \, \theta_{i-1} = b \, \theta_i \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_L}{RT}\right)$$ Rearranging: $$\theta_i = \frac{a}{b} \, P \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_L}{RT}\right) \, \theta_{i-1}$$ Define a second constant $x \equiv \frac{a P}{b} \exp(E_L/RT)$. By iteration from layer 2 upward: $$\theta_2 = x \, \theta_1, \quad \theta_3 = x \, \theta_2 = x^2 \theta_1, \quad \ldots, \quad \theta_i = x^{i-1} \theta_1 = x^{i-1} y \, \theta_0$$ ### Step 3 — Express all coverages in terms of $\theta_0$ Combining the results: $$\theta_0 = \theta_0, \quad \theta_1 = y \, \theta_0, \quad \theta_2 = y x \, \theta_0, \quad \theta_3 = y x^2 \, \theta_0, \quad \ldots$$ The general term is $\theta_i = y \, x^{i-1} \, \theta_0$ for $i \geq 1$. ### Step 4 — Apply the normalization condition $$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \theta_i = \theta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} y x^{i-1} \theta_0 = \theta_0 \left[1 + y \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} x^{i}\right] = 1$$ The infinite geometric series $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} x^{i} = \frac{1}{1-x}$ converges only when $x < 1$, i.e., $P < P_0$. We obtain: $$\theta_0 = \frac{1}{1 + y \cdot \frac{1}{1-x}} = \frac{1 - x}{1 - x + y}$$ ### Step 5 — Sum the total volume adsorbed $$V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \, \theta_i = V_m \, \theta_0 \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \, y x^{i-1}$$ The series $\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i x^{i-1} = \frac{1}{(1-x)^2}$. Therefore: $$V = V_m \, \theta_0 \, y \, \frac{1}{(1-x)^2} = V_m \, \frac{(1-x) \, y}{(1-x)^2 \, (1 - x + y)} = V_m \, \frac{y}{(1-x)\left[1 - x + y\right]}$$ ### Step 6 — Introduce the BET constant $C$ Recognize that the constant $y$ at $P = P_0$ (i.e., $x = 1$) becomes: $$y_0 = \frac{a_1 P_0}{b_1} \exp(E_1/RT)$$ And $x = P/P_0$, so $y = y_0 \cdot (P/P_0) = y_0 \cdot x$. The ratio $y/x$ at saturation gives a constant: $$C \equiv \frac{y_0}{x_{\text{at } P=P_0}} = \frac{a_1 b_2}{a_2 b_1} \exp\!\left(\frac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right)$$ Since $x = P/P_0$, we have $y = C \cdot x$. Substituting: $$V = V_m \, \frac{C x}{(1 - x)\left[1 + (C - 1) x\right]}$$ This is the **non-linear BET equation**, valid for $0 \leq P/P_0 < 1$. ### Step 7 — Convert to the linear form Invert and multiply by $(P_0 - P)/P$: $$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1 - x}{V_m \, C x} \left[1 + (C-1) x\right] = \frac{1 - x + (C-1) x}{V_m \, C x} = \frac{1 - x + C x - x}{V_m \, C x} = \frac{1 + (C-1) x - 2x}{V_m \, C x}$$ After careful algebra (carrying the $(1 - x) = (P_0 - P)/P_0$ consistently): $$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C - 1}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$ This is the canonical **linear BET form** with intercept $1/(V_m C)$ and slope $(C-1)/(V_m C)$. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Slopes, Intercepts, and KTU High-Yield Relationships | Quantity | Expressed via Slope ($S$) and Intercept ($I$) | Numerical Interpretation | |---|---|---| | Monolayer volume | $V_m = \dfrac{1}{S + I}$ | Always positive | | BET constant | $C = \dfrac{S}{I} + 1$ | $C > 0$ required | | Total surface area | $S_{tot} = \dfrac{V_m \, N_A \, a_m}{V_{STP}}$ | In m² | | Specific surface area | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{S_{tot}}{m_{sample}}$ | In m²/g | | Heat of first layer | $E_1 - E_L = RT \ln C$ | Used to characterize surface energetics | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A catalyst sample of mass **0.500 g** adsorbs **nitrogen at 77 K**. The following data were obtained: | $P/P_0$ | Volume adsorbed $V$ (cm³/g at STP) | |---|---| | 0.05 | 32.0 | | 0.10 | 45.0 | | 0.15 | 54.0 | | 0.20 | 61.0 | | 0.25 | 67.0 | | 0.30 | 73.0 | Determine (a) the monolayer capacity $V_m$, (b) the BET constant $C$, and (c) the specific surface area. ### Step (a) — Compute the BET function $\frac{P/P_0}{V(1 - P/P_0)}$ | $P/P_0$ | $V$ (cm³/g) | $1 - P/P_0$ | BET function (g/cm³) | |---|---|---|---| | 0.05 | 32.0 | 0.95 | 0.05 / (32.0 × 0.95) = **0.001645** | | 0.10 | 45.0 | 0.90 | 0.10 / (45.0 × 0.90) = **0.002469** | | 0.15 | 54.0 | 0.85 | 0.15 / (54.0 × 0.85) = **0.003268** | | 0.20 | 61.0 | 0.80 | 0.20 / (61.0 × 0.80) = **0.004098** | | 0.25 | 67.0 | 0.75 | 0.25 / (67.0 × 0.75) = **0.004975** | | 0.30 | 73.0 | 0.70 | 0.30 / (73.0 × 0.70) = **0.005869** | ### Step (b) — Linear regression on $(P/P_0)$ vs. BET function Using two-point slope as a check (full regression would be used in practice): $$S = \frac{0.005869 - 0.001645}{0.30 - 0.05} = \frac{0.004224}{0.25} = 0.01690 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ $$I = 0.001645 - (0.01690)(0.05) = 0.001645 - 0.000845 = 0.000800 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ ### Step (c) — Calculate $V_m$ and $C$ $$V_m = \frac{1}{S + I} = \frac{1}{0.01690 + 0.000800} = \frac{1}{0.01770} = 56.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g (STP)}$$ $$C = \frac{S}{I} + 1 = \frac{0.01690}{0.000800} + 1 = 21.13 + 1 \approx 22$$ ### Step (d) — Calculate $S_{BET}$ $$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \, N_A \, a_m}{V_{STP}} = \frac{56.5 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20}}{22414} \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$ $$S_{BET} = \frac{56.5 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.62 \times 10^{-19}}{22414} = \frac{5.514 \times 10^{6}}{22414} \approx 246.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$ > [!NOTE] > This $S_{BET}$ value (~246 m²/g) is characteristic of a high-quality **γ-alumina catalyst support**, a result typical of industrial hydrotreating catalysts. ## 3.4 Validation of Assumptions and Limitations | Limitation | Cause | Consequence | |---|---|---| | Restricted to **$P/P_0 \in [0.05, 0.30]$** | At higher $P/P_0$, capillary condensation occurs in pores | BET plot deviates upward | | **Microporous solids** (pore < 2 nm) | Pore-filling precedes multilayer formation | $S_{BET}$ overestimated by 20–30% | | **Strong adsorbate–adsorbent** ($C \to \infty$) | Singular first-layer behavior | Isotherm becomes Type II with sharp knee | | **Weak interaction** ($C \approx 2$) | Multilayer forms before monolayer is complete | Type III isotherm, BET plot concave | | **Chemisorption contamination** | True chemical bonds, not physical | Skews intercept; use outgassing at high $T$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — BET Measurement Workflow The following Mermaid diagram maps the **instrumental and computational flow** of a BET surface-area measurement, from gas dosing to final specific surface area report. This is the standard **Micromeritics ASAP / Quantachrome Autosorb** type flow. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sample Preparation
Outgas at 573 K
10 to the power of minus 5 Torr] --> B[Weigh Empty and Loaded Tubes
mass m in grams] B --> C[Install Sample Tube
in BET Analyzer Station] C --> D[Cryogen Bath
Liquid Nitrogen 77 K] D --> E[Dose Known Volume of N2
Incrementally Increase P] E --> F[Measure Equilibrium Pressure P
and Adsorbed Volume V at each step] F --> G[Build Adsorption Isotherm
V versus P slash P0] G --> H[Compute BET Function
Y equals P over V times P0 minus P] H --> I[Linear Regression
P slash P0 versus Y in range 0.05 to 0.30] I --> J[Extract Slope S and Intercept I] J --> K[Calculate Vm equals 1 over S plus I] J --> L[Calculate C equals S over I plus 1] K --> M[Compute SBET equals Vm NA am over m VSTP] L --> N[Validate C is positive
and within expected range] M --> O[Report Specific Surface Area
in m squared per gram] N --> O ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mathematical Architecture of the BET Equation This diagram captures the **layer-resolved rate balance** that forms the conceptual core of BET. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Bare Surface
theta 0] -->|condensation
a1 times P| B1[Layer 1
theta 1] B1 -->|condensation
a times P| C1[Layer 2
theta 2] C1 -->|condensation| D1[Layer 3
theta 3] D1 -->|condensation| E1[Layer n Infinite
theta n] E1 -->|geometric series sum| F1[Summation yields
V equals Vm times C x over 1 minus x in brackets] F1 --> G1[Linearize to
P over V P0 minus P equals 1 over Vm C plus C minus 1 over Vm C times x] B1 -.->|evaporation E1| A1 C1 -.->|evaporation EL| B1 D1 -.->|evaporation EL| C1 E1 -.->|evaporation EL| D1 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic of the BET Linear Plot A textual schematic of the canonical BET linear plot, showing the intercept, slope, and valid fitting region: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AX["X Axis: Relative Pressure P over P0"] X0[0.00] --> X1[0.05 Valid Start] X1 --> X2[0.15 Sweet Spot] X2 --> X3[0.30 Valid End] end subgraph AY["Y Axis: BET Function P over V times P0 minus P"] Y0[Origin] --> Y1[Intercept 1 over Vm C] Y1 --> Y2[Slope equals C minus 1 over Vm C] Y2 --> Y3[Steep linear rise] end AX --- AY ``` ## 4.4 Application Domain Map ```mermaid flowchart TD BET[BET Surface Area] --> CAT[Industrial Catalysts
FCC, HDS, Reforming] BET --> PHARMA[Pharmaceutical Powders
USP 846 Compliance] BET --> BATTERY[Energy Materials
Battery Electrodes, Supercapacitors] BET --> ENV[Environmental Sorbents
CO2 Capture, VOC Removal] BET --> CERAMIC[Advanced Ceramics
Dense Membrane Supports] BET --> BIO[Biosensors and Biomaterials
Scaffold Surface for Cell Growth] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **"State the five fundamental assumptions of the BET theory of multilayer adsorption."** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** The five fundamental assumptions of BET theory are: 1. **Multilayer adsorption is permitted:** Gas molecules can form an unlimited number of layers on the surface; there is no saturation at the monolayer. 2. **Homogeneous surface:** The catalyst surface has a **uniform array of energetically equivalent adsorption sites** — no preferred sites or "hot spots." 3. **Distinct first layer:** The **first layer** has its own characteristic heat of adsorption ($E_1$), while **all subsequent layers** share the heat of liquefaction ($E_L$) of the bulk adsorbate. 4. **No lateral interactions:** Molecules within a given layer do **not** interact sideways with each other; the model is mean-field in the horizontal direction. 5. **Dynamic equilibrium at every layer:** At equilibrium, the rate of condensation onto a layer exactly balances the rate of evaporation from that layer, layer by layer. ### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **"Why is nitrogen at 77 K the standard adsorbate for BET surface area measurements? Mention two alternative adsorbates and their applications."** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Why N₂ at 77 K:** - The cross-sectional area of N₂ is accurately known as **0.162 nm²**. - **77 K** (boiling point of liquid nitrogen) is a convenient, stable cryogenic temperature, allowing reproducible isotherms. - N₂ is inert, inexpensive, and non-toxic, ensuring it interacts only via physisorption. **Two alternatives:** - **Krypton (Kr) at 77 K:** Used for **low surface-area samples** (< 1 m²/g) because its low saturation vapor pressure ($P_0 \approx 2.5$ torr) provides higher resolution at low $P/P_0$. - **Argon (Ar) at 87 K:** Used to avoid the quadrupole-specific interactions of N₂ with certain ionic or polar surfaces, providing a more "neutral" probe of surface area. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B, 14 Marks) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Derive the BET equation for multilayer adsorption starting from the postulates of the theory. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 Marks) **(b)** The following adsorption data were obtained for N₂ at 77 K on a 0.850 g catalyst sample: | $P/P_0$ | Volume adsorbed (cm³ at STP) | |---|---| | 0.05 | 18.0 | | 0.10 | 26.0 | | 0.15 | 31.0 | | 0.20 | 35.0 | | 0.25 | 38.0 | | 0.30 | 41.0 | Calculate the **monolayer capacity ($V_m$)**, the **BET constant ($C$)**, and the **specific surface area** of the catalyst. Take $a_m = 0.162$ nm² for N₂. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution to Part A(a) **Assumptions (already stated in Question A.1):** (½ Mark each = 2.5 Marks) **Derivation outline (graded steps):** Define $\theta_0, \theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_i$ as fractional coverages of bare surface and layers 1, 2, …, i. (½ Mark) Equilibrium at layer 1 (condensation on bare surface vs. evaporation from layer 1): (1 Mark) $$a_1 P \theta_0 = b_1 \theta_1 e^{-E_1/RT} \implies \theta_1 = y \theta_0$$ Equilibrium at upper layers (for $i \geq 2$, all having $E_L$): (1 Mark) $$\theta_i = x \theta_{i-1} \implies \theta_i = y x^{i-1} \theta_0$$ Normalization: (1 Mark) $$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \theta_i = 1 \implies \theta_0 = \frac{1 - x}{1 - x + y}$$ Volume adsorbed sum using geometric series: (1 Mark) $$V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i y x^{i-1} \theta_0 = \frac{V_m y}{(1-x)(1 - x + y)}$$ Define $C = y_0 / x|_{P=P_0}$ and substitute $y = Cx$ to obtain the final non-linear and linear BET forms: (1 Mark) $$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$ #### Model Solution to Part A(b) Compute the BET function for each data point: (1 Mark) | $P/P_0$ | $V$ (cm³) | $1 - P/P_0$ | $Y = P/[V(P_0-P)]$ (cm⁻³) | |---|---|---|---| | 0.05 | 18.0 | 0.95 | 0.05 / (18.0 × 0.95) = 0.002924 | | 0.10 | 26.0 | 0.90 | 0.10 / (26.0 × 0.90) = 0.004274 | | 0.15 | 31.0 | 0.85 | 0.15 / (31.0 × 0.85) = 0.005693 | | 0.20 | 35.0 | 0.80 | 0.20 / (35.0 × 0.80) = 0.007143 | | 0.25 | 38.0 | 0.75 | 0.25 / (38.0 × 0.75) = 0.008772 | | 0.30 | 41.0 | 0.70 | 0.30 / (41.0 × 0.70) = 0.010452 | Linear regression over $P/P_0$ vs. $Y$ (1 Mark): Using end points as a check: $$S = \frac{0.010452 - 0.002924}{0.30 - 0.05} = \frac{0.007528}{0.25} = 0.03011$$ $$I = 0.002924 - (0.03011)(0.05) = 0.002924 - 0.001506 = 0.001418$$ Monolayer volume: (1 Mark) $$V_m = \frac{1}{S + I} = \frac{1}{0.03011 + 0.001418} = \frac{1}{0.03153} = 31.72 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ at STP}$$ BET constant: (1 Mark) $$C = \frac{S}{I} + 1 = \frac{0.03011}{0.001418} + 1 = 21.23 + 1 = 22.23$$ Specific surface area: (2 Marks) $$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m N_A a_m}{m \, V_{STP}} = \frac{31.72 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20}}{0.850 \times 22414}$$ $$S_{BET} = \frac{3.094 \times 10^{6}}{19052} = 162.4 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$ **Final Answer:** $V_m = 31.72$ cm³ (STP), $C = 22.2$, $S_{BET} \approx 162$ m²/g. ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` (Internal Choice Alternative) **(a)** Discuss the **limitations of the BET theory**. In which class of solids is the BET method strictly inapplicable, and why? (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare the **Langmuir and BET isotherms** in terms of (i) number of layers, (ii) heat of adsorption assumption, (iii) range of validity, and (iv) typical adsorption isotherm shape. Explain with neat sketches why a Type II isotherm arises from the BET model. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution to Part B(a) — Limitations of BET (7 Marks) 1. **Restricted pressure range** (1 Mark): The BET equation is strictly valid only in the relative pressure window **$0.05 < P/P_0 < 0.30$**. Below 0.05, the surface is too sparsely covered (assumptions break down); above 0.30, **capillary condensation** in pores deviates the isotherm from the BET prediction. 2. **Inapplicable to microporous solids** (2 Marks): BET assumes **infinite multilayer growth** on a flat surface. In **micropores (< 2 nm)** such as in zeolites and activated carbons, **pore filling** occurs *before* the monolayer is complete, violating the layer-by-layer premise. The BET method **overestimates** the surface area of microporous materials by 20–30%. 3. **Homogeneous surface assumption** (1 Mark): Real catalyst surfaces are heterogeneous, with varying adsorption energies. The BET constant $C$ is therefore a **weighted average** rather than a true thermodynamic quantity. 4. **No lateral interaction assumption** (1 Mark): At high coverage, adsorbate–adsorbate interactions become non-negligible; the mean-field approximation fails. 5. **Identical upper layers** (1 Mark): The assumption that all layers above the first have heat of adsorption equal to the bulk heat of liquefaction is only approximate. In reality, the second layer is often *slightly* different from the third and beyond. 6. **Inability to distinguish physisorption from chemisorption** (1 Mark): If chemisorption occurs simultaneously, the measured "BET area" is contaminated; **rigorous outgassing** at high temperature is required before measurement. **Strictly inapplicable to:** **Microporous solids** (pores < 2 nm — e.g., zeolites ZSM-5, MOFs, activated carbons), where the multilayer premise fails fundamentally. #### Model Solution to Part B(b) — Langmuir vs BET Comparison (7 Marks) | Feature | Langmuir Isotherm | BET Isotherm | |---|---|---| | (i) Number of layers | **Monolayer only** (1 Mark) | **Multilayer, infinite** (1 Mark) | | (ii) Heat of adsorption | Constant $\Delta H$ for all sites (1 Mark) | $\Delta H_1$ for first layer; $\Delta H_L$ (liquefaction) for layers $\geq 2$ (1 Mark) | | (iii) Range of validity | Low to moderate $P/P_0$ (up to ~0.1) (1 Mark) | $0.05 < P/P_0 < 0.30$ (1 Mark) | | (iv) Isotherm shape | **Type I** (Langmuir) — saturates at monolayer (1 Mark) | **Type II** (non-porous) — sharp knee at monolayer, then steady multilayer rise (1 Mark) | **Why Type II arises from BET (1 Mark):** As $P/P_0$ increases from 0, the first layer forms rapidly (sharp rise), then a **"knee"** appears at the monolayer coverage, followed by a slow, near-linear rise as additional layers stack up. This concave-then-convex shape is the **Type II physisorption isotherm**, characteristic of **non-porous or macroporous** solids and is the direct geometric prediction of the BET model. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in BET Theory Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to list ALL FIVE assumptions** of BET — KTU examiners allocate ½ mark each, totaling 2.5 of the 7 marks for derivation. > 2. **Sign error in linearized form:** Writing $\frac{C+1}{V_m C}$ instead of $\frac{C-1}{V_m C}$ in the slope — a single algebraic slip that costs 2–3 marks. > 3. **Using full $V$ (total cm³) instead of $V$ per gram** in the BET function calculation. Surface area requires **specific volume adsorbed (cm³/g)**. > 4. **Not stating the units explicitly** in the final answer — KTU penalizes missing units (m²/g) by 0.5 mark. > 5. **Forgetting to validate $C > 0$ and $P/P_0 < 0.30$** — examiners love to ask "what happens if your data extends to $P/P_0 = 0.9$?"
