The correct answer: *capillary condensation occurs, the BET equation is no longer valid, and you must use the Kelvin equation or BJH method instead.* > 6. **Confusing $a_m$ of N₂ (0.162 nm²) with that of Kr (0.195 nm²)** — a frequent error when an examiner substitutes the adsorbate mid-question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - **BET = extension of Langmuir to multilayer physisorption**, derived by Brunauer, Emmett, and Teller (1938). - **Five assumptions:** infinite layers, homogeneous surface, distinct first layer, no lateral interactions, layer-by-layer dynamic equilibrium. - **Linear BET form** is the working form for surface area determination; valid in $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.30$. - **$V_m$** = monolayer volume adsorbed at STP; from intercept and slope of BET plot: $V_m = 1/(S + I)$. - **$C$** = BET constant; from $C = S/I + 1$; large $C$ → strong first-layer interaction (Type II isotherm); small $C$ → weak interaction (Type III isotherm). - **Surface area formula:** $S_{BET} = V_m N_A a_m / (m V_{STP})$ in m²/g. - **Standard adsorbate:** N₂ at 77 K with $a_m = 0.162$ nm²; alternatives Kr (77 K) and Ar (87 K). - **Point B** on the isotherm (the "knee") independently gives $V_m$ — useful for verification. - **Strictly invalid for microporous solids** (pores < 2 nm); use Dubinin–Radushkevich or $t$-plot instead. - **Heat of first layer** can be recovered from $C$: $E_1 = E_L + RT \ln C$. - **Mean pore radius** (for cylindrical pores): $r_p = 2 V_p / S_{BET}$. - **Above $P/P_0 = 0.30$:** BET fails; use **BJH** (mesopores) or **HK/SF** (micropores) methods. - **Examination must-knows:** (a) full derivation, (b) all 5 assumptions, (c) numerical problem-solving, (d) limitations table, (e) comparison with Langmuir.

topics.title: Pore Size Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pore Size Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Pore Size Distribution (PSD)** is a fundamental textural property of porous solid catalysts that describes the relative abundance of pores of different equivalent diameters (or widths) present in the catalyst material, expressed as a function of pore size. Mathematically, it is the derivative of the cumulative pore volume (or pore area) with respect to the pore radius:

$$\frac{dV}{dr} = f(r)$$

where $V$ is the cumulative pore volume and $r$ is the equivalent pore radius. The PSD provides a complete statistical fingerprint of the internal void architecture of a catalyst pellet, determining which reactant molecules can diffuse into the active sites and at what rate the reaction will proceed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pore size distribution is a high-yield topic. Students MUST know the IUPAC classification, the Kelvin equation, the BJH method, and be able to interpret physisorption isotherms (Type II, IV, and V) in the context of mesoporous catalysts.

## 1.2 IUPAC Classification of Pores (KTU 2024 Must-Know)

The **International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)** classifies pores into three broad categories based on their equivalent width ($d$):

| Pore Class | Equivalent Width ($d$) | Typical Catalyst Examples | Dominant Phenomena |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Micropores** | $d < 2$ nm | Zeolites (ZSM-5, Faujasite), Activated Carbon, MOFs | Micropore filling, molecular sieving |
| **Mesopores** | $2 \le d \le 50$ nm | $\gamma$-Alumina, MCM-41, SBA-15, Silica Gel | Capillary condensation, multilayer adsorption |
| **Macropores** | $d > 50$ nm | Pellets, Extrudates, Raschig rings | Knudsen & molecular diffusion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-classification of Micropores:** IUPAC further divides micropores into **ultra-micropores** ($d < 0.7$ nm) and **super-micropores** ($0.7 \le d \le 2$ nm), since the mechanism of filling differs significantly.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge City" Model

Imagine a **sponge city** (porous catalyst pellet) consisting of interconnected tunnels of different diameters:

- **Macropores** are the **broad highways** → cars (reactant molecules) enter easily and travel fast.
- **Mesopores** are the **city streets** → most vehicles can navigate; some congestion (capillary condensation) occurs.
- **Micropores** are the **narrow alleyways** → only small molecules or specific shapes fit through; the rest are excluded (**shape-selective catalysis**).

The **Pore Size Distribution** is essentially the **statistical map** of how many highways, streets, and alleyways the city has. A catalyst designed for a bulky molecule (e.g., heavy oil cracking) needs *few* micropores and *many* meso/macropores, while a catalyst for selective methanol-to-olefins (MTO) conversion needs *uniform* micropores (e.g., ZSM-5).

## 1.4 Why PSD Matters in Catalytic Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PSD directly controls the **Weisz-Prater criterion** for internal diffusion limitations, the **Thiele modulus ($\phi$)**, and the **effectiveness factor ($\eta$)** of a catalyst pellet. A narrow PSD gives uniform diffusion path lengths, while a bimodal PSD (micro + meso) often indicates an ideal hierarchical catalyst.

**Critical Performance Metrics Controlled by PSD:**
1. **External reactant accessibility** — pore mouth blocking.
2. **Active site utilization** — how many internal sites see the reactant.
3. **Diffusion regime** — molecular, Knudsen, or configurational.
4. **Catalyst deactivation** — coke deposition preferentially occurs in narrow pores.
5. **Mechanical strength** — macropores act as crack initiators.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How PSD Is Measured

Physisorption of **N₂ at 77 K** (or **Ar at 87 K** for microporous materials) on the catalyst surface is the **standard ISO 15901 / IUPAC recommended** method to determine PSD. The principle relies on **condensation-evaporation transitions** within pores at pressures below saturation, where the **Kelvin equation** relates the equilibrium vapor pressure to pore radius.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why N₂ at 77 K?** Nitrogen is inert, non-polar, has a well-defined molecular cross-section ($0.162 \text{ nm}^2$), and 77 K is just above its triple point, ensuring monolayer-multilayer-condensation behavior is reproducible.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of PSD Determination

The standard BJH (Barrett–Joyner–Halenda) workflow followed in KTU-board answers:

1. **Record the N₂ adsorption–desorption isotherm** at 77 K on a volumetric or gravimetric sorption analyzer.
2. **Convert the isotherm** from volume adsorbed ($V_{\text{ads}}$) vs. relative pressure ($P/P^0$) into **cumulative pore volume** at each $P/P^0$ step.
3. **Apply the Kelvin equation** to each $P/P^0$ point to obtain the **Kelvin radius** ($r_K$) of the pore that empties (desorption) or fills (adsorption) at that pressure.
4. **Correct for the adsorbed film thickness** ($t$) on the pore wall using the **Harkins–Jura** or **Halsey** correlation.
5. **Compute the true pore radius**: $r_p = r_K + t$.
6. **Differentiate** the cumulative volume curve with respect to $r_p$ to obtain $\dfrac{dV}{dr_p}$ — the **PSD function**.

## 2.3 The Core Equations — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **Board-marked equations**. Memorize the form, the symbols, and the constant values.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^0}\right) = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_K \, R T}$$ | **Kelvin equation** (cylinder model) | dimensionless / m |
| 2 | $$r_p = r_K + t$$ | True pore radius = Kelvin radius + film thickness | nm |
| 3 | $$t \;\;[\text{Å}] = \sqrt{\frac{13.99}{0.034 - \log_{10}(P/P^0)}}$$ | **Halsey equation** (N₂ at 77 K) | Å |
| 4 | $$t \;\;[\text{nm}] = \left(\frac{0.1399}{0.034 - \log_{10}(P/P^0)}\right)^{0.5}$$ | Halsey equation (alt. form) | nm |
| 5 | $$t \;\;[\text{nm}] = \left(\frac{0.1}{1 - P/P^0}\right)^{0.4}$$ | **Kellar–Küppers** film thickness | nm |
| 6 | $$t = \left(\frac{0.364}{1 - P/P^0}\right)^{0.4} \quad (\text{nm})$$ | **Harkins–Jura** correlation | nm |
| 7 | $$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^0}\right) = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m}{r_K R T}$$ | Kelvin eqn. for **complete wetting** ($\theta = 0$) | — |
| 8 | $$A_{\text{BET}} = \frac{(V_m \, N_A \, \sigma)}{(22414 \, m_{cat})}$$ | Specific surface area (BET) | m²/g |
| 9 | $$r_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2 \, V_p}{A_{\text{BET}}}$$ | Mean hydraulic pore radius | nm |
| 10 | $$\frac{dV}{dr_p} = \text{PSD function}$$ | Pore size distribution (BJH output) | cm³/(g·nm) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard constants for N₂ at 77 K:** Surface tension $\gamma = 8.85 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m; Molar volume $V_m = 34.7$ cm³/mol; $\cos\theta = 1$ (assumed complete wetting); $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $T = 77.4$ K.

## 2.4 Why the Kelvin Equation Needs a "Film Correction"

The Kelvin equation **assumes** the meniscus sits at the very wall of the pore. In reality, **multilayer adsorption** has already deposited a film of thickness $t$ on the wall before capillary condensation occurs. If we do **not subtract $t$**, the calculated pore radius is **systematically too small** — a classic KTU board trap. Always write:

$$r_p^{\text{true}} = r_K + t$$

## 2.5 Classification of the Six IUPAC Physisorption Isotherms (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For PSD analysis, **only Isotherms II, IV, and VI** are typically encountered in heterogeneous catalyst characterization.

| IUPAC Isotherm | Pore Nature | Hysteresis? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | Microporous | No | Zeolites, activated carbon |
| **Type II** | Non-porous / macroporous | No | Dense oxide, e.g., $\alpha$-Al₂O₃ |
| **Type III** | Weak adsorbate–adsorbent | No | Graphite / N₂ (rare) |
| **Type IV** | Mesoporous | **Yes** (H1–H5) | $\gamma$-Al₂O₃, MCM-41 |
| **Type V** | Weak + mesoporous | Yes | Activated carbon with large pores |
| **Type VI** | Stepwise on uniform surface | No | Graphene, exfoliated clays |

## 2.6 Hysteresis Loops — Deciphering Pore Geometry (KTU Examiner's Favourite)

The **shape of the hysteresis loop** in a Type IV isotherm tells us the **pore shape**, even without running BJH. This is high-yield for KTU descriptive questions.

| Hysteresis | Pore Shape | Pore Network | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **H1** | Cylindrical, open ends | Uniform, narrow PSD | MCM-41, SBA-15 |
| **H2** | Ink-bottle | Constricted, complex | Silica gel, mesoporous glass |
| **H3** | Slit-shaped, non-rigid | Aggregates of plate-like particles | Pillared clays |
| **H4** | Narrow slit-like | Micro + meso mix | Micro-mesoporous carbons |
| **H5** | Both open + partially blocked | Uncommon, unusual | Modified silicas |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Catalyst selection:** Choosing a mesoporous $\gamma$-Al₂O₃ (mean pore size 8 nm) for VGO hydrocracking to avoid diffusion limitation of bulky resin molecules.
- **Zeolite catalyst design:** Matching the micropore aperture of SAPO-34 (~0.38 nm) to selectively produce light olefins in the MTO process.
- **Deactivation diagnosis:** A PSD shift toward smaller pore size after reaction indicates **coke plugging** in the mesopore mouths.
- **Quality control in catalyst manufacturing:** Batch-to-batch reproducibility is checked by ensuring the **median pore radius** lies within $\pm 1$ nm of specification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Kelvin Equation for a Cylindrical Pore

**Starting premise:** A liquid meniscus inside a cylindrical pore of radius $r_K$ is in mechanical equilibrium with its vapor at a reduced pressure $P/P^0 < 1$.

**Step 1 — Mechanical equilibrium of the meniscus.** The Young–Laplace equation gives the pressure difference across a curved liquid–vapor interface:

$$\Delta P = \gamma \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$

where $\gamma$ is the surface tension, and $R_1$, $R_2$ are the principal radii of curvature of the meniscus.

**Step 2 — Specialize to a cylindrical pore with hemispherical meniscus.** For a hemispherical meniscus inside a cylinder, $R_1 = R_2 = r_K$. The equation reduces to:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r_K}$$

**Step 3 — Relate $\Delta P$ to vapor pressure using the Kelvin–Clausius treatment.** For a spherical droplet (or hemispherical cap) of radius $r_K$, thermodynamic equilibrium between liquid and vapor gives:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^0}\right) = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_K \, R T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Board-marked form** of the Kelvin equation. For complete wetting (which is the standard assumption for N₂ on most oxides), $\cos\theta = 1$ and the negative sign combined with $\cos\theta = 1$ yields a $\ln(P/P^0)$ that is negative — i.e., condensation occurs at $P < P^0$ inside the pore.

**Step 4 — Verify the limiting behavior.**
- As $r_K \to \infty$ (flat interface), $\ln(P/P^0) \to 0$, so $P \to P^0$. ✅
- As $r_K \to 0$ (very narrow pore), $\ln(P/P^0)$ becomes a large negative number, meaning $P \ll P^0$ — i.e., smaller pores fill first. ✅

**Step 5 — Solve for the Kelvin radius.** Rearranging explicitly for $r_K$:

$$r_K = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{R T \,\ln(P/P^0)}$$

For N₂ at 77 K with $\theta = 0$:

$$r_K \;\;[\text{nm}] = \frac{0.415}{\log_{10}(P^0/P)} = \frac{0.415}{\log_{10}(1/(P/P^0))} = \frac{0.415}{-\log_{10}(P/P^0)}$$

This numerical constant of **0.415 nm** (or 4.15 Å) arises from substituting $\gamma = 8.85$ mN/m, $V_m = 34.7$ cm³/mol, $T = 77.4$ K, and converting log base.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — BJH Pore Volume Increment (Board-Favourite)

Consider two successive desorption points: $i$ and $i-1$, with relative pressures $x_i = (P/P^0)_i$ and $x_{i-1}$ where $x_{i-1} > x_i$ (desorption direction is from high to low).

**Step 1 — Define the volume quantities.**
- $V_{i-1}$: total volume of N₂ desorbed from the pore between radii $r_{K,i-1}$ and $r_{K,i}$.
- $V_{\text{film},i-1}$: volume of N₂ lost from the multilayer film on previously emptied pore walls.
- $\Delta V_{\text{des},i}$: incremental desorbed volume measured experimentally.

**Step 2 — Apply mass balance.**

$$\Delta V_{\text{des},i} = V_{i-1} + V_{\text{film},i-1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the incremental pore volume $V_{i-1}$.**

$$V_{i-1} = \Delta V_{\text{des},i} - V_{\text{film},i-1}$$

**Step 4 — Express the film volume term using average film thickness.** For pores of radius $r_p$ in the cylindrical model, the wall area is proportional to $2\pi r_p L$ where $L$ is the pore length. The incremental film volume is:

$$V_{\text{film},i-1} = \Delta t_{i-1} \cdot A_{c,i-1}$$

where $\Delta t_{i-1} = t_{i-1} - t_i$ is the change in film thickness, and $A_{c,i-1}$ is the cumulative surface area of all previously emptied pores.

**Step 5 — Compute the pore radius for this increment.**

$$r_{p,i-1} = r_{K,i-1} + \bar{t}_{i-1}$$

where $\bar{t}_{i-1}$ is the average film thickness in the interval.

**Step 6 — Iterate over all pressure steps** to build up the cumulative pore volume curve $V(r_p)$, then differentiate numerically:

$$\left(\frac{dV}{dr_p}\right)_i = \frac{V_{i-1}}{r_{p,i-1} - r_{p,i}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The final plot of $dV/dr_p$ vs. $r_p$ is the **PSD curve** that KTU asks you to draw or interpret.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Hydraulic Pore Radius (Board-Favourite Numerical)

**Given:** A catalyst has a BET surface area $A_{\text{BET}} = 320$ m²/g and a total pore volume $V_p = 0.45$ cm³/g (measured at $P/P^0 = 0.99$).

**Step 1 — Use the hydraulic model assumption.** For a collection of open-ended cylindrical pores, the mean pore radius relates $V_p$ and $A_{\text{BET}}$ by:

$$r_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2 V_p}{A_{\text{BET}}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert units consistently.**

$$V_p = 0.45 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g} = 0.45 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{g}$$

$$A_{\text{BET}} = 320 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$r_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2 \times 0.45 \times 10^{-6}}{320} = \frac{0.9 \times 10^{-6}}{320} = 2.8125 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to nanometres.**

$$r_{\text{mean}} = 2.8125 \text{ nm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d_{\text{mean}} = 5.625 \text{ nm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board answer:** "The catalyst has a mean pore diameter of ~5.6 nm, which lies in the **mesopore range**, indicating it is suitable for reactions involving moderately sized organic molecules."

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kelvin Radius at a Specified $P/P^0$ (Worked Numerical)

**Problem:** A mesoporous silica shows a sharp desorption step at $P/P^0 = 0.74$. Compute the Kelvin radius using the Kelvin equation (N₂ at 77 K).

**Step 1 — Write the Kelvin equation solved for $r_K$:**

$$r_K = \frac{0.415 \text{ nm}}{-\log_{10}(P/P^0)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $P/P^0 = 0.74$:**

$$-\log_{10}(0.74) = -(−0.1308) = 0.1308$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $r_K$:**

$$r_K = \frac{0.415}{0.1308} = 3.173 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the film-thickness correction** using the Halsey equation at $P/P^0 = 0.74$:

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{0.1399}{0.034 - \log_{10}(0.74)}} \text{ nm} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1399}{0.034 + 0.1308}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1399}{0.1648}} = \sqrt{0.849} = 0.921 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the true pore radius:**

$$r_p = r_K + t = 3.173 + 0.921 = 4.094 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 6 — True pore diameter:**

$$d_p = 2 r_p = 8.19 \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board answer:** The desorption step at $P/P^0 = 0.74$ corresponds to a **mesopore of diameter ~8.2 nm** in the cylindrical BJH model. This is the typical pore size of MCM-41 type silicas.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison for PSD Determination

| Method | Pore Range | Principle | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BJH (desorption branch)** | 2 – 50 nm | Kelvin + film thickness | Standard, reproducible | Ink-bottle pores give false small size |
| **BJH (adsorption branch)** | 2 – 50 nm | Kelvin + film thickness | Better for open pores | Tensile strength artifact (forced closure) |
| **DH (Dollimore–Heal)** | 2 – 50 nm | Modified Kelvin, no arbitrary constants | More accurate for slit pores | Computationally complex |
| **t-plot (Harkins–Jura)** | Micro + meso | Statistic thickness | Quick micropore vol. estimate | No detailed PSD |
| **αs-plot (Sing)** | Micro + meso | Reference isotherm | IUPAC recommended | Requires reference material |
| **NLDFT** | Micro to meso | Molecular statistical | Highly accurate, modern | Requires kernel, slow |
| **Horvath–Kawazoe (HK)** | 0.4 – 1.5 nm | Adsorption potential | Best for micropores | Slit model only |
| **Mercury Intrusion** | 7 nm – 100 µm | Washburn equation | Mesopore + macropore | Destroys fragile structures |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — IUPAC Pore Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Porous Solid Catalyst] --> B{Equivalent Pore Width d}
    B -->|d less than 2 nm| C[MICROPORES]
    B -->|2 nm to 50 nm| D[MESOPORES]
    B -->|d greater than 50 nm| E[MACROPORES]
    C --> C1[Ultra-micropores d less than 0.7 nm]
    C --> C2[Super-micropores 0.7 to 2 nm]
    D --> D1[Capillary condensation active]
    D --> D2[Hysteresis observed Type IV]
    E --> E1[Knudsen and molecular diffusion]
    E --> E2[No capillary condensation]
    C1 --> Z[Typical: Zeolites, MOFs, Activated Carbon]
    C2 --> Z
    D1 --> Y[Typical: gamma-Al2O3, MCM-41, SBA-15]
    D2 --> Y
    E1 --> X[Typical: Pellets, Extrudates]
    E2 --> X
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Pore Size Distribution Function Concept

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Catalyst Pellet Volume] --> B1[Macropore Volume]
    A --> B2[Mesopore Volume]
    A --> B3[Micropore Volume]
    B1 --> C1[Transport pores]
    B2 --> C2[Reaction pores]
    B3 --> C3[Active site pores]
    C1 --> D[PSD Curve dV versus dr]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Peak Position indicates Modal Pore Size]
    D --> F[Peak Width indicates PSD Breadth]
    D --> G[Peak Area equals Pore Volume]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — BJH Computational Workflow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[N2 Adsorption-Desorption Isotherm at 77 K] --> S1[Step 1: Tabulate Vads versus P over P0]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Compute Kelvin radius rK at each point]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute film thickness t using Halsey or HJ]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: True pore radius rp equals rK plus t]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute incremental pore volume delV]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Build cumulative volume V versus rp curve]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Numerically differentiate to obtain dV versus dr]
    S7 --> OUT[Final PSD Plot dV versus dr versus rp]
    S2 -.uses.-> KE[Kelvin Equation]
    S3 -.uses.-> HFE[Halsey Film Thickness]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Pore Shape vs. Hysteresis Loop Identification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[H1 Hysteresis] --> SH1[Cylindrical uniform open pores]
    H1 --> EX1[Example: MCM-41, SBA-15]
    H2[H2 Hysteresis] --> SH2[Ink-bottle pores with narrow necks]
    H2 --> EX2[Example: Silica gel, mesoporous glass]
    H3[H3 Hysteresis] --> SH3[Slit-shaped pores in aggregates]
    H3 --> EX3[Example: Pillared clays, lamellar solids]
    H4[H4 Hysteresis] --> SH4[Narrow slit-like combined with micropores]
    H4 --> EX4[Example: Micro-mesoporous carbons]
    H5[H5 Hysteresis] --> SH5[Unusual mixed morphology]
    H5 --> EX5[Example: Modified functionalised silicas]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Types of Physisorption Isotherms (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IUPAC Physisorption Isotherms] --> T1[Type I: Microporous Langmuir type]
    A --> T2[Type II: Non-porous or macroporous]
    A --> T3[Type III: Weak interactions]
    A --> T4[Type IV: Mesoporous with hysteresis]
    A --> T5[Type V: Weak plus mesoporous]
    A --> T6[Type VI: Stepwise uniform surface]
    T1 --> N1[ZSM-5, Faujasite, Activated Carbon]
    T2 --> N2[alpha-Al2O3, dense oxides]
    T4 --> N3[gamma-Al2O3, MCM-41, SBA-15]
    T4 --> H[Sub-loops H1 to H5 identify pore shape]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Define pore size distribution. How does the IUPAC classification divide the pores present in a catalyst? Give two examples of catalysts belonging to each class.

**Model Answer:**

**Pore Size Distribution (PSD)** is the distribution of pore volume (or pore area) with respect to pore size in a porous solid. Mathematically, it is expressed as $\dfrac{dV}{dr_p}$ as a function of $r_p$, the equivalent pore radius.

**IUPAC Classification of Pores:**

$$\text{Pore Type} = \begin{cases} \text{Micropore} & \text{if } d < 2 \text{ nm} \\ \text{Mesopore} & \text{if } 2 \text{ nm} \le d \le 50 \text{ nm} \\ \text{Macropore} & \text{if } d > 50 \text{ nm} \end{cases}$$

**Examples:**
- **Micropores:** ZSM-5 zeolite, Faujasite (Y-zeolite), Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs).
- **Mesopores:** $\gamma$-Al₂O₃, MCM-41, SBA-15, mesoporous silica.
- **Macropores:** Catalyst extrudates, Raschig rings, pelletised supports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks split (3M):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for IUPAC table, 1 mark for examples.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
What is the Kelvin equation? State its significance in the determination of pore size distribution.

**Model Answer:**

The **Kelvin equation** relates the equilibrium vapor pressure over a curved liquid meniscus inside a pore to the radius of curvature of the meniscus:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^0}\right) = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_K \, R T}$$

**Significance in PSD:**
1. It provides the **theoretical link** between the experimentally measured relative pressure $P/P^0$ (from the adsorption isotherm) and the **Kelvin radius $r_K$** of the pore.
2. By recording the desorption or adsorption branch of the isotherm, one can compute $r_K$ at every $P/P^0$ point.
3. After **film thickness correction** ($r_p = r_K + t$), the true pore radius $r_p$ is obtained.
4. Differentiating cumulative pore volume vs. $r_p$ yields the **PSD curve**.
5. Without the Kelvin equation, no quantitative PSD determination from N₂ physisorption would be possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks split (3M):** 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for significance.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**(a)** Describe the **Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method** for the determination of pore size distribution of mesoporous catalysts. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A mesoporous silica catalyst exhibits a sharp desorption step at $P/P^0 = 0.65$ in the N₂ isotherm at 77 K. Calculate the **Kelvin radius**, the **film thickness** (using Halsey), and the **true pore diameter**. Comment on the IUPAC classification of the pore. (7 Marks)

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(a)

**1. Principle of BJH Method (1 Mark):** BJH is the standard IUPAC-recommended method for PSD in the mesopore range (2–50 nm). It combines the **Kelvin equation** with a **multilayer film thickness correction**.

**2. Experimental Requirement (1 Mark):** A complete N₂ adsorption–desorption isotherm at 77 K must be recorded on the catalyst using a volumetric sorption analyzer (e.g., Micromeritics ASAP).

**3. Step-by-Step Procedure (4 Marks):**
- **Step 1:** Tabulate the cumulative volume of N₂ desorbed at successive relative pressures $P/P^0$.
- **Step 2:** Apply the Kelvin equation to each $P/P^0$ to compute the Kelvin radius $r_K$.
- **Step 3:** Calculate the adsorbed film thickness $t$ at each $P/P^0$ using the Halsey equation:

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{0.1399}{0.034 - \log_{10}(P/P^0)}} \text{ nm}$$

- **Step 4:** Compute the true pore radius $r_p = r_K + t$.
- **Step 5:** Determine the incremental pore volume $\Delta V_p$ desorbed in each pressure step.
- **Step 6:** Build the cumulative pore volume vs. $r_p$ curve.
- **Step 7:** Numerically differentiate to obtain the PSD function $\dfrac{dV}{dr_p}$.

**4. Use of Desorption Branch (1 Mark):** The desorption branch is thermodynamically more reliable for BJH than the adsorption branch, although the latter is used for open-ended pores. The choice depends on the pore-network connectivity.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Given:** $P/P^0 = 0.65$, N₂ at 77 K.

**Step 1 — Kelvin Radius (2 Marks):**

$$r_K = \frac{0.415 \text{ nm}}{-\log_{10}(0.65)} = \frac{0.415}{0.1871} = 2.218 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 2 — Film Thickness via Halsey (2 Marks):**

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{0.1399}{0.034 - \log_{10}(0.65)}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1399}{0.034 + 0.1871}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1399}{0.2211}} = \sqrt{0.6327} = 0.7954 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 3 — True Pore Radius and Diameter (1 Mark):**

$$r_p = 2.218 + 0.7954 = 3.013 \text{ nm}$$

$$d_p = 2 r_p = 6.027 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 4 — IUPAC Classification (1 Mark):** Since $d_p = 6.03$ nm lies in the range 2 nm $\le d \le$ 50 nm, the pore is classified as a **mesopore**.

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation (1 Mark):** A mesopore of ~6 nm diameter is well-suited for catalytic reactions involving organic molecules of moderate size (e.g., hydrocracking of middle distillates, Friedel–Crafts alkylations, and bulky olefin transformations). The narrow PSD (sharp step) indicates a templated, uniform mesoporous structure such as MCM-41 or SBA-15.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the **IUPAC classification of pores** with suitable examples. Discuss how the **shape of the hysteresis loop** (H1–H5) in a Type IV isotherm provides information about the pore geometry. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A catalyst has $A_{\text{BET}} = 250$ m²/g and $V_p = 0.40$ cm³/g. Calculate the **mean hydraulic pore radius** and **pore diameter**. Identify the IUPAC pore class. (7 Marks)

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a)

**1. IUPAC Pore Classification (2 Marks):**

| Pore Type | Width | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Micropore | $d < 2$ nm | ZSM-5, Faujasite, MOFs |
| Mesopore | $2 \le d \le 50$ nm | $\gamma$-Al₂O₃, MCM-41, SBA-15 |
| Macropore | $d > 50$ nm | Extrudates, Raschig rings |

**2. Hysteresis Loop Types (4 Marks):**

- **H1 — Vertical, parallel branches:** Cylindrical pores with open ends, narrow PSD. Example: MCM-41.
- **H2 — Triangular, with a steep desorption branch:** Ink-bottle pores with narrow constrictions. Example: silica gel.
- **H3 — No plateau at high $P/P^0$:** Slit-shaped pores in aggregates of plate-like particles. Example: pillared clays.
- **H4 — Nearly horizontal, parallel branches:** Narrow slit-like pores combined with micropores. Example: micro-mesoporous carbons.
- **H5 — Unusual shape:** Uncommon, partially blocked pores in modified silicas.

**3. Why Hysteresis Occurs (1 Mark):** Capillary condensation in the mesopore occurs at a higher $P/P^0$ on adsorption than evaporation on desorption because of the geometry-dependent difference in meniscus curvature between the two processes.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Mean Hydraulic Radius (3 Marks):**

$$r_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2 V_p}{A_{\text{BET}}} = \frac{2 \times 0.40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{g}}{250 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}} = 3.2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 3.2 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 2 — Mean Pore Diameter (2 Marks):**

$$d_{\text{mean}} = 2 r_{\text{mean}} = 6.4 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 3 — IUPAC Pore Class (1 Mark):** $d_{\text{mean}} = 6.4$ nm lies between 2 and 50 nm → **Mesoporous**.

**Step 4 — Engineering Significance (1 Mark):** A mesoporous catalyst with $d_{\text{mean}} \approx 6.4$ nm offers a high accessible internal surface for medium-sized reactant molecules (e.g., $\sim 1$ nm diameter) while remaining free of severe Knudsen diffusion limitations, making it suitable for processes such as VGO hydrotreating, FCC additive matrices, and bulky olefin epoxidations.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake 1 (HIGH FREQUENCY):** Students often write the Kelvin equation as $\ln(P/P^0) = +\dfrac{2\gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_K RT}$. The **correct sign is NEGATIVE**. The negative sign indicates that condensation occurs at $P < P^0$ inside small pores. Losing this sign loses 1 mark.
>
> **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **film thickness correction**. Writing $r_p = r_K$ directly (without adding $t$) underestimates the true pore radius by ~0.7–1.2 nm, leading to incorrect classification of borderline mesopores as micropores.
>
> **Common Mistake 3:** Using the **adsorption branch** of the isotherm without justification. Always state that the **desorption branch is thermodynamically more reliable** for BJH because it is closer to true equilibrium, except in cases of pore blocking (ink-bottle pores).
>
> **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **t-plot** with **BJH**. The t-plot gives only the **micropore volume**, not the full PSD. KTU questions that ask for "complete PSD" require the BJH plot.
>
> **Common Mistake 5:** Writing the IUPAC classification limits as $d < 2$ Å instead of $d < 2$ nm. Always double-check the unit.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PSD = derivative of cumulative pore volume vs. pore radius:** $\dfrac{dV}{dr_p}$ vs. $r_p$.
- **IUPAC pore classes:** Micropore ($d < 2$ nm), Mesopore ($2 \le d \le 50$ nm), Macropore ($d > 50$ nm).
- **Standard physisorption:** N₂ at 77 K or Ar at 87 K; adsorption–desorption isotherm is the experimental raw data.
- **Kelvin equation** is the heart of PSD analysis: $r_K = \dfrac{0.415 \text{ nm}}{-\log_{10}(P/P^0)}$ for N₂ at 77 K.
- **True pore radius:** $r_p = r_K + t$ (Kelvin + film thickness).
- **Film-thickness correlations:** Halsey, Harkins–Jura, Kellar–Küppers — Halsey is the KTU default.
- **Hysteresis loops (H1–H5):** H1 → cylindrical open pores (MCM-41); H2 → ink-bottle (silica gel); H3 → slit-shaped (pillared clays); H4 → narrow slit + micropore (carbons); H5 → unusual.
- **IUPAC Isotherms:** Type I (microporous), Type II (non-porous), Type IV (mesoporous — most relevant for PSD), Type V (weak + mesoporous), Type VI (uniform stepwise).
- **Mean hydraulic radius:** $r_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{2 V_p}{A_{\text{BET}}}$.
- **Methods for PSD:** BJH (mesopore), HK (micropore), t-plot (micropore volume), NLDFT (modern, accurate all-ranges), Hg porosimetry (macropore + meso).
- **Sign convention in Kelvin equation:** Negative sign — condensation at $P < P^0$.
- **Catalyst design rule:** Match the modal pore size to the **critical diameter** of the reactant molecule to avoid configurational diffusion limitations.
- **Hysteresis is observed only in Type IV and V isotherms** — Type I and II show no hysteresis.
- **The mean hydraulic radius formula** assumes open-ended cylindrical geometry — it is a rough estimate, not an exact measure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Porosimetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Porosimetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Porosimetry** is a branch of analytical techniques used in catalyst characterization to determine the **pore volume**, **pore size distribution**, **surface area**, and **average pore diameter** of porous solid materials (such as catalysts, supports, and adsorbents) by measuring the volume of a non-wetting or wetting fluid that penetrates the pore network as a function of applied pressure.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT412)**, porosimetry is grouped under *Textural Characterization of Catalysts* and is a mandatory topic in **Module 2** because pore architecture directly dictates the catalytic activity, selectivity, diffusion limitations, and active site accessibility of industrial catalysts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Porosimetry critical for catalysts?**
> Most industrial catalysts (e.g., zeolites, alumina, silica, FCC catalysts) are **porous solids**. Reactions occur *inside* these pores, so:
> - Pore size controls molecular diffusion (Knudsen / molecular / configurational).
> - Surface area (m²/g) determines the number of active sites.
> - Pore volume (cm³/g) determines the loading capacity for active metals.
> - Without porosimetry data, catalyst performance is unpredictable.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge City" Intuition

Imagine a **sponge** (porous catalyst particle). Now imagine two ways to "map" the tunnels inside it:

1. **Flooding Method (Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry, MIP)** – Squeeze a non-wetting liquid (mercury) into the sponge. Small tunnels need higher pressure to fill. By recording *pressure vs. volume filled*, you map tunnel size.  
   *→ This is the "pressurized plumber's approach."*

2. **Sticking Method (Gas Adsorption, BET/BJH)** – Cool the sponge, expose it to nitrogen gas. Gas molecules adsorb layer-by-layer on the internal walls. The amount adsorbed tells you the *internal surface area*, and the pressure at which capillary condensation occurs tells you the *pore width*.  
   *→ This is the "molecular ruler" approach.*

Both methods together give a **complete pore architecture map** of the catalyst.

---

## 1.3 Standard IUPAC Classification of Pores

| Pore Type | Width (nm) | Dominant Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Micropores** | $<$ 2 | Gas adsorption (CO₂, N₂ at 77 K) |
| **Mesopores** | 2 – 50 | Gas adsorption (N₂ at 77 K, BJH) |
| **Macropores** | $>$ 50 | Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The textbook constants you **must memorize** for the KTU board exam:
> - Surface tension of mercury: **$\gamma_{Hg} = 0.485 \text{ N/m}$** (sometimes 0.480 N/m)
> - Contact angle of mercury on oxide catalysts: **$\theta_{Hg} = 140°$** (cos 140° = –0.766)
> - Cross-sectional area of N₂ molecule at 77 K: **$a_m = 0.162 \text{ nm}^2$**
> - Molar volume of liquid nitrogen: **$V_m = 34.7 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$**
> - Surface tension of liquid N₂ at 77 K: **$\gamma_{N_2} = 8.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}$**

---

## 1.4 Major Techniques in Porosimetry

1. **Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry (MIP)** – for **macro- and mesopores** (3.5 nm – 500 µm).
2. **Nitrogen Physisorption (BET, BJH, t-plot, HK, SF)** – for **micro- and mesopores** (0.4 – 50 nm).
3. **Helium Pycnometry** – for **true (skeletal) density**.
4. **Water Porosimetry** – for hydrophilic catalysts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry (MIP) — Theory

In MIP, mercury (a non-wetting liquid) is forced into evacuated pores under progressively increasing pressure. The relationship between pressure and pore diameter is governed by the **Washburn equation** (1921), assuming cylindrical pores.

### Step-by-step Logic of Washburn's Model

- **Step 1 — Young-Laplace Equation:** A curved meniscus between two immiscible fluids generates a pressure jump:
$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

- **Step 2 — Geometry:** For a *cylindrical* pore, $r = D/2$, hence:
$$\Delta P = \frac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{D}$$

- **Step 3 — Substitution into Washburn form** (note $\cos\theta < 0$ for Hg on oxides, so the negative sign yields a positive diameter):
$$D = -\frac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{P}$$

- **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** As $P$ increases, smaller pores are filled. By plotting $V_{intruded}$ vs. $P$, the pore size distribution is obtained.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\cos\theta$ is negative:** Mercury does *not* wet oxide surfaces. The contact angle $\theta$ is obtuse ($> 90°$), so $\cos\theta < 0$. The negative sign in the Washburn equation is added explicitly to make the diameter positive.

---

## 2.2 Gas Adsorption Porosimetry — Theory

### 2.2.1 BET Theory (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, 1938)

BET extends the Langmuir monolayer model to **multilayer physisorption**. The linearized BET equation is:

$$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m c} + \frac{(c - 1)}{V_m c} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$

where:
- $V$ = volume of gas adsorbed at relative pressure $P/P_0$
- $V_m$ = monolayer capacity (cm³ STP/g)
- $c$ = BET constant (related to heat of adsorption)

**Linear plot:** $P/[V(P_0 - P)]$ vs. $P/P_0$ gives slope $s = (c-1)/(V_m c)$ and intercept $i = 1/(V_m c)$.

Solving: 
$$V_m = \frac{1}{s + i}, \qquad c = \frac{s}{i} + 1$$

**Total Surface Area (BET):**
$$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot a_m}{V_{molar}}$$

where $N_A$ = Avogadro's number, $a_m$ = cross-sectional area, $V_{molar} = 22{,}414$ cm³/mol (STP).

### 2.2.2 Kelvin Equation (for Mesopore Width)

Capillary condensation of N₂ in a mesopore occurs when the relative pressure satisfies:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_k R T}$$

where $r_k$ = Kelvin radius (radius of curvature of the meniscus). For a cylindrical pore, the actual pore radius:
$$r_p = r_k + t$$

where $t$ is the statistical thickness of the adsorbed layer (Halsey or Harkins-Jura equation).

### 2.2.3 BJH Method (Barrett–Joyner–Halenda, 1951)

The BJH method uses the **Kelvin equation** applied *step-wise* to the desorption branch of the isotherm to compute the **mesopore size distribution** ($V_p$ vs. $D_p$).

### 2.2.4 t-plot (Harkins–Jura)

A plot of *volume adsorbed* vs. *statistical thickness $t$* of the adsorbed layer. A straight line passing through origin indicates **non-porous** material; deviation reveals micropore filling.

### 2.2.5 HK & SF Methods (Micropores)

- **Horvath–Kawazoe (HK):** Slit-pore model for microporous carbons/zeolites.
- **Saito–Foley (SF):** Cylindrical pore model for zeolites.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the only formulas KTU will test.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Washburn (MIP)** | $D = -\dfrac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{P}$ | $D$ = pore diameter, $\gamma$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle | Meso/Macropores |
| 2 | **BET Linear** | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m c} + \dfrac{(c-1)}{V_m c} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | $V, V_m, c, P_0$ | $0.05 \le P/P_0 \le 0.30$ |
| 3 | **Monolayer Capacity** | $V_m = \dfrac{1}{s + i}$ | $s$ = slope, $i$ = intercept | From BET plot |
| 4 | **BET Constant** | $c = \dfrac{s}{i} + 1$ | — | Energy of adsorption |
| 5 | **BET Surface Area** | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m N_A a_m}{V_{molar}}$ | $a_m = 0.162$ nm² (N₂) | cm²/g |
| 6 | **Kelvin Equation** | $\ln(P/P_0) = -\dfrac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_k R T}$ | $r_k$ = Kelvin radius | Mesopores |
| 7 | **Pore Radius (cyl.)** | $r_p = r_k + t$ | $t$ = stat. thickness | BJH |
| 8 | **Porosity** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{pore}}{V_{pore} + V_{skeletal}}$ | $V_{pore}$ from MIP | $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ |
| 9 | **Bulk Density** | $\rho_{bulk} = \dfrac{m}{V_{bulk}}$ | $m$ = mass, $V_{bulk}$ = envelope volume | g/cm³ |
| 10 | **True Density** | $\rho_{true} = \dfrac{m}{V_{skeletal}}$ | He pycnometry | g/cm³ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Washburn Equation

### Setup
Consider a cylindrical pore of diameter $D$ in a catalyst pellet. Mercury (non-wetting liquid) sits on top with contact angle $\theta > 90°$ on the solid. The gas pressure $P$ is applied to push mercury into the pore.

### Step 1 — Young-Laplace Pressure Across a Curved Interface

For a spherical meniscus of radius $r_m$ between two immiscible fluids, the pressure jump across the interface is:
$$\Delta P = P_{inside} - P_{outside} = \frac{2 \gamma}{r_m}$$

**Text Row:** "This is the basic Young-Laplace result: curvature creates a pressure difference that resists interface deformation."

### Step 2 — Geometry of the Meniscus in a Cylindrical Pore

For a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$, the meniscus is approximately hemispherical. Its radius of curvature equals the pore radius: $r_m = r_p$.

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma}{r_p}$$

**Text Row:** "We equate the meniscus radius to the pore radius because the mercury surface conforms to the pore wall in a hemispherical cap shape."

### Step 3 — Incorporating the Contact Angle

When the solid wall meets the meniscus at contact angle $\theta$, the *vertical* component of surface tension balances the pressure. The effective radius perpendicular to the axis becomes:

$$r_m = \frac{r_p}{\cos\theta}$$

Substituting back:
$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_p}$$

**Text Row:** "The contact angle modifies the effective curvature — for non-wetting mercury, $\cos\theta < 0$, meaning an *external* pressure must be applied to push the liquid in."

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Diameter

Since $r_p = D/2$:
$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{D/2} = \frac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{D}$$

### Step 5 — Final Washburn Equation

Rearranging for the pore diameter:
$$D = \frac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{\Delta P}$$

Since $\cos\theta < 0$ for mercury on oxides, we write the conventional form:
$$\boxed{D = -\frac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{P}}$$

**Text Row:** "This is the master equation of MIP: higher applied pressure P fills progressively smaller pores. For $\gamma_{Hg} = 0.485$ N/m and $\theta = 140°$ (cos 140° = –0.766), the constant $-4\gamma\cos\theta \approx 1486$ nm·MPa."

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the BET Equation

### Step 1 — Assumptions

1. The surface has $S$ equivalent adsorption sites.
2. The heat of adsorption for the *first* layer is $E_1$ (constant).
3. Layers 2, 3, ... have heat of adsorption $E_L$ equal to the heat of liquefaction.
4. No lateral interaction between adsorbed molecules.

### Step 2 — Statistical Mechanics Result (Brunauer et al.)

The volume of gas adsorbed at relative pressure $x = P/P_0$ is:
$$V = \frac{V_m c x}{(1 - x)\left[1 + (c - 1) x\right]}$$

where $c \approx \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right]$.

**Text Row:** "The constant c exponentially reflects the difference between the first-layer heat of adsorption and the condensation heat. For N₂ on most oxides, $c$ lies between 50 and 300."

### Step 3 — Linearization

Multiply numerator and denominator appropriately:
$$\frac{x}{V (1 - x)} = \frac{1}{V_m c} + \frac{(c - 1)}{V_m c} \, x$$

Let $y = \dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{x}{V(1-x)}$. A plot of $y$ vs. $x = P/P_0$ yields:

$$\text{slope } s = \frac{c - 1}{V_m c}, \qquad \text{intercept } i = \frac{1}{V_m c}$$

**Text Row:** "This linear plot is the workhorse of BET surface area analysis. The linear region is typically $0.05 \le P/P_0 \le 0.30$."

### Step 4 — Solving for $V_m$ and $c$

Adding the two relations:
$$s + i = \frac{c - 1 + 1}{V_m c} = \frac{1}{V_m}$$

$$\boxed{V_m = \frac{1}{s + i}}$$

Dividing:
$$\frac{s}{i} = c - 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{c = \frac{s}{i} + 1}$$

**Text Row:** "Once $V_m$ (cm³ STP per gram) is known, we convert to monolayer moles and then to area using the cross-sectional footprint of the N₂ molecule."

### Step 5 — Surface Area Calculation

The number of moles in the monolayer per gram of catalyst:
$$n_m = \frac{V_m}{V_{molar}}$$

The total area per gram:
$$S_{BET} = n_m \cdot N_A \cdot a_m = \frac{V_m N_A a_m}{V_{molar}}$$

**Text Row:** "Here $a_m = 0.162$ nm² for N₂ at 77 K, $V_{molar} = 22{,}414$ cm³/mol, and $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹."

### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example

A catalyst sample (1.000 g) gives the following BET data:

| $P/P_0$ | Volume adsorbed $V$ (cm³ STP) |
|---|---|
| 0.05 | 35.2 |
| 0.10 | 41.8 |
| 0.15 | 47.5 |
| 0.20 | 52.4 |
| 0.25 | 57.1 |
| 0.30 | 61.3 |

**Compute $y = P/[V(P_0 - P)]$ for each point:**

For $P/P_0 = 0.05$, $V = 35.2$:
$$y = \frac{0.05}{35.2 \times (1 - 0.05)} = \frac{0.05}{33.44} = 1.495 \times 10^{-3}$$

For $P/P_0 = 0.10$:
$$y = \frac{0.10}{41.8 \times 0.90} = \frac{0.10}{37.62} = 2.658 \times 10^{-3}$$

For $P/P_0 = 0.15$:
$$y = \frac{0.15}{47.5 \times 0.85} = \frac{0.15}{40.375} = 3.715 \times 10^{-3}$$

For $P/P_0 = 0.20$:
$$y = \frac{0.20}{52.4 \times 0.80} = \frac{0.20}{41.92} = 4.771 \times 10^{-3}$$

For $P/P_0 = 0.25$:
$$y = \frac{0.25}{57.1 \times 0.75} = \frac{0.25}{42.825} = 5.838 \times 10^{-3}$$

For $P/P_0 = 0.30$:
$$y = \frac{0.30}{61.3 \times 0.70} = \frac{0.30}{42.91} = 6.992 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Linear Regression of $y$ vs. $P/P_0$:**

Using the six points, by least-squares:
- Slope $s = 2.183 \times 10^{-2}$ (cm³ STP)⁻¹
- Intercept $i = 3.93 \times 10^{-4}$ (cm³ STP)⁻¹

**Text Row:** "We perform linear regression; the slope is in the range $10^{-2}$ and the intercept is small but non-zero, characteristic of a high-c BET plot."

**Compute $V_m$ and $c$:**
$$V_m = \frac{1}{s + i} = \frac{1}{2.183 \times 10^{-2} + 3.93 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{2.222 \times 10^{-2}} = 45.0 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ STP/g}$$

$$c = \frac{s}{i} + 1 = \frac{2.183 \times 10^{-2}}{3.93 \times 10^{-4}} + 1 = 55.5 + 1 = 56.5$$

**Text Row:** "A $c$-value of 56.5 indicates strong adsorbent-adsorbate interaction, consistent with a typical oxide catalyst."

**Compute $S_{BET}$:**
$$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot a_m}{V_{molar}} = \frac{45.0 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m}^2}{22{,}414 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}}$$

$$S_{BET} = \frac{45.0 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.62 \times 10^{-19}}{22{,}414}$$

$$S_{BET} = \frac{45.0 \times 0.0976}{22{,}414} \times 10^{4} = \frac{4.39}{22{,}414} \times 10^{4} \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

$$S_{BET} = 1.96 \times 10^{2} \text{ m}^2/\text{g} = 196 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

**Text Row:** "This value (~196 m²/g) is typical of a medium-porosity alumina or silica-alumina catalyst."

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Kelvin Equation (Cylindrical Pore)

### Step 1 — Thermodynamic Equilibrium

A liquid in a capillary is in equilibrium with its vapor when the chemical potentials are equal:
$$\mu_l + V_l \Delta P = \mu_v + RT \ln(P/P_0)$$

where $\Delta P$ is the Laplace pressure across the meniscus.

### Step 2 — Apply Young-Laplace

For a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$ and hemispherical meniscus, $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_p}$.

**Text Row:** "The Laplace pressure is the same as in the Washburn equation, but here it represents equilibrium between capillary liquid and vapor."

### Step 3 — Use $\mu_l \approx \mu_v$ for a planar interface at $P_0$

The standard thermodynamic identity $\mu_v - \mu_l = RT \ln(P/P_0)$ then yields:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{V_m \Delta P}{RT} = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_p R T}$$

**Text Row:** "This is the Kelvin equation. It shows that the equilibrium vapor pressure over a curved meniscus is *lower* than over a flat surface (for $\cos\theta > 0$ for wetting fluids), so condensation occurs at $P < P_0$."

### Step 4 — Real Pore Radius (BJH Correction)

The Kelvin radius $r_k$ corresponds to the *empty* core of the pore after desorption, but the actual pore wall has a pre-adsorbed film of thickness $t$. The true pore radius:
$$r_p = r_k + t$$

**Text Row:** "The $t$-correction (Halsey equation: $t = \left[\dfrac{5}{2.303 \log(P_0/P)}\right]^{1/3} \times 0.354$ nm) prevents underestimating pore widths in BJH analysis."

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Porosity from MIP Data

**Total Intruded Volume:**
$$V_{total} = \int_{P_{min}}^{P_{max}} \frac{dV}{dP} \, dP$$

**Porosity:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{total}}{V_{total} + (m/\rho_{true})}$$

**Text Row:** "Here $m$ is the sample mass and $\rho_{true}$ is the skeletal (true) density measured by helium pycnometry. Porosity is dimensionless, typically 0.3 to 0.7 for most industrial catalysts."

**Average Pore Diameter (assuming cylindrical pores):**
$$\bar{D} = \frac{4 V_{total}}{S_{BET}}$$

**Text Row:** "This is the standard 4V/A relation derived for cylindrical capillaries. KTU often asks students to compute $\bar{D}$ from BET and MIP data together."

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mercury Intrusion Porosimeter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[High Pressure Pump] --> A2[Penetrometer Sample Holder]
    A2 --> A3[Catalyst Pellet under Vacuum]
    A3 --> A4[Mercury Reservoir]
    A4 --> A5[Capillary Stem Sensor]
    A5 --> A6[Pressure Transducer 0 to 400 MPa]
    A5 --> A7[Volume Change Capacitance Sensor]
    A6 --> A8[Data Acquisition System]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Software: Pore Size Distribution Algorithm]
    A9 --> A10[Output: Pore Volume vs Pore Diameter]
    A8 --> A11[Output: Cumulative Intrusion Curve]
```

> **Caption:** *Pressure is increased stepwise. At each step, the volume of mercury forced into the pores is recorded. The Washburn equation converts (P, V) → (D, V), generating the pore size distribution.*

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Gas Adsorption (BET) Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[N2 Gas Cylinder 99.999 percent] --> B2[Dewar with Liquid N2 at 77 K]
    B2 --> B3[Sample Tube Containing Catalyst]
    B3 --> B4[He Gas for Dead Volume Calibration]
    B4 --> B5[Manifold with Known Volume]
    B5 --> B6[Pressure Transducer 0 to 1000 torr]
    B5 --> B7[Dosing Valve Incremental N2]
    B7 --> B6
    B6 --> B8[Computer Controller]
    B8 --> B9[Adsorption Isotherm V vs P by P0]
    B9 --> B10[BET Plot P over V P0 minus P vs P by P0]
    B10 --> B11[Slope and Intercept Extraction]
    B11 --> B12[Vm and c Computation]
    B12 --> B13[SBET Surface Area]
    B10 --> B14[Desorption Branch]
    B14 --> B15[BJH Pore Size Distribution]
```

> **Caption:** *Modern automated physisorption analyzers (e.g., Micromeritics ASAP, Quantachrome Autosorb) integrate both BET and BJH analyses from a single isotherm.*

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow Chart — Which Porosimetry Technique to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start: Characterize Pore Structure] --> Q2{Approximate Pore Size?}
    Q2 -->|Less than 2 nm Micropores| R1[Use N2 or CO2 Adsorption]
    R1 --> R2[Apply HK or SF Method]
    R2 --> R3[Use t-plot for External Area]
    Q2 -->|2 to 50 nm Mesopores| R4[Use N2 Adsorption at 77 K]
    R4 --> R5[Apply BET for Surface Area]
    R4 --> R6[Apply BJH on Desorption Branch]
    R6 --> R7[Obtain Mesopore Size Distribution]
    Q2 -->|Greater than 50 nm Macropores| R8[Use Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry]
    R8 --> R9[Apply Washburn Equation]
    R9 --> R10[Obtain Macro and Meso Distribution]
    Q2 -->|True Density Required| R11[Use Helium Pycnometry]
    R11 --> R12[Compute Porosity from Bulk minus True]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic of a Typical Physisorption Isotherm (IUPAC Type IV — Mesoporous)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Low P by P0: 0 to 0.05] -->|Monolayer Formation| S2[Mid P by P0: 0.05 to 0.30]
    S2 -->|Multilayer Growth| S3[High P by P0: 0.30 to 0.80]
    S3 -->|Capillary Condensation Steep Step| S4[Near P by P0: 0.80 to 0.95]
    S4 -->|Hysteresis Loop H1 to H5| S5[Saturation P by P0: 1.0]
    S1 -->|BET Analysis| S3
    S3 -->|BJH Analysis| S4
    S5 -->|Total Pore Volume at P by P0 equals 0.99| S5
```

> **Caption:** *Type IV isotherm is the fingerprint of mesoporous catalysts. The hysteresis loop encodes pore shape (cylindrical = H1, ink-bottle = H2, slit = H3).*
