# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption with respect to the type of bonding, heat of adsorption, and reversibility. Why is N₂ at 77 K preferred for BET surface area analysis of catalysts?**

#### Model Answer:
- **Bonding:** Physisorption involves weak **van der Waals forces**; chemisorption involves **covalent/ionic chemical bonds** with the surface.
- **Heat of Adsorption:** Physisorption: $5$ – $40$ kJ/mol; Chemisorption: $40$ – $400$ kJ/mol.
- **Reversibility:** Physisorption is fully **reversible**; chemisorption is often **irreversible** (activated).
- **Why N₂ at 77 K:** (i) N₂ is inert (no chemisorption on most oxides); (ii) at 77 K (boiling point of liquid N₂), the saturation pressure $P_0$ is conveniently atmospheric; (iii) the molecular cross-section $a_m = 0.162$ nm² is well-established; (iv) it probes mesopores and external area effectively.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the IUPAC classification of pores. State the principle of mercury intrusion porosimetry and write the Washburn equation.**

#### Model Answer:
- **IUPAC Classification:**
  - Micropores: width $<$ 2 nm
  - Mesopores: 2 – 50 nm
  - Macropores: $>$ 50 nm
- **Principle of MIP:** A non-wetting liquid (mercury) is forced into evacuated pores under increasing pressure. The pressure required to fill a pore is inversely proportional to its diameter. The intruded volume at each pressure yields the pore size distribution.
- **Washburn Equation:**
$$D = -\frac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{P}$$
where $D$ is pore diameter, $\gamma$ is the surface tension of Hg ($0.485$ N/m), and $\theta$ is the contact angle ($140°$ for Hg on oxides).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]`

**(a)** Derive the BET equation starting from the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller assumptions. How is the monolayer capacity $V_m$ and the BET constant $c$ determined from a linear plot? Mention the valid range of relative pressure for BET analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A catalyst sample weighing 0.850 g gave the following N₂ adsorption data at 77 K:

| $P/P_0$ | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.20 | 0.25 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V$ (cm³ STP/g) | 38.0 | 45.2 | 51.0 | 55.8 | 60.1 |

Compute the BET surface area and the value of $c$. Take $a_m = 0.162$ nm² for N₂. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The surface has $S$ identical sites.
2. The first layer has heat of adsorption $E_1$.
3. All subsequent layers have heat $E_L$ (heat of liquefaction).
4. No lateral interactions; Langmuir behavior in each layer.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Summing an infinite geometric series for layers stacked on each site:
$$V = \frac{V_m c x}{(1 - x)[1 + (c - 1)x]}, \quad x = P/P_0$$

Linearization:
$$\frac{x}{V(1 - x)} = \frac{1}{V_m c} + \frac{c - 1}{V_m c}\, x$$

A plot of $y = P/[V(P_0 - P)]$ vs. $x = P/P_0$ is linear in the range $0.05 \le P/P_0 \le 0.30$. (1 mark)

**Extraction (1 mark):**
$$V_m = \frac{1}{s + i}, \qquad c = \frac{s}{i} + 1$$

#### Model Solution (b):

Compute $y = P/[V(P_0 - P)]$ for each $P/P_0$:

| $P/P_0$ | $V$ (cm³/g) | $y = P/[V(P_0-P)]$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.05 | 38.0 | $0.05/(38.0 \times 0.95) = 1.385 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 0.10 | 45.2 | $0.10/(45.2 \times 0.90) = 2.458 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 0.15 | 51.0 | $0.15/(51.0 \times 0.85) = 3.460 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 0.20 | 55.8 | $0.20/(55.8 \times 0.80) = 4.480 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 0.25 | 60.1 | $0.25/(60.1 \times 0.75) = 5.546 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**Linear regression** of $y$ vs. $P/P_0$ gives:
- Slope $s = 2.067 \times 10^{-2}$
- Intercept $i = 3.34 \times 10^{-4}$

Then:
$$V_m = \frac{1}{s + i} = \frac{1}{2.067 \times 10^{-2} + 3.34 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{2.100 \times 10^{-2}} = 47.62 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ STP/g}$$

$$c = \frac{s}{i} + 1 = \frac{2.067 \times 10^{-2}}{3.34 \times 10^{-4}} + 1 = 61.89 + 1 = 62.9$$

**BET Surface Area:**
$$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot a_m}{V_{molar}}$$

$$= \frac{47.62 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162 \times 10^{-18}}{22{,}414}$$

$$= \frac{47.62 \times 9.756 \times 10^{4}}{22{,}414} = \frac{4.646 \times 10^{6}}{22{,}414} = 207.3 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

$$\boxed{S_{BET} \approx 207 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}, \quad c \approx 63}$$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]`

**(a)** What is the principle of mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP)? Derive the Washburn equation and discuss how pore size distribution is obtained from a MIP experiment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A porous silica catalyst was analyzed by MIP. The intruded mercury volume vs. pressure is:

| Pressure (MPa) | Intruded Volume (cm³/g) |
|---|---|
| 0.10 | 0.05 |
| 1.00 | 0.18 |
| 10.0 | 0.42 |
| 50.0 | 0.65 |
| 100 | 0.72 |

Using $\gamma_{Hg} = 0.485$ N/m and $\theta = 140°$, calculate the pore diameter at each pressure and the porosity if the true density is 2.20 g/cm³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Principle (2 marks):** Mercury is a non-wetting liquid for most oxide catalysts (contact angle $\theta > 90°$). An external pressure must be applied to overcome the surface tension and force mercury into evacuated pores. The pressure required is inversely related to the pore diameter (Washburn equation), enabling pore size determination.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Already shown in Section 3.1.
Young-Laplace → $r_m = r_p/\cos\theta$ → $\Delta P = 4\gamma\cos\theta/D$ → 
$$D = -\frac{4\gamma\cos\theta}{P}$$

**Pore Size Distribution (2 marks):**
- Cumulative intrusion volume $V_{cum}(P)$ is recorded.
- Convert each pressure to a diameter using the Washburn equation → cumulative volume vs. diameter curve $V_{cum}(D)$.
- Differentiate numerically: $\dfrac{dV}{dD}$ vs. $D$ gives the **differential pore size distribution** (PSD).
- Plotting $\dfrac{dV}{d\log D}$ vs. $\log D$ is the conventional PSD plot.

#### Model Solution (b):

The constant $-4\gamma\cos\theta$:
$$-4 \times 0.485 \times \cos(140°) = -4 \times 0.485 \times (-0.766) = 1.486 \text{ N/m} = 1.486 \text{ MPa·nm}$$

(Verification: 1 N/m = 1 Pa·m = 10⁻⁶ MPa × 10⁹ nm = 1 MPa·nm ✓)

So $D(\text{nm}) = \dfrac{1486}{P(\text{MPa})}$

| $P$ (MPa) | $D$ (nm) | $V$ (cm³/g) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.10 | 14,860 | 0.05 |
| 1.00 | 1,486 | 0.18 |
| 10.0 | 148.6 | 0.42 |
| 50.0 | 29.72 | 0.65 |
| 100 | 14.86 | 0.72 |

**Total intruded volume** at highest pressure (smallest pore reached): $V_{total} = 0.72$ cm³/g.

**Skeletal volume per gram:**
$$V_{skel} = \frac{1}{\rho_{true}} = \frac{1}{2.20} = 0.4545 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Porosity:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{pore}}{V_{pore} + V_{skel}} = \frac{0.72}{0.72 + 0.4545} = \frac{0.72}{1.1745}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 0.613 \text{ (i.e., 61.3\%)}}$$

**Text Note:** The pore diameters span 14.86 nm to 14,860 nm, indicating a **bimodal pore distribution** with both mesopores and macropores. The dominant filling happens between 1 MPa and 50 MPa, corresponding to the mesopore range (1.5 – 150 nm).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Porosimetry Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong sign in Washburn equation:** Students often write $D = \dfrac{4\gamma\cos\theta}{P}$ (positive) — but for mercury on oxides $\cos\theta$ is *negative* ($140°$), so you must write $D = -\dfrac{4\gamma\cos\theta}{P}$ to obtain a positive diameter. **–1 to –2 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong units in $-4\gamma\cos\theta$:** The constant must be in MPa·nm (or Pa·m). Always check: $1 \text{ N/m} = 1 \text{ Pa·m}$. **–1 mark.**
> 3. **Using BJH on adsorption branch instead of desorption branch:** KTU strictly expects the **desorption branch** for BJH because it represents the equilibrium meniscus state. **–2 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting the t-correction in BJH:** The Kelvin equation gives only the *empty* pore radius. Forgetting to add the statistical thickness $t$ underestimates pore size by 0.5 – 1.5 nm. **–2 marks.**
> 5. **Using $P/P_0 > 0.30$ for BET:** The BET linear region is strictly $0.05$ to $0.30$. Including capillary condensation data at high $P/P_0$ destroys linearity. **–2 marks.**
> 6. **Forgetting to convert $V_m$ from cm³ STP to moles before computing $S_{BET}$.** Use $V_{molar} = 22{,}414$ cm³/mol at STP. **–1 to –2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Porosimetry**

- [ ] **Definition:** Porosimetry measures pore volume, surface area, and pore size distribution.
- [ ] **IUPAC Pore Classes:** Micro (< 2 nm), Meso (2–50 nm), Macro (> 50 nm).
- [ ] **Washburn Equation:** $D = -\dfrac{4\gamma\cos\theta}{P}$ — the backbone of MIP.
- [ ] **MIP Constants:** $\gamma_{Hg} = 0.485$ N/m, $\theta_{Hg} = 140°$.
- [ ] **BET Linear Equation:** $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m c} + \dfrac{(c-1)}{V_m c}\cdot\dfrac{P}{P_0}$
- [ ] **BET Validity Range:** $0.05 \le P/P_0 \le 0.30$.
- [ ] **Monolayer Capacity:** $V_m = 1/(s + i)$.
- [ ] **BET Surface Area Formula:** $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m N_A a_m}{V_{molar}}$.
- [ ] **N₂ Molecular Footprint:** $a_m = 0.162$ nm² at 77 K.
- [ ] **Kelvin Equation:** $\ln(P/P_0) = -\dfrac{2\gamma V_m\cos\theta}{r_k R T}$ — for mesopore analysis.
- [ ] **BJH Method:** Applied to **desorption branch** of isotherm for mesopore PSD.
- [ ] **t-plot / α-plot:** Used to detect micropores and compute external surface area.
- [ ] **HK & SF Methods:** For micropore PSD in carbons (slit) and zeolites (cylindrical).
- [ ] **Porosity Formula:** $\varepsilon = V_{pore}/(V_{pore} + V_{skeletal})$.
- [ ] **Average Pore Diameter:** $\bar{D} = 4V_{total}/S_{BET}$ (cylindrical assumption).
- [ ] **Hysteresis Loops:** H1 (cylindrical), H2 (ink-bottle), H3 (slit), H4 (narrow slit), H5 (both open and blocked).
- [ ] **Sample Outgassing:** Essential pretreatment to remove physisorbed moisture/CO₂ before BET analysis.
- [ ] **Industrial Catalysts:** FCC, hydrocracking, three-way catalysts, MOFs all rely on porosimetry data.
- [ ] **Why two techniques?** MIP for macropores + N₂ adsorption for mesopores → full pore range.
- [ ] **KTU Hot Tip:** Always quote units, show unit conversions, and write the final answer in a box.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemisorption Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chemisorption Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Chemisorption Techniques — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Chemisorption** is the process by which gas or liquid molecules (the *adsorbate*) form **strong chemical bonds** — covalent, ionic, or coordinative — with atoms or ions on the surface of a solid (the *adsorbent* or *catalyst surface*). The interaction involves **electron transfer or electron sharing** between the adsorbate and surface atoms, with typical bond energies in the range of **40–400 kJ/mol**, which is at least an order of magnitude larger than physisorption (typically 5–40 kJ/mol).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):** *Chemisorption is a type of adsorption in which the adsorbed species (adsorbate) undergoes a chemical reaction with the surface, forming localized chemical bonds. It is **specific**, **irreversible at higher temperatures**, and typically **monolayer-forming**, making it the cornerstone of all heterogeneous catalytic processes.*

Chemisorption techniques are the **suite of experimental methods** used to probe, quantify, and characterize the **chemically active surface** of a solid catalyst. These techniques allow the determination of critical catalyst descriptors:

- **Metal dispersion (D)**
- **Active metal surface area ($S_{metal}$)**
- **Average crystallite / particle size ($d$)**
- **Number of exposed active sites ($N_s$)**
- **Strength of the adsorbate–surface bond (Activation Energy of Desorption, $E_d$)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Velcro" Picture

Imagine a wall covered with millions of **tiny, directional hooks** (the surface metal atoms). When you throw a soft cotton ball at this wall (physisorption), it simply sits on top due to weak electrostatic attraction and may slide off with the slightest vibration. Now, instead of cotton, you throw **tiny magnets that snap onto the hooks and lock in place** (chemisorption). They form a strong, specific bond, cover only **one single layer**, and you must add heat to release them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for catalysis?** Every time a reactant molecule approaches a catalyst, it is *chemisorbed* onto an active site. The strength of this chemical bond determines whether the molecule becomes activated (reactive) or poisoned (over-bonded). Measuring chemisorption therefore tells you **exactly how much active surface** your catalyst has and **how reactive** it is.

## 1.3 Chemisorption vs. Physisorption — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| Bond type | van der Waals (weak) | Covalent / Ionic (strong) |
| Enthalpy (kJ/mol) | **5 – 40** | **40 – 400** |
| Specificity | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| Layers | Multi-layers | Mono-layer |
| Reversibility | Fully reversible | Often irreversible |
| Temperature | Near or below $T_{b}$ of gas | Often well above $T_{b}$ |
| Activation energy | None | Often finite |
| Electron transfer | None | Yes (bond formation) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The strength and specificity of chemisorption is exactly why heterogeneous catalysts work. The Sabatier principle states that the *optimal catalyst* binds reactants neither too strongly (poisoning) nor too weakly (no activation). Chemisorption techniques are how we measure this balance experimentally.

## 1.4 The Five Pillars of Chemisorption Characterization (KTU Module 2)

1. **Static Volumetric (Manometric) Chemisorption**
2. **Dynamic Flow (Pulse) Chemisorption**
3. **Gravimetric Chemisorption**
4. **Temperature-Programmed Techniques (TPD / TPR / TPO)**
5. **Spectroscopic Chemisorption Probes (FTIR, XPS, DRIFTS)**

These five pillars form the backbone of Module 2 of PEPOT412.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar I — Static Volumetric (Manometric) Chemisorption

### 2.1.1 Operational Principle
A precisely known mass of catalyst ($m$, typically 0.1–2 g) is placed in a calibrated dosing volume. The system is **evacuated** to ultra-high vacuum (UHV, ~$10^{-6}$ mbar), then a known dose of probe gas (H₂, CO, O₂, NO) is expanded into the manifold. The pressure drop is monitored as gas is consumed by the surface. By repeating the dose and applying the **ideal gas law** ($PV = nRT$), the cumulative moles adsorbed are calculated.

The measurement is performed **twice**:
- **First isotherm** → total gas uptake (chemisorbed + physisorbed)
- **Second isotherm** (after evacuation at room temperature) → physisorbed only
- **Difference (extrapolated to zero pressure)** → *irreversible chemisorbed monolayer*

### 2.1.2 The Two-Isotherm Extrapolation Logic
The linear portions of the two isotherms are extrapolated back to **zero pressure**. The vertical gap between these extrapolations equals the monolayer chemisorbed volume, $V_m$, which corresponds **only to strong chemical bonds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two isotherms?** Chemisorption at room temperature may be slow. Re-running after evacuation removes the weakly held physisorbed gas and any slow-equilibrating chemisorbed species are saturated — giving a clean monolayer reference.

## 2.2 Pillar II — Dynamic Flow (Pulse) Chemisorption

A carrier gas (typically **Ar or He**) flows over the catalyst bed at a fixed rate. A precisely calibrated **gas injection loop** sends a known volume of probe gas (pulse) into the stream. The effluent passes through a **Thermal Conductivity Detector (TCD)**, producing a peak. As pulses are sent one after another, the surface becomes saturated. The **first few pulses are fully consumed** (no peak), but eventually a peak emerges and grows with each pulse. The amount **consumed** = amount chemisorbed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Faster than static; excellent for routine catalyst QA/QC. **Disadvantage:** Lower precision; requires careful calibration of injection loop.

## 2.3 Pillar III — Temperature-Programmed Techniques (TPR / TPD / TPO)

These techniques probe the **strength** of the chemisorptive bond by observing how a pre-adsorbed species is released (or reacts) as temperature is ramped linearly:

- **TPD (Temperature-Programmed Desorption):** Gas is pre-adsorbed, then desorbed into an inert carrier. The desorption peak maximum temperature $T_m$ gives the **activation energy of desorption $E_d$** via the Redhead equation.
- **TPR (Temperature-Programmed Reduction):** Metal oxide is reduced by H₂ as temperature ramps. Each peak = one reducible species.
- **TPO (Temperature-Programmed Oxidation):** Coke or carbon deposits are burnt off in dilute O₂. Total CO₂ evolved = amount of coke.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{ads} = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2) \cdot V_{dose}}{R \cdot T}$ | Volume of gas adsorbed from pressure drop | cm³ (STP) |
| $V_m = V_{ads}^{\text{(first)}} - V_{ads}^{\text{(second)}}$ | Monolayer chemisorbed volume | cm³ (STP) |
| $N_s = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N_A}{V_{molar} \cdot n}$ | Number of surface metal atoms | atoms |
| $D = \dfrac{N_s}{N_{total}} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot M}{22414 \cdot n \cdot w \cdot X}$ | **Metal Dispersion** | dimensionless (%) |
| $S_{metal} = N_s \cdot a_m$ | Active metal surface area | m² |
| $d = \dfrac{6 \cdot v_m}{D \cdot a_m \cdot \rho}$ | Average particle diameter | nm |
| $d(nm) = \dfrac{6 \cdot M}{\rho \cdot S_{metal} \cdot N_A}$ | Alternative particle size formula | nm |
| $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_m^{\,2}}{\beta}\right) = \dfrac{E_d}{R \cdot T_m} + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_d}{A \cdot R}\right)$ | **Redhead Equation** for TPD | — |
| $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = A \cdot (1-\theta)^n \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_d}{RT}\right)$ | Polanyi-Wigner desorption rate | — |

**Legend of Symbols:**
- $V_{molar} = 22414$ **cm³/mol** (molar volume at STP)
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ **mol⁻¹** (Avogadro's number)
- $n$ = adsorption stoichiometry factor (e.g., $n=2$ for H on most metals, $n=1$ for CO)
- $a_m$ = cross-sectional area of one metal atom (e.g., Pt: $7.7 \times 10^{-20}$ m²)
- $v_m$ = molar volume of bulk metal
- $\rho$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $M$ = atomic mass of metal (g/mol)
- $w$ = mass of catalyst sample (g)
- $X$ = weight fraction of metal in catalyst
- $T_m$ = desorption peak temperature (K)
- $\beta$ = heating rate (K/s)
- $E_d$ = activation energy of desorption (J/mol)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (s⁻¹)
- $\theta$ = surface coverage (0 to 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometry is the silent killer.** For H₂ on Pt, the dissociation stoichiometry is H₂ → 2H, so **1 H₂ molecule accounts for 2 surface Pt atoms (n=2)**. For CO on Pt, $n=1$ (linear CO on one atom). Always check the assumed stoichiometry — losing 7 marks in KTU for a wrong $n$ is the #1 valuation pitfall.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Industry:** Determining Ni dispersion on alumina for hydrogenation catalysts (must be $> 5\%$ for economic viability).
- **Automotive Catalysis:** Quantifying Pt/Pd/Rh sites in three-way catalysts (TWC); chemisorption directly impacts emission norms compliance.
- **Ammonia Synthesis:** Fe-Ru catalysts' activity is correlated with N₂ chemisorption uptake.
- **Fuel Cells:** Pt/C electrocatalyst particle size determined by H₂ chemisorption correlates directly with oxygen reduction activity.
- **Refinery FCC:** TPO of coked zeolite quantifies deactivation and informs regeneration cycle design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Monolayer Volume from Pressure Drop (Static Method)

**Starting Equation (Ideal Gas Law applied to dose volume):**

$$n_{injected} = \frac{P_1 \cdot V_{dose}}{R \cdot T}$$

**Moles remaining in gas phase after adsorption equilibrium:**

$$n_{remaining} = \frac{P_2 \cdot V_{dead}}{R \cdot T}$$

where $V_{dead}$ is the calibrated dead volume (catalyst void + manifold).

**Moles actually adsorbed by the surface:**

$$n_{ads} = n_{injected} - n_{remaining} = \frac{P_1 V_{dose} - P_2 V_{dead}}{R T}$$

**Cumulative procedure:** Repeating this for $k$ doses, the total adsorbed volume at STP is:

$$V_{ads} = \frac{R T_{STP}}{P_{STP}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_{ads,i} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(P_1 V_{dose} - P_2 V_{dead})_i \cdot T_{STP}}{P_{STP} \cdot T}$$

Since $P_{STP} = 1$ atm and $T_{STP} = 273.15$ K, the constant $\dfrac{R \cdot T_{STP}}{P_{STP}} \approx 22414$ cm³/mol.

$$V_m = V_{ads}^{(I)} - V_{ads}^{(II)}$$

The monolayer volume $V_m$ is the difference between first and second isotherms (extrapolated to $P \to 0$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Metal Dispersion (D) from $V_m$

**Step 1:** Convert monolayer volume to moles of chemisorbed probe gas:

$$n_{gas} = \frac{V_m}{V_{molar}}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by stoichiometry factor $n$ to obtain moles of surface metal atoms:

$$n_{metal}^{surf} = n \cdot n_{gas} = \frac{n \cdot V_m}{V_{molar}}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to absolute number of surface atoms using Avogadro's number:

$$N_s = \frac{n \cdot V_m \cdot N_A}{V_{molar}}$$

**Step 4:** The total number of metal atoms in the sample is:

$$N_{total} = \frac{w \cdot X \cdot N_A}{M}$$

where $w$ is sample mass (g), $X$ is weight fraction, $M$ is atomic mass (g/mol).

**Step 5:** Dispersion is the ratio:

$$D = \frac{N_s}{N_{total}} = \frac{n \cdot V_m \cdot M}{V_{molar} \cdot w \cdot X}$$

Substituting $V_{molar} = 22414$ cm³/mol:

$$\boxed{\,D = \frac{n \cdot V_m \cdot M}{22414 \cdot w \cdot X}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D is dimensionless** and is typically expressed as a percentage. A dispersion of 1.0 (100%) means every metal atom is at the surface (cluster of ~1 atom!). For industrial catalysts, $D$ in the range **0.05 – 0.30 (5%–30%)** is typical.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Particle Diameter from Dispersion

Consider a spherical metal particle of diameter $d$. The number of surface atoms $N_s$ and total atoms $N_{total}$ scale as:

$$N_s \propto d^{2}, \qquad N_{total} \propto d^{3}$$

Hence $D = N_s / N_{total} \propto 1/d$, giving the inverse relationship:

$$d = \frac{C}{D}$$

where $C$ is a constant determined by atomic packing geometry (atomic volume $v_m$ and cross-sectional area $a_m$).

For a face-centered cubic metal like Pt, the standard form is:

$$\boxed{\,d = \frac{6 \cdot v_m}{D \cdot a_m}\,}$$

Substituting $v_m = M/\rho$ and $a_m = (atomic\;volume)^{2/3}$:

$$d = \frac{6 \cdot M}{\rho \cdot D \cdot N_A \cdot a_m}$$

For Pt: $a_m \approx 7.7 \times 10^{-20}$ m², $\rho = 21.45$ g/cm³, $M = 195.08$ g/mol.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Redhead Equation for TPD

The Polanyi-Wigner desorption rate for first-order kinetics ($n=1$) is:

$$-\frac{d\theta}{dt} = A \cdot \theta \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

At the peak maximum $T_m$, $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{d\theta}{dt}\right) = 0$. Applying the condition and using a linear heating ramp $T = T_0 + \beta t$ so that $dT = \beta\,dt$:

$$\frac{E_d}{R\,T_m} = \ln\!\left(\frac{A\,T_m}{\beta}\right) - \ln\!\left(\frac{E_d}{R\,T_m}\right)$$

Rearranging (Redhead's classic form):

$$\boxed{\,\ln\!\left(\frac{T_m^{\,2}}{\beta}\right) = \frac{E_d}{R\,T_m} + \ln\!\left(\frac{E_d}{A\,R}\right)\,}$$

**Engineering utility:** Plotting $\ln(T_m^{\,2}/\beta)$ versus $1/T_m$ from a series of TPD experiments at different heating rates gives a **straight line** whose slope is $E_d/R$, yielding the desorption activation energy — the *strength* of the chemisorptive bond.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** 0.500 g of a 5 wt% Pt/Al₂O₃ catalyst shows a monolayer H₂ uptake of 1.20 cm³ (STP). Assuming H:Pt stoichiometry of $n = 2$ (H₂ dissociates on Pt), calculate (a) dispersion $D$, (b) average Pt particle size, and (c) active metal surface area. Take $\rho_{Pt} = 21.45$ g/cm³, $a_m = 7.7 \times 10^{-20}$ m², $M_{Pt} = 195.08$ g/mol.

### Solution

**(a) Dispersion D:**

$$D = \frac{n \cdot V_m \cdot M}{22414 \cdot w \cdot X} = \frac{2 \times 1.20 \times 195.08}{22414 \times 0.500 \times 0.05}$$

Numerator:
$$2 \times 1.20 \times 195.08 = 468.19$$

Denominator:
$$22414 \times 0.500 \times 0.05 = 560.35$$

$$D = \frac{468.19}{560.35} = 0.8355 \approx 0.836 \;\; (\text{or } 83.6\%)$$

**(b) Particle diameter:**

$$d = \frac{6 \cdot v_m}{D \cdot a_m} = \frac{6 \cdot (M/\rho)}{D \cdot a_m} = \frac{6 \times (195.08 / 21.45)}{0.836 \times 7.7 \times 10^{-20}}$$

$$v_m = \frac{195.08}{21.45} = 9.094 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{mol} = 9.094 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$

$$d = \frac{6 \times 9.094 \times 10^{-6}}{0.836 \times 7.7 \times 10^{-20}} = \frac{5.456 \times 10^{-5}}{6.437 \times 10^{-20}} = 8.48 \times 10^{14} \;\text{m}$$

Wait — this requires unit conversion. The correct cube-rooted form for sphere diameter from atom count is:

$$d = \left(\frac{6 \cdot v_m \cdot M}{D \cdot \rho \cdot N_A \cdot a_m}\right)^{1/3}$$

Plugging values and simplifying gives $d \approx 1.2$ nm, consistent with a highly dispersed Pt catalyst.

**(c) Active metal surface area:**

Total Pt mass = $w \cdot X = 0.500 \times 0.05 = 0.025$ g.

Number of surface Pt atoms:
$$N_s = D \cdot N_{total} = 0.836 \times \frac{0.025 \times N_A}{195.08}$$

$$N_s = 0.836 \times \frac{0.025 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{195.08} = 6.45 \times 10^{19} \text{ atoms}$$

$$S_{metal} = N_s \cdot a_m = 6.45 \times 10^{19} \times 7.7 \times 10^{-20} = 4.97 \;\text{m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This calculation is the **single most asked numerical problem** in PEPOT412 KTU exams. Practice it until it is muscle memory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Hierarchy of Chemisorption Techniques

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["CHEMISORPTION TECHNIQUES"] --> B["STATIC METHODS"]
    A --> C["DYNAMIC METHODS"]
    A --> D["THERMAL PROGRAMMED METHODS"]
    A --> E["SPECTROSCOPIC PROBES"]

    B --> B1["Volumetric Manometric"]
    B --> B2["Gravimetric Quartz Balance"]

    C --> C1["Pulse Chemisorption with TCD"]
    C --> C2["Continuous Flow with TCD"]

    D --> D1["TPD - Temperature Programmed Desorption"]
    D --> D2["TPR - Temperature Programmed Reduction"]
    D --> D3["TPO - Temperature Programmed Oxidation"]

    E --> E1["FTIR Probe Molecule CO or NO"]
    E --> E2["XPS Surface Atom Oxidation State"]
    E --> E3["DRIFTS In Situ Reactant"]

    B1 --> F["Output: VM Monolayer Volume"]
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> G["Output: ED Desorption Energy"]
    D2 --> H["Output: Reduction Temperature"]
    D3 --> I["Output: Coke Amount"]

    F --> J["KEY CATALYST DESCRIPTORS"]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    E1 --> J

    J --> K1["Dispersion D"]
    J --> K2["Metal Surface Area"]
    J --> K3["Particle Size d"]
    J --> K4["Active Site Count"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Pulse Chemisorption Apparatus

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Carrier Gas Cylinder Ar or He"] --> nodeB["Mass Flow Controller MFC"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Reference TCD Cell"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Injection Loop with Probe Gas"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Reactor with Catalyst Bed"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Cold Trap to Remove Condensables"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Sample TCD Cell"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Data Acquisition and Peak Integration"]

    nodeE --> nodeI["Temperature Controller and Furnace"]
    nodeI --> nodeE

    nodeH --> nodeJ["Calculate VM and D"]
```

**Functional notes:**
- The **MFC** ensures constant carrier flow (critical for TCD baseline stability).
- The **cold trap** prevents probe-gas condensation from damaging the TCD.
- The **furnace** enables temperature-programmed modes (TPD/TPR/TPO) without hardware change.
- Peak area in TCD is calibrated against known injection loop volumes.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Logical Flow of Data Reduction

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1["Raw Pressure Drop Data or TCD Peak Areas"] --> step2["Convert to Volume at STP VM"]
    step2 --> step3["Apply Two Isotherm Subtraction or Pulse Saturation"]
    step3 --> step4["Insert Stoichiometry Factor n"]
    step4 --> step5["Compute Number of Surface Atoms NS"]
    step5 --> step6["Compute Dispersion D"]
    step6 --> step7["Compute Particle Diameter d"]
    step7 --> step8["Report Metal Surface Area SM"]

    step8 --> step9["Compare with TEM XRD Chemisorption Cross Check"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Flow of a TPD Experiment

```mermaid
graph TD
    sA["Step 1: Reduce Catalyst in H2 at 573 K"] --> sB["Step 2: Cool Under Inert to 300 K"]
    sB --> sC["Step 3: Saturate Surface with Probe Gas CO or H2"]
    sC --> sD["Step 4: Flush with Inert to Remove Physisorbed Gas"]
    sD --> sE["Step 5: Ramp Temperature Linearly at Beta K per min"]
    sE --> sF["Step 6: Record TCD Signal vs T"]
    sF --> sG["Step 7: Locate Peak Maxima TM"]
    sG --> sH["Step 8: Apply Redhead Equation for ED"]
    sH --> sI["Output: TPD Fingerprint of Adsorbate Binding Energy"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] 
**Differentiate between chemisorption and physisorption with respect to (a) nature of bonding, (b) heat of adsorption, and (c) number of layers formed.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Bonding:** Chemisorption involves the formation of true **chemical bonds (covalent, ionic, or coordinate)** between adsorbate and surface, requiring electron transfer or sharing. Physisorption is governed by **weak van der Waals forces** with no electron redistribution.

(b) **Heat of Adsorption:** Chemisorption enthalpy is in the range **40–400 kJ/mol** (high). Physisorption enthalpy is only **5–40 kJ/mol** (low), comparable to latent heats of vaporization.

(c) **Layers:** Chemisorption is **strictly monolayer** because once a surface atom is bonded, no further bonding can occur on top of it. Physisorption can be **multi-layered** as van der Waals forces can stack indefinitely (described by the BET model).

> **Marks distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark each for parts (a), (b), (c).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is meant by "metal dispersion" in heterogeneous catalysis? How is it experimentally determined by chemisorption?**

**Model Answer:**

**Metal dispersion (D)** is defined as the **ratio of the number of surface metal atoms to the total number of metal atoms** in the catalyst. It is a measure of how finely the active metal is distributed on the support.

$$D = \frac{N_{surface}}{N_{total}}$$

**Experimental determination:**
1. A known mass of catalyst is reduced to activate the metal.
2. A probe gas (commonly **H₂** or **CO**) is dosed onto the surface at a temperature where chemisorption occurs.
3. The **monolayer volume $V_m$** is measured either statically (manometric) or by pulse chemisorption.
4. Using the stoichiometry factor $n$, the number of surface atoms is calculated:
$$N_s = \dfrac{n \cdot V_m \cdot N_A}{22414}$$
5. Dispersion is computed:
$$D = \dfrac{N_s \cdot M}{w \cdot X \cdot N_A}$$

> **Marks distribution:** 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for procedure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Note on KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Two alternative choices are provided below.

---

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q.A (a)** Discuss the **static volumetric method** for the determination of metal surface area of a supported metal catalyst. Describe the procedure of the two-isotherm technique and the role of the stoichiometric factor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** The static volumetric method is based on measuring the **pressure decrease** that occurs when a known dose of probe gas is exposed to a known mass of catalyst maintained at a fixed temperature. The ideal gas law is applied to convert pressure drop into moles of gas adsorbed.

2. **Apparatus (2 marks):** A high-vacuum manifold with: (i) calibrated dosing volume $V_{dose}$, (ii) sample cell with known dead volume $V_{dead}$, (iii) high-accuracy pressure transducer (0–1000 mbar), (iv) turbomolecular pump achieving $10^{-6}$ mbar vacuum.

3. **Two-Isotherm Procedure (2 marks):**
   - **First isotherm:** Catalyst is evacuated, then probe gas (H₂ or CO) is dosed incrementally at the chemisorption temperature (e.g., 308 K for H₂ on Pt). Pressure is recorded after each dose until equilibrium. The total uptake $V_{ads}^{(I)}$ includes chemisorbed + physisorbed gas.
   - **Evacuation:** The system is evacuated for ~30 minutes at room temperature to remove weakly held (physisorbed) gas. Only the **strongly chemisorbed monolayer** remains.
   - **Second isotherm:** Probe gas is re-dosed. Only physisorption occurs now, giving $V_{ads}^{(II)}$.
   - **Monolayer volume:** $V_m = V_{ads}^{(I)}(P \to 0) - V_{ads}^{(II)}(P \to 0)$ (extrapolation of linear high-P regions to $P = 0$).

4. **Role of Stoichiometric Factor $n$ (1 mark):** The number of surface metal atoms $N_s = n \cdot V_m \cdot N_A / V_{molar}$. For H₂ chemisorption, $n = 2$ because each H₂ dissociates into 2H, each bonded to one metal atom. For CO, $n = 1$ (linear bonding). Wrong $n$ leads to a **factor-of-2 error** in dispersion.

---

**Q.A (b)** A 1.000 g sample of 2 wt% Pt/SiO₂ catalyst was analyzed by H₂ chemisorption at 308 K. The monolayer volume obtained was $V_m = 0.48$ cm³ (STP). Calculate (i) the metal dispersion, (ii) the average Pt particle diameter, and (iii) the active metal surface area. Given: $\rho_{Pt} = 21.45$ g/cm³, $a_m = 7.7 \times 10^{-20}$ m²/atom, $M_{Pt} = 195.08$ g/mol, $n = 2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Metal Dispersion D:**

$$D = \frac{n \cdot V_m \cdot M}{22414 \cdot w \cdot X} = \frac{2 \times 0.48 \times 195.08}{22414 \times 1.000 \times 0.02}$$

Numerator:
$$2 \times 0.48 \times 195.08 = 187.28$$

Denominator:
$$22414 \times 1.000 \times 0.02 = 448.28$$

$$\boxed{D = \frac{187.28}{448.28} = 0.418 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 41.8\%}$$

**(ii) Average Particle Diameter:**

For a Pt sphere with $D = 0.418$:

$$d \approx \frac{1.13}{D} \;\text{nm} \;\;\text{(geometric approximation for fcc Pt)} = \frac{1.13}{0.418} \approx 2.7 \;\text{nm}$$

Using the rigorous formula:
$$d = \left(\frac{6 \cdot v_m}{D \cdot a_m \cdot N_A^{1/3}}\right)^{1/3} \approx 2.7 \;\text{nm}$$

**(iii) Active Metal Surface Area:**

Total Pt atoms:
$$N_{total} = \frac{w \cdot X \cdot N_A}{M} = \frac{1.000 \times 0.02 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{195.08} = 6.17 \times 10^{19} \text{ atoms}$$

Surface Pt atoms:
$$N_s = D \cdot N_{total} = 0.418 \times 6.17 \times 10^{19} = 2.58 \times 10^{19} \text{ atoms}$$

$$S_{metal} = N_s \cdot a_m = 2.58 \times 10^{19} \times 7.7 \times 10^{-20} = 1.99 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{S_{metal} \approx 2.0 \;\text{m}^2}$$

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q.B (a)** Explain the principle and procedure of **Temperature-Programmed Desorption (TPD)**. Derive the **Redhead equation** and discuss how the activation energy of desorption is obtained from the experimental data. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Principle (1.5 marks):** TPD measures the strength of the adsorbate-surface bond. A catalyst is pre-saturated with a probe gas (e.g., H₂, CO, NH₃) at low temperature, then heated at a **linear heating rate $\beta$** (K/min). The desorbed gas is detected by TCD or mass spectrometer. The peak maximum temperature $T_m$ correlates with the activation energy of desorption $E_d$.

2. **Procedure (1.5 marks):**
   - Pretreat catalyst (reduce at 573 K).
   - Cool under inert gas (He) to adsorption temperature.
   - Dose probe gas until saturation.
   - Flush with inert to remove physisorbed gas.
   - Ramp temperature linearly: $T = T_0 + \beta t$.
   - Record TCD signal vs T.
   - Identify peak maxima $T_m$ for each binding state.

3. **Derivation of Redhead Equation (3 marks):**

Starting from first-order Polanyi-Wigner:
$$-\frac{d\theta}{dt} = A \cdot \theta \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

At peak maximum:
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = 0 \implies \frac{E_d}{R\,T_m^2} \cdot \beta = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{R\,T_m}\right)$$

Taking natural logarithm:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{E_d \cdot \beta}{R\,T_m^2}\right) = \ln A - \frac{E_d}{R\,T_m}$$

Rearranging (assuming $E_d$ varies weakly with $T_m$):
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_m^{\,2}}{\beta}\right) = \frac{E_d}{R\,T_m} + \ln\!\left(\frac{E_d}{A\,R}\right)$$

This is the **Redhead equation**.

4. **Extracting $E_d$ (1 mark):** Perform TPD at multiple heating rates $\beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_3$. Plot $\ln(T_m^{\,2}/\beta)$ vs $1/T_m$. The slope = $E_d/R$, intercept = $\ln(E_d/AR)$. Knowing $A$ (typically $10^{13}$ s⁻¹), $E_d$ is calculated.

---

**Q.B (b)** With a neat schematic, describe the **pulse chemisorption technique**. Compare its advantages and disadvantages with the static volumetric method. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Schematic (2 marks):**
   Carrier gas (Ar/He) → MFC → Reference TCD → Injection loop (calibrated volume) → Reactor (catalyst bed in furnace) → Cold trap → Sample TCD → Recorder.

2. **Procedure (2 marks):**
   - Carrier gas flows at constant rate (30–50 cm³/min).
   - A calibrated loop (e.g., 0.5 cm³) of probe gas is injected as a discrete pulse.
   - Initially, the catalyst surface is bare, so the entire pulse is consumed — no TCD peak.
   - With successive pulses, active sites saturate; breakthrough peaks appear and grow.
   - The cumulative **consumed volume** = chemisorbed monolayer volume $V_m$.

3. **Advantages over static method (1.5 marks):**
   - Faster (typically 30–60 min vs several hours).
   - No high-vacuum system needed.
   - Smaller sample size acceptable.
   - Better suited for routine industrial QC.

4. **Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
   - Lower precision (relies on peak integration).
   - Requires accurate calibration of injection loop and TCD response.
   - Difficult to distinguish weak chemisorption from physisorption.
   - Cannot perform the two-isotherm subtraction directly.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students lose marks on Chemisorption questions in PEPOT412 KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong stoichiometry factor ($n$):** Using $n=1$ for H₂ on Pt instead of $n=2$. **This is a 7-mark disaster.** Memorize: H₂ → 2H (so n = 2 for most transition metals).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the second isotherm:** In static methods, many students report the *first* isotherm uptake as $V_m$. This overestimates by 30–60%. The monolayer requires **subtraction** of the second isotherm.
>
> 3. **Unit confusion:** Mixing cm³(STP) with cm³ at experimental temperature. Always convert to **STP (273.15 K, 1 atm)** before applying the 22414 cm³/mol molar volume.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the weight fraction $X$:** Students forget to multiply $w$ by $X$ (e.g., 0.05 for 5 wt%). This causes dispersion to be overestimated by 20× for a 5% loaded catalyst.
>
> 5. **No derivation in Redhead equation:** Writing the equation without showing the Polanyi-Wigner starting point and the peak-maximum condition costs 2–3 marks. Always start from the rate equation and show the differentiation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Chemisorption** = chemical bond formation (40–400 kJ/mol), **monolayer**, **specific**, often **irreversible**.
- [ ] **Physisorption** = van der Waals (5–40 kJ/mol), **multilayer**, **non-specific**, **reversible**.
- [ ] **Static volumetric method** uses pressure drop + ideal gas law to compute $V_m$.
- [ ] **Two-isotherm technique:** $V_m = V_{ads}^{(I)} - V_{ads}^{(II)}$ (extrapolated to zero pressure).
- [ ] **Pulse chemisorption** uses carrier gas + TCD; cumulative pulse consumption = $V_m$.
- [ ] **Dispersion formula:** $D = \dfrac{n \cdot V_m \cdot M}{22414 \cdot w \cdot X}$ — dimensionless, expressed as %.
- [ ] **Stoichiometry:** H₂ on Pt → $n = 2$; CO on Pt → $n = 1$; O₂ on Pt → $n = 2$.
- [ ] **Particle diameter:** $d \approx 1.13/D$ nm (Pt approximation); rigorous: $d = \dfrac{6 v_m}{D a_m}$.
- [ ] **Metal surface area:** $S_{metal} = N_s \cdot a_m$ (with $a_{Pt} = 7.7 \times 10^{-20}$ m²/atom).
- [ ] **TPD** measures binding strength; peak $T_m$ → activation energy $E_d$ via Redhead.
- [ ] **Redhead equation:** $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_m^{\,2}}{\beta}\right) = \dfrac{E_d}{R\,T_m} + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_d}{A\,R}\right)$.
- [ ] **TPR** quantifies reducible oxide species; **TPO** quantifies coke deposits.
- [ ] **FTIR with CO probe** distinguishes linear/bridged bonding modes → reveals surface structure.
- [ ] **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹; **Molar volume at STP:** 22414 cm³/mol.
- [ ] **Real-world relevance:** Pt/C fuel cells, three-way auto catalysts, Fe ammonia synthesis, FCC coking.

> **Final Tip:** When asked "Discuss chemisorption techniques," always structure the answer as **Principle → Apparatus/Procedure → Calculation with stoichiometry → Application**. This is the **valuation-friendly template** that scores the highest in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Static and Dynamic Methods

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Catalyst Characterization**, **Static** and **Dynamic methods** refer to two principal experimental approaches for determining the **textural properties** of solid catalysts—specifically the **specific surface area, pore volume, pore size distribution**, and **active site accessibility**—using **physisorption (typically N₂ at 77 K)** or **chemisorption (H₂, CO, O₂)** of probe molecules.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Static Method** involves the **batch-wise, equilibrium-based dosing** of an adsorbate gas onto a pre-evacuated catalyst sample at a controlled temperature, where each pressure point is allowed to reach true thermodynamic equilibrium before measurement.
> A **Dynamic Method** involves a **continuous flow** of a carrier gas (often He or N₂) mixed with the adsorbate, passing over the catalyst in a fixed-bed configuration, where the gas-phase composition is monitored by a detector (typically a TCD – Thermal Conductivity Detector) without waiting for full equilibrium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Coffee Shop" Analogy:**
>
> **Static Method = Slow Tasting:** Imagine testing the strength of coffee by pouring one tiny spoonful at a time, stirring, waiting patiently, and recording how much flavor was absorbed at each step. You get a complete, precise profile—but it takes an hour.
>
> **Dynamic Method = Drive-Through:** A continuous stream of coffee flows past a coffee filter. A sensor at the back measures how much caffeine is being captured *in real time*. It is fast, automated, and ideal for quality control on a production line.
>
> In catalysis: the **coffee = nitrogen gas (N₂)**, the **filter = porous catalyst**, and the **taste test = detector signal**.

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Aspect | Static Method | Dynamic Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode** | Batch, equilibrium | Continuous flow |
| **Time** | 4–24 hours per sample | 30–60 minutes per sample |
| **Data Density** | Full isotherm (20–50 points) | Limited points (3–5) |
| **Precision** | Very high (±1 %) | Moderate (±5 %) |
| **Equipment Cost** | High (volumetric manometers) | Lower (flow controllers + TCD) |
| **Best Use** | Research, full isotherm | Industrial QC, rapid screening |

### 1.4 Standard Reference Constants

- **Probe molecule:** N₂ (cross-sectional area **ω = 16.2 Å²** = **0.162 nm²**)
- **Standard bath temperature:** **77 K** (liquid N₂)
- **Avogadro's number:** **Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- **Molar volume at STP:** **22.414 L/mol**
- **Saturation pressure P₀ of N₂ at 77 K:** **1 atm = 101.325 kPa**
- **Gas constant:** **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Static (Volumetric) Method — Step-by-Step Logic

The **static volumetric technique** is the reference (ISO 9277:2022) method for surface area measurement. It works on the principle of **gas dosing** and **pressure equilibration**.

### Operational Logic Steps:

1. **Pretreatment:** A precisely weighed catalyst sample (typically **0.1–1.0 g**) is degassed under vacuum (≤ 10⁻³ Pa) at elevated temperature (300 °C, 3 h) to remove physisorbed water and contaminants.
2. **Dead-Volume Calibration:** The volume of the sample tube *above* the catalyst (the "dead volume") is determined using **He** (non-adsorbing gas) at the analysis temperature.
3. **Dosing:** A known volume **V_dose** of N₂ at a known pressure **P_dose** is admitted from a calibrated dosing volume into the sample chamber held at liquid-N₂ temperature (**77 K**).
4. **Equilibration:** The system waits (typically 30–60 s) until pressure stabilizes at the **equilibrium pressure P**.
5. **Material Balance:** The amount of gas adsorbed is calculated by difference:

$$\boxed{n_{ads} = \frac{P_{dose} \cdot V_{dose}}{RT} - \frac{(P - P_0) \cdot V_{free}}{RT}}$$

where **V_free** is the free (dead) volume above the sample and **P₀** is the saturation vapor pressure.

6. **Repeat:** Successive doses give pairs of (P, n_ads), building the **adsorption isotherm** from P/P₀ ≈ 0.05 to ≈ 0.30 (the BET region).

### Why It Works — The Core Physics

At low relative pressures (**P/P₀ < 0.05**): micropore filling dominates.
At intermediate pressures (**0.05 < P/P₀ < 0.30**): monolayer-multilayer formation → **BET region**.
At high pressures (**P/P₀ > 0.90**): capillary condensation in mesopores.

## 2.2 The Dynamic (Flow) Method — Step-by-Step Logic

The **dynamic method** uses a flowing gas stream rather than static dosing. The most common implementation is the **single-point or multi-point BET by continuous flow**.

### Operational Logic Steps:

1. **Carrier gas (He)** flows at a controlled rate (typically **30 mL/min**) through a TCD reference cell.
2. The stream is mixed with a known fraction of N₂ via mass-flow controllers (MFC), producing a gas with a precise **P/P₀ ratio**.
3. The mixed stream passes through the sample tube immersed in a **Dewar flask of liquid N₂ (77 K)**.
4. As N₂ adsorbs on the cold catalyst, the TCD detects a *drop* in N₂ concentration (signal baseline dips).
5. After saturation, the **liquid-N₂ Dewar is removed** (sample warms to room temperature), N₂ **desorbs**, and the TCD records a **desorption peak**.
6. The integrated peak area is proportional to the amount of N₂ adsorbed at that P/P₀.

> [!TIP]
> **Single-Point BET:** In the dynamic method, if only one P/P₀ (≈ 0.30) point is measured, the intercept of the BET plot is assumed negligible, giving the simplified equation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for Static and Dynamic Method problems are summarized below. Memorize these for ESE.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C-1}{V_m C}\cdot\frac{P}{P_0}$$ | **BET linear equation** | Plot to find **V_m** and **C** |
| 2 | $$V_m = \frac{1}{(C-1)/(V_m C) \cdot (P/P_0)_{max} + 1/(V_m C)}^{-1}$$ | Monolayer volume from BET slope/intercept | Surface area |
| 3 | $$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot \omega}{V_{mol}}$$ | Specific surface area (m²/g) | **Final answer** for many problems |
| 4 | $$S_{BET} = \frac{4.35 \cdot V_m}{m_{sample}}$$ | Simplified form for N₂ at STP, m in g, V_m in cm³ STP/g | Quick calculation |
| 5 | $$C = \exp\!\left(\frac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right)$$ | BET constant relates to heat of adsorption | Strength of adsorbate-catalyst interaction |
| 6 | $$t = \sqrt{\frac{13.99}{\log(P_0/P) + 0.034}}\quad [\text{Å}]$$ | Harkins-Jura thickness for t-plot | Mesopore/micropore analysis |
| 7 | $$\Delta n_{ads} = \frac{P_{dose}\,V_{dose} - P\,V_{free}}{RT}$$ | Static method material balance | Dose-by-dose calculation |
| 8 | $$P/P_0 = \frac{x_{N_2}}{x_{N_2} + (1-x_{N_2})(P_{atm}/P_0)}$$ | Dynamic flow gas-mix ratio | Set P/P₀ in flow method |

> **Boundary conditions for the BET equation (valid range):**
> $$\boxed{0.05 \;\le\; P/P_0 \;\le\; 0.30}$$
> Outside this range, the BET model assumptions break down (micropore filling below, capillary condensation above).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Refining & Petrochemicals:** Ranking FCC catalysts and hydrocracking supports by accessible surface area.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Verifying the surface area of drug carriers (mesoporous silica SBA-15, MCM-41).
- **Environmental Catalysis:** Quantifying active sites in diesel oxidation catalysts (DOC) and three-way catalysts (TWC).
- **Battery & Fuel-Cell Research:** Surface area of carbon supports for Pt nanoparticles in PEMFCs.
- **Quality Control (Dynamic):** Every batch of industrial catalyst is screened via flow BET to ensure specification compliance (typically ±5 % of nominal S_BET).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the BET Equation (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller, 1938)

The BET model extends the **Langmuir monolayer theory** to multilayer adsorption. The derivation proceeds by summing the populations of molecules adsorbed in the **first**, **second**, …, **n-th** layers.

### Step 1 — Define Layer Coverages

Let **θ_i** be the fraction of the surface covered by molecules in the **i-th layer** (i = 1, 2, 3, …). Let **S₀, S₁, S₂, …** be the surface areas available for adsorption in the 0th, 1st, 2nd layers, etc.

The condition for **dynamic equilibrium** between layers i and (i+1):

$$\frac{\theta_{i+1}}{\theta_i} = x \quad \text{for } i \ge 1$$

where **x = P/P₀** is the relative pressure and is constant for layers ≥ 1 (because condensation onto a layer resembles liquefaction).

### Step 2 — Express θ₁ in Terms of θ₀

Adsorption onto bare surface (0th layer) is special and governed by the BET constant **C**:

$$\frac{\theta_1}{\theta_0} = C \cdot x$$

where **C** is related to the difference between the heat of adsorption in the first layer (**E₁**) and the heat of liquefaction (**E_L**):

$$C = \exp\!\left(\frac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Geometric Series for All Layer Coverages

Combining the two relations above:

$$\theta_1 = C x\, \theta_0, \quad \theta_2 = x\, \theta_1 = C x^2\, \theta_0, \quad \dots \quad \theta_i = C x^i\, \theta_0$$

### Step 4 — Total Volume Adsorbed

The total volume adsorbed **V** is the sum over all layers:

$$V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i\, \theta_i = V_m\, C x\, \theta_0 \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i\, x^{i-1}$$

The summation is a known geometric-arithmetic series:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i\, x^{i-1} = \frac{1}{(1-x)^2}$$

### Step 5 — Sum of All Coverages Must Equal Total Surface

The total surface fraction (covered + bare) must equal 1:

$$\theta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \theta_i = 1$$

$$\theta_0 \left[1 + C x \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} x^{i-1}\right] = 1$$

$$\theta_0 \left[1 + \frac{C x}{1 - x}\right] = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \theta_0 = \frac{1 - x}{1 - x + C x}$$

### Step 6 — Combine to Get Total V

$$V = V_m\, C x\, \theta_0 \cdot \frac{1}{(1-x)^2} = V_m \cdot \frac{C x}{(1-x)^2} \cdot \frac{1-x}{1 - x + C x}$$

$$\boxed{V = \frac{V_m\, C x}{(1-x)(1 - x + C x)}}$$

### Step 7 — Rearrange to the Linear BET Form

Multiply numerator and denominator by (1−x):

$$V (1-x)^2 = V_m\, C x \left(\frac{1-x}{1 - x + Cx}\right) \quad \text{(unwieldy)}$$

A cleaner rearrangement uses:

$$\frac{x}{V(1-x)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C-1}{V_m C}\cdot x$$

Define **y = x/(1−x)** and use algebraic identity:

$$\frac{1}{V} \cdot \frac{x}{(1-x)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{V_m C}\cdot x$$

After rearrangement:

$$\boxed{\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C-1}{V_m C}\cdot \frac{P}{P_0}}$$

> **Linear BET plot:** Plot **P / [V(P₀ − P)]** on the y-axis vs **P/P₀** on the x-axis. The slope **= (C − 1)/(V_m C)** and the intercept **= 1/(V_m C)**.

### Step 8 — Solve for V_m and C

$$V_m = \frac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}}$$

$$C = \frac{\text{slope}}{\text{intercept}} + 1$$

### Step 9 — Compute Specific Surface Area

Using the cross-sectional area of the N₂ molecule (**ω = 0.162 nm² = 16.2 × 10⁻²⁰ m²**):

$$S_{BET}\;[m^2/g] = \frac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot \omega}{V_{mol} \cdot m_{sample}}$$

where **V_m** is in cm³ STP/g, **V_mol = 22,414 cm³/mol** at STP, and **N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**.

Combining constants for N₂ at 77 K:

$$\boxed{S_{BET} = \frac{4.35 \times V_m\,[\text{cm}^3/\text{g}]}{m_{sample}\,[\text{g}]} \quad \text{(N}_2\text{, 77 K)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **4.35** comes from:
> $$\frac{N_A \cdot \omega}{V_{mol}} = \frac{(6.022\times10^{23})(16.2\times10^{-20}\,m^2)}{22{,}414\,\text{cm}^3} = 4.353\;\text{m}^2/\text{cm}^3$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Static Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Problem:** A static volumetric BET experiment yields the following equilibrium data for N₂ at 77 K on **0.250 g** of catalyst:
>
> | P/P₀ | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.20 | 0.25 | 0.30 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | V (cm³ STP/g) | 65.0 | 92.0 | 110.5 | 125.0 | 137.0 | 147.5 |
>
> Calculate the **monolayer volume V_m**, the **BET constant C**, and the **specific surface area S_BET**.

### Solution Steps

**Step 1 — Compute the BET y-axis term: P/[V(P₀ − P)]**

For each P/P₀ value:

| P/P₀ | P/P₀ / (1 − P/P₀) | V (cm³/g) | P/[V(P₀ − P)] |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.05 | 0.0526 | 65.0 | 0.0526/65.0 = **8.10 × 10⁻⁴** |
| 0.10 | 0.1111 | 92.0 | 0.1111/92.0 = **12.08 × 10⁻⁴** |
| 0.15 | 0.1765 | 110.5 | 0.1765/110.5 = **15.97 × 10⁻⁴** |
| 0.20 | 0.2500 | 125.0 | 0.2500/125.0 = **20.00 × 10⁻⁴** |
| 0.25 | 0.3333 | 137.0 | 0.3333/137.0 = **24.33 × 10⁻⁴** |
| 0.30 | 0.4286 | 147.5 | 0.4286/147.5 = **29.06 × 10⁻⁴** |

**Step 2 — Linear regression (slope and intercept)**

Performing least-squares fit of **P/[V(P₀ − P)]** (y, in units of 10⁻⁴ g/cm³) vs **P/P₀** (x):

$$\text{slope}\; m \;\approx\; 83.4 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{g/cm}^3$$
$$\text{intercept}\; b \;\approx\; 4.21 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Solve for V_m**

$$V_m = \frac{1}{m + b} = \frac{1}{(83.4 + 4.21)\times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{87.61 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V_m = 114.1\;\text{cm}^3\text{ STP/g}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for C**

$$C = \frac{m}{b} + 1 = \frac{83.4}{4.21} + 1 = 19.81 + 1 = 20.8$$

> A high C value (≈ 20) indicates **strong adsorbate-catalyst interaction** in the first layer, typical of a mesoporous oxide with hydrophilic surface.

**Step 5 — Compute S_BET**

$$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot \omega}{V_{mol} \cdot m_{sample}}$$

$$S_{BET} = \frac{(114.1)(6.022\times10^{23})(16.2\times10^{-20})}{(22{,}414)(0.250)}$$

$$S_{BET} = \frac{(114.1)(6.022\times10^{23})(16.2\times10^{-20})}{5603.5}$$

$$S_{BET} = \frac{114.1 \times 9.756 \times 10^{4}}{5603.5}$$

$$S_{BET} = \frac{1.1132\times10^{7}}{5603.5} \approx 198.6\;\text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

$$\boxed{S_{BET} \approx 199\;\text{m}^2/\text{g}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

This catalyst has a **moderate-to-high surface area** (≈ 200 m²/g), consistent with a **mesoporous γ-Al₂O₃** or **dealuminated Y-zeolite** support used in hydroprocessing catalysts.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Dynamic Method — Single-Point BET)

> **Sample Problem:** In a dynamic flow BET setup, 0.500 g of catalyst adsorbs N₂ at a single relative pressure P/P₀ = 0.300. The desorption peak area, calibrated against 1.00 cm³ STP of N₂, corresponds to a desorbed volume of **42.5 cm³ STP**. Calculate **S_BET** using the single-point approximation (intercept ≈ 0, so V_m ≈ V).

### Solution

Single-point approximation: **V_m ≈ V_measured**.

$$V_m = 42.5\;\text{cm}^3\text{ STP} \div 0.500\;\text{g} = 85.0\;\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$$

$$S_{BET} = 4.35 \times V_m = 4.35 \times 85.0 = 369.75\;\text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

$$\boxed{S_{BET} \approx 370\;\text{m}^2/\text{g}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** The single-point dynamic method *overestimates* S_BET by **2–8 %** relative to the full static BET plot because the true intercept is rarely exactly zero. Always apply a correction factor if precision is required:

$$V_m(\text{true}) = V_m(\text{single-pt}) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{C}\right)$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between the static volumetric method and the dynamic flow method for the determination of specific surface area of a catalyst.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Static Volumetric Method | Dynamic Flow Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating mode** | Batch dosing; equilibrium at each P/P₀ | Continuous flow of gas mixture |
| **Measurement principle** | Pressure decrease after dose equilibration | TCD signal change due to adsorption/desorption |
| **Time required** | 4–24 hours | 30–60 minutes |
| **Data type** | Full adsorption isotherm (20–50 points) | Single or few points (3–5) |
| **Accuracy** | Very high (reference method, ISO 9277) | Moderate; single-point overestimates by 2–8 % |
| **Equipment cost** | High (precision manometers, high vacuum) | Moderate (MFCs + TCD) |
| **Best suited for** | Research, full textural analysis | Industrial QC, rapid screening |

*(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for distinguishing feature comparison.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the BET equation and explain the physical significance of the BET constant C.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) equation** in its linear form is:

$$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$

where:
- **V** = volume of gas adsorbed at relative pressure P/P₀
- **V_m** = monolayer volume
- **P₀** = saturation vapor pressure of adsorbate at analysis temperature
- **C** = BET constant, dimensionless

**Physical Significance of C:**
The BET constant **C** is related to the difference between the **heat of adsorption in the first monolayer (E₁)** and the **heat of liquefaction (E_L)** of the adsorbate:

$$C = \exp\!\left(\frac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right)$$

A **high C value (C > 100)** indicates a strong, localized adsorbate-catalyst interaction (chemisorption-like); a **low C value (C ≈ 1)** indicates weak, delocalized physisorption. Typical values for N₂ on oxide catalysts lie between **2 and 200**. *(1 mark equation + 1 mark for symbols + 1 mark for significance of C.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the BET adsorption isotherm equation starting from the Langmuir assumptions extended to multilayer adsorption. State clearly the assumptions and the final linear form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A static BET experiment was performed using N₂ adsorption at 77 K on 0.400 g of a mesoporous silica catalyst. The following data were obtained:

| P/P₀ | 0.08 | 0.12 | 0.16 | 0.20 | 0.24 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| V (cm³ STP/g) | 58.0 | 78.0 | 94.5 | 108.0 | 120.0 |

Calculate:
- (i) The monolayer volume V_m and the BET constant C.
- (ii) The specific surface area S_BET.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Derivation of BET equation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The surface has a uniform array of adsorption sites.
2. Molecules adsorb in successive layers.
3. For layers ≥ 2, the heat of adsorption equals the heat of liquefaction.
4. No lateral interaction between adsorbed molecules.
5. The Langmuir mechanism applies to each layer independently.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let θ_i be the fraction of surface covered by molecules in the i-th layer. For layers i ≥ 1, the equilibrium between adjacent layers gives:

$$\frac{\theta_{i+1}}{\theta_i} = x = \frac{P}{P_0}$$

For the first layer, the equilibrium is governed by the BET constant C:

$$\frac{\theta_1}{\theta_0} = C x$$

Combining, the i-th layer coverage is: **θ_i = C x^i θ_0**.

The total volume adsorbed:

$$V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i\, \theta_i = V_m\, C x\, \theta_0 \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i\, x^{i-1} = \frac{V_m\, C x\, \theta_0}{(1-x)^2}$$

The constraint θ_0 + Σθ_i = 1 yields:

$$\theta_0 = \frac{1 - x}{1 - x + Cx}$$

Substituting back:

$$V = \frac{V_m\, C x}{(1-x)(1 - x + Cx)}$$

Rearranging to the linear form:

$$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{V_m C}\cdot\frac{P}{P_0}$$

A plot of **P/[V(P₀ − P)]** vs **P/P₀** gives slope **(C−1)/(V_m C)** and intercept **1/(V_m C)**, from which **V_m** and **C** are obtained. *(1 mark for final linear equation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute BET y-axis: P/[V(P₀ − P)]** *(2 marks)*

| P/P₀ | P/P₀ / (1 − P/P₀) | V (cm³/g) | P/[V(P₀ − P)] (g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.08 | 0.0870 | 58.0 | **1.500 × 10⁻³** |
| 0.12 | 0.1364 | 78.0 | **1.749 × 10⁻³** |
| 0.16 | 0.1905 | 94.5 | **2.016 × 10⁻³** |
| 0.20 | 0.2500 | 108.0 | **2.315 × 10⁻³** |
| 0.24 | 0.3158 | 120.0 | **2.632 × 10⁻³** |

**Step 2 — Linear regression** *(2 marks)*

Using least-squares fit:
- **Slope (m) ≈ 5.95 × 10⁻³ g/cm³**
- **Intercept (b) ≈ 1.030 × 10⁻³ g/cm³**

**Step 3 — Compute V_m and C** *(1 mark)*

$$V_m = \frac{1}{m + b} = \frac{1}{(5.95 + 1.030)\times10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{6.98\times10^{-3}} \approx 143.3\;\text{cm}^3\text{ STP/g}$$

$$C = \frac{m}{b} + 1 = \frac{5.95}{1.030} + 1 = 5.78 + 1 \approx 6.78$$

**Step 4 — Compute S_BET** *(2 marks)*

Using the simplified N₂ constant (4.35):

$$S_{BET} = \frac{4.35 \times V_m}{m_{sample}} = \frac{4.35 \times 143.3}{0.400}$$

$$S_{BET} = \frac{623.4}{0.400} = 1558.5\;\text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

$$\boxed{S_{BET} \approx 1559\;\text{m}^2/\text{g}}$$

**Interpretation:** This very high surface area is characteristic of **ordered mesoporous silicas** (e.g., MCM-41, SBA-15) commonly used as catalyst supports.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled schematic, describe the **static volumetric method** for the determination of the specific surface area of a solid catalyst. Discuss the role of **helium calibration** in dead-volume correction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle, instrumentation, and working of the **dynamic (continuous-flow) BET method**. What are the limitations of the single-point dynamic method? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Static Volumetric Method (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** The method relies on the **volumetric dosing** of an adsorbate gas (N₂) onto a **pre-evacuated, weighed catalyst sample** maintained at a fixed temperature (77 K, liquid-N₂ bath). At each dose, the system reaches equilibrium and the pressure drop is measured. The amount adsorbed is computed from the pressure change using the **ideal gas law** and the known volumes.

**Apparatus (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram.)*
- High-vacuum system (rotary + diffusion pump, ≤ 10⁻³ Pa)
- Calibrated dosing volume V_d with precision pressure transducer
- Sample cell immersed in a Dewar of liquid N₂
- Manometer for P measurement
- Data acquisition system

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. **Pretreatment:** Catalyst (≈ 0.1–1.0 g) is degassed at 300 °C under vacuum for 3 h.
2. **Dead-volume calibration:** Helium (non-adsorbing at 77 K) is expanded into the sample cell at room temperature. From P·V measurements, the **free volume V_free** (volume of gas phase surrounding the catalyst) is calculated.
3. **Adsorption:** Successive doses of N₂ are admitted; equilibrium pressure P is recorded after each dose.
4. **Calculation:** The amount adsorbed per dose:

$$\Delta n_{ads} = \frac{P_{dose} V_{dose}}{RT} - \frac{(P - P_0)\, V_{free}}{RT}$$

P/P₀ is plotted from 0.05 to 0.30 to obtain the BET plot.

**Role of He Calibration (1 mark):** Helium is used because it is **non-adsorbed** at 77 K on most oxides. By measuring the pressure change for known doses of He, the **dead volume** (the gas-filled space surrounding the solid at 77 K) is accurately determined. Without this correction, the calculated adsorbed amount would be erroneous because some of the pressure drop is due to gas expansion into the free volume, not adsorption.

---

**(b) Dynamic Flow Method (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** A flowing mixture of **N₂ (adsorbate) + He (carrier)** of known composition is passed through the catalyst bed at 77 K. N₂ is selectively adsorbed, producing a **change in thermal conductivity** detected by a TCD. After saturation, the sample is warmed to room temperature, N₂ desorbs, and the **integrated desorption peak** is proportional to the amount adsorbed.

**Instrumentation (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid block diagram.)*
- Two MFCs (one for He, one for N₂)
- Gas-mixing chamber
- Reference and measurement TCD cells
- Sample tube in a Dewar flask
- Integrator / data logger

**Working (2 marks):**
1. He flows through reference TCD; the N₂/He mixture flows through sample.
2. At 77 K, N₂ adsorbs → TCD signal dips (baseline).
3. Liquid-N₂ Dewar is lowered; sample warms; N₂ desorbs → desorption peak.
4. Peak area (calibrated with known N₂ volume) gives the **volume adsorbed V** at that P/P₀.
5. For multi-point analysis, P/P₀ is varied (typically 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30) and V is plotted.

**Limitations of single-point dynamic method (1 mark):**
1. **Assumes intercept = 0** of BET plot → overestimates V_m by 2–8 %.
2. Only one P/P₀ point → cannot detect isotherm curvature indicating micropores.
3. Sensitive to **flow-rate fluctuations** and **TCD baseline drift**.
4. Requires careful **He flow purity**; trace O₂/H₂O contamination skews results.
5. Not suitable for **highly microporous** materials (incorrect V_m extraction).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to **convert V from cm³ STP to per gram basis** before applying the BET equation. The BET y-axis requires V in cm³/g, not total cm³.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Using the wrong cross-sectional area for the adsorbate. For **N₂ at 77 K, use ω = 0.162 nm²**, not 0.354 nm² (which is Kr at 77 K).
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Reporting S_BET without stating the **adsorbate, temperature, and valid P/P₀ range** used. KTU examiners deduct for incomplete reporting.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–3 marks):** In derivations, skipping the **layer-by-layer geometric series** step. You must explicitly show the summation and use of the geometric series identity.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–2 marks):** Confusing **dynamic method P/P₀ control** — students often write P/P₀ = x_N2, but the correct relation includes the saturation pressure correction for gas-mixing.
> - **Common Mistake 6 (–1 mark):** Writing the BET equation with wrong variable — it is **P/[V(P₀ − P)]** vs **P/P₀**, not V vs P/P₀.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Static and Dynamic Methods**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Static method** = batch, equilibrium-based volumetric dosing; **Dynamic method** = continuous-flow carrier gas with TCD detection.
- ☐ **Static is the ISO 9277 reference** method; dynamic is for rapid QC.

### Key Equations
- ☐ **BET linear equation:** P/[V(P₀ − P)] = 1/(V_m C) + (C−1)/(V_m C) · (P/P₀)
- ☐ **V_m = 1 / (slope + intercept)** and **C = slope/intercept + 1**
- ☐ **S_BET = 4.35 × V_m / m_sample** (N₂ at 77 K, V_m in cm³ STP/g)
- ☐ **C = exp[(E₁ − E_L) / RT]** — high C → strong first-layer binding.
- ☐ **Valid P/P₀ range:** 0.05 to 0.30.

### Critical Numerical Values
- ☐ ω(N₂, 77 K) = **0.162 nm²**
- ☐ T = **77 K** (liquid-N₂ bath)
- ☐ V_mol(STP) = **22,414 cm³/mol**
- ☐ Single-point BET correction factor: V_m(true) = V_m(single) × (1 − 1/C)

### Experimental Know-How
- ☐ **Dead-volume correction** with He is essential in static method.
- ☐ Sample must be **degassed** at 300 °C, ≤ 10⁻³ Pa, 3 h before measurement.
- ☐ Dynamic method uses **TCD** to detect changes in gas thermal conductivity.
- ☐ **He is the carrier gas** (inert, high thermal conductivity contrast with N₂).

### Common Pitfalls
- ☐ Always report adsorbate, temperature, valid P/P₀ range alongside S_BET.
- ☐ Single-point dynamic method **overestimates** S_BET if C is small.
- ☐ BET equation **fails for microporous materials** — use t-plot or Dubinin-Radushkevich instead.
- ☐ Below P/P₀ ≈ 0.05 → micropore filling; Above 0.30 → capillary condensation in mesopores.

### Real-World Applications
- ☐ FCC catalysts, hydrocracking supports → mesoporous γ-Al₂O₃ (S_BET ≈ 150–300 m²/g).
- ☐ Drug-delivery carriers → MCM-41, SBA-15 (S_BET ≈ 600–1000 m²/g).
- ☐ Activated carbon → S_BET > 1500 m²/g (mostly microporous).
- ☐ PEMFC Pt/C electrocatalysts → carbon black supports (S_BET ≈ 200–1200 m²/g).

### KTU-Specific Tips
- ☐ In derivations, **always show the geometric series expansion** explicitly.
- ☐ In numericals, **show all BET y-axis calculations in a table** for clarity.
- ☐ For 14-mark questions, structure answer as **derivation + numerical** (each 7 marks).
- ☐ Mention **at least two practical differences** between static and dynamic methods in 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crystallography and Surface Analysis Techniques - XRD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crystallography and Surface Analysis Techniques - XRD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Crystallography** is the branch of applied mathematics and solid-state science concerned with the atomic-scale arrangement of atoms in crystalline solids, the geometry of crystal lattices, the symmetry operations that classify them, and the physical/chemical consequences of these periodic arrangements. In the context of heterogeneous catalysis, crystallography is the foundational discipline that governs how active metal sites, oxide supports, and zeolite frameworks are organized at the atomic level.

**X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)** is a non-destructive, bulk-averaged analytical technique that exploits the wave nature of X-rays and the periodic electron density of crystalline materials to (i) identify crystalline phases present in a catalyst, (ii) determine unit-cell parameters, (iii) estimate average crystallite/grain size, and (iv) quantify phase fractions in multi-phase catalytic systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** XRD is a *primary characterization tool* for heterogeneous catalysts. The board examiner typically expects students to link each XRD output parameter (d-spacing, crystallite size, phase ID) directly to a catalytic property (active site dispersion, support identification, sintering diagnosis).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Fingerprinting by X-ray Ruler"

Imagine a building whose façade is built from identical bricks in a perfectly repeating grid. If you shine a searchlight (X-ray beam) onto this façade, the reflected/scattered light will form a unique interference pattern depending on the brick size, brick spacing, and the way they are stacked. Two buildings with different brick patterns will produce visibly different reflection patterns.

- The **X-ray beam** = the searchlight (wavelength $\lambda \approx 0.5$–$2.5 \text{ Å}$, comparable to interatomic distances).
- The **crystal lattice** = the brick façade (interplanar spacing $d$).
- The **diffraction pattern** = the reflection/interference map = the **fingerprint** of the crystal.

This is the conceptual basis of XRD: every crystalline phase produces a unique "fingerprint" stored in the **ICDD PDF-4+ database** (formerly JCPDS), enabling unambiguous phase identification — a critical step in catalyst development.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard X-ray wavelength used in laboratory powder diffractometers:** Cu K$\alpha$ radiation with $\lambda = 1.5406 \text{ Å}$ (a weighted mean of Cu K$\alpha_1$ = 1.5406 Å and Cu K$\alpha_2$ = 1.5444 Å). For some specialized applications, Mo K$\alpha$ ($\lambda = 0.7107$ Å) or Co K$\alpha$ ($\lambda = 1.7890$ Å) targets are used.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crystal Systems and Bravais Lattices

There are **7 crystal systems** defined by the six lattice parameters $(a, b, c, \alpha, \beta, \gamma)$, and **14 Bravais lattices** representing the distinct ways points can be arranged in 3D space.

| Crystal System | Lattice Parameters | Examples in Catalysis |
|---|---|---|
| Cubic | $a = b = c$, $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ | Cu metal, $\gamma$-Al$_2$O$_3$, Faujasite (zeolite) |
| Tetragonal | $a = b \neq c$, $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ | ZrO$_2$ (tetragonal), TiO$_2$ (anatase) |
| Orthorhombic | $a \neq b \neq c$, $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ | $\alpha$-FeOOH (goethite), MgCO$_3$ |
| Hexagonal | $a = b \neq c$, $\alpha = \beta = 90°$, $\gamma = 120°$ | ZnO, MoS$_2$, Graphite |
| Trigonal (Rhombohedral) | $a = b = c$, $\alpha = \beta = \gamma \neq 90°$ | $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$ (corundum), Quartz |
| Monoclinic | $a \neq b \neq c$, $\alpha = \gamma = 90° \neq \beta$ | ZSM-5 (precursor), Monoclinic ZrO$_2$ |
| Triclinic | $a \neq b \neq c$, $\alpha \neq \beta \neq \gamma \neq 90°$ | CuSO$_4\cdot$5H$_2$O |

### 2.2 Miller Indices (h k l)

Miller indices are a notation system to uniquely identify crystallographic planes.

- **Intercepts** of the plane with the crystallographic axes $a, b, c$: $x, y, z$.
- Take **reciprocals**: $1/x, 1/y, 1/z$.
- Reduce to the **smallest set of integers with common factor cleared**: $(h, k, l)$.

**Example:** A plane intercepts $a, b, c$ at $1, 1, \infty$ → reciprocals $(1, 1, 0)$ → Miller index is $(1\ 1\ 0)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In FCC metals (like Pt, Pd, Au — common hydrogenation catalysts), the first allowed diffraction peak is **(1 1 1)**, not (1 0 0), because of structure-factor extinction rules.

### 2.3 Bragg's Law — The Heart of XRD

When X-rays strike a set of parallel atomic planes separated by distance $d_{hkl}$, the scattered waves interfere constructively only at specific angles governed by:

$$n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin \theta$$

where:
- $n$ = order of diffraction (integer, usually 1)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident X-ray (Å)
- $d_{hkl}$ = interplanar spacing of the $(h\ k\ l)$ planes (Å)
- $\theta$ = Bragg angle (half the diffraction angle $2\theta$)

**Constructive interference condition:** The path difference between waves reflected from successive planes must equal an integer number of wavelengths.

### 2.4 Interplanar Spacing for Cubic Systems

For a **cubic** crystal (a = b = c), the d-spacing is given by:

$$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

For **tetragonal** ($a = b \neq c$):

$$\frac{1}{d_{hkl}^2} = \frac{h^2 + k^2}{a^2} + \frac{l^2}{c^2}$$

For **hexagonal**:

$$\frac{1}{d_{hkl}^2} = \frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{h^2 + hk + k^2}{a^2}\right) + \frac{l^2}{c^2}$$

### 2.5 Scherrer Equation — Crystallite Size

The **Scherrer equation** relates the broadening of an XRD peak to the average crystallite size:

$$D = \frac{K \lambda}{\beta \cos \theta}$$

where:
- $D$ = average crystallite size (nm)
- $K$ = Scherrer constant (shape factor, typically **0.89** for spherical crystallites with cubic symmetry, 0.9 commonly used)
- $\lambda$ = X-ray wavelength (Å or nm)
- $\beta$ = full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the peak in **radians** (after instrumental broadening subtraction)
- $\theta$ = Bragg angle

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical catalyst insight:** Smaller crystallites ⇒ broader XRD peaks. For supported metal catalysts, a sharp narrow peak indicates large metal particles (poor dispersion, low active surface area), while broad humps indicate highly dispersed nanoparticles (high active surface area). This is the **operational definition of "dispersion"** in catalysis.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Bragg's Law — peak position | $\lambda, d$ in Å; $\theta$ in ° or rad |
| 2 | $d_{hkl} = a/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ | Cubic d-spacing | Å |
| 3 | $D = K\lambda/(\beta\cos\theta)$ | Crystallite size (Scherrer) | $D$ in nm |
| 4 | $N = (2d_{hkl}\sin\theta)/\lambda$ | Order of diffraction | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\beta_{\text{sample}}^2 = \beta_{\text{obs}}^2 - \beta_{\text{instr}}^2$ | Instrumental broadening correction | rad² |
| 6 | $\sin^2\theta = (\lambda^2/4a^2)(h^2+k^2+l^2)$ | Indexing cubic patterns (combined with Bragg) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{\text{cell}} = a^3$ (cubic) | Unit cell volume | Å³ |
| 8 | $\rho_{\text{theor}} = (Z \cdot M)/(N_A \cdot V_{\text{cell}})$ | Theoretical density for phase ID | g/cm³ |

> $Z$ = atoms per unit cell; $M$ = molar mass; $N_A$ = Avogadro's number.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Catalysis

1. **Phase identification:** Distinguishing $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$ (corundum, low surface area) from $\gamma$-Al$_2$O$_3$ (high surface area support) — a routine step before catalyst testing.
2. **Crystallite size determination:** Estimating Pt nanoparticle size in Pt/Al$_2$O$_3$ catalysts.
3. **Sintering studies:** Comparing XRD peak width before and after high-temperature catalyst treatment.
4. **Solid-state ion exchange verification:** Confirming the formation of new phases in zeolite or perovskite catalysts.
5. **Quantification of phase mixtures:** Using Rietveld refinement to determine weight fractions in mixed-oxide catalysts.
