# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define catalyst poisoning. Distinguish clearly between reversible and irreversible poisoning, citing one industrial example for each.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks — Total 60-80 words expected)

**Definition (1 mark):** Catalyst poisoning is the strong chemisorption of an impurity (the *poison*) onto the active sites of a catalyst, rendering those sites catalytically inactive. The activity is reduced according to $\phi = 1 - \theta_p$ for geometric blocking.

**Reversible (1 mark):** The poison adsorbs weakly enough to be desorbed by changing operating conditions (e.g., temperature, pressure, or atmosphere). *Example:* CO adsorbed on Cu catalyst at low temperature can be desorbed by heating or by switching to inert gas.

**Irreversible (1 mark):** The poison forms a strong, often covalent, bond or a new chemical compound that cannot be removed by mild regeneration. *Example:* H₂S reacting with Ni to form NiS, which is catalytically dead and cannot be regenerated without burning out the sulfur under aggressive conditions.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper]`

**Explain the concept of *selective poisoning*. How does it differ from non-selective poisoning, and why is it sometimes deliberately employed in industrial catalysis?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Selective poisoning is the preferential adsorption of a poison onto those active sites that catalyze the *undesired* (side or consecutive) reaction, while leaving the sites for the *desired* reaction relatively untouched.

**Difference (1 mark):** Non-selective poisoning blocks all active sites indiscriminately, lowering overall activity but not changing the *relative* rates. In selective poisoning, the selectivity $S = S_0 / (1 - \theta_p)$ is *improved* because the side-reaction rate is suppressed more than the main-reaction rate.

**Industrial Use (1 mark):** It is deliberately employed to boost selectivity. *Example:* Addition of trace Pb or Bi to Pt catalysts in selective hydrogenation of alkynes suppresses hydrogenolysis and over-hydrogenation, giving higher alkene yield.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the various mechanisms by which catalyst poisoning occurs. Use specific examples to illustrate site-blocking, electronic modification, and compound formation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrogenation catalyst suffers exponential deactivation. Its activity falls from 1.0 to 0.45 in 200 hours of operation. Calculate: **(i)** the deactivation rate constant $k_d$, **(ii)** the time required for the activity to drop to 0.10, and **(iii)** the half-life of the catalyst activity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Solution to (a) — Mechanisms of Poisoning (7 Marks)

**1. Site-Blocking (Geometric) Mechanism (2.5 marks)**
The poison adsorbs on the active site and physically prevents the reactant from accessing it. The electronic character of the site is unchanged, but the geometric availability is lost. The activity is reduced in direct proportion to the fraction of blocked sites:
$$\phi = 1 - \theta_p$$
*Example:* Sulfur atoms chemisorbed on a Ni(111) surface occupy 3-fold hollow sites, blocking the H₂ dissociation step required for hydrogenation.

**2. Electronic (Ligand) Modification (2.5 marks)**
The poison donates electron density to (or withdraws from) the metal's d-band, modifying the chemisorption properties of the *unpoisoned neighboring* sites. This can either inhibit or enhance particular reactions. Activation energy changes are diagnostic of this mechanism.
*Example:* CO adsorbed on Pt or Pd surfaces donates electron density to the metal, weakening alkene π-adsorption and suppressing hydrogenation rates. The effect extends to several atomic distances around the adsorbed CO.

**3. Compound Formation (True Chemical Reaction) (2 marks)**
The poison reacts with the active phase to form a new, catalytically inactive compound. This is the most destructive and is generally **irreversible**, often requiring complete catalyst replacement.
*Example:* $\text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{Ni} \rightarrow \text{NiS} + \text{H}_2$ — converts the catalytically active metallic nickel to inactive nickel sulfide. Similarly, $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Cu} \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2$ deactivates Cu catalysts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "CO is a poison for Pt" without specifying *which mechanism*. Always state **both** the type (reversible/irreversible) and the mechanism (blocking/electronic/compound) in your answer for full marks.

---

#### ✅ Solution to (b) — Numerical on Exponential Deactivation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\phi(0) = 1.0$ (fresh catalyst)
- $\phi(200) = 0.45$
- Model: $\phi(t) = \exp(-k_d t)$

**Part (i): Deactivation rate constant $k_d$ (3 marks)**

Apply the model at $t = 200$ h:
$$0.45 = \exp(-k_d \times 200)$$

Take natural log on both sides:
$$\ln(0.45) = -200 \, k_d$$
$$-0.7985 = -200 \, k_d$$
$$k_d = \frac{0.7985}{200} = 3.992 \times 10^{-3} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{k_d \approx 3.99 \times 10^{-3} \text{ h}^{-1}}$$

**Part (ii): Time for activity to drop to 0.10 (2 marks)**

$$0.10 = \exp(-3.992 \times 10^{-3} \times t)$$

$$\ln(0.10) = -3.992 \times 10^{-3} \times t$$
$$-2.3026 = -3.992 \times 10^{-3} \times t$$
$$t = \frac{2.3026}{3.992 \times 10^{-3}} = 576.8 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 577 \text{ hours}}$$

**Part (iii): Half-life of catalyst activity (2 marks)**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_d} = \frac{0.6931}{3.992 \times 10^{-3}} = 173.6 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} \approx 173.6 \text{ hours}}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Present a detailed classification of catalyst deactivation processes. For each type, give one industrial example and outline a suitable mitigation strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between catalyst activity $\phi$ and the fraction of poisoned sites $\theta_p$ for *uniform* poisoning. Then, applying this to a *parallel reaction* $A \to B$ (desired, $k_1$) and $A \to C$ (undesired, $k_2$), derive an expression for the new selectivity $S$ when a fraction $\theta_p$ of *type-2 sites* is selectively poisoned. Discuss how this principle is exploited industrially. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Solution to (a) — Classification of Deactivation (7 Marks)

| # | Type | Description (1 mark) | Industrial Example (0.5 mark) | Mitigation (0.5 mark) |
|:-:|:-----|:---------------------|:------------------------------|:----------------------|
| 1 | **Poisoning (Chemical)** | Strong chemisorption of impurity on active sites | H₂S on Ni in steam reforming | Pre-desulfurization of feed |
| 2 | **Chemical Degradation** | Loss of active phase via volatile compound formation | Formation of Ni(CO)₄ from Ni under CO pressure | Operate below CO pressure limit |
| 3 | **Fouling / Coking (Mechanical)** | Physical deposition of carbonaceous matter on/within pores | Coke on zeolite in fluid catalytic cracking | Regeneration by controlled coke burn-off in air |
| 4 | **Attrition (Mechanical)** | Physical breakdown of catalyst particles into fines | Catalyst loss in fluidized-bed reactors | Use attrition-resistant binders, control fluidization velocity |
| 5 | **Sintering (Thermal)** | Loss of active surface area via crystallite growth | Pt sintering in automotive catalysts | Thermal stabilizers, low-T operation, use of supports |
| 6 | **Phase Transformation (Thermal)** | Solid-state change of active phase to inactive phase | $\gamma$-Al₂O₃ → $\alpha$-Al₂O₃ above 800 °C | Operate below phase-transition temperature |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse *fouling* and *poisoning*. Fouling is a **physical** deposition (e.g., coke) that can usually be burned off. Poisoning is a **chemical** adsorption (e.g., sulfur) that often cannot be reversed. The mitigation strategies are completely different.

---

#### ✅ Solution to (b) — Derivation of Activity vs. $\theta_p$ and Selective Poisoning (7 Marks)

**Part 1: Derivation of $\phi = 1 - \theta_p$ (3 marks)**

Let $N_0$ be the total number of active sites per gram of fresh catalyst. After poisoning, $N_p = N_0 \theta_p$ sites are occupied and $N_{\text{free}} = N_0 (1 - \theta_p)$ are free.

The rate of reaction is proportional to the number of free sites (assuming uniform geometric blocking, electronic effects negligible):

$$r_0 = k \cdot N_0 \cdot f(C_A, T)$$
$$r_t = k \cdot N_0 (1 - \theta_p) \cdot f(C_A, T)$$

Activity is defined as the ratio:
$$\phi = \frac{r_t}{r_0} = \frac{k \cdot N_0 (1 - \theta_p) \cdot f(C_A, T)}{k \cdot N_0 \cdot f(C_A, T)} = 1 - \theta_p$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 1 - \theta_p}$$

**Part 2: Selectivity derivation for selective poisoning (3 marks)**

The parallel reaction scheme is:
- Desired: $A \xrightarrow{k_1, \text{site type 1}} B$
- Undesired: $A \xrightarrow{k_2, \text{site type 2}} C$

The initial selectivity is $S_0 = \frac{k_1 N_1}{k_2 N_2}$.

After selective poisoning (a fraction $\theta_p$ of type-2 sites are blocked; type-1 sites are unaffected):

Rate of B: $r_B = k_1 N_1 f(C_A, T)$ — *unchanged*

Rate of C: $r_C = k_2 N_2 (1 - \theta_p) f(C_A, T)$ — *reduced*

New selectivity:
$$S = \frac{r_B}{r_C} = \frac{k_1 N_1 f(C_A, T)}{k_2 N_2 (1 - \theta_p) f(C_A, T)} = \frac{S_0}{1 - \theta_p}$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{S_0}{1 - \theta_p}}$$

**Part 3: Industrial exploitation (1 mark)**

In industrial practice, a small, carefully controlled amount of a selective poison is introduced into the feed to *boost* the selectivity of the desired reaction. As the formula shows, when $\theta_p \to 1$ (all bad sites blocked), $S \to \infty$. The trade-off is that the *overall activity* drops, so the optimum $\theta_p$ is chosen to balance yield and throughput.

*Example:* The addition of trace quantities of **Pb or Bi** to supported **Pt catalysts** in the selective hydrogenation of alkynes (e.g., phenylacetylene to styrene) suppresses the *hydrogenolysis* and *over-hydrogenation* pathways, dramatically increasing alkene selectivity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing "inhibitor" with "poison"** — An inhibitor is mild and reversible; a poison is strong and often irreversible. Marks lost if you use the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the units of $k_d$** — Always state the units of the deactivation rate constant (typically h⁻¹ for first-order). Marks are deducted if missing.
> 3. **Writing "S = S₀ (1 − θ_p)"** — This is a *very common* student error. The *correct* expression is $S = S_0 / (1 - \theta_p)$ — they are not the same. The denominator goes in the *denominator*.
> 4. **Skipping the natural-log step** — In numericals, the examiner *must* see the explicit $\ln(\cdot)$ step. Jumping directly to the answer loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the assumption of uniform poisoning** — The relation $\phi = 1 - \theta_p$ only holds for *uniform geometric* poisoning. For pore-mouth poisoning, $\phi = \exp(-k_d t)$ is the correct form. Misapplying one to the other is a frequent mistake.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **READ THIS TWICE BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL**

### **Core Definitions**
- ☐ Catalyst deactivation = loss of activity and/or selectivity with time on stream.
- ☐ Catalyst poisoning = *strong chemisorption* of an impurity on active sites.
- ☐ Activity $\phi = r_t / r_0$; when $\phi \to 0$, catalyst is dead.

### **Master Classification (Six Types)**
- ☐ Chemical: Poisoning, Chemical Degradation (e.g., volatilization)
- ☐ Mechanical: Fouling/Coking, Attrition/Erosion
- ☐ Thermal: Sintering, Phase Transformation
- ☐ Corrosive: Feedstream impurity attack

### **Four Sub-Types of Poisoning**
- ☐ **Reversible** vs. **Irreversible** (regenerable vs. not)
- ☐ **Selective** vs. **Non-selective** (changes S vs. only changes $\phi$)
- ☐ **Uniform** vs. **Pore-mouth** (linear vs. exponential decay)
- ☐ **Transient** vs. **Permanent** (steady-state vs. monotonic)

### **Three Poisoning Mechanisms**
- ☐ **Site-blocking (geometric)**: e.g., S on Ni
- ☐ **Electronic (ligand) modification**: e.g., CO on Pt
- ☐ **Compound formation**: e.g., H₂S + Ni → NiS

### **Must-Memorize Equations (10 Formulas)**
- ☐ $\phi = r_t / r_0$
- ☐ $\phi = 1 - \theta_p$ (uniform)
- ☐ $\theta_p = K_p C_p / (1 + K_p C_p)$ (Langmuir)
- ☐ $\phi(t) = 1 - k_d t$ (linear)
- ☐ $\phi(t) = \exp(-k_d t)$ (exponential / pore-mouth)
- ☐ $\phi(t) = 1/(1 + k_d t)^n$ (power-law)
- ☐ $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_d$
- ☐ $S = S_0 / (1 - \theta_p)$ (selective poisoning, parallel)
- ☐ $\alpha = -(1/\phi)(d\phi/dt)$ (deactivation rate)
- ☐ $\text{DISP} = 1/\text{TOF}$

### **Industrial Examples (Recall Cold)**
- ☐ H₂S poisons Ni (steam reforming, hydrogenation)
- ☐ CO reversibly poisons Cu (methanol synthesis, WGS)
- ☐ Pb irreversibly poisons Pt (automotive)
- ☐ Pb/Bi selectively poisons Pt (alkyne hydrogenation → alkene)
- ☐ N-bases poison acid zeolites (FCC)
- ☐ Sulfur poisons reforming Pt/Al₂O₃

### **Numerical Skills Checklist**
- ☐ Can I derive $\phi = 1 - \theta_p$ from first principles?
- ☐ Can I derive $\phi(t) = \exp(-k_d t)$ from a first-order ODE?
- ☐ Can I compute $k_d$ given $\phi$ at two time points?
- ☐ Can I compute $t_{1/2}$ from $k_d$ and vice versa?
- ☐ Can I apply $S = S_0/(1 - \theta_p)$ to a parallel-reaction problem?
- ☐ Can I explain why pore-mouth poisoning gives exponential decay even though local mechanism is uniform?

### **Examiner Mindset Reminders**
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** (uniform vs. pore-mouth, geometric vs. electronic).
- ☐ Always show the **natural log step** in numericals.
- ☐ Always cite **at least one industrial example** for full marks in theory questions.
- ☐ Always include **units** for rate constants and selectivity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisoning of Metallic Catalysts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Poisoning of Metallic Catalysts # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of heterogeneous catalysis, **catalyst poisoning** is defined as the **strong, often irreversible chemisorption of impurity species (poisons) onto the active sites of a metallic catalyst**, thereby reducing the number of available sites for the principal reactants and causing a progressive decline in catalytic activity, selectivity, or both. For metallic catalysts (e.g., Ni, Pt, Pd, Cu, Fe, Ag), poisoning is predominantly a **surface phenomenon** because the catalytic action is confined to surface metal atoms (the so-called *active centres*). A *poison* is, by IUPAC convention, a substance that reduces the activity of a catalyst. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically categorises poisoning under the broader umbrella of **catalyst deactivation**, alongside **sintering, coking (fouling), and thermal degradation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** > Poisoning is treated as a *chemical deactivation mode* that is distinct from thermal (sintering) and mechanical (attrition) modes. The classification — *reversible vs. irreversible* and *selective vs. non-selective* — is **high-weightage** for ESE questions. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **busy restaurant kitchen** with five stoves (active sites) and five chefs. Under normal operation, all five stoves cook food for customers (reactants). Now suppose a **rival restaurant owner sneaks in and parks a heavy, immovable delivery box on one of the stoves** — that stove is now useless for cooking. Worse, if the box is *welded* to the stove, even the chef cannot remove it (irreversible poisoning). If it can be lifted off, the stove becomes available again (reversible poisoning). In catalysis, the **rival owner = the poison** (commonly a sulfur, phosphorus, or heavy-metal compound), the **stove = an active metal site**, and the **food = the reactant molecules**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The poison does not necessarily have to be present in large quantities. Even **trace levels (ppb–ppm) of sulfur compounds** can halve the activity of supported Pt or Ni catalysts — a critical concern in petroleum refining and ammonia synthesis. ## 1.3 General Characteristics of a Poison - **Strong chemisorption** on the active site (chemisorption enthalpy typically **> 200 kJ/mol**). - Forms a **strong covalent or ionic bond** with the surface metal atoms. - Usually involves species with **lone pairs of electrons** (H₂S, NH₃, CO, phosphines) or **partially filled d-orbitals** (heavy metal ions like Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺). - Often **electronegative elements** (S, N, P, halogens) compared to the metal. The strength of poisoning is conventionally quantified using the **relative toxicity** or **poisoning coefficient**, $\alpha_p$, defined in Section 2. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Catalyst Poisoning The poisoning of metallic catalysts is classified along **two orthogonal axes**: ### 2.1.1 Axis 1: Reversibility - **Reversible (Temporary) Poisoning** - The poison is **weakly chemisorbed** or **physisorbed** on the active site. - Original activity can be **regenerated** by simple treatments: purging with inert gas, raising temperature, or feeding a competitive adsorbate. - **Examples:** CO poisoning of Pt at low temperatures; NH₃ poisoning of Cu in methanol synthesis. - Industrial term: *deactivation by reversible adsorption*. - **Irreversible (Permanent) Poisoning** - The poison forms a **strong, often stoichiometric, chemical bond** (covalent, ionic, or alloy-type) with the surface metal atoms. - Activity **cannot be economically restored**; the catalyst must be replaced or undergo harsh regeneration. - **Examples:** H₂S poisoning of Ni (forms surface NiS), Pb poisoning of Pt (forms Pt-Pb alloy), As poisoning of Cu. ### 2.1.2 Axis 2: Selectivity of Action - **Non-Selective (Uniform) Poisoning** - The poison **adsorbs uniformly** on all types of active sites. - Activity decreases for **all reactions** occurring on the catalyst. **No change in selectivity** of the desired product. - Most common mode for H₂S on Pt/Ni catalysts. - **Selective Poisoning** - The poison **preferentially blocks a specific type of active site**, often a step, kink, or terrace edge. - The desired reaction (which may occur on a *different* site type) is **less affected**, while the side reaction is **suppressed**. - This is sometimes exploited **intentionally** to improve selectivity — these are called *catalyst modifiers* or *selectivity enhancers*. - **Example:** Partial sulfur poisoning of Pt in petroleum reforming selectively poisons hydrogenolysis sites (which favour C–C bond breaking) while leaving the isomerisation and dehydrogenation sites relatively active. This is why Cl/S-modified Pt/Al₂O₃ is the industrial standard. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Pearl:** Selective poisoning is the *only* form of deactivation that can **increase product selectivity**. Memorise the Pt-reforming example — it is a favourite 7-mark question in KTU ESE. ## 2.2 Mechanism of Poisoning (Step-by-Step) For a metallic surface with active site concentration $C_t$ (mol sites per kg catalyst), the poisoning sequence is: 1. **Transport of the poison** from the bulk fluid phase to the external surface of the catalyst pellet (external mass transfer). 2. **Pore diffusion** of the poison into the internal porous structure. 3. **Chemisorption** of the poison on an active metal site: $$ \text{M}_{(s)} + \text{P}_{(g)} \rightleftharpoons \text{M--P}_{(s)} $$ with an equilibrium (or Langmuir) constant $K_p$. 4. **Site blocking**: The covered site M–P can no longer adsorb the reactant. If the coverage by poison is $\theta_p$, the fraction of *uncovered* active sites is $(1 - \theta_p)$, and the apparent rate constant becomes: $$ k_{\text{app}} = k_0 \, (1 - \theta_p) $$ 5. For a strong poison, $\theta_p \to 1$ at very low gas-phase concentrations, which explains the **dramatic effect of trace poisons**. ## 2.3 Common Poisons for Metallic Catalysts | Poison | Typical Source | Affected Metals | Mode | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **H₂S, SO₂, COS, CS₂** | Sulfur in fossil feedstocks | Ni, Pt, Pd, Cu, Ag | Strongly irreversible (forms metal sulfide) | | **NH₃, amines** | Nitrogen impurities | Pt, Ni, Cu | Reversible at low T | | **CO** | Incomplete combustion / syngas | Pt, Pd, Cu | Reversible at high T | | **P, PH₃, PCl₃** | Phosphorus compounds | Ni, Pt, Rh, Pd | Irreversible (forms metal phosphide) | | **As, AsH₃** | Arsenic compounds | Pt, Cu, Ni | Highly irreversible | | **Hg, Pb, Cd, Zn, Bi** | Heavy metals | Pt, Pd, Cu, Ag | Irreversible (alloy/surface compound) | | **O₂, H₂O (excess)** | Over-oxidation | Fe, Cu, Ag | Reversible (forms surface oxide) | | **Halides (Cl⁻, F⁻)** | Refinery contaminants | Pt, Ni | Mostly reversible at moderate T | | **Coke (carbonaceous deposits)** | Hydrocarbon side reactions | Ni, Pt, Pd | Coking — covered separately in Module 3 | > [!WARNING] > **Note on Coke:** Although coke deposition is a form of deactivation, the KTU 2024 syllabus treats **coking/fouling** as a **distinct module** from poisoning. Examiners will deduct marks if you confuse the two in ESE answers. ## 2.4 Industrial Examples of Poisoning 1. **Ammonia Synthesis (Haber–Bosch):** Fe catalyst is poisoned by O₂, H₂O, CO, CO₂, and sulfur compounds. The syngas feed is therefore purified to **< 0.1 ppm H₂S** before contacting the catalyst. 2. **Catalytic Reforming (Pt/Al₂O₃):** Sulfur permanently deactivates the metallic function. Pt/Cl/Al₂O₃ catalysts can tolerate only **< 10 ppb S** in the feed. 3. **Steam Reforming of CH₄ (Ni/Al₂O₃):** Sulfur compounds are the *primary* poison. Even **0.5 ppm H₂S** reduces activity by 50%. 4. **Methanol Synthesis (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃):** Sensitive to S, Cl, and Fe carbonyls. 5. **Automotive Three-Way Catalyst (Pt/Rh/Pd):** Lead (from leaded petrol) and S cause permanent damage — one of the major historical motivations for unleaded fuel. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table summarises the **must-know equations** for ESE questions on this topic. | # | Equation / Symbol | Meaning | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $a = 1 - \theta_p$ | Fractional activity remaining | Dimensionless, $0 \le a \le 1$ | | 2 | $\theta_p = \dfrac{K_p \, C_p}{1 + K_p \, C_p}$ | Langmuir isotherm for poison | $K_p$ in m³/mol, $C_p$ in mol/m³ | | 3 | $\dfrac{da}{dC_p} = -\alpha_p \, a$ | Differential activity decay | $\alpha_p$ is *relative toxicity* (m³/mol) | | 4 | $a = e^{-\alpha_p \, C_p}$ | Integrated form (for strong poison, low coverage) | Linear in $\ln a$ vs $C_p$ | | 5 | $r = \dfrac{k \, K_A \, P_A}{1 + K_A \, P_A + K_P \, P_P}$ | Rate with poison term in denominator | Site balance on metallic surface | | 6 | $S_{ij} = \dfrac{k_i}{k_j} \cdot \dfrac{(1-\theta_p)^{n_i}}{(1-\theta_p)^{n_j}}$ | Selectivity ratio after poisoning | $n_i$ = kinetic order in sites for reaction $i$ | | 7 | $D_{\text{eff}} = D_b \, \dfrac{\varepsilon_p}{\tau}$ | Effective pore diffusivity | $\tau$ = tortuosity (typically 2–6) | | 8 | $\Phi = L \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Thiele modulus (intrinsic) | Used when poison affects internal sites | | 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh \Phi}{\Phi}$ | Effectiveness factor | For first-order reaction in slab geometry | | 10 | $t_{0.5} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\alpha_p \, C_{p,\text{feed}}}$ | Half-life of catalyst under poisoning | Useful for reactor design / cycle time | | 11 | $\Delta G_{\text{ads}}^{\circ}$ (poison) $< -200$ kJ/mol | Threshold for "irreversible" | Heuristic only — depends on $T$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Table Delimiters:** In the LaTeX body of the answer (not the markdown table), use `\vert` or `\mid` to denote **absolute value** or **conditional probability** — for example, write $\vert \theta_p \vert$ or $\mid a \mid$, not $\vert \theta_p \vert$ with raw pipe characters, to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. ## 2.6 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice Understanding poisoning is **non-negotiable** for: - **Feedstock purification design** (amine scrubbers, ZnO or activated carbon guard beds for sulfur removal upstream of reactors). - **Reactor cycle-time estimation** (when to switch or regenerate catalyst beds). - **Economics of catalyst replacement** in petroleum refining, ammonia, methanol, and hydrogen plants. - **Environmental catalysis** (sulfur tolerance limits of automotive catalysts after fuel-quality regulations). - **Process safety** — a poisoned catalyst can cause runaway exotherms when activity is suddenly restored. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides the **rigorous derivations** that the KTU examiner expects for full marks on numerical or theoretical 7/14-mark sub-parts. ## 3.1 Derivation: Activity Decay Under Uniform Poisoning (First-Order in Activity) Let $a$ be the *fractional activity* of the catalyst at cumulative poison exposure $C_p$ (mol poison fed per mol of active sites). **Step 1 — Site balance:** The number of unpoisoned active sites at any instant is proportional to $a$. The rate of loss of activity is proportional to the *unpoisoned* sites available to receive the poison: $$ \frac{da}{dC_p} = -\alpha_p \, a $$ **Reasoning:** The negative sign indicates activity *decreases* with exposure. The constant $\alpha_p$ is the *relative toxicity* — larger $\alpha_p$ means each unit of poison kills more sites (a "stronger" poison). **Step 2 — Separation of variables:** $$ \frac{da}{a} = -\alpha_p \, dC_p $$ **Step 3 — Integration from initial activity $a_0 = 1$ at $C_p = 0$ to activity $a$ at exposure $C_p$:** $$ \int_{1}^{a} \frac{da}{a} = -\alpha_p \int_{0}^{C_p} dC_p $$ **Step 4 — Evaluation:** $$ \ln a = -\alpha_p \, C_p $$ **Step 5 — Final closed-form expression:** $$ \boxed{\,a = e^{-\alpha_p \, C_p}\,} $$ This is **Equation 4** in the cheat sheet. The result is fundamental — it tells us that activity decays *exponentially* with poison exposure for a uniform, non-selective poison, and that the **slope of a $\ln a$ vs $C_p$ plot equals $-\alpha_p$**, a directly measurable quantity. ## 3.2 Derivation: Langmuir-Type Isotherm for the Poison (Steady-State Site Coverage) For a poison P competing with reactant A for the same surface, the site balance is: $$ \theta_A + \theta_P + \theta_V = 1 $$ where $\theta_V$ is the *vacant* site fraction. **Step 1 — Langmuir adsorption constants:** $$ \theta_A = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_P P_P}, \quad \theta_P = \frac{K_P P_P}{1 + K_A P_A + K_P P_P} $$ **Step 2 — Rate of the principal reaction (assumed first-order in $\theta_A$):** $$ r = k \, \theta_A = \frac{k \, K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_P P_P} $$ **Step 3 — Limiting cases:** - **No poison** ($P_P \to 0$): denominator reduces to $(1 + K_A P_A)$, giving the classical Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate. At *low* $P_A$ (so $K_A P_A \ll 1$), $r \to k K_A P_A$, a clean first-order rate. - **Strong poison** ($K_P P_P \gg 1 + K_A P_A$): $r \to \dfrac{k K_A P_A}{K_P P_P}$, i.e., the rate becomes **inversely proportional to the poison partial pressure**. This explains why even trace poisons ($P_P$ very small) cause *order-of-magnitude* rate drops when $K_P$ is huge. **Step 4 — Apparent activity:** $$ a = \frac{r_{\text{poisoned}}}{r_{\text{unpoisoned}}} = \frac{1 + K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_P P_P} $$ This is the Langmuir basis of poisoning — the poison term simply **adds to the denominator** and divides the rate. ## 3.3 Derivation: Selectivity Change Under Selective Poisoning Consider two parallel reactions occurring on the *same* surface, with intrinsic rate constants $k_1$ and $k_2$, and kinetic orders in active sites $n_1$ and $n_2$ (often $n_1 = n_2 = 1$ for structure-insensitive reactions, but $n_1 \neq n_2$ for structure-sensitive ones). **Step 1 — Intrinsic selectivity (no poison):** $$ S_{12}^{\circ} = \frac{r_1}{r_2} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} $$ **Step 2 — After poisoning (fractional uncovered sites $= 1 - \theta_p$):** Assuming the rate scales as $(1 - \theta_p)^n$: $$ r_1 = k_1 (1 - \theta_p)^{n_1}, \quad r_2 = k_2 (1 - \theta_p)^{n_2} $$ **Step 3 — New selectivity:** $$ S_{12} = \frac{k_1 (1 - \theta_p)^{n_1}}{k_2 (1 - \theta_p)^{n_2}} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} (1 - \theta_p)^{(n_1 - n_2)} $$ **Step 4 — Final boxed result:** $$ \boxed{\,S_{12} = S_{12}^{\circ} \cdot (1 - \theta_p)^{(n_1 - n_2)}\;} $$ **Interpretation:** - If $n_1 > n_2$: poison *decreases* $S_{12}$ (the high-order reaction is suppressed more). - If $n_1 < n_2$: poison *increases* $S_{12}$ (this is the basis of *intentional* selective poisoning for selectivity gain). - If $n_1 = n_2$: selectivity is **unchanged** by any amount of poison — only activity changes (uniform poisoning case). ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Past-Year KTU Pattern) **Problem (modeled on a 7-mark sub-part):** A Pt/Al₂O₃ reforming catalyst initially converts 90 % of n-hexane at 773 K. After exposure to 0.20 mmol of an H₂S-containing impurity per gram of catalyst, conversion drops to 60 %. Assuming uniform, non-selective poisoning with first-order decay in activity, calculate: (a) The relative toxicity $\alpha_p$ of H₂S for this catalyst. (b) The cumulative poison exposure at which conversion will fall to 30 %. **Solution:** Let $C_p$ be cumulative exposure in mmol poison per g catalyst. The fractional activity is $a = X / X_0$, where $X$ is conversion. **Part (a):** $$ a_1 = \frac{0.60}{0.90} = \frac{2}{3} $$ Using $a = e^{-\alpha_p C_p}$: $$ \frac{2}{3} = e^{-\alpha_p (0.20)} $$ Taking natural log on both sides: $$ \ln\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) = -\alpha_p (0.20) $$ $$ \alpha_p = \frac{-\ln(2/3)}{0.20} = \frac{0.4055}{0.20} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\alpha_p = 2.027 \text{ (mmol/g)}^{-1}\;} $$ **Part (b):** For $a_2 = 30 / 90 = 1/3$: $$ \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\alpha_p \, C_{p,2} $$ $$ C_{p,2} = \frac{-\ln(1/3)}{2.027} = \frac{1.0986}{2.027} $$ $$ \boxed{\,C_{p,2} \approx 0.542 \text{ mmol/g}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Validation:** Notice that $C_{p,2} \approx 2.71 \times C_{p,1}$ for activity to drop from 2/3 to 1/3. This is consistent with the exponential decay: halving the activity requires a **fixed** increment in $C_p$ equal to $\ln 2 / \alpha_p = 0.342$ mmol/g. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mechanism Flow Diagram — Poisoning of a Metallic Catalyst The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **complete pathway by which a poison species deactivates a metallic catalyst**, from bulk fluid to the active site. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Bulk Fluid Phase
Reactant A and Poison P] --> B[External Film
External Mass Transfer] B --> C[Pore Diffusion
Internal Surface] C --> D{Active Metal Site
M on support} D --> E[Physisorption
Weak, reversible] D --> F[Chemisorption
Strong M-P bond] F --> G[Site Blocked
Activity Loss] F --> H[Surface Compound
e.g., NiS, PtAs] H --> I[Irreversible Deactivation] G --> J[Reversible Deactivation] J --> K[Regeneration:
Heat, Oxidative Purge] I --> L[Catalyst Replacement] K --> M[Restored Activity] L --> M M --> N[Reactor Continues Operation] E --> K style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#8B0000,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF style M fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Classification Block — Modes of Poisoning This second Mermaid diagram maps the **classification axes and consequences** in a single visual framework, useful for writing 3-mark or 7-mark introductory answers. ```mermaid flowchart LR Root[Poisoning of Metallic Catalysts] --> R1[Reversible] Root --> R2[Irreversible] Root --> S1[Selective] Root --> S2[Non-Selective] R1 --> R1a[CO on Pt at low T] R1 --> R1b[NH3 on Cu] R2 --> R2a[H2S on Ni and Pt] R2 --> R2b[Pb, Hg, As on Pt] S1 --> S1a[S on Pt reforming
Selectivity GAIN] S2 --> S2a[Uniform Activity Loss
No Selectivity Change] style Root fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF style S1a fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Activity–Time / Activity–Exposure Curve (Schematic) The block diagram below shows how activity typically declines over cumulative poison exposure for the four combinations of the two classification axes. This is the **"poisoning fingerprint"** most often asked in ESE. ```mermaid flowchart LR X[Cumulative Poison Exposure, Cp] --> Y1[Linear Decline
Mild uniform poison] X --> Y2[Exponential Decline
Strong uniform poison] X --> Y3[Concave Decline
Strong selective poison, easy sites first] X --> Y4[Step Decline
Threshold-type poison] Y1 --> Z1[Activity a] Y2 --> Z1 Y3 --> Z1 Y4 --> Z1 style Y1 fill:#87CEEB style Y2 fill:#FF6347 style Y3 fill:#FFD700 style Y4 fill:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF ``` **How to read the curves:** - **Linear** (mild reversible poison at very low $\alpha_p$): approximate Taylor expansion of $e^{-\alpha_p C_p}$ for small $\alpha_p C_p$. - **Exponential** (the classical uniform irreversible case — derived in Section 3.1). - **Concave** (selective poison: easily poisoned terrace sites are killed first, leaving resistant kink/edge sites). - **Step** (threshold-type poison: a critical poison coverage must be exceeded before a phase change like sulfide formation occurs; below the threshold, activity is essentially intact). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Distribution Convention (KTU 2024 ESE):** > - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (answer in ~5–6 lines each). > - **Part B:** Either-Or choice × 1 question × 14 marks = 14 marks (with sub-parts a and b, 7 marks each). > - Total for this module question in ESE = 14 marks. > - Cognitive levels per KTU 2024: **L1 (Remember)**, **L2 (Understand)**, **L3 (Apply)**, **L4 (Analyse)** — most module-3 questions target **L2–L3**. --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Define catalyst poisoning. Distinguish between *reversible* and *irreversible* poisoning of metallic catalysts with one industrial example of each.** **Model Answer (5–6 lines):** Catalyst poisoning is the strong chemisorption of impurity species on the active sites of a metallic catalyst, which reduces the number of available sites and causes a decline in catalytic activity, selectivity, or both. **Reversible poisoning** is one in which the poison is weakly bound to the active site and the original activity can be restored by mild treatments such as inert purge or temperature increase; *example:* CO poisoning of Pt at low temperatures. **Irreversible poisoning** involves a strong chemical bond (covalent, ionic, or alloy-type) between the poison and the metal, and the activity cannot be economically regenerated; *example:* H₂S poisoning of Ni forming surface NiS in steam reforming. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] **Explain *selective poisoning* and state how it can improve the selectivity of a desired reaction on a metallic catalyst. Give one industrial example.** **Model Answer (5–6 lines):** Selective poisoning is the preferential adsorption of a poison on a *specific type* of active site (e.g., terrace vs. step sites), thereby suppressing a *particular* reaction while leaving other reactions relatively unaffected. It can **improve selectivity** when the desired reaction occurs on sites that are *resistant* to poisoning, while the side reaction (e.g., hydrogenolysis or coke precursor formation) is suppressed because it requires the poisoned site type. **Industrial example:** Sulfur-modified Pt/Al₂O₃ in catalytic reforming — partial sulfur coverage selectively blocks hydrogenolysis sites that catalyse C–C bond breaking, while leaving the dehydrogenation and isomerisation sites that produce high-octane aromatics relatively active, *increasing* the yield of aromatics. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** *Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.* ### ▸ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Adapted]** **(a)** Classify the poisoning of metallic catalysts along two axes: (i) reversibility and (ii) selectivity. For each class, give **one** example and write the corresponding **rate equation** showing how the poison affects the rate. *(7 marks)* **(b)** For a Pt/Al₂O₃ reforming catalyst, the activity decays exponentially with cumulative sulfur exposure according to $a = e^{-\alpha_p C_p}$. The catalyst's activity drops from 1.0 to 0.4 after exposure to 0.30 mmol S per gram catalyst. Calculate: (i) the relative toxicity $\alpha_p$ of sulfur, (ii) the exposure $C_p$ at which the activity will fall to 0.2, and (iii) the half-life exposure $C_{p,0.5}$. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution — Question A:** **(a) Classification and Rate Equations:** | Class | Sub-class | Example | Rate Equation Form | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Reversibility | Reversible | CO on Pt (low T) | $r = k K_A P_A / (1 + K_A P_A + K_P P_P)$ with small $K_P$ | | Reversibility | Irreversible | H₂S on Ni | $r = k_0 (1 - \theta_p)$, $\theta_p \to 1$ permanently | | Selectivity | Selective | S on Pt reforming | $S_{12} = (k_1 / k_2) (1 - \theta_p)^{(n_1 - n_2)}$ | | Selectivity | Non-selective | H₂S on Cu methanol cat. | $r = r_0 (1 - \theta_p)$, no selectivity change | Each class is shown with a distinct rate form; for the **selective** case, the exponent $n_1 - n_2$ determines whether the selectivity increases or decreases. (Full 7 marks: 1 mark for each class + 1 mark for example + 1 mark for equation logic.) **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Part (i):** Use $a = e^{-\alpha_p C_p}$ with $a = 0.4$ at $C_p = 0.30$ mmol/g: $$ \ln(0.4) = -\alpha_p (0.30) $$ $$ -0.9163 = -\alpha_p (0.30) $$ $$ \boxed{\,\alpha_p = 3.054 \text{ (mmol/g)}^{-1}\;} $$ **Part (ii):** For $a = 0.2$: $$ \ln(0.2) = -\alpha_p \, C_{p,2} $$ $$ -1.6094 = -3.054 \times C_{p,2} $$ $$ C_{p,2} = \frac{1.6094}{3.054} \approx 0.527 \text{ mmol/g} $$ $$ \boxed{\,C_{p,2} \approx 0.527 \text{ mmol/g}\;} $$ **Part (iii):** For $a = 0.5$: $$ \ln(0.5) = -\alpha_p \, C_{p,0.5} $$ $$ C_{p,0.5} = \frac{0.6931}{3.054} \approx 0.227 \text{ mmol/g} $$ $$ \boxed{\,C_{p,0.5} \approx 0.227 \text{ mmol/g}\;} $$ (For full 7 marks: 2 marks each for (i) and (ii), 1 mark for formula in (iii), 2 marks for evaluation in (iii).) --- ### ▸ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style — Adapted]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the mechanism of catalyst poisoning on a metallic surface. With the help of a **Langmuir-based rate expression**, show how the presence of a strongly adsorbed poison affects: (i) the rate of a first-order reaction $A \to B$ and (ii) the apparent activation energy of the reaction. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A steam-reforming Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst operates in a feed containing 0.5 ppm H₂S by volume. The relative toxicity of H₂S at 1073 K is $\alpha_p = 8.0 \times 10^3$ m³/mol. (i) Calculate the **half-life** of the catalyst in hours if the feed rate is $1.0 \times 10^4$ mol CH₄/h (use H₂S:CH₄ molar ratio = 0.5 × 10⁻⁶). (ii) Comment on the **industrial implications** and one design strategy to extend catalyst life. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution — Question B:** **(a) Mechanism and Langmuir Rate Analysis:** The poisoning of a metallic surface proceeds through the following steps (as in the flowchart in Section 4.1): 1. **Bulk diffusion** of poison to the external catalyst surface. 2. **Pore diffusion** into the porous pellet interior. 3. **Chemisorption** on the active metal site via the formation of a covalent or ionic bond. 4. **Site blocking**, which reduces the fraction of unpoisoned sites to $(1 - \theta_p)$. For a first-order reaction $A \to B$ on the metal surface, the Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate in the presence of a poison is: $$ r = \frac{k \, K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_P P_P} $$ **(i) Effect on rate:** The denominator increases by the term $K_P P_P$. For a strong poison, $K_P P_P \gg (1 + K_A P_A)$, so: $$ r \to \frac{k \, K_A P_A}{K_P P_P} \propto \frac{1}{P_P} $$ Hence the rate becomes **inversely proportional** to the poison partial pressure. **(ii) Effect on apparent activation energy:** Although the intrinsic $k$ follows Arrhenius behaviour with $E_a$, the presence of $K_P(T)$ in the denominator — which has its **own** temperature dependence (heat of poison adsorption, $-\Delta H_{\text{ads},P}$) — makes the apparent activation energy: $$ E_{a,\text{app}} = E_a + (-\Delta H_{\text{ads},A}) - (-\Delta H_{\text{ads},P}) $$ For a strongly adsorbed exothermic poison ($-\Delta H_{\text{ads},P}$ large positive), the **apparent activation energy increases** with poisoning — the reaction appears to "slow down" because the energy barrier has effectively risen. This is a classic signature of strong poison chemisorption. (Full 7 marks: 2 for mechanism steps, 2 for the Langmuir equation derivation, 1.5 for (i), 1.5 for (ii).) **(b) Numerical and Design Solution:** **Part (i) — Half-life calculation:** Molar feed rate of H₂S: $$ F_{H_2S} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.0 \times 10^4 = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/h} $$ The half-life exposure in mol poison per mol active sites: $$ t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\alpha_p \, (P_{H_2S} / P_{\text{total}})} \quad \text{(in time units)} $$ Assuming ideal gas and feed $P \approx 1$ atm so $P_{H_2S}/P = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$: $$ t_{0.5} = \frac{0.6931}{8.0 \times 10^3 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.6931}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} $$ $$ t_{0.5} \approx 173.3 \text{ time units (per-unit exposure basis)} $$ In terms of operating time, with $C_{p,\text{feed}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/h per unit active-site loading and assuming 1 mol active site per kg catalyst for simplicity: $$ \boxed{\,t_{0.5} \approx 173 \text{ h} \approx 7.2 \text{ days}\,} $$ (Exact numerical credit depends on the assumed catalyst loading; the formula chain and order-of-magnitude estimate is the key. 4 marks for formula chain, 2 marks for final value, 1 mark for unit.) **Part (ii) — Industrial Implications and Design Strategy:** A half-life of only ~7 days is **catastrophically short** for a commercial reformer (typical industrial target: 1–5 years). This shows that: - Even **trace sulfur** in natural gas / naphtha feed is a **showstopper** for nickel-based reforming catalysts. - Feedstock must be desulfurised to **< 0.1 ppm S** upstream of the reformer. **Design strategy:** Install a **ZnO guard bed** (or activated carbon / activated Ni-Mo adsorber) immediately upstream of the reformer. ZnO reacts with H₂S to form ZnS (highly stable, $\Delta G_f^\circ$ very negative) and reduces the H₂S concentration by 2–4 orders of magnitude, extending catalyst life from days to years. Alternatively, a **Co-Mo or Ni-Mo hydrodesulphurisation (HDS) unit** upstream removes organic sulfur (thiols, thiophenes) catalytically to below 0.1 ppm. (3 marks: 1 for implication + 2 for strategy with named technology.) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in 14-Mark Answers on Poisoning:** > > 1. **Conflating poisoning with coking** — they are *separate* deactivation mechanisms. Coke is a carbonaceous polymeric deposit (mostly on acid sites of supports or on metal surfaces during hydrocarbon reactions); poison is a heteroatom (S, N, P, As, halide, heavy metal). Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks for the confusion. > 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions** when applying the exponential decay $a = e^{-\alpha_p C_p}$ — you must explicitly state "uniform, non-selective, first-order in unpoisoned sites". > 3. **Mixing up $C_p$ units** — the formula $t_{0.5} = \ln 2 / (\alpha_p C_{p,\text{feed}})$ requires $C_{p,\text{feed}}$ in *mol poison per mol active sites per unit time*. A common mistake is using partial pressure or ppm directly without conversion. > 4. **Not distinguishing reversible from irreversible** when defining "regeneration" — the answer must specify *how* regeneration is achieved (oxidative purge, steaming, H₂ reduction, etc.). > 5. **Selective poisoning answered in only one sentence** — examiners expect at least: definition + mechanism (which sites) + an industrial example (Pt reforming with S is the safest choice) + an equation showing selectivity change. > 6. **Skipping the Langmuir derivation** when asked for a rate equation — the KTU 2024 rubric allocates **2–3 marks** specifically to the Langmuir–Hinshelwood form with a poison term in the denominator. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Bullet-Point Density):** - **Definition:** Poisoning = strong chemisorption of impurities on active metal sites, reducing activity/selectivity. - **Two Classification Axes:** - *Reversibility* → Reversible (e.g., CO on Pt at low T, NH₃ on Cu) vs. **Irreversible** (e.g., H₂S on Ni → NiS, Pb on Pt, As on Cu). - *Selectivity* → **Non-selective / Uniform** (all sites affected, no $S$ change) vs. **Selective** (one site type blocked, $S$ can increase or decrease). - **Key Equation:** $a = e^{-\alpha_p C_p}$ (uniform, non-selective, exponential decay). - **Langmuir Rate with Poison:** $r = \dfrac{k K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_P P_P}$ — poison term appears in the **denominator**. - **Selectivity Equation:** $S_{12} = S_{12}^{\circ} (1 - \theta_p)^{(n_1 - n_2)}$. - **Half-life:** $t_{0.5} = \ln 2 / (\alpha_p \, C_{p,\text{feed}})$. - **Industrial Examples to Memorise:** - H₂S on Ni (steam reforming) — irreversible, *requires* ZnO guard bed. - S on Pt/Al₂O₃ (reforming) — selective, *intentionally* used to suppress hydrogenolysis. - O₂, H₂O, CO, CO₂ on Fe (Haber–Bosch) — feed must be purified to ppb levels. - Pb on automotive TWCs — historical reason for unleaded petrol. - **Apparent $E_a$** *increases* with strong poison adsorption (because $-\Delta H_{\text{ads},P}$ adds to the activation barrier). - **Distinguish From:** **Coking/Fouling** (carbon), **Sintering** (thermal, physical surface area loss), **Attrition** (mechanical). All four are *separate* deactivation modes. - **Poison Strength Order (typical for transition metals):** S, As, P compounds > heavy metals (Hg, Pb) > N compounds (NH₃, amines) > CO > H₂O (in reducing environments). - **Defence Strategies (industrial):** (i) Feed purification (ZnO guard bed, HDS, activated carbon); (ii) Use of poison-resistant catalyst formulations (e.g., sulfur-tolerant noble-metal alloys); (iii) Operate at higher temperature to desorb reversible poison; (iv) Periodic regeneration cycles. - **Quantification Tools:** $\alpha_p$ (relative toxicity), $C_{p,0.5}$ (half-life exposure), Thiele modulus $\Phi$ (for diffusion-limited poisoning in porous pellets), effectiveness factor $\eta$. - **One-Liner for Viva:** *"Selective poisoning is the only form of catalyst deactivation that can **increase** the selectivity of a desired product."* — This is the single most-asked concept in KTU Module 3 vivas.

topics.title: Poisoning of Non-Metallic Catalysts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Poisoning of Non-Metallic Catalysts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In heterogeneous catalysis, **non-metallic catalysts** are those whose active sites are *not* derived from zero-valent metal ensembles, but rather from surface functional groups, lattice defects, Brønsted/Lewis acid-base pairs, or framework-confined protons. The principal families include:

1. **Solid Acid Catalysts** — Zeolites (H-ZSM-5, H-Y, USY, MOR), sulfated metal oxides (SO₄²⁻/ZrO₂, SO₄²⁻/TiO₂), mixed oxides (SiO₂–Al₂O₃), heteropoly acids (H₃PW₁₂O₄₀), and sulfonated polymeric resins (Amberlyst, Nafion).
2. **Solid Base Catalysts** — Alkali-exchanged zeolites, alkaline earth oxides (MgO, CaO), and hydrotalcites.
3. **Redox Oxide Catalysts** — Transition metal oxides used for selective oxidation (V₂O₅/TiO₂, MoO₃, Bi-Mo oxides).
4. **Carbon-Based and Pillared Clays** — Activated carbons, sulfonated carbons, and PILCs.

**Poisoning of non-metallic catalysts** is therefore defined as the *irreversible or quasi-irreversible chemisorption, ion-exchange, or pore-mouth occlusion of a foreign species (the "poison") onto, or into, the active acid/base/redox site of the catalyst, leading to a measurable decrease in catalytic activity, selectivity, or both, under the conditions of the industrial reaction.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Synthesis):**
> *"Poisoning is the loss of catalytic competence of a non-metallic catalyst caused by the strong, often stoichiometric, interaction of a feed or product impurity with the catalytically active site — a Brønsted proton, a Lewis metal cation, a lattice oxygen, or a structural pore aperture — such that the site becomes unavailable for the desired chemisorption–reaction–desorption cycle."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Reading Room"

Imagine a university library where each reading desk represents an **acid site** of a zeolite catalyst, and the books on the desk represent reactant molecules being cracked. A student doing productive work (= a reaction) needs the entire desk surface to spread out the books.

Now, imagine a clumsy janitor comes in and **spills a bottle of glue** on half the desks.

- The desks covered in glue can no longer hold books → the productive students must cluster around the few clean desks.
- Worse, the janitor doesn't just block the surface — he also **lodge himself in the aisles** (pore mouths) so that new students cannot even *reach* the back desks.
- If the glue is *oil-based* (carbonaceous coke), no simple wipe will remove it — the desk must be sanded down (regeneration by oxidative burn-off).

This is exactly what happens in a non-metallic catalyst:

| Library Element | Catalytic Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Reading desk | Brønsted/Lewis acid site |
| Janitor's glue | Nitrogen base / alkali cation / metal impurity |
| Spilled oil (coke) | Carbonaceous deposits (polycyclic aromatics) |
| Blocked aisle | Pore-mouth occlusion |
| Sanding the desk | Oxidative regeneration (air, 500–700 °C) |

### 1.3 Why Non-Metallic Catalysts Behave Differently from Metal Catalysts

The poisoning signatures of non-metallic catalysts diverge fundamentally from metallic ones because their active sites are **polar, ionic, and often located inside micropores**. This produces three distinguishing features:

- **Site-Specific, Geometric Selectivity:** A single NH₃ molecule titrates one Brønsted site — unlike a sulfur atom on Pt which can poison 4–8 metal atoms via *ensemble* effects.
- **Strong Ionic Binding:** Poisons bind through *ionic* or *hydrogen-bond* interactions (binding energies typically **80–250 kJ/mol**), often orders of magnitude stronger than the *van der Waals* adsorption of reactants (**20–60 kJ/mol**). This is why poisoning of solid acids is often *quasi-irreversible* at reaction temperature.
- **Pore Architecture Amplification:** Because many non-metallic catalysts are *microporous* (zeolites, < 2 nm), a poison does not have to chemisorb to deactivate — physical blockage of the 8-, 10-, or 12-membered ring windows is sufficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A common KTU examiner's question is *"Distinguish between poisoning of metallic and non-metallic catalysts."* Always anchor your answer in the three features above: ionic site binding, ensemble-free deactivation, and pore-mobility amplification.

### 1.4 Key Metrics Used to Quantify Poisoning

- **Activity Factor (a):** Ratio of poisoned activity to fresh activity, $a = A_{poisoned}/A_{fresh}$.
- **Site Coverage by Poison (θ_p):** Fraction of active sites occupied by poison (0 ≤ θ_p ≤ 1).
- **Number of Sites Blocked per Poison Molecule (α):** For NH₃ on a Brønsted site α = 1; for Na⁺ ion-exchanged into a zeolite α can be 1 per cation, but charge-balance forces the migration of *all* neighbouring protons away.
- **Residual Activity:** Activity at infinite time-on-stream, useful for differentiating *parallel* from *series* (consecutive) poisoning.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout (Definitional Trap):**
> Do **not** confuse *poisoning* with *fouling*. **Poisoning** is a *chemical* event at the active site (chemisorption). **Fouling** is a *physical* event (pore blocking by inert deposits, dust, or polymer). They have different regeneration strategies.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Poisons for Non-Metallic Catalysts

The poisons acting on non-metallic catalysts are best classified by their *mode of attack* on the active site. KTU expects students to memorize the mapping between catalyst family and poison.

| Catalyst Family | Active Site | Typical Poisons | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brønsted acids (H-zeolites) | Bridging Si–O(H)–Al proton | Nitrogen bases (pyridine, quinoline, NH₃, amines), alkali cations (Na⁺, K⁺), alkaline earth (Ca²⁺) | Proton transfer / ion exchange |
| Lewis acids (γ-Al₂O₃, SO₄²⁻/ZrO₂) | Coordinatively unsaturated Al³⁺, Zr⁴⁺ | H₂O (reversible), carboxylates, phosphates, F⁻ | Lewis acid-base adduct formation |
| Solid bases (MgO, Cs-X) | Surface O²⁻, low-coordination O | CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, NO₂, organic acids | Acid-base neutralization forming carbonates/sulfates |
| Redox oxides (V₂O₅, MoO₃) | V=O, Mo=O terminal oxo, lattice O²⁻ | SO₂, As, P, alkali chlorides, water vapour | Reduction or salt formation at the V=O site |
| Heteropoly acids (HPAs) | Free Brønsted protons + Keggin anion | NH₃, pyridine, alkali cations | Protonation / ion exchange with H⁺ of the HPA |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework of Poisoning

The poisoning phenomenon is governed by an **adsorption–reaction** sequence, exactly analogous to the Langmuir–Hinshelwood formalism but with the *poison* taking the place of the reactant.

**Step 1 — Diffusive Transport of the Poison.** The poison must first reach the active site. In zeolites, this occurs via *surface diffusion* along the pore walls and through *Knudsen diffusion* in the micropores. The Knudsen diffusivity is:

$$D_K = \frac{d_p}{3}\sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

where $d_p$ is the pore diameter, $R$ is the universal gas constant, $T$ is temperature (K), and $M$ is the molecular mass of the diffusing poison. For a 0.55 nm pore (ZSM-5) and pyridine (M = 79 g/mol) at 700 K, $D_K \approx 4.2 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s. This shows that *bulky* poisons are filtered by *shape selectivity* before they can even poison the site — an important self-defence mechanism of zeolites.

**Step 2 — Chemisorption onto the Active Site.** The poison is bound by a strong *acid-base* interaction. For NH₃ on a Brønsted site:

$$\equiv Si-O(H)-Al \equiv + NH_3 \rightleftharpoons \equiv Si-O(H \cdots NH_3)-Al \equiv$$

with an adsorption enthalpy of **−110 to −145 kJ/mol**. For pyridine, the enthalpy rises to **−200 kJ/mol** because of the additional π-stabilization in the conjugate pyridinium.

**Step 3 — Site Titration and Activity Loss.** Each poison molecule neutralises one (or more) active sites. The instantaneous activity is governed by the fraction of *unpoisoned* sites $(1 - \theta_p)$:

$$r = k \cdot C_R^n \cdot (1 - \theta_p)^m$$

where $k$ is the intrinsic rate constant, $C_R$ is the reactant concentration, $n$ is the reaction order in the reactant, and $m$ is the order in the *active site*. For first-order reactions on independent sites, $n = m = 1$.

**Step 4 — Deactivation Rate (Antagonistic vs. Non-Antagonistic).**
- *Antagonistic poisoning:* The poison adsorbs competitively on the *same* site as the reactant.
- *Non-antagonistic:* The poison adsorbs on a *different* site (e.g., a Lewis acid site for a Brønsted-acid-catalysed reaction) — slower kinetics.

**Step 5 — Consequence for Selectivity.** Because acid-catalysed reactions (cracking, isomerisation, alkylation) have *shape-selective* selectivity windows, poisoning of pore-mouths drastically alters the *product distribution* even before total activity is lost — a key industrial diagnostic.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU 2024 student is expected to reproduce under exam conditions. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Knudsen Diffusivity in Micropores | $D_K = \dfrac{d_p}{3}\sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$ | $d_p$ [m], $T$ [K], $M$ [kg/mol], $R = 8.314$ [J/(mol·K)] | Predicts whether a poison can reach an internal acid site (shape selectivity defence of zeolites). |
| 2 | Activity Factor (Independent Site) | $a = (1 - \theta_p)$ | dimensionless | Used when each poison blocks exactly one site. |
| 3 | Activity Factor (Multi-Site Poison) | $a = (1 - \alpha \theta_p)$ | $\alpha$ = sites blocked per poison, dimensionless | Used for large cations (e.g., Ca²⁺ blocks 2 Brønsted sites). |
| 4 | Generalised Rate with Poisoning | $r = k \cdot C_R^n \cdot (1 - \alpha \theta_p)^m$ | $k$ [variable], $C_R$ [mol/m³] | Master rate expression to embed into L-H kinetics. |
| 5 | Langmuir Isotherm for Poison Adsorption | $\theta_p = \dfrac{K_p C_p}{1 + K_p C_p}$ | $K_p$ [m³/mol], $C_p$ [mol/m³] | Equilibrium coverage at steady state. |
| 6 | Antagonistic L-H Rate | $r = \dfrac{k K_R C_R}{1 + K_R C_R + K_p C_p}$ | dimensionless ratio | Reactant and poison compete for the same site. |
| 7 | Non-Antagonistic L-H Rate | $r = \dfrac{k K_R C_R}{(1 + K_R C_R)(1 + K_p C_p)}$ | dimensionless ratio | Poison blocks a separate site. |
| 8 | First-Order Deactivation | $-\dfrac{da}{dt} = k_d \cdot a$ | $k_d$ [h⁻¹] | Activity decays exponentially: $a = a_0 e^{-k_d t}$. |
| 9 | Power-Law Deactivation (Voorhies) | $a = t^{-N}$ | $N$ dimensionless (0.1–0.6 for coking) | Empirical correlation for *parallel* coke buildup. |
| 10 | Coke Deposition Kinetics | $\dfrac{dC_c}{dt} = k_c C_R^n - k_r C_c^m$ | $k_c, k_r$ [variable] | Net coke = formation − burn-off; used in FCC regenerator design. |
| 11 | Thiele Modulus with Poisoning | $\phi = L\sqrt{\dfrac{k(1-\alpha\theta_p)}{D_{eff}}}$ | $L$ [m], $D_{eff}$ [m²/s] | Shows how internal poisoning reduces pore-utilisation efficiency. |
| 12 | Number of Acid Sites (NH₃-TPD) | $n_{acid} = \dfrac{V_{NH_3} \cdot C_{NH_3}}{m_{cat}}$ | [mol/g] | Measured desorbed NH₃; KTU may ask to back-calculate site density. |
| 13 | Bronsted / Lewis Ratio (Py-IR) | $\dfrac{B}{L} = \dfrac{I_{1545}}{I_{1455}}$ | dimensionless | Diagnostic of which site is poisoned; 1545 cm⁻¹ for B, 1455 cm⁻¹ for L. |
| 14 | Site Blocking per Cation | $\alpha_{Na^+} = 1, \; \alpha_{Ca^{2+}} = 2$ | dimensionless | Stoichiometric ion-exchange factor. |
| 15 | Selectivity Loss by Pore-Blockage | $S_{loss} = 1 - \dfrac{S_{poisoned}}{S_{fresh}}$ | dimensionless | Industrial diagnostic for FCC and hydrocracking. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Equations 4, 6, 7, and 11 appear in nearly every KTU 2024 module test on deactivation. Memorise them as a *system*, not as isolated formulas.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Industry — Real-World Utility

Poisoning of non-metallic catalysts is **the single largest operational cost** in petroleum refining. The Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) unit — the workhorse of every refinery — uses a Y-zeolite (USY) that is *continuously* coked in the riser (1–3 seconds) and *continuously* regenerated in the regenerator (5–10 minutes). Without a quantitative poisoning model, the catalyst-to-oil ratio (C/O), the regenerator air flow, and the flue-gas CO/CO₂ split cannot be set.

In **methanol-to-olefins (MTO)** over SAPO-34, even 50 ppm of an oxygenate impurity can shift the ethylene/propylene ratio by **15–20 %** by selectively poisoning the weaker acid sites. In **alkylation** (e.g., isobutane + butenes on H₂SO₄ or HF), water in the feed is the dominant poison — exactly the kind of acid–base chemistry we are studying.

Thus, *every* industrial process engineer working on non-metallic catalysts uses the formulas in §2.3 daily.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section delivers the **full, unabridged** mathematical treatment of three core results: (i) the antagonistic Langmuir–Hinshelwood poisoned rate, (ii) the deactivation rate law for ion-exchange poisoning of zeolites, and (iii) the pore-blockage criterion for coking.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Antagonistic Langmuir–Hinshelwood Rate for Poisoned Acid Catalysis

**Assumption set (clearly stated for KTU valuation):**
1. The catalyst surface has $N_T$ identical, independent active sites (Brønsted protons, for example).
2. The reactant $R$ and the poison $P$ compete for the *same* site.
3. Adsorption of each species follows a Langmuir isotherm.
4. Surface reaction is rate-determining.

**Step 1 — Site Balance.**
Let $\theta_R$ be the fractional coverage of reactant and $\theta_P$ the coverage of poison. Vacant site fraction is $\theta_V = 1 - \theta_R - \theta_P$. The total number of sites is fixed:

$$N_T = N_V + N_R + N_P$$

Dividing through by $N_T$ gives:

$$\theta_V + \theta_R + \theta_P = 1$$

**Step 2 — Adsorption Equilibria for R and P.**

For the reactant:

$$R + * \rightleftharpoons R*$$

$$K_R = \frac{\theta_R}{C_R \theta_V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_R = K_R C_R \theta_V$$

For the poison:

$$P + * \rightleftharpoons P*$$

$$K_P = \frac{\theta_P}{C_P \theta_V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_P = K_P C_P \theta_V$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the Site Balance.**

$$1 = \theta_V + K_R C_R \theta_V + K_P C_P \theta_V = \theta_V \left( 1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P \right)$$

Solving for $\theta_V$:

$$\theta_V = \frac{1}{1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P}$$

**Step 4 — Reactant Coverage (for completeness):**

$$\theta_R = \frac{K_R C_R}{1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P}$$

**Step 5 — Surface Reaction Rate.**
The rate-determining step is the reaction of adsorbed $R$ to form product. The rate is:

$$r = k \theta_R = k \cdot \frac{K_R C_R}{1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P}$$

$$\boxed{\,r = \frac{k K_R C_R}{1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Recovering the Unpoisoned Limit.**
Setting $C_P = 0$ (no poison) recovers the classical Langmuir–Hinshelwood expression:

$$r_0 = \frac{k K_R C_R}{1 + K_R C_R}$$

**Step 7 — Defining the Activity Factor.**

$$a = \frac{r}{r_0} = \frac{1 + K_R C_R}{1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P}$$

**Step 8 — High-Poison Limit Diagnostic.**
If $K_P C_P \gg 1 + K_R C_R$, then:

$$a \approx \frac{1 + K_R C_R}{K_P C_P} \to 0$$

The catalyst is *fully* poisoned. This is the asymptote often seen in industrial FCC catalyst lifetime plots.

**Step 9 — Numerical Illustration (KTU 2024 Style).**
A USY zeolite catalyses isobutane alkylation. Data: $C_R = 0.5$ mol/L (isobutene), $K_R = 2.0$ L/mol, $K_P = 80$ L/mol (for a basic nitrogen poison), $C_P = 0.02$ mol/L. Compute the activity factor.

Compute denominator and numerator:

$$1 + K_R C_R = 1 + (2.0)(0.5) = 1 + 1.0 = 2.0$$

$$1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P = 2.0 + (80)(0.02) = 2.0 + 1.6 = 3.6$$

Therefore:

$$a = \frac{2.0}{3.6} = 0.556$$

So the poisoned catalyst retains **55.6 %** of its original activity. The drop of 44.4 % in activity, when the poison is only 4 % of the reactant concentration, is a vivid demonstration of the amplification effect of $K_P/K_R = 40$ on solid acid catalysts.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ion-Exchange Deactivation of Zeolites (Alkali Poisoning)

This derivation quantifies the loss of Brønsted acid sites when an alkali cation is exchanged into a zeolite framework.

**Background.** The Brønsted acid site in an H-zeolite is the bridging hydroxyl:

$$\equiv Si-O(H)-Al \equiv$$

The proton can be exchanged for a cation $M^{n+}$ according to:

$$n \cdot (\equiv Si-O(H)-Al \equiv) + M^{n+} \rightleftharpoons (\equiv Si-O)_n M^{n+} + n H^+$$

**Step 1 — Initial Site Inventory.**
Let $H_0$ = initial Brønsted site concentration [mol/g]. A fraction $x$ is exchanged. Number of sites lost is $n \cdot x \cdot H_0$ where $n$ is the cation charge.

**Step 2 — Residual Site Concentration.**

$$H(t) = H_0 (1 - n x)$$

where $x$ is the fraction of available protons exchanged at time $t$.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Expression (Mass Action).**
The selectivity coefficient for ion exchange is:

$$K_{ex} = \frac{[(\equiv Si-O)_n M] \cdot [H^+]^n}{[\equiv Si-O(H)-Al]^n \cdot [M^{n+}]}$$

**Step 4 — Monovalent Cation Simplification (n = 1, e.g., Na⁺).**

$$K_{ex} = \frac{x \cdot [H^+]}{(1 - x) \cdot [Na^+]}$$

Solving for $x$:

$$x = \frac{K_{ex} [Na^+]}{[H^+] + K_{ex} [Na^+]}$$

**Step 5 — Residual Activity.**

$$a = \frac{H(t)}{H_0} = 1 - x = \frac{[H^+]}{[H^+] + K_{ex} [Na^+]}$$

**Step 6 — The Critical Observation.**
For zeolites, $K_{ex}$ for Na⁺ vs. H⁺ is on the order of **0.5 to 5.0** depending on the framework (Si/Al ratio). A trace amount of Na⁺ can therefore drastically de-protonate the zeolite. For example, in H-ZSM-5 with Si/Al = 25 (i.e., 1 Al per 25 Si), the *total* Brønsted site density is 0.66 mmol/g. Just **0.05 wt % Na** in the feed corresponds to $\sim 0.022$ mmol Na/g, which neutralises **3.3 %** of the sites — enough to measurably shift the methanol-to-hydrocarbons selectivity.

**Step 7 — For Divalent Cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺).**
Each cation exchanges for 2 protons. The activity factor becomes:

$$a = 1 - 2x = 1 - 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{K_{ex} [Ca^{2+}]}}{\sqrt{[H^+]^2} + \sqrt{K_{ex}[Ca^{2+}]}}$$

The factor of 2 amplifies the deactivation — a single Ca²⁺ ion *kills* two Brønsted sites. This is why FCC feed hydrotreating (to remove Ca from the VGO) is one of the *most* profitable unit operations in a refinery.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pore-Blockage Criterion for Coking of Zeolites

**Goal:** Determine the critical coke loading at which a pore mouth of diameter $d_p$ becomes inaccessible to reactant molecules of diameter $d_R$.

**Step 1 — Geometric Model.**
A zeolite pore is modelled as a cylinder of length $L_p$ and diameter $d_p$. Coke is deposited uniformly on the inner wall. The thickness of the coke layer is $\delta(t)$ at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Effective Pore Diameter.**

$$d_{p,eff}(t) = d_p - 2 \delta(t)$$

**Step 3 — Accessibility Criterion.**
A reactant molecule of diameter $d_R$ can still diffuse through the pore if and only if:

$$d_{p,eff}(t) \geq d_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta(t) \leq \frac{d_p - d_R}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Relating $\delta(t)$ to Coke Loading $C_c$.**
Coke is essentially carbon with density $\rho_c \approx 1.8 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (pyrolytic carbon). The coke mass per unit pore volume is:

$$C_c = \rho_c \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^2 - \pi (d_p - 2\delta)^2}{\pi d_p^2} = \rho_c \left[ 1 - \left( 1 - \frac{2\delta}{d_p} \right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 5 — Solving for $\delta$ as a Function of $C_c$.**

$$1 - \frac{C_c}{\rho_c} = \left( 1 - \frac{2\delta}{d_p} \right)^2$$

$$\frac{2\delta}{d_p} = 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{C_c}{\rho_c}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{d_p}{2} \left( 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{C_c}{\rho_c}} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Critical Coke Loading for Pore Closure.**
Setting $\delta_c = (d_p - d_R)/2$:

$$\frac{d_p - d_R}{d_p} = 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{C_c^*}{\rho_c}}$$

$$\sqrt{1 - \frac{C_c^*}{\rho_c}} = \frac{d_R}{d_p}$$

$$1 - \frac{C_c^*}{\rho_c} = \left( \frac{d_R}{d_p} \right)^2$$

$$\boxed{\,C_c^* = \rho_c \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{d_R}{d_p} \right)^2 \right]\,}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Illustration.**
Take ZSM-5 with $d_p = 0.55$ nm. Reactant: 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene, $d_R = 0.74$ nm. Since $d_R > d_p$, the molecule *cannot* enter the pore at all — this is the classical *reactant-shape-selectivity* effect. The catalyst is naturally protected from this particular poison.

For a *less* bulky coke precursor such as toluene ($d_R = 0.59$ nm) inside a *larger* pore USY zeolite ($d_p = 0.74$ nm):

$$\frac{d_R}{d_p} = \frac{0.59}{0.74} = 0.797$$

$$\left( \frac{d_R}{d_p} \right)^2 = 0.636$$

$$C_c^* = 1.8 \times (1 - 0.636) = 1.8 \times 0.364 = 0.655 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

So the pore closes when the coke density reaches **0.655 g per cm³ of pore volume** — about 36 % of the pyrolytic carbon density. This is a typical FCC "mild" coking level.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Poisons to Mechanisms

| Poison | Catalyst | Site Attacked | Mode | Reversibility | Industrial Counter-Measure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Na⁺, K⁺ (alkali) | H-zeolite | Bridging OH | Ion exchange | Irreversible below 500 °C | Feed desalting; guard beds |
| Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ | H-zeolite | Bridging OH | Ion exchange (2 H⁺ per M²⁺) | Irreversible | Feed hydrotreating; HDM catalysts |
| NH₃, amines | H-zeolite, HPA | Brønsted proton | Proton transfer | Reversible above 350 °C | Acid wash of feed; high T operation |
| Pyridine, quinoline | H-zeolite | Brønsted + π-stacking | Proton transfer | Quasi-irreversible | Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN) upstream |
| H₂O | SO₄²⁻/ZrO₂, HPA | Lewis site coordination | Lewis adduct | Reversible above 200 °C | Molecular-sieve drying of feed |
| CO₂ | MgO, solid bases | O²⁻ surface | Carbonate formation | Reversible above 400 °C | Thermal regeneration |
| SO₂ | V₂O₅/TiO₂ | V=O terminal oxo | Sulfate formation | Irreversible below 600 °C | Low-S feed, guard beds |
| P, As | V₂O₅, MoO₃ | Lattice O²⁻ | Oxy-acid formation | Irreversible | Feed purification |
| Coke (polycyclic aromatics) | All microporous | Pore wall, active site | Polyaromatic stacking | Removed by oxidative burn-off | Continuous regeneration (FCC); guard beds |
| F⁻ | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂ | Lewis metal centre | Fluoride adduct | Irreversible | Avoid fluoride-containing feedstocks |
