## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Poisoning Workflow for Non-Metallic Catalysts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Non-Metallic Catalyst] --> B[Active Site Identification]
    B --> B1[Bronsted Proton]
    B --> B2[Lewis Metal Centre]
    B --> B3[Lattice Oxygen]
    B --> B4[Pore Aperture]

    B1 --> C1[Acid Base Attack by Basic Poison]
    B2 --> C2[Lewis Pairing by Anion]
    B3 --> C3[Reduction or Salt Formation]
    B4 --> C4[Physical Pore Blockage]

    C1 --> D1[Protonated Poison on Site]
    C2 --> D2[Coordinated Poison Complex]
    C3 --> D3[Modified Oxidation State]
    C4 --> D4[Diffusion Barrier Formed]

    D1 --> E[Activity Decline]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F1{Industrial Process}
    F1 --> F2[FCC Unit]
    F1 --> F3[MTO Methanol to Olefins]
    F1 --> F4[Alkylation]
    F1 --> F5[Selective Oxidation]

    E --> G[Regeneration Loop]
    G --> G1[Oxidative Burn Off]
    G --> G2[Ion Wash]
    G --> G3[Thermal Treatment]

    G1 --> H[Partially Restored Catalyst]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H

    H --> I{Activity Restored Above Threshold}
    I --> J[Return to Reactor]
    I --> K[Discard as Spent Catalyst]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Deactivation Kinetics Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Analysis] --> B{Is the Poison Adsorbing on the Same Site as the Reactant?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Antagonistic Poisoning]
    B -- No --> D[Non Antagonistic Poisoning]

    C --> E[Apply Langmuir Hinshelwood Antagonistic Equation]
    D --> F[Apply Langmuir Hinshelwood Non Antagonistic Equation]

    E --> G[Compute Activity Factor]
    F --> G

    G --> H{Is the Catalyst Microporous and Smaller than the Reactant?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply Pore Blockage Criterion]
    H -- No --> J[Skip Pore Analysis]

    I --> K[Compute Critical Coke Loading]
    J --> K

    K --> L[Plot Activity versus Time on Stream]
    L --> M[Recommend Regeneration Frequency]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Coke Deposition and Regeneration Cycle in FCC

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Feed as Hydrocarbon Feed
    participant Cat as USY Zeolite Catalyst
    participant Ris as Riser Reactor
    participant Reg as Regenerator
    participant Air as Air Stream

    Feed->>Ris: Vaporize and Contact Catalyst
    Ris->>Cat: Crack to Gasoline and Light Olefins
    Cat->>Cat: Coke Deposits on Acid Sites
    Cat->>Reg: Spent Catalyst Transported
    Reg->>Air: Combustion Air Injected
    Air->>Cat: Oxidative Burn Off of Coke at 700 C
    Cat->>Ris: Hot Regenerated Catalyst Returned
    Ris->>Feed: New Cycle Begins
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Site-Specific Poisoning Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Poison Molecule Enters Reactor] --> Q{Diffusion Stage}
    Q -->|Cannot Pass Pore Mouth| R[Excluded by Shape Selectivity]
    Q -->|Enters Pore| S{Adsorption Stage}
    S -->|Weak Physisorption| T[Temporarily Adsorbed]
    S -->|Strong Chemisorption| U[Permanently Bound to Site]
    T --> V[May Desorb at Reaction Temperature]
    U --> W[Site Lost Forever]
    V --> X{Does it Read before Time Limit}
    X -->|Yes| Y[Returns to Gas Phase]
    X -->|No| Z[Reacts and Forms Coke]
    W --> AA[Activity Loss Recorded]
    Z --> AA
    Y --> AB[No Activity Loss]
    AA --> AC[Regeneration Decision]
    AC --> AD[Oxidative Burn]
    AC --> AE[Acid Wash]
    AC --> AF[Replace Catalyst]
    AB --> AG[Continue Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** Each diagram above traces a distinct logical path: the first presents the *catalyst lifecycle*; the second gives a *decision tree for kinetic modelling*; the third illustrates the *continuous regeneration loop*; the fourth exposes the *diffusion–adsorption–reaction* cascade. Together they form a complete mental model of the topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*"Distinguish between poisoning and fouling of a non-metallic catalyst. Give one industrial example of each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Poisoning** is a *chemical* event in which a foreign species forms a *strong, often stoichiometric* bond with the active site of the catalyst, making the site unavailable for the desired reaction. *(1 mark)*
- **Fouling** is a *physical* event in which inert material (dust, polymer, salt) deposits on the catalyst surface or in the pores, blocking access *without* chemical bonding. *(1 mark)*
- **Example of poisoning:** Na⁺ ions exchanging with the Brønsted protons of an H-ZSM-5 catalyst in the methanol-to-hydrocarbons process, killing acid sites. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Example of fouling:** Iron sulphide (FeS) scale deposited on FCC catalyst particles from feed contaminants, physically plugging the macropores. *(0.5 mark)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*"Why is pyridine a stronger poison than ammonia for H-ZSM-5 zeolite? Support your answer with a numerical/structural argument."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- Pyridine and NH₃ both act as Brønsted bases, but pyridine's conjugate acid (pyridinium, pKₐ ≈ 5.2) is more stable than ammonium (pKₐ ≈ 9.25) because of **aromatic resonance stabilization**. *(1 mark)*
- Therefore, pyridine has a *higher* proton affinity and a *larger* adsorption enthalpy on H-ZSM-5 (≈ −200 kJ/mol vs. ≈ −120 kJ/mol for NH₃). *(1 mark)*
- Additionally, the planar aromatic ring of pyridine π-stacks with the zeolite wall, gaining a further **~30 kJ/mol** of binding energy. *(0.5 mark)*
- Hence, pyridine is both thermodynamically and kinetically a more effective poison, often used as a *quantitative probe molecule* in IR spectroscopy (Py-IR) for Brønsted/Lewis site titrations. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Pattern**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*(a)* Define a non-metallic catalyst. List the major families of non-metallic catalysts and identify the nature of the active site in each. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* With the help of a Langmuir–Hinshelwood derivation, obtain the rate expression for a reaction that is antagonistically poisoned by an impurity. Discuss the high-poison limit. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Non-Metallic Catalysts and Their Active Sites:**

A non-metallic catalyst is a heterogeneous catalyst whose active sites are **not zero-valent metal atoms** but rather acid–base centres, lattice ions, or framework-confined functional groups. *(1 mark)*

Major families with active sites:

| Family | Active Site | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Brønsted acid | Bridging hydroxyl Si–O(H)–Al | H-ZSM-5 |
| Lewis acid | Coordinatively unsaturated Al³⁺ | γ-Al₂O₃ |
| Solid base | Surface O²⁻ | MgO |
| Redox oxide | Terminal V=O, Mo=O | V₂O₅/TiO₂ |
| Heteropoly acid | Free H⁺ in Keggin anion | H₃PW₁₂O₄₀ |
| Pillared clay | Interlayer acid sites | Al-PILC |

*(4 marks for correct listing and identification)*

For each, mention *why* it is "non-metallic" — no metallic d-band is involved; the active site is an *ionic* centre. *(1 mark)*

Industrial relevance: these catalysts are the workhorse of *petroleum refining* (FCC), *petrochemicals* (MTO, alkylation), and *fine chemicals* (selective oxidations). *(1 mark)*

**(b) Antagonistic Langmuir–Hinshelwood Rate Expression:**

The derivation in §3.1 must be reproduced step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Site balance:** $\theta_V + \theta_R + \theta_P = 1$ *(0.5 mark)*
**Step 2 — Adsorption equilibria:** $K_R = \theta_R / (C_R \theta_V)$ and $K_P = \theta_P / (C_P \theta_V)$ *(1 mark)*
**Step 3 — Substituting:** $\theta_V = 1 / (1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P)$ *(0.5 mark)*
**Step 4 — Rate expression:** $r = k \theta_R = k K_R C_R / (1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P)$ *(1 mark)*
**Step 5 — Activity factor:** $a = (1 + K_R C_R) / (1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P)$ *(1 mark)*
**Step 6 — High-poison limit discussion:**
If $K_P C_P \gg 1 + K_R C_R$, then $a \approx (1 + K_R C_R) / (K_P C_P)$. This means the activity decays *inversely* with poison concentration. In a real reactor, the poison concentration grows with time-on-stream due to slow upstream breakthrough, producing a hyperbolic activity decline rather than exponential. *(2 marks)*
**Numerical demonstration** (as in §3.1 Step 9): $a = 0.556$ for the given data. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> A common mistake is to forget the *vacant site fraction* in the site balance. If you write $\theta_R + \theta_P = 1$, you will get a wrong expression that misses the $1$ in the denominator. KTU examiners *specifically* award 2 marks for the correct site balance — losing it can drop your score from full to 11/14.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*(a)* Explain, with a derivation, the ion-exchange mechanism by which alkali cations poison a Brønsted-acid zeolite. How does the valency of the cation influence the deactivation? **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* A USY zeolite catalyst ($d_p = 0.74$ nm) is used for cracking of vacuum gas-oil. The coke precursor is modelled as pyrolytic carbon ($\rho_c = 1.8$ g/cm³). If the average reactant diameter is $d_R = 0.50$ nm, calculate the critical coke loading at which pore blockage occurs. Comment on the implication for the regenerator design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ion-Exchange Poisoning of Zeolites:**

The derivation in §3.2 is to be reproduced. Key points:

**Step 1 — The exchange equilibrium:**
$n \cdot (\equiv Si-O(H)-Al \equiv) + M^{n+} \rightleftharpoons (\equiv Si-O)_n M^{n+} + n H^+$ *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Site balance and equilibrium expression:**
For monovalent $M^+$ (e.g., Na⁺), $K_{ex} = x[H^+]/((1-x)[M^+])$ and solving yields $x = K_{ex}[M^+]/([H^+] + K_{ex}[M^+])$. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Activity factor:** $a = (1 - x) = [H^+]/([H^+] + K_{ex}[M^+])$ *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Valency effect:** Each Ca²⁺ removes *two* protons; the activity factor becomes $a = 1 - 2x$. Therefore divalent cations are *more potent* poisons per mole — and even *more potent* per gram (because the molar mass is only ~40 g/mol for Ca²⁺ vs. 23 g/mol for Na⁺, but each Ca²⁺ removes 2 sites). A 0.1 wt % Ca²⁺ contamination in a feed can neutralise **2–3 %** of the Brønsted sites of a zeolite. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Industrial counter-measure:** Hydrotreating of feed, use of guard beds (e.g., MgO or attapulgite clay) upstream of the FCC reactor to capture Na, Ca, Fe, Ni, V. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Critical Coke Loading for Pore Blockage:**

Use the formula derived in §3.3:

$$C_c^* = \rho_c \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{d_R}{d_p} \right)^2 \right]$$

**Substitute the values:**

$$\frac{d_R}{d_p} = \frac{0.50}{0.74} = 0.6757$$

$$\left( \frac{d_R}{d_p} \right)^2 = (0.6757)^2 = 0.4566$$

$$C_c^* = 1.8 \times (1 - 0.4566) = 1.8 \times 0.5434 = 0.978 \text{ g/cm}^3 \quad \text{of pore volume}$$

*(2 marks for the algebra; 1 mark for the final value)*

**Convert to weight percent on catalyst:**
Typical USY pore volume $V_p = 0.30$ cm³/g. So $C_c^* (\text{mass/mass}) = 0.978 \times 0.30 = 0.293$ g coke per g catalyst, i.e. **~29 wt % coke**. *(1 mark)*

This is a *very* high coke loading — well above the practical operating limit (typically **1–3 wt %** in commercial FCC) because the operating limit is set by *activity*, not pore closure. *(1 mark)*

**Implication for regenerator design:**
- The regenerator must supply enough oxygen to burn off coke at a rate matching the riser coke deposition rate (typically 1–3 seconds residence time, 0.5–1.0 kg coke per kg catalyst per pass). *(1 mark)*
- For a feedstock producing 1 wt % coke per pass, the regenerator air requirement is ~10 kg air per kg coke. *(0.5 mark)*
- Since pore closure is *not* the bottleneck, the regenerator design is governed by *heat balance* (regenerator supplies endothermic heat to the riser) and *CO/CO₂ ratio* control, not by pore blockage. *(0.5 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> In numerical problems, students frequently forget to include the *units* of $C_c^*$. KTU board examiners deduct **1 mark** for missing units and another **1 mark** for not clearly stating the *assumption* (uniform coke deposition, pyrolytic-carbon density). Always box your final numerical answer with units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Core Definition:** Poisoning of non-metallic catalysts is the *chemical* loss of active sites (Brønsted, Lewis, redox oxide, or basic sites) by strong adsorption, ion exchange, or covalent bond formation with a poison.
- ✅ **Three Distinguishing Features vs. Metals:** (i) ionic, (ii) site-specific (no ensemble effect), (iii) pore-architecture amplified.
- ✅ **Master Equations to Memorise:**
  * Antagonistic L-H: $r = k K_R C_R / (1 + K_R C_R + K_P C_P)$
  * Non-antagonistic L-H: $r = k K_R C_R / [(1 + K_R C_R)(1 + K_P C_P)]$
  * Knudsen diffusivity: $D_K = (d_p/3)\sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$
  * Critical coke loading: $C_c^* = \rho_c[1 - (d_R/d_p)^2]$
  * Ion-exchange activity (monovalent): $a = [H^+] / ([H^+] + K_{ex}[M^+])$
- ✅ **Poison–Catalyst Pairs (Top 5 for KTU):**
  1. Na⁺ / K⁺ on H-zeolite (ion exchange)
  2. Pyridine / quinoline on H-zeolite (Brønsted base)
  3. H₂O on SO₄²⁻/ZrO₂ (Lewis coordination)
  4. SO₂ on V₂O₅/TiO₂ (sulfate formation)
  5. Polycyclic aromatics → coke in all microporous catalysts
- ✅ **Diagnostic Techniques:**
  * NH₃-TPD → total acid site count
  * Py-IR (1545 cm⁻¹ = B, 1455 cm⁻¹ = L) → B/L ratio
  * TGA → coke loading
  * ICP-OES → trace metals (Na, Ca, V, Ni, Fe)
- ✅ **Industrial Counter-Measures:**
  * Feed hydrotreating (HDN, HDS, HDM)
  * Guard beds (MgO, clay)
  * Continuous catalyst regeneration (FCC)
  * Oxidative burn-off at 500–700 °C
- ✅ **Numerical Defaults:** $\rho_c = 1.8$ g/cm³, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $K_{ex}$ (Na/H in ZSM-5) ≈ 1–3, $K_{ex}$ (Ca/2H in Y) ≈ 5–20.
- ✅ **Commonly Tested "Trick" Distinctions:**
  * Poisoning ≠ Fouling ≠ Sintering ≠ Coking — they have *different* regeneration protocols.
  * Brønsted site poisoning is *proton transfer*; Lewis site poisoning is *electron pair donation*.
  * For zeolites, the *pore aperture* acts as a *molecular sieve* — the catalyst can *self-defend* against poisons larger than the pore mouth.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisoning of Bifunctional Catalysts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Poisoning of Bifunctional Catalysts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bifunctional Catalysts — Formal Definition

A **bifunctional catalyst** is a heterogeneous catalytic system possessing **two distinct types of active sites** that work in a coordinated, sequential manner to carry out a multi-step reaction pathway. In most industrial contexts, these two functions are:

| Function Type | Typical Active Site | Reaction Type Catalyzed |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Metallic (Hydrogenation/Dehydrogenation)** | Dispersed transition metal (Pt, Pd, Ni, Pt-Re) | Hydrogenation, dehydrogenation, hydrogenolysis |
| **Acidic (Isomerization/Cracking)** | Brønsted/Lewis acid (Zeolite, Chlorided Alumina, Sulfated Zirconia) | Skeletal isomerization, alkylation, hydrocracking, oligomerization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The two functions operate **in tandem**. The product formed on the metal site must desorb, migrate (via surface or gas-phase diffusion), and readsorb on the acid site for the next transformation. This is the famous **"M-A-M" (Metal-Acid-Metal) mechanism** described by Mills et al. (1953) and Coonradt & Garwood.

**Examples of Industrial Bifunctional Catalysts:**
- **Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃** — used in light naphtha isomerization (C₅/C₆ → high-octane isomers).
- **Pt/Y-Zeolite** — hydrocracking of heavy vacuum gas oil.
- **Pt-Re/Al₂O₃ + Cl** — catalytic reforming (CCR platforming).
- **Ni-Mo / Ni-W on acidic supports** — hydrodesulfurization (HDS) + hydrocracking.

## 1.2 Catalyst Poisoning — Formal Definition

**Catalyst poisoning** is the **irreversible (or very slowly reversible) chemisorption of feed or impurity species onto active catalytic sites**, resulting in a permanent loss of activity and/or selectivity. According to the **IUPAC definition (1986)**, a poison is *"a substance that reduces the effectiveness of a catalyst by chemically altering or blocking its active sites."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Poisoning vs. Fouling vs. Sintering vs. Coking — KTU Distinction**
> - **Poisoning**: Strong chemisorption of impurities (S, N, metals, O) on active sites. Selective, often irreversible.
> - **Fouling**: Physical deposition of species (e.g., dust, coke precursors) on the external surface blocking pores.
> - **Sintering**: Thermal loss of active surface area (metal crystallite growth).
> - **Coking**: Carbonaceous deposits on acid sites, semi-reversible via oxidative regeneration.
>
> In a **bifunctional system**, poisoning is uniquely complex because a poison may attack **only one function** while leaving the other untouched.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Engine Aircraft"

Imagine a **two-engine aircraft** where:
- **Engine 1 (Metal site)** = "Take-off engine" — gets the plane airborne (dehydrogenation of n-paraffin → olefin).
- **Engine 2 (Acid site)** = "Cruise engine" — keeps it at altitude (skeletal isomerization → iso-olefin).
- **Fuel line** = Intermediate diffusion of olefin between sites.

If **sulfur enters the fuel**:
- It preferentially cripples **Engine 1 (the platinum metal)** because Pt has a strong affinity for S, forming Pt–S bonds.
- **Engine 2 (acid sites)** still works — but receives **no fuel (no olefin intermediate)**.
- **Result:** The plane is grounded, even though Engine 2 is technically healthy. The catalyst is **"selectively poisoned."**

This is the essence of bifunctional catalyst poisoning: **a poison can cripple the entire system by attacking just one function**.

## 1.4 Classification of Poisons

| Class | Common Examples | Typical Site Attacked | Strength |
|-------|----------------|----------------------|----------|
| **Group 15 & 16 non-metals** | H₂S, SO₂, thiophene, NH₃, quinoline | Metal sites (Pt, Pd, Ni) | Strong, often irreversible |
| **Heavy metals** | Ni, V, Fe, As, Pb (from feed) | Metal & acid sites | Irreversible, pore-blocking |
| **Oxygenates / Water** | H₂O, CO, CO₂ | Metal (reversible) or acid (Cl leaching) | Mild, often reversible |
| **Basics** | NH₃, pyridine, organic N | Acid sites (Brønsted) | Often reversible by H₂ stripping |
| **Coke precursors** | Polyaromatics, olefins | Acid sites | Reversible by burn-off |
| **Lewis bases** | CO, CN⁻ | Metal | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's View:** When asked about poisoning of bifunctional catalysts, students MUST distinguish between:
> (a) **Metal-site poisoning** — affects hydrogenation/dehydrogenation capacity.
> (b) **Acid-site poisoning** — affects isomerization/cracking capacity.
> (c) **Selective poisoning** — one site attacked preferentially, altering the **M-A balance**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bifunctional Reaction Sequence — Foundation

The classical bifunctional reaction (e.g., n-hexane → iso-hexane) proceeds through the following **mandatory sequence** (Mills, Heinemann, Milliken & Oblad, 1953):

1. **Adsorption** of n-paraffin on the **metal site**.
2. **Dehydrogenation** → n-olefin.
3. **Desorption** of n-olefin.
4. **Diffusion** of n-olefin (gas-phase or surface) to the **acid site**.
5. **Protonation** on Brønsted acid site → secondary carbenium ion.
6. **Skeletal rearrangement** → iso-carbenium ion.
7. **De-protonation** → iso-olefin.
8. **Diffusion** of iso-olefin back to **metal site**.
9. **Hydrogenation** → iso-paraffin product.

> [!NOTE]
> If **any one step is poisoned**, the entire cascade breaks. The reaction becomes **diffusion-limited at the poisoned interface**.

## 2.2 Types of Poisoning in Bifunctional Systems

### (A) Metal-Site Poisoning (MSP)
- **Mechanism:** Strong chemisorption of poison atoms (e.g., S) onto the metal d-band.
- **Effect:** Reduces **dehydrogenation rate (r₁)**, suppressing formation of the olefinic intermediate.
- **Consequence:** Even if acid sites are intact, **substrate cannot reach them** through the metal-mediated pathway.
- **Selectivity Shift:** Suppresses hydrocracking → favors hydroisomerization or hydrodesulfurization.

**Example:** Sulfur poisoning of Pt in Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃ during n-pentane isomerization. Even 50 ppm S in feed can cut activity by 50%.

### (B) Acid-Site Poisoning (ASP)
- **Mechanism:** Strong chemisorption of basic nitrogen (NH₃, quinoline, pyridine) onto Brønsted acid sites.
- **Effect:** Reduces **isomerization rate (r₂)**.
- **Consequence:** Olefinic intermediates accumulate; they may oligomerize → **coke**, accelerating deactivation.
- **Selectivity Shift:** Suppresses isomerization → favors simple hydrogenation (saturation).

**Example:** Quinoline poisoning of Pt/Y-Zeolite during hydrocracking of vacuum gas oil. The acid zeolite function is destroyed while Pt remains active for HDS.

### (C) Selective Poisoning of One Function — The **M-A Balance**
- **Definition:** Preferential attack on either metal or acid site, **upsetting the delicate balance** required for optimum performance.
- **Criterion (Weisz & Swegler, 1957):** For ideal bifunctional behavior,
  $$r_1 \approx r_2 \quad \text{(rate-matching condition)}$$
  where $r_1$ = metal function rate, $r_2$ = acid function rate.

- If $r_1 \gg r_2$: **Too much olefin, too little acid** → cracking dominates → over-cracking to gas.
- If $r_1 \ll r_2$: **Too little olefin, too much acid** → acid sites idle, deactivation by coking.
- Poisoning of metal → mimics "weak metal" → under-isomerization.
- Poisoning of acid → mimics "weak acid" → over-hydrogenation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Selective poisoning is **used industrially** as a tool. For example, in catalytic reforming, **controlled sulfur passivation (CS₂ or DMS injection)** is used to suppress the metal hydrogenolysis function and improve selectivity to aromatics — a process called **"SULFIDING"** or **"selective site poisoning"**.

### (D) Parallel vs. Consecutive Poisoning
- **Parallel (feed-borne):** Poison enters with feed (e.g., H₂S in naphtha).
- **Consecutive (product-borne):** Product decomposes to form poison (e.g., N-paraffins → CO from cracking).

## 2.3 Quantitative Deactivation Models

### Model 1: Linear Poisoning (Weak Adsorption)
$$a(t) = 1 - \alpha \cdot C_p \cdot t$$
- $a(t)$ = activity at time $t$
- $C_p$ = poison concentration in feed
- $\alpha$ = proportionality constant

### Model 2: Exponential Decay (Strong, Irreversible Poisoning)
$$a(t) = \exp(-\beta \cdot C_p \cdot t)$$
- $\beta$ = poisoning severity constant (units: m³/kg·s)

### Model 3: Pore Mouth Poisoning (Levenspiel, 1972)
For non-uniform distribution of poison in pellet:
$$\frac{a(t)}{a_0} = \frac{1}{\psi} \left[ 1 - e^{-\psi} \right]$$
where $\psi$ = poisoning intensity parameter.

### Model 4: Strong Site-Blocking (Bartholomew, 2001)
For acidic bifunctional catalysts:
$$a(t) = \frac{1}{1 + K_p C_p \cdot t}$$
- $K_p$ = equilibrium adsorption constant of poison on site

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Table for Bifunctional Catalyst Poisoning**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $r_1 \approx r_2$ | Rate-matching condition (Weisz-Swegler) | mol/g·s |
| 2 | $a(t) = 1 - \alpha C_p t$ | Linear (weak) poisoning | dimensionless |
| 3 | $a(t) = \exp(-\beta C_p t)$ | Exponential (irreversible) poisoning | dimensionless |
| 4 | $a(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p C_p t}$ | Strong site-blocking | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\theta_p = \dfrac{K_p C_p}{1 + K_p C_p}$ | Langmuir poison coverage | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial t} = k_p C_p$ | Poisoning intensity growth | m⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\mathrm{TON} = \dfrac{r_0 - r}{r_0} = \theta_p$ | Turnover number loss = site coverage | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\mathrm{DS} = -\dfrac{d a / d t}{a}$ | Deactivation function (Szepe-Silverton) | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\mathrm{COR} = \dfrac{S_{\text{desired}}}{S_{\text{undesired}}}$ | Catalyst Optimization Ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta H_{\text{ads,poison}} \leq -200 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Threshold for **irreversible** chemisorption | kJ/mol |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refining:** Designing guard beds (Ni-Mo, ZnO) to remove S and N before they contact Pt.
- **Reforming:** Regulating sulfur level to 0.1–1 ppm in naphtha feed.
- **Hydrocracking:** Maintaining acid/metal balance (Si/Al ratio) to avoid coking.
- **Environmental Catalysis:** Selective poisoning of NOₓ storage sites vs. oxidation sites.
- **Pharma / Fine Chemicals:** Selective poisoning of one enantioselective site to enhance ee.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rate-Matching Condition (Weisz-Swegler Criterion)

**Premise:** For a bifunctional catalyst to deliver the target product (e.g., iso-paraffin), the rate of intermediate formation on the metal site must equal or exceed the rate of its consumption on the acid site.

**Step 1: Rate of n-paraffin dehydrogenation (metal site):**
$$r_1 = \frac{k_1 \cdot P_n}{1 + K_S P_S + K_n P_n}$$
where $P_n$ = partial pressure of n-paraffin, $P_S$ = partial pressure of sulfur poison, $K_S$ = adsorption constant of sulfur on metal.

**Step 2: Rate of olefin isomerization (acid site):**
$$r_2 = \frac{k_2 \cdot P_o}{1 + K_N P_N + K_o P_o}$$
where $P_o$ = partial pressure of olefin, $K_N$ = adsorption constant of nitrogen poison on acid.

**Step 3: Apply steady-state approximation on olefin intermediate:**
$$\frac{dP_o}{dt} = r_1 - r_2 = 0 \implies r_1 = r_2$$

**Step 4: Substitute to obtain Weisz-Swegler condition:**
$$\frac{k_1 P_n}{1 + K_S P_S + K_n P_n} = \frac{k_2 P_o}{1 + K_N P_N + K_o P_o}$$

**Step 5: For the system to be in "matched bifunctional" mode:**
$$\boxed{\;r_1 \approx r_2 \quad \text{(Matched Bifunctional Mode)}\;}$$

If $r_1 \gg r_2$ (acid poisoned) → olefin piles up → over-cracking.
If $r_1 \ll r_2$ (metal poisoned) → olefin starved → under-isomerization, simple hydrogenation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Site-Coverage (Langmuir) for Poison

**Step 1:** Single-site adsorption equilibrium for poison $P$ on a free active site $S$:
$$S + P \rightleftharpoons S\!-\!P, \quad K_p = \frac{[S\!-\!P]}{[S][P]}$$

**Step 2:** Site balance — total sites $[S]_0$:
$$[S]_0 = [S] + [S\!-\!P]$$

**Step 3:** Solve for fractional coverage $\theta_p = [S\!-\!P] / [S]_0$:
$$\theta_p = \frac{K_p [P]}{1 + K_p [P]}$$

**Step 4:** Activity is proportional to fraction of free sites:
$$a = 1 - \theta_p = \frac{1}{1 + K_p [P]}$$

**Step 5:** At low poison concentration ($K_p [P] \ll 1$):
$$a \approx 1 - K_p [P] \quad \text{(linear regime)}$$

**Step 6:** At high poison concentration ($K_p [P] \gg 1$):
$$a \approx \frac{1}{K_p [P]} \quad \text{(reciprocal regime)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Bartholomew Deactivation Function

For **strong, irreversible** chemisorption, Bartholomew proposed:

$$-\frac{da}{dt} = \psi_p \cdot C_p^n \cdot a^m$$

where $\psi_p$ = poisoning rate constant, $n$ = reaction order in poison, $m$ = order in activity.

**Step 1:** Separating variables for $m = 1$, $n = 1$:
$$\frac{da}{a} = -\psi_p C_p \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from $a_0 = 1$ at $t = 0$ to $a$ at $t$:
$$\int_1^a \frac{da'}{a'} = -\psi_p C_p \int_0^t dt'$$

$$\ln(a) = -\psi_p C_p t$$

**Step 3:** Exponentiate:
$$\boxed{\;a(t) = \exp(-\psi_p C_p t)\;}$$

This is the **exponential decay model** for irreversible poisoning. The time at which activity drops to half is:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\psi_p C_p}$$

**Step 4:** Reciprocal relation to poison concentration:
$$t_{1/2} \propto \frac{1}{C_p}$$

**Industrial use:** Doubling the sulfur in feed **halves the catalyst life**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Poison Type vs. Site Attacked vs. Effect

| Poison | Site Attacked | Mechanism | Effect on Selectivity | Reversibility |
|--------|---------------|-----------|----------------------|--------------|
| **H₂S, SO₂, thiophene** | Metal (Pt, Pd, Ni) | d-band electron donation | ↓ Dehydrogenation, ↑ Hydrogenation | Reversible (H₂ strip) |
| **NH₃, pyridine, quinoline** | Brønsted acid | Proton abstraction | ↓ Isomerization, ↑ Cracking suppression | Reversible (high-T strip) |
| **Ni, V (metal porphyrins)** | Both sites | Pore blocking, sintering | ↓↓ Activity, ↓ Selectivity | **Irreversible** |
| **H₂O** | Acid (Cl leaching) | Replaces Cl from Al₂O₃ | ↓ Isomerization, ↑ Cracking | Reversible (rechlorination) |
| **CO, CO₂** | Metal | Displaces H₂ | ↓ Dehydrogenation | Reversible |
| **As, Pb, Hg** | Metal (alloy poisoning) | Electronic perturbation | ↓ Selectivity, irreversible | **Irreversible** |
| **Coke (PAHs)** | Acid | Carbon deposition | ↓ Acid, mass transfer limit | Reversible (oxidative burn-off) |
| **O₂ (in H₂ stream)** | Metal | Surface oxide | ↓ Activity | Reversible (H₂ reduction) |

## 3.5 Deactivation Coupling in Bifunctional Systems — A Numerical Illustration

**Given:**
- Pt/Al₂O₃–Cl isomerization catalyst.
- Initial activity $a_0 = 1.0$ at $t = 0$.
- Sulfur feed concentration $C_S = 0.5$ ppm.
- Poisoning rate constant $\psi_p = 0.02$ hr⁻¹·ppm⁻¹.

**Calculate the activity at $t = 200$ hours:**

Using the exponential model:
$$a(200) = \exp(-\psi_p \cdot C_S \cdot t)$$

$$a(200) = \exp(-0.02 \times 0.5 \times 200)$$

$$a(200) = \exp(-2.0)$$

$$a(200) = 0.1353$$

**Interpretation:** After 200 hours, only **13.5% activity** remains — the catalyst is essentially spent and must be regenerated or replaced.

**Time to 50% activity:**
$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\psi_p C_S} = \frac{0.693}{0.02 \times 0.5} = 69.3 \text{ hours}$$

**Insight for design:** A **guard bed (Co-Mo/Al₂O₃ HDS reactor)** upstream, reducing S to 0.05 ppm, would extend $t_{1/2}$ to **693 hours** — a **10× improvement** in catalyst life.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bifunctional Catalyst Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FEED["FEEDSTOCK n-PARAFFIN + IMPURITIES"]
        F1[n-PARAFFIN C5-C12]
        F2[SULFUR H2S THIOPHENE]
        F3[NITROGEN NH3 QUINOLINE]
        F4[HEAVY METALS NI V FE]
    end

    subgraph GUARD["GUARD BED HDS HDN"]
        G1[Co-Mo or Ni-Mo SULFIDE]
        G2[REMOVES S AND N]
    end

    subgraph CATALYST["BIFUNCTIONAL CATALYST PELLET"]
        subgraph METAL["METAL FUNCTION Pt or Pd or Pt-Re"]
            M1[ADSORPTION n-PARAFFIN]
            M2[DEHYDROGENATION n-OLEFIN]
            M3[HYDROGENATION ISO-OLEFIN]
        end
        subgraph ACID["ACID FUNCTION Cl-Al2O3 or ZEOLITE Y"]
            A1[PROTONATION n-OLEFIN]
            A2[ISOMERIZATION CARBENIUM ION]
            A3[DEPROTONATION ISO-OLEFIN]
        end
        M2 -- OLEFIN DIFFUSION --> A1
        A3 -- ISO-OLEFIN DIFFUSION --> M3
    end

    subgraph PRODUCT["PRODUCT STREAM"]
        P1[ISO-PARAFFIN HIGH OCTANE]
        P2[GAS BYPRODUCT C1-C4]
        P3[COKE ON ACID SITES]
    end

    F2 -. POISONS .-> METAL
    F3 -. POISONS .-> ACID
    F4 -. PERMANENT DEACTIVATION .-> CATALYST

    F1 --> GUARD
    GUARD --> CATALYST
    M3 --> P1
    A2 --> P2
    A1 -. COKE .-> P3
```

## 4.2 Mechanism of Poisoning — Site-Selective Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NORMAL["NORMAL BIFUNCTIONAL OPERATION"]
        N1[Pt SITE: n-C6 → n-HEXENE]
        N2[ACID SITE: n-HEXENE → i-HEXENE]
        N3[Pt SITE: i-HEXENE → i-HEXANE]
        N1 --> N2 --> N3
    end

    subgraph METALP["METAL-SITE POISONED"]
        MP1[Pt SITE: Pt-S BLOCKED]
        MP2[ACID SITE: IDLE NO OLEFIN FEED]
        MP3[LOW ACTIVITY]
        MP1 --> MP2 --> MP3
    end

    subgraph ACIDP["ACID-SITE POISONED"]
        AP1[Pt SITE: n-C6 → n-HEXENE OK]
        AP2[ACID SITE: PROTONATED BY NH3]
        AP3[OLEFIN OLIGOMERIZATION TO COKE]
        AP1 --> AP2 --> AP3
    end

    subgraph BALANCE["SELECTIVE S-PASSIVATION REFORMING"]
        B1[Pt SITE: PARTIALLY SULFIDED]
        B2[ACID SITE: ACTIVE]
        B3[HIGH SELECTIVITY TO AROMATICS]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end
```

## 4.3 Poison Transport and Deactivation Profile Across Pellet

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHELL["PELLET CROSS-SECTION"]
        direction LR
        S1[OUTER SHELL: POISON-RICH SLOW PORE MOUTH BLOCKAGE]
        S2[MIDDLE SHELL: GRADUAL POISON FRONT]
        S3[CORE: RELATIVELY UNPOISONED FRESH SURFACE]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3
    end

    subgraph PROFILE["ACTIVITY PROFILE"]
        direction LR
        P1[LOW ACTIVITY a=0.1]
        P2[MEDIUM ACTIVITY a=0.5]
        P3[HIGH ACTIVITY a=0.9]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3
    end

    S1 -. CORRESPONDS .-> P1
    S2 -. CORRESPONDS .-> P2
    S3 -. CORRESPONDS .-> P3
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Industrial Reformer with Poison Management

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CRUDE NAPHTHA FEED] --> B[HYDROTREATER HDS HDN GUARD]
    B --> C[STRIPPER H2S NH3 REMOVAL]
    C --> D[REACTOR 1 Pt-Re ON Cl-Al2O3]
    D --> E[REACTOR 2 Pt-Re ON Cl-Al2O3]
    E --> F[REACTOR 3 Pt-Re ON Cl-Al2O3]
    F --> G[STABILIZER COLUMN]
    G --> H[REFORMATE HIGH OCTANE PRODUCT]

    B -. REMOVES .-> B1[SULFUR 0.5 ppm to 0.05 ppm]
    B -. REMOVES .-> B2[NITROGEN 1 ppm to 0.1 ppm]

    D -. CONTROLLED S INJECTION .-> D1[CS2 OR DMS 0.1 to 1 ppm]
    D1 -. TUNES .-> D2[METAL-ACID BALANCE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Reading Note for KTU Students:** In an Mermaid flowchart, each rectangular node represents a process unit or a chemical state, and arrows represent the **direction of mass or signal flow**. In SECTION 4.3, the `PELLET CROSS-SECTION` block shows how a poison front progresses from the **outer shell (highest poison exposure)** toward the **pellet core (lowest exposure)**. This is a classic case of **pore-mouth poisoning** (Levenspiel).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define bifunctional catalyst and give two industrial examples. (3 Marks)
**[Model Answer]:**
A bifunctional catalyst is a heterogeneous catalyst that contains two chemically distinct types of active sites working in a coordinated manner to catalyze a multi-step reaction sequence. Typically, one site is metallic (for hydrogenation/dehydrogenation) and the other is acidic (for isomerization/cracking).
**Industrial examples:**
1. **Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃** — used in light naphtha isomerization.
2. **Pt/Y-Zeolite** — used in hydrocracking of vacuum gas oil.
*(Alternatively: Pt-Re/Al₂O₃ in catalytic reforming; Ni-Mo on acidic zeolite in hydrodesulfurization.)*

### Q2. List any three common catalyst poisons and the site they attack in a bifunctional catalyst. (3 Marks)
**[Model Answer]:**

| Poison | Site Attacked | Example System |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| H₂S / Thiophene | Metal site (Pt, Pd, Ni) | Pt/Al₂O₃ reforming |
| NH₃ / Quinoline | Brønsted acid site | Pt/Y-Zeolite hydrocracking |
| Ni / V porphyrins | Both sites + pore blocking | Heavy feed hydrotreating |

*(Compactly tabulated form is acceptable for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A: 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain the **Weisz-Swegler rate-matching criterion** for bifunctional catalysts. Why is it critical in industrial catalytic reforming? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃ isomerization catalyst is operated with a sulfur-laden feed ($C_S = 1.0$ ppm). The poisoning rate constant is $\psi_p = 0.015$ hr⁻¹·ppm⁻¹. Using the **Bartholomew exponential model**, calculate:
- (i) The activity after 100 hours.
- (ii) The half-life of the catalyst.
- (iii) The time required for activity to drop to 10%. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Weisz-Swegler Criterion (7 Marks)**

The Weisz-Swegler criterion (1957) states that for an **ideal bifunctional catalyst**, the rate of intermediate formation on the metal site must equal the rate of its consumption on the acid site:

$$r_{\text{metal}} \approx r_{\text{acid}}$$

**Reasoning:**
- If metal function is **too fast** (acid limiting), olefinic intermediates accumulate and undergo secondary cracking → loss of liquid yield.
- If acid function is **too fast** (metal limiting), olefin feed is insufficient → acid sites idle and coking occurs.

**Industrial relevance in catalytic reforming:**
In reforming, the **metal function (Pt-Re)** provides dehydrogenation of n-paraffins → olefins → aromatics (via dehydrocyclization). The **acid function (Cl-promoted Al₂O₃)** provides isomerization. The criterion dictates the **Cl content (0.8–1.2 wt%)** and **Pt dispersion** to be optimized. If sulfur poisons the metal function, the operator can **inject controlled CS₂ (0.1–1 ppm)** to "sulfide" the metal and suppress over-cracking — improving **aromatics selectivity** without sacrificing yield.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

Given: $C_S = 1.0$ ppm, $\psi_p = 0.015$ hr⁻¹·ppm⁻¹, $t = 100$ hr.

The exponential deactivation model:
$$a(t) = \exp(-\psi_p \cdot C_S \cdot t)$$

**(i) Activity after 100 hours:**

$$a(100) = \exp(-0.015 \times 1.0 \times 100) = \exp(-1.5) = 0.2231$$

**Activity ≈ 22.3%** of original.

**(ii) Half-life:**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\psi_p \cdot C_S} = \frac{0.6931}{0.015 \times 1.0} = 46.21 \text{ hours}$$

**Half-life ≈ 46.2 hours.**

**(iii) Time for 10% activity:**

Setting $a(t) = 0.10$:
$$0.10 = \exp(-0.015 \times 1.0 \times t)$$

$$\ln(0.10) = -0.015 \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{-\ln(0.10)}{0.015} = \frac{2.3026}{0.015} = 153.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Time to 10% activity ≈ 153.5 hours.**

**Engineering Interpretation:** With 1 ppm sulfur, the catalyst is effectively exhausted within ~150 hours. Installing a guard bed to reduce S to 0.1 ppm would **10× the catalyst life**.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Discuss with a neat schematic the **mechanism of metal-site poisoning** and **acid-site poisoning** in a bifunctional Pt/Zeolite catalyst. How does selective poisoning alter selectivity? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A naphtha feed contains 50 ppb sulfur. The Pt-Re catalyst has a sulfur adsorption equilibrium constant $K_S = 5 \times 10^4$ M⁻¹ (in terms of liquid-phase equivalent). Using the **Langmuir isotherm**, calculate the **fractional poison coverage** $\theta_S$. If activity is proportional to free-site fraction, what is the catalyst activity? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Mechanism of Selective Poisoning (7 Marks)**

**Metal-site poisoning (MSP):**
- Sulfur (H₂S, thiophene) chemisorbs on Pt via **3p→5d electron donation**.
- The Pt–S bond is **partially covalent**, $\Delta H_{\text{ads}} \approx -200$ kJ/mol.
- This blocks the **hydrogenation/dehydrogenation function**.
- For Pt, even 0.1 ppm S reduces activity by 50%.
- Mechanism: $S^* + \text{Pt} \rightarrow \text{Pt–S}$ (irreversible without oxidative regeneration).

**Acid-site poisoning (ASP):**
- Basic nitrogen (NH₃, pyridine, quinoline) binds to Brønsted acid protons of the zeolite.
- The acid proton is neutralized: $\text{Z–OH} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{Z–O}^- \text{NH}_4^+$.
- This destroys the **isomerization/cracking** function.
- For Pt/Y-zeolite, quinoline poisoning is a major deactivation route in VGO hydrocracking.

**Effect on selectivity (selective poisoning → M-A balance upset):**

| Poisoning Type | Effect on Selectivity |
|----------------|----------------------|
| Metal poisoned | ↓ Isomerization, ↑ Hydrogenation, ↓ Aromatics (in reforming) |
| Acid poisoned | ↑ Hydrogenation to n-paraffin, ↓ Branched products |
| Both poisoned | Catalyst fully deactivated |
| Controlled metal poisoning (S, As) | ↑ Aromatics selectivity in reforming (industrial use!) |

**Schematic (to be drawn):** Show a catalyst pellet with Pt sites (left) and acid sites (right). Show S atoms blocking Pt and N atoms blocking acid. Indicate the M-A balance arrow.

**(b) Langmuir Coverage Calculation (7 Marks)**

Given:
- $C_S = 50$ ppb = $50 \times 10^{-9}$ (mass basis, treat as molar fraction in dilute regime).
- $K_S = 5 \times 10^4$ M⁻¹.

For dilute solution, convert ppb to approximate molar concentration. Assuming naphtha density ≈ 0.75 g/mL and average MW = 100 g/mol:
$$C_S \approx \frac{50 \times 10^{-9} \text{ g S}}{1 \text{ g naphtha}} \times \frac{0.75 \text{ g/mL}}{1} \times \frac{1000 \text{ mL}}{1 \text{ L}} \times \frac{1 \text{ mol S}}{32 \text{ g}} \times \frac{1}{100 \text{ g/mol (naphtha)}}$$

A simpler approximate treatment for **dimensionless product** $K_S \cdot C_S$:

Treat $C_S$ in compatible units. Taking $C_S$ as **dimensionless mole fraction equivalent**:
$$C_S \approx \frac{50 \text{ ppb}}{10^9} \times \frac{\text{MW}_{\text{naphtha}}}{\text{MW}_S} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-9} \times 100}{32} \approx 1.56 \times 10^{-7}$$

(Note: For KTU, students may be given $K_S \cdot C_S$ directly or asked to compute the dimensionless group.)

$$K_S \cdot C_S = 5 \times 10^4 \times 1.56 \times 10^{-7} = 7.8 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Langmuir coverage:**
$$\theta_S = \frac{K_S C_S}{1 + K_S C_S} = \frac{7.8 \times 10^{-3}}{1 + 7.8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.0078}{1.0078} \approx 0.00774$$

**Activity** (proportional to free site fraction):
$$a = 1 - \theta_S = 1 - 0.00774 = 0.9923 \approx 99.2\%$$

**Interpretation:** At 50 ppb sulfur, the catalyst retains **~99% activity** — well within industrial design limits (typically <0.05 ppm S is targeted for reformer feeds). This validates the design rule that **Pt-Re catalysts tolerate low ppb-level sulfur**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing poisoning with coking** — both deactivate, but coke is **reversible by oxidation**, while poisoning is often **irreversible**.
> 2. **Forgetting the M-A balance** — always state which function (metal or acid) is being poisoned in bifunctional systems; partial credit is lost otherwise.
> 3. **Wrong units in exponential model** — $\psi_p$ has units of (concentration × time)⁻¹; mixing hours with ppm vs. ppb is a frequent error.
> 4. **Sign errors in $\ln$ calculation** — when solving for time at given activity, ensure negative sign is handled correctly: $\ln(a) = -\psi_p C_p t$.
> 5. **Linearizing exponential without justification** — KTU expects the **full exponential form**; linear approximations only valid for $t \ll t_{1/2}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 3 Topic: Poisoning of Bifunctional Catalysts — Quick Revision**

- ✅ **Bifunctional catalyst** = Metal site (dehydrogenation) + Acid site (isomerization/cracking) acting in tandem.
- ✅ **M-A-M mechanism** (Mills, 1953): Metal → Acid → Metal sequence with olefin as the diffusing intermediate.
- ✅ **Three poisoning modes**: Metal-site, Acid-site, Selective (M-A balance upset).
- ✅ **Common metal-site poisons**: H₂S, SO₂, thiophene, CO, O₂, As, Pb, Hg.
- ✅ **Common acid-site poisons**: NH₃, pyridine, quinoline, H₂O (via Cl leaching), organic N.
- ✅ **Weisz-Swegler criterion**: $r_1 \approx r_2$ — rates of metal and acid functions must match.
- ✅ **Bartholomew exponential model**: $a(t) = \exp(-\psi_p C_p t)$ — for irreversible poisoning.
- ✅ **Langmuir coverage**: $\theta_p = \frac{K_p C_p}{1 + K_p C_p}$ — relates poison concentration to site blockage.
- ✅ **Half-life formula**: $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / (\psi_p C_p)$ — inversely proportional to poison concentration.
- ✅ **Threshold for irreversible chemisorption**: $\Delta H_{\text{ads}} \leq -200$ kJ/mol.
- ✅ **Selective poisoning is an industrial tool**: Controlled S-injection in reforming improves aromatics selectivity.
- ✅ **Guard beds (Ni-Mo, Co-Mo)** are used upstream to remove S and N before they contact precious metal.
- ✅ **Pore-mouth poisoning** (Levenspiel) is dominant for large-molecule poisons (Ni, V porphyrins).
- ✅ **Distinguish poisoning from coking, fouling, and sintering** — KTU questions often test this discrimination.
- ✅ **Regeneration strategies**: Oxidative burn-off (coke), H₂ stripping (reversible S), oxidative chlorination (Cl recovery), guard bed replacement (irreversible metals).
- ✅ **Activity ∝ free site fraction**: $a = 1 - \theta_p = \frac{1}{1 + K_p C_p}$.
- ✅ **Hydrocracking feed S < 10 ppm, N < 2 ppm** — typical industrial specifications to protect acid + metal sites.
- ✅ **Reformer feed S < 0.5 ppm** — Pt-Re is highly S-sensitive; ultra-low sulfur is mandatory.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coke Formation on Catalysts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coke Formation on Catalysts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coke Formation (Coking)** is the progressive deposition of high-molecular-weight, carbonaceous (C/H-rich) by-products on the **active sites** and within the **pore network** of a heterogeneous catalyst during hydrocarbon processing, leading to **reversible or irreversible loss of catalytic activity** over time on stream.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme lexicon for `PEPOT412`, coke is **not pure carbon** but rather a complex, hydrogen-deficient polymeric/graphitic residue with a typical empirical formula:

$$\text{Coke} \equiv \text{C}_n\text{H}_m \quad \text{where} \quad \frac{n}{m} \gg 1 \quad \text{(typically } n/m \approx 5\text{–}50\text{)}$$

The **coke content** of a spent catalyst is reported in **weight percent (wt%)**, normally determined by **Thermo-Gravimetric Analysis (TGA)** under oxidising atmosphere, and the standard industrial acceptability threshold is generally:

$$C_{\text{coke}}^{*} \le 15 \text{ wt\%} \quad \text{(for fluid catalytic cracking, FCC)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Focus:** Coke is treated as a *mechanism of catalyst deactivation* (Type I – chemical deactivation by strong chemisorption or fouling) and is distinguished from **poisoning** (Type II, by heteroatoms like S, N, metals) and **thermal sintering** (Type III).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Blocked Kitchen Sink" Analogy 🍳🚰**
> Imagine cooking in a kitchen sink that slowly gets clogged by grease (analogy of coke). Even though water (the reactant) keeps flowing in:
> - **Days 1–10** — The drain is open. Sinks drain perfectly. (Fresh catalyst → full activity).
> - **Days 10–30** — A thin greasy film coats the pipe walls. Water flow begins slowing. (Mild coking → partial pore-mouth blockage).
> - **Days 30–60** — A thick crust forms. Only a trickle passes. (Heavy coking → pore blocking + active-site coverage).
> - **Day 61** — You pour a strong acid/degreaser (regeneration) and the sink is restored.

The **grease = coke**, the **pipe wall = catalyst pore wall**, and the **degreaser = oxidative regeneration in air at 500–700 °C**. The catalyst is *recoverable* — that is what makes coke fundamentally different from poisoning.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

| Symbol | Definition | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_c$ | Coke content on spent catalyst | 0.1 – 20 | **wt %** |
| $n/m$ | C/H atomic ratio of coke | 5 – 50 | dimensionless |
| $r_c$ | Rate of coke formation | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-1}$ | **g·g$_{cat}^{-1}$·h$^{-1}$** |
| $\tau_d$ | Time-on-stream deactivation | hours – months | **h** |
| $E_a^{(c)}$ | Apparent activation energy of coking | 40 – 150 | **kJ·mol$^{-1}$** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Coke

Coke is **NOT a single substance**. KTU 2024 expects you to recognise at least three structurally distinct families:

### 2.1.1 By Location on the Catalyst
- **(a) Catalytic Coke** — Deposited directly on **active sites** (metal crystallites or acid sites). Highly hydrogen-deficient; essentially graphitic at high $T$.
- **(b) Coke in Pores** — Non-selective condensation of heavy polyaromatics inside the mesopore/macropore network (a *diffusion-controlled* phenomenon).
- **(c) Coke on External Surface** — Encapsulates the entire pellet, blocking all pore mouths.

### 2.1.2 By Chemical Nature
- **(i) Amorphous / Polymeric Coke** — Low temperature (200–350 °C), high H content ($n/m \approx 1.5$–3). Formed on acidic zeolites via oligomerisation.
- **(ii) Filamentous / Whisker Carbon** — Forms on **transition metals** (Ni, Fe, Co) by dissolution-diffusion-precipitation of C through the metal particle. Causes **pore break-out (crumbling)**.
- **(iii) Graphitic / Pyrolytic Coke** — High temperature ($> 500$ °C), $n/m > 30$. Forms by radical polymerisation of cracked fragments.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Whenever the question mentions *acidic catalyst* → polymeric coke; *transition metal* → filamentous coke; *high temperature reformer* → graphitic coke.

## 2.2 Mechanistic Pathways of Coke Formation

There are **three primary mechanistic routes** that KTU 2024 Module 3 explicitly lists:

### Pathway A — On Acidic Sites (Zeolites, Alumina, Silica-Alumina)
$$\text{Olefin} \xrightarrow{H^{+}} \text{Oligomer} \xrightarrow{\text{cyclisation}} \text{Naphthene} \xrightarrow{\text{H-transfer}} \text{Aromatic} \xrightarrow{\text{condensation}} \text{Coke}$$

Key elementary step: **Hydride transfer** from a paraffinic species to a carbocation — the Bartolomew–Froment mechanism.

### Pathway B — On Transition Metals (Pt, Pd, Ni, Rh)
$$n\,\text{C}_x\text{H}_y \xrightarrow{\text{dehydrogenation}} n\,\text{C}^*_{(s)} + \tfrac{n y}{2}\,\text{H}_2$$
The carbon dissolves in the metal lattice, diffuses to the rear face, and precipitates as a **carbon whisker**, lifting the metal particle off the support (decohesion).

### Pathway C — On Bifunctional Catalysts (Pt/Al$_2$O$_3$ reforming)
A combination of (A) + (B): metal dehydrogenates, acid site cyclises → polyaromatic coke precursor → graphitic encapsulation of the metal.

## 2.3 Effect of Coke on Catalyst Performance

| Effect | Consequence | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Active site coverage** | Loss of surface metal area; $S_{\text{BET}}$ largely unchanged | Oxidative regeneration |
| **Pore blocking** | Reduced $D_{eff}$; increased Thiele modulus | Steam/air decoking |
| **Pore-mouth narrowing** | Diffusion-limited reactions suffer first | Mild burn-off |
| **Sintering acceleration** | Coke-bound metals agglomerate | Chlorination/oxychlorination |
| **Pellet disintegration** | Whisker growth cracks pellets | Sulphur passivation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Limitation / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a = \exp(-\alpha C_c)$ | **Activity decay** (Elbridge–Satterfield exponential form) | For $C_c \le 10$ wt% |
| 2 | $C_c = A \, t^{n}$ | **Voorhies correlation** ($n \approx 0.5$) | Empirical; valid for FCC |
| 3 | $r_c = k_c \, P_{hc}^{m} \, a$ | **Coking rate** (Langmuir-type) | $a$ is instantaneous activity |
| 4 | $\phi_p = L\sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$ | **Thiele modulus** (coke reduces $D_{eff}$) | Determines pore vs. surface control |
| 5 | $\eta = \tanh(\phi)/\phi$ | **Effectiveness factor** | Used to find coked-pellet rate |
| 6 | $D_{eff} = D_0(1-\theta_c)^n$ | **Coke-narrowed diffusivity** | $n=1$–3; $\theta_c$ = pore-filling |
| 7 | $t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k_d}$ | **Half-life of activity** (1st-order decay) | $k_d$ = deactivation constant |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Coke management is the **single largest operating cost** in:
- **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** — the *regenerator* in a UOP-riser unit is 30 % of capital cost, solely to burn coke.
- **Catalytic Reforming** — Pt-Re/Al$_2$O$_3$ is regenerated every 24–72 h in CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration).
- **Steam Cracking Furnaces** — coke in radiant coils reduces heat transfer, raising TMT (tube metal temp) and forcing decoke cycles every 30–90 days.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Voorhies Power-Law Coking

**Statement:** For many hydrocarbon reactions (notably FCC), the instantaneous coke content $C_c$ on a catalyst correlates empirically with time-on-stream $t$ as

$$C_c(t) = A\, t^{n}$$

where $A$ has units of **g-coke / g-cat · h$^{-n}$** and $n$ is a dimensionless exponent.

**Derivation (from first-order elementary coking step):**

Assume the **net coking rate** is the difference between coke formation and coke gasification (steam or H$_2$ decoking), with the formation step being *first order in vacant active sites*:

$$r_c = k_c\, \theta_v \, P_{hc} - k_g \, \theta_c \, P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$$

At low steam partial pressure and short time, gasification is negligible, so $r_c \approx k_c\,\theta_v P_{hc}$. Now, at **low coke loading**, $\theta_v \approx 1$ (sites still vacant), giving constant rate — a *linear* regime. At **higher loading**, $\theta_v$ is reduced self-consistently: a site covered by coke can no longer catalyse coking on the *same* site, but adjacent sites are still active. This gives a **diffusion-like** $\sqrt{t}$ behaviour. The empirical Voorhies form $C_c = A t^{n}$ is fitted with $n \approx 0.5$ for diffusion-controlled coke accumulation.

Therefore, the **instantaneous coking rate** is:

$$\frac{dC_c}{dt} = n A t^{n-1}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style 3-mark):** A FCC equilibrium catalyst shows $C_c = 2.4\,\text{wt\%}$ after 2 h and $C_c = 6.0\,\text{wt\%}$ after 8 h. Find $A$ and $n$.

$$2.4 = A(2)^n \quad ; \quad 6.0 = A(8)^n$$

$$\frac{6.0}{2.4} = \left(\frac{8}{2}\right)^{n} \;\Rightarrow\; 2.5 = 4^{n} \;\Rightarrow\; n = \frac{\ln 2.5}{\ln 4} = 0.806$$

$$A = \frac{2.4}{2^{0.806}} = \frac{2.4}{1.751} = 1.371 \;\text{wt\%·h}^{-0.806}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Activity Decay with Coke (Exponential Form)

**Statement:** The relative activity $a$ of a coked catalyst decays with coke content as

$$a = \frac{\text{Rate (coked)}}{\text{Rate (fresh)}} = \exp(-\alpha C_c)$$

**Derivation:** The number of *uncovered* active sites at coke loading $C_c$ (g-coke/g-cat) is proportional to the fractional free surface:

$$\theta_v(C_c) = \theta_v^{0}\,\exp(-\alpha C_c)$$

The factor $\alpha$ (units: **g-cat/g-coke**) is the *coking susceptibility constant* — physically it is the area of catalyst surface deactivated per gram of coke deposited. Assuming the rate is first-order in $\theta_v$:

$$a = \frac{r_c^{\text{coked}}}{r_c^{\text{fresh}}} = \frac{\theta_v(C_c)}{\theta_v^{0}} = \exp(-\alpha C_c)$$

Combining with Voorhies $C_c = A t^{n}$:

$$\boxed{\,a(t) = \exp\!\left(-\alpha A\, t^{n}\right)\,}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a *stretched-exponential decay*. The half-life of activity, defined by $a = 0.5$, is:

$$t_{1/2} = \left(\frac{\ln 2}{\alpha A}\right)^{1/n}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pore-Blockage and Modified Thiele Modulus

**Statement:** Coke deposited on pore walls narrows the effective pore radius from $r_p$ to $r_{\text{eff}} = r_p - \delta$, where $\delta$ is the average coke thickness. Show that the Thiele modulus increases sharply.

**Derivation:**

The Knudsen diffusivity in a cylindrical pore of radius $r$ is:

$$D_K(r) = \frac{2}{3} r \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

After coking, the effective diffusivity becomes:

$$D_{eff}(C_c) = D_{K,0}\!\left(1 - \frac{\delta}{r_p}\right) = D_{K,0}(1 - \theta_c)$$

where $\theta_c = \delta/r_p$ is the fractional pore-narrowing. Since $\delta$ is proportional to $C_c$ (with proportionality constant $\beta$, g-coke per g-cat per unit thickness):

$$D_{eff} = D_{K,0}\,(1 - \beta C_c)$$

The Thiele modulus for a first-order reaction in a slab of half-thickness $L$ is:

$$\phi = L \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_{eff}}}$$

Substituting:

$$\phi_{\text{coked}} = \phi_0 \left(1 - \beta C_c\right)^{-1/2}$$

The **catalyst effectiveness factor** then follows from:

$$\eta = \frac{\tanh \phi_{\text{coked}}}{\phi_{\text{coked}}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Even a 20 % reduction in pore radius $(1-\beta C_c = 0.8)$ raises $\phi$ by $\approx 12\,\%$ and the observed rate $\eta k C_b$ drops *non-linearly* — explaining why catalytic cracking yields *fall rapidly* with coke even at low $C_c$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mass Balance in a PFR with Coking

For a PFR carrying out a hydrocarbon conversion reaction with simultaneous coking, the steady-state balances are:

**Reactant (A):**

$$\frac{dF_A}{dz} = -r_A\, a(C_c)\, \pi R^2$$

**Coke accumulation on catalyst:**

$$\rho_b \frac{\partial C_c}{\partial t} = r_c\, a(C_c) - r_{\text{burn}}$$

At pseudo-steady state, $r_{\text{burn}} \to 0$ (no in-situ regeneration):

$$\rho_b \frac{\partial C_c}{\partial t} = r_c\, \exp(-\alpha C_c)$$

Integrating with $C_c(0)=0$:

$$e^{\alpha C_c} = 1 + \frac{\alpha r_c t}{\rho_b}$$

$$C_c(t) = \frac{1}{\alpha}\ln\!\left(1 + \frac{\alpha r_c t}{\rho_b}\right)$$

This is the **logarithmic coking law** — characteristic of *self-poisoning* on acidic catalysts such as H-ZSM-5 in methanol-to-gasoline (MTG).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between catalyst poisoning and catalyst coking. Can both be reversed by the same regeneration strategy? Justify."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Poisoning | Coking |
|---|---|---|
| Causative species | Heteroatoms — S, N, P, As, heavy metals (V, Ni, Fe) | Carbonaceous polymeric/graphitic deposits |
| Mode of action | Strong chemisorption (often irreversible) on active sites | Physical coverage + pore blocking |
| Reversibility | Generally **irreversible** for chemisorbed poisons; partially reversible for physisorbed | **Reversible** by oxidative burn-off (air, 500–700 °C) |
| Regeneration | Often impossible; catalyst replaced | Standard industrial practice (FCC regenerator, CCR) |

**(1 mark)** definition difference, **(1 mark)** mode difference, **(1 mark)** reversibility conclusion — **same strategy is NOT applicable; coke needs oxidation, poisoning often cannot be undone by oxidation.**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Explain why filamentous (whisker) carbon causes physical disintegration of a metal-loaded catalyst pellet."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Whisker carbon forms by **dissolution–diffusion–precipitation**: hydrocarbon dissociates on the front face of the metal crystallite → C dissolves in metal → diffuses to rear face → precipitates as a graphitic filament. **(1 mark)**
- The growing filament exerts a **mechanical stress** that physically **lifts the metal particle off the support** and **splits the pore wall**. **(1 mark)**
- Result: pellet crumbling, fines generation, pressure-drop rise, and **irreversible loss of metal area** (the active metal is no longer atomically dispersed). **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** Derive the **Voorhies power-law** form for coke accumulation $C_c = A t^n$ starting from a first-order coking rate expression $r_c = k_c\,\theta_v\,P_{hc}$, and discuss its limitations when applied to steam-reforming catalysts. **(7 marks)**

|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_c$ (wt %) | 0.8 | 1.7 | 3.2 | 4.3 |

Fit the data to the Voorhies equation and determine the deactivation constant $n$ and the pre-exponential $A$. If the catalyst is regenerated when $a = 0.30$, and the activity decay obeys $a = \exp(-\alpha C_c)$ with $\alpha = 0.18$ g-cat/g-coke, find the cycle time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **concept of pore-mouth blocking** by coke. Show mathematically how the Thiele modulus is modified when an average coke layer of thickness $\delta$ narrows a pore of radius $r_p$. Comment on the impact on effectiveness factor. **(7 marks)**

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Three mechanistic pathways of coke formation**

1. **Acidic oxide catalysts (H-ZSM-5, SiO$_2$-Al$_2$O$_3$):**
   - Initiation: Brønsted acid site protonates an alkene to give a **surface alkoxide / carbenium ion**.
   - Propagation: oligomerisation → cyclisation → formation of alkylaromatics.
   - Termination: **Hydride transfer** from a paraffinic species or naphthene to the carbocation — this dehydrogenates the donor and generates a new carbocation that propagates the chain, while the donor itself becomes a surface aromatic.
   - Polycyclic condensation of aromatics gives the polymeric coke ($n/m \approx 1.5$–3).

2. **Transition-metal catalysts (Ni, Fe, Pt):**
   - Hydrocarbon dissociatively chemisorbs on the metal crystallite surface: C$_{(g)}$ → C$^*$.
   - Carbon dissolves into the bulk metal (especially in Ni, Fe) and **diffuses through the lattice**.
   - Carbon precipitates at the rear face, growing a **graphitic whisker** that mechanically ejects the metal particle from the support.
   - Hydride transfer is replaced by **H$_2$ associative desorption**.

3. **Bifunctional reforming catalysts (Pt/Al$_2$O_3$ or Pt-Cl/Al$_2$O_3$):**
   - **Metal function:** paraffin → olefin (dehydrogenation).
   - **Acid function:** olefin → cyclopentane → cyclohexane → aromatic.
   - The aromatic, being the thermodynamic sink, accumulates and undergoes **further condensation + hydride transfer** to give coke encapsulating both the metal and the adjacent acid site.
   - Hence reforming catalysts suffer **simultaneous metal-site poisoning and acid-site blocking**.

**(b) Derivation of Voorhies law**

Starting from $r_c = k_c\,\theta_v P_{hc}$ and assuming $\theta_v$ is the fraction of un-coked sites:

$$\rho_b\frac{dC_c}{dt} = r_c \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{dC_c}{dt} = \frac{k_c P_{hc}}{\rho_b}\,\theta_v$$

The un-coked site fraction decays with accumulated coke (assuming each coke molecule blocks one site, and Coke is immobile):

$$\theta_v = 1 - \frac{C_c}{C_c^{\max}}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dC_c}{dt} = \frac{k_c P_{hc}}{\rho_b}\left(1 - \frac{C_c}{C_c^{\max}}\right)$$

This is a separable ODE. Integration with $C_c(0) = 0$:

$$C_c(t) = C_c^{\max}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{k_c P_{hc}}{\rho_b C_c^{\max}}\,t\right)\right]$$

For **early times** ($k_c P_{hc} t \ll \rho_b C_c^{\max}$), Taylor-expanding the exponential:

$$C_c(t) \approx C_c^{\max} \cdot \frac{k_c P_{hc}}{\rho_b C_c^{\max}}\, t = \frac{k_c P_{hc}}{\rho_b}\,t \quad\Rightarrow\quad n = 1 \;(\text{linear regime})$$

For **intermediate times** when coke near the pore mouth begins to slow diffusion, the empirical fit gives $n \approx 0.5$, leading to the **Voorhies power law**:

$$C_c(t) = A\,t^{n}, \qquad n \approx 0.5$$

**Limitations for steam reforming:**
- Steam reformers operate at very high $T$ (800–900 °C) and have **simultaneous gasification** of coke by steam (C + H$_2$O → CO + H$_2$). Voorhies ignores this; net coking is $r_c^{\text{net}} = r_{\text{form}} - r_{\text{gas}}$.
- The catalyst is **pellet-shaped** with internal temperature gradients — Voorhies assumes uniform coke distribution.
- Hence Voorhies is best applied to **adiabatic, isothermal, low-steam FCC** data, not to **endothermic, steam-rich reforming**.

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Fitting the Voorhies model**

| $t$ (h) | $\ln t$ | $C_c$ (wt%) | $\ln C_c$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.000 | 0.8 | −0.2231 |
| 4 | 1.386 | 1.7 | 0.5306 |
| 16 | 2.773 | 3.2 | 1.1632 |
| 36 | 3.584 | 4.3 | 1.4586 |

Linear regression on $(\ln t, \ln C_c)$:

Slope $n = \dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)}{\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2}$

$$\bar x = 1.936, \quad \bar y = 0.732$$

$$\sum(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y) = (0-1.936)(-0.955) + (1.386-1.936)(-0.201) + (2.773-1.936)(0.431) + (3.584-1.936)(0.727)$$

$$= 1.849 + 0.111 + 0.361 + 1.199 = 3.520$$

$$\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2 = 3.747 + 0.302 + 0.701 + 2.713 = 7.464$$

$$n = 3.520 / 7.464 = 0.472 \approx 0.5 \quad \text{(diffusion-controlled — Voorhies expected)}$$

Intercept: $\ln A = \bar y - n \bar x = 0.732 - 0.472 \times 1.936 = 0.732 - 0.914 = -0.182$

$$A = e^{-0.182} = 0.834 \;\text{wt\%·h}^{-0.472}$$

**Cycle time:** Given $a = 0.30$ and $a = \exp(-\alpha C_c)$ with $\alpha = 0.18$:

$$\ln(0.30) = -0.18\,C_c \;\Rightarrow\; C_c = \frac{\ln(1/0.30)}{0.18} = \frac{1.204}{0.18} = 6.69\;\text{wt\%}$$

$$6.69 = 0.834\,t^{0.472} \;\Rightarrow\; t^{0.472} = 8.02 \;\Rightarrow\; t = 8.02^{1/0.472} = 8.02^{2.119}$$

$$t = e^{2.119 \ln 8.02} = e^{2.119 \times 2.082} = e^{4.413} = 82.6 \;\text{h}$$

**Cycle time ≈ 82.6 h (≈ 3.4 days)**. Industry typically regenerates reforming catalyst at 60–100 h on stream. ✓

**(b) Pore-mouth blocking and Thiele-modulus modification**

A catalyst pellet of half-thickness $L$ contains cylindrical pores of radius $r_p$. Coke of mean thickness $\delta$ deposits uniformly on the pore wall, narrowing the radius to:

$$r_{\text{eff}} = r_p - \delta = r_p(1 - \theta_c), \qquad \theta_c = \delta/r_p$$

The Knudsen diffusivity in a cylindrical pore is:

$$D_K = \frac{2}{3}\,r_p \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

Thus the **coke-narrowed** diffusivity is:

$$D_{\text{eff}}(C_c) = \frac{2}{3}\,r_p(1 - \theta_c)\sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} = D_{K,0}(1 - \theta_c)$$

For a slab geometry, the Thiele modulus is:

$$\phi = L\sqrt{\frac{k_v}{D_{\text{eff}}}} = L\sqrt{\frac{k_v}{D_{K,0}(1 - \theta_c)}} = \phi_0\,(1 - \theta_c)^{-1/2}$$

with $\phi_0 = L\sqrt{k_v/D_{K,0}}$ being the *fresh* Thiele modulus.

**Effectiveness factor:**

$$\eta = \frac{\tanh \phi_{\text{coked}}}{\phi_{\text{coked}}} = \frac{\tanh\!\left[\phi_0(1-\theta_c)^{-1/2}\right]}{\phi_0(1-\theta_c)^{-1/2}}$$

**Numerical illustration:** Take $\phi_0 = 1.0$ and $\theta_c = 0.2$ (20 % pore narrowing).

$$\phi_{\text{coked}} = 1.0 \times (0.8)^{-0.5} = 1.118$$

$$\eta_{\text{coked}} = \frac{\tanh(1.118)}{1.118} = \frac{0.807}{1.118} = 0.722$$

vs. fresh $\eta_0 = \tanh(1.0)/1.0 = 0.762$. So a mere 20 % pore narrowing has reduced the *observed* rate by $0.722/0.762 = 0.948$ — i.e., 5.2 % loss.

For $\theta_c = 0.4$ (severe coking): $\phi_{\text{coked}} = 1.291$, $\eta = 0.671$, observed rate loss = 12 %.

> The *non-linearity* is the key engineering message: coking does not just block sites — it amplifies diffusion limitations in a strongly non-linear way.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ⚠**
> 1. **Don't confuse "coke" with "carbon"** — coke is a *family of polymeric/aromatic residues* with $n/m$ in the range 1.5–50, not pure graphite.
> 2. **Do not write "regeneration = air burn-off" without specifying temperature** — examiners will not award the second mark. State **500–700 °C in air**, with possible Pt recovery above 700 °C.
> 3. **Voorhies exponent $n$ is empirical, not theoretical** — don't derive it as a universal 0.5. In data fits it ranges 0.3–0.8; you must state this.
> 4. **Coke kinetics ≠ Poisoning kinetics** — coking is *self-inhibiting* (negative feedback) because product blocks site; poisoning by heteroatom is *non-self-limiting* in rate.
> 5. **Filamentous coke** is NOT the same as graphitic coke — examiners specifically test the difference (whisker growth on Ni/Fe vs. layered encapsulation on Pt).
> 6. **Thiele modulus derivation marks require the explicit $L\sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$ form**; forgetting the $\sqrt{}$ loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Coke is reported in wt%, NOT mol%** — for a 3-mark short question, examiners expect the unit and an *explanation* of TGA measurement.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- 🔹 **Coke = carbonaceous deposit**, C/H-rich, not pure carbon.
- 🔹 **Three mechanistic pathways**: acid-site (polymeric), metal (filamentous), bifunctional (graphitic).
- 🔹 **Hydride transfer** is the *signature elementary step* on acid catalysts; **dissolution–diffusion–precipitation** is the *signature* on metals.
- 🔹 **Effect**: active-site coverage (intrinsic) + pore blocking (diffusion) + metal encapsulation (synergy).
- 🔹 **Voorhies law**: $C_c = A t^n$, with $n \approx 0.5$ for diffusion-controlled coking.
- 🔹 **Activity decay**: $a = \exp(-\alpha C_c)$; half-life $t_{1/2} = (\ln 2/\alpha A)^{1/n}$.
- 🔹 **Thiele modulus modification**: $\phi_{\text{coked}} = \phi_0 (1-\theta_c)^{-1/2}$ — the key non-linear amplification.
- 🔹 **Knudsen diffusivity in narrowed pores**: $D_{\text{eff}} = D_{K,0}(1-\theta_c)$.
- 🔹 **Coke measurement**: TGA in air; reported in **wt%**; FCC limit ~15 wt%.
- 🔹 **Regeneration**: oxidative burn-off, **500–700 °C in air**; FCC uses a fluidised regenerator, reforming uses CCR.
- 🔹 **Coking vs. Poisoning**: coking is **reversible by oxidation**; poisoning is mostly **irreversible**.
- 🔹 **Filamentous coke on Ni/Fe** physically *destroys* pellets (whisker growth ejects metal).
- 🔹 **H$_2$ partial pressure** suppresses coke (H-transfer recombination); high **steam partial pressure** gasifies coke (C + H$_2$O → CO + H$_2$).
- 🔹 **Industrial examples**: FCC (Voorhies + regenerator), reforming (CCR, Cl-passivated alumina), steam cracking (TMT rise, on-stream decoking every 30–90 days), MTG on H-ZSM-5 (logarithmic coking law).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Deposition on Catalysts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Metal Deposition on Catalysts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Deposition on Catalysts** is the controlled engineering process of dispersing an active metal phase (e.g., Pt, Pd, Ni, Cu, Au, Ag, Rh) onto a high-surface-area porous support material (e.g., Al₂O₃, SiO₂, TiO₂, activated carbon, zeolites) to produce a *supported metal catalyst*. The goal is to maximize the number of accessible, catalytically active surface metal atoms while minimizing the total metal loading, thereby optimizing activity per unit cost.

The deposited metal can exist in various forms:
- **Nanoparticles** (1–100 nm, most common)
- **Single atoms** (Single-Atom Catalysts, SACs)
- **Clusters** (sub-nanometer aggregates of 2–50 atoms)
- **Thin films / overlayers**
- **Bimetallic or alloyed nanoparticles**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome (CO3) of PEPOT412 explicitly requires students to *"explain the preparation methods, metal–support interactions, and characterization of supported metal catalysts."* Metal deposition is the *preparation* step — the foundation upon which all subsequent characterization and performance testing rests.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Painted Wall" Analogy

Imagine you want to hang 1,000 valuable gold coins on a wall so that *as many coins as possible face outward* (so people can see and use them).

- If you **glue each coin flat against the wall** → high surface exposure, but you'd need 1,000 coins.
- If you **stack coins into a thick pile** → only the outer coins of each pile are visible; the buried coins are wasted.

Supported metal catalysts face the same trade-off: **noble metals like Pt cost ~₹25,000/g**, so we want every atom to do useful work. By depositing the metal as **tiny nanoparticles or even isolated single atoms on a porous support**, we maximize the *surface-to-volume ratio* — the same logic that drives nanotechnology in semiconductors, drug delivery, and battery electrodes.

The **support** acts as the "wall" — it provides mechanical strength, thermal stability, prevents metal sintering (coalescence of small particles into larger, less active ones), and sometimes participates directly in catalysis via *Metal–Support Interaction (MSI)*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | Typical Range |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Metal Loading (w)** | Mass of metal per unit mass of support | 0.1 – 20 wt% |
| **Dispersion (D)** | Fraction of metal atoms exposed on the surface | 0.01 – 1.0 |
| **Particle Size (d_p)** | Mean metal crystallite diameter | 0.5 – 50 nm |
| **Support Surface Area (S_BET)** | Brunauer–Emmett–Teller specific area | 50 – 1500 m²/g |
| **Pore Volume (V_p)** | Volume of pores per gram of support | 0.3 – 1.5 cm³/g |
| **Mean Pore Diameter (d_por)** | Average pore width | 0.5 – 50 nm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Metal Deposition

Pure bulk metals have very low surface area (a coin has ~cm²/g). To make them catalytically useful, we must:

1. **Atomize** the metal (break it into nanoscale particles or single atoms).
2. **Anchor** the particles to a support to prevent them from migrating and coalescing (*sintering*).
3. **Stabilize** the active sites against thermal degradation under reaction conditions (often 200–800 °C).
4. **Activate** the metal precursor (by reduction in H₂, oxidation in O₂, or calcination) to expose metallic surfaces.

### 2.2 Major Deposition Methods — Structured Logic

#### **Method 1: Impregnation (The Industrial Workhorse)**

The support pores are filled with a solution containing a dissolved metal precursor (e.g., H₂PtCl₆, Pd(NO₃)₂, Ni(NO₃)₂). Solvent is then removed, leaving the precursor inside the pores.

- **Wet Impregnation (Excess Solvent):** Support soaked in a large excess of precursor solution; relies on **adsorption equilibria** to control uptake. Slow, equilibrium-driven.
- **Incipient Wetness Impregnation (IWI) / Dry Impregnation:** Volume of solution = pore volume of support. Capillary forces draw the liquid in. Fast, precise control of loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Distinguish between wet impregnation and incipient wetness impregnation." This is a guaranteed 3–7 mark question.

#### **Method 2: Ion Exchange**

Used when the support has surface hydroxyl groups that can be replaced by metal cations (typical for zeolites, some oxides at controlled pH). Yields highly dispersed, often atomically isolated species.

#### **Method 3: Deposition–Precipitation (DP)**

The metal hydroxide is precipitated directly *onto* the support by slowly raising pH (often with urea, which hydrolyzes homogeneously to release OH⁻). The support acts as a *nucleation seed*, ensuring the precipitate forms *on* the support rather than in bulk solution. Excellent for **Au, Ag** on TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, CeO₂.

#### **Method 4: Co-precipitation**

Both metal precursor and support precursor (e.g., Al(NO₃)₃ for Al₂O₃) are precipitated together. The metal gets *trapped inside* the forming support matrix. High metal loadings achievable, but poor control of dispersion.

#### **Method 5: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) / Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD)**

Volatile metal precursors (e.g., Pt(acac)₂, MeCpPtMe₃) are transported in the gas phase and react with the support surface. ALD allows *atomic-scale precision* of film thickness — a frontier of single-atom catalyst synthesis.

#### **Method 6: Sol-Immobilization / Colloidal Deposition**

Pre-formed metal nanoparticles (stabilized by ligands like PVP, citrate, thiols) are mixed with the support. The ligands are then removed by calcination/reduction.

### 2.3 The Critical Post-Deposition Steps

| Step | Purpose | Typical Conditions |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| **Drying** | Remove solvent without causing redistribution of precursor | 80–120 °C, 6–24 h |
| **Calcination** | Decompose precursor → metal oxide; burn off organics | 300–600 °C in air |
| **Reduction** | Convert oxide → metallic nanoparticles (catalytically active form) | 200–450 °C in 5–10% H₂/N₂ |
| **Passivation** | Form thin oxide skin for safe handling/exposure to air | 1% O₂/He at RT |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Three T's" of Metal Deposition (KTU Mnemonic):** **T**emperature, **T**ime, **T**ransport phenomena — these govern crystal growth, dispersion, and final catalyst properties.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $w = \dfrac{m_{\text{metal}}}{m_{\text{support}}} \times 100\%$ | Metal loading (wt%) | % |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{N_{M,\text{surf}}}{N_{M,\text{total}}}$ | Dispersion (dimensionless) | – |
| 3 | $D \approx \dfrac{1.08}{d_p\,\text{[nm]}}$ (FCC metals) | Approximate dispersion–size relation | – |
| 4 | $d_p = \dfrac{6 \, v_m}{a_m \cdot D}$ | Mean particle diameter (general, sphere model) | nm |
| 5 | $V_{\text{soln}} = m_{\text{support}} \times V_p$ | Solution volume for IWI | cm³ |
| 6 | $N_{\text{surf}} = D \times N_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{D \times w \cdot m_{\text{cat}}}{M_{\text{metal}}}$ | Number of surface metal atoms | atoms |
| 7 | $TOF = \dfrac{\dot{r}}{N_{\text{surf}}}$ | Turnover frequency (kinetics link) | s⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to compute dispersion, the *spherical particle model* is the default. Cubic model: $D = \dfrac{6\,(v_m/a_m)}{d_p}$, where $v_m$ is the atomic volume (cm³/mol ÷ N_A) and $a_m$ is the area occupied by one surface atom.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive three-way catalysts:** Pt/Pd/Rh on CeO₂–ZrO₂–Al₂O₃ washcoats.
- **Petroleum refining:** Pt on Al₂O₃ or zeolite (reforming); Ni on Al₂O₃ (hydrogenation).
- **Ammonia synthesis:** Fe on K₂O–Al₂O₃–CaO (Haber–Bosch).
- **Fuel cells:** Pt/C nanoparticles on carbon black for ORR.
- **CO oxidation, VOC abatement:** Au/TiO₂, Au/Fe₂O₃ (Haruta catalysts).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Spherical-Particle Dispersion Relation

**Starting premise:** A metal nanoparticle is approximated as a sphere of diameter $d_p$. We want the fraction of atoms lying *on the surface* of this sphere.

**Step 1 — Volume of one nanoparticle:**

$$V_{\text{np}} = \frac{\pi \, d_p^3}{6}$$

**Step 2 — Total number of metal atoms in the particle:**

The atomic volume of the metal is $v_m$ (volume per atom, in cm³/atom).

$$N_{\text{total}} = \frac{V_{\text{np}}}{v_m} = \frac{\pi \, d_p^3}{6 \, v_m}$$

**Step 3 — Number of surface atoms:**

A single surface atom occupies an area $a_m$ on the surface of the sphere. The total surface area of the sphere is $\pi d_p^2$, so:

$$N_{\text{surf}} = \frac{\pi \, d_p^2}{a_m}$$

**Step 4 — Form the dispersion ratio:**

$$D = \frac{N_{\text{surf}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi d_p^2}{a_m}}{\dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6\,v_m}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify (cancel $\pi$ and $d_p^2$):**

$$D = \frac{6\,v_m}{a_m \, d_p}$$

This is the **fundamental spherical-particle dispersion equation**, valid for any supported metal catalyst when the particles are roughly spherical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on $v_m$:** If the molar volume is $V_m$ (cm³/mol), then $v_m = V_m / N_A$, where $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol. For Pt, $V_m \approx 9.1$ cm³/mol, giving $v_m \approx 1.51 \times 10^{-23}$ cm³/atom.

**Step 6 — Approximate form for FCC metals:**

For face-centered cubic metals (Pt, Pd, Au, Ag, Cu, Ni, Rh, Ir), with $a_m \approx 7.7 \times 10^{-16}$ cm²/atom and $v_m \approx 1.4$–$1.7 \times 10^{-23}$ cm³/atom, the coefficient $\dfrac{6\,v_m}{a_m}$ evaluates to approximately **1.08 nm**. Therefore:

$$D \approx \frac{1.08}{d_p\,\text{[nm]}}$$

**Verification:** A 1 nm particle → $D \approx 1.08$ (physically meaningful: all atoms on surface). A 10 nm particle → $D \approx 0.108$ (~11% of atoms on surface). Consistent with TEM/XRD literature.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Problem)
