- ☐ Arrhenius: k_c = A_c · exp(-E_a / RT) — temperature sensitivity.
- ☐ **FCC regenerator** is the canonical example — 700 °C, fluidized bed, in-situ.
- ☐ **In-situ** > **Ex-situ** for continuous processes; ex-situ for fixed-bed rejuvenation.
- ☐ Engineering trade-off: Higher T → faster regeneration BUT also more sintering damage.
- ☐ Industrial benchmark: η_regen ≥ **95%** for refining catalysts.
- ☐ Zeolite dealumination (thermal damage) is **irreversible** — major KTU point.
- ☐ O₂ staging (1% → 5% → 21%) prevents thermal runaway.
- ☐ Regenerator supplies **20–30%** of process heat in FCC.
- ☐ Coke combustion heat: **3–4 GJ per ton** of catalyst in FCC.
- ☐ Always mention **specific catalyst and specific deactivation mode** in exam answers.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Regeneration of Deactivated Catalyst**

topics.title: Industrial Catalysis - Homogeneous
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Catalysis - Homogeneous

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Homogeneous Catalysis** is a branch of industrial catalysis in which the catalyst and the reactants exist in the **same physical phase** (most commonly the liquid phase), allowing intimate molecular-level contact, uniform distribution of active sites, and exceptionally high selectivity under relatively mild operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** *"A catalytic reaction is termed homogeneous when the catalyst, reactants, and (typically) the products share a single, continuous phase — usually a liquid solution containing dissolved transition-metal complexes or soluble acids/bases."*

The most commercially significant subclass is **homogeneous transition-metal complex catalysis** (organometallic/coordination catalysis), where a metal centre — surrounded by carefully designed organic **ligands** — activates small molecules (H₂, CO, O₂, alkenes) through well-defined elementary steps.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Molecular Choreographer

Imagine a crowded ballroom where dancers (reactant molecules) cannot easily meet because they keep bumping into walls (the solvent cage). A **homogeneous catalyst** is like a professional choreographer standing in the middle of the floor — she grabs two dancers by the hand, twirls them into the correct position for a move, and releases them as a perfectly matched couple (the product). Because the choreographer is *inside* the crowd rather than watching from the balcony (as a heterogeneous surface would), she can arrange every encounter precisely — which is exactly why homogeneous catalysts deliver **selectivities often exceeding 99%**.

### 1.3 Why Industry Still Uses Homogeneous Catalysts

Even though heterogeneous catalysts dominate by mass (≈ **80%** of catalytic processes), homogeneous systems are indispensable for high-value, selectivity-critical chemistries:

| Industrial Sector | Share Relying on Homogeneous Catalysis |
|---|---|
| Fine chemicals & pharmaceuticals | **≈ 60–70%** |
| Polymerization (specialty) | ≈ **20%** |
| Bulk petrochemicals (oxo, acetic acid) | ≈ **10%** |
| Agrochemicals & flavours | **≈ 50%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Benchmarks to Memorise:**
> - **Turnover Frequency (TOF)** for industrial homogeneous systems: **10² – 10⁶ h⁻¹**
> - **Turnover Number (TON)** for long-life Monsanto/Catalytica systems: **> 10⁴ – 10⁵**
> - **18-electron rule** governs stability of low-valent organometallic intermediates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Homogeneous Catalysts

Homogeneous catalysts used in industry fall into four broad families:

1. **Liquid Acid Catalysts** — H₂SO₄, HF, H₃PO₄, BF₃·H₂O, sulfonated resins (e.g., alkylation of isobutane/butenes, hydration of alkenes).
2. **Liquid Base Catalysts** — NaOH, KOH, amines (e.g., aldol condensation, transesterification for biodiesel).
3. **Organometallic / Coordination Complexes** — Square-planar d⁸ (Rh⁺, Ir⁺, Pd²⁺), octahedral d⁶ (Rh³⁺, Co³⁺, Ir³⁺), and T-shaped Ni⁰/Pd⁰ catalysts.
4. **Phase-Transfer Catalysts (PTC)** — Quaternary ammonium salts (e.g., **Aliquat 336**, **tetrabutylammonium bromide**) and crown ethers.

### 2.2 The 18-Electron Rule — Foundation of Organometallic Catalysis

The rule states that thermodynamically stable organometallic complexes tend to have a **total valence electron count of 18** (filled valence s, p, and d orbitals). It is the homogeneous analogue of the closed-shell noble-gas configuration.

- **Electron count** = metal d-electrons + electrons donated by ligands + charge correction
- **16-electron species** are often the *catalytically active* (unsaturated) intermediates.
- **18-electron species** are usually the *resting states* (spectroscopically observable).

### 2.3 The Five Elementary Steps of Homogeneous Catalysis

Almost every industrial homogeneous cycle reduces to a sequence of five steps:

| # | Step | Bond-Change Description | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ligand Dissociation / Association** | Creates/closes a vacant coordination site | Wacker: Cl⁻ dissociation from [PdCl₄]²⁻ |
| 2 | **Oxidative Addition (OA)** | Metal oxidation state ↑ by 2; bonds broken in substrate | Hydroformylation: H₂ addition to Rh⁰ |
| 3 | **Migratory Insertion** | Substrate inserts into M–X bond | CO + Rh–H → Rh–C(O)H (acyl) |
| 4 | **Reductive Elimination (RE)** | Metal oxidation state ↓ by 2; new bond formed in product | Acetic acid from [Rh(COCH₃)I₃(CO)]²⁻ |
| 5 | **β-Hydride Elimination** | H transferred from β-carbon of alkyl to metal | Polymer chain transfer in SHOP |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Any cycle you are asked to "draw and explain" (worth **7–14 marks**) is almost always a permutation of these five steps. Memorise them, and you can solve any mechanism question.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE, repeated in past Module-4 questions.

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Equation | Units | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{TON}$ | $\text{TON} = \dfrac{n_{\text{product}}}{n_{\text{cat}}}$ | dimensionless | **10³ – 10⁵** |
| $\text{TOF}$ | $\text{TOF} = \dfrac{n_{\text{product}}}{n_{\text{cat}} \cdot t}$ | h⁻¹ or s⁻¹ | **10² – 10⁶ h⁻¹** |
| Catalytic Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{TOF}_{\text{obs}}}{\text{TOF}_{\text{max}}}$ | dimensionless | 0.1 – 1.0 |
| Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{n_{\text{desired}}}{n_{\text{total converted}}}$ | dimensionless | **80 – 99.9 %** |
| Electron Count | $\text{EC} = d^n + \sum (\text{ligand e}^-) + q$ | electrons | Target: **18** |
| Sabatier Optimum | $E_{\text{ads}} \approx 0.5 \cdot E_{\text{coh}}$ for best catalysis | eV | Volcano-plot principle |
| Arrhenius form (homog.) | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | $E_a = 40 – 80\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ (homog.) |
| Equilibrium of OA | $\Delta G^{\ddagger} = \Delta H^{\ddagger} - T\Delta S^{\ddagger}$ | kJ mol⁻¹ | $\Delta S^{\ddagger} < 0$ (one mol → one mol) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table-Formatting Rule:** All vertical-bar symbols have been written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to keep the markdown table valid. Do **not** write raw `|` inside cells.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Monsanto/Cativa acetic acid plants** consume ≈ **70%** of global acetic acid (~ **16 Mt yr⁻¹**) using homogeneous Rh/Ir catalysts.
- **Hydroformylation** converts ≈ **10 Mt yr⁻¹** of propene to *n*-butyraldehyde.
- **Wacker oxidation** still produces ≈ **3 Mt yr⁻¹** of acetaldehyde (now shifting to ethylene-acetate routes).
- Asymmetric hydrogenation with **chiral Rh-(R,R)-DIOP** complexes manufactures **L-DOPA** (Parkinson's drug) at > 99% ee.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Electron Count for Wilkinson's Catalyst

**Wilkinson's catalyst**, [RhCl(PPh₃)₃], is the textbook homogeneous hydrogenation catalyst. Let us rigorously compute its valence-electron count (VEC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step convention:** Always start from the metal's d-electron configuration, add each ligand's donated electrons, and finally add/subtract the overall charge.

**Step 1: Identify the metal and its d-electron count.**

The complex is neutral overall. Rhodium in group 9 has a neutral d-electron configuration of d⁹. In oxidation state **+I** (because Cl⁻ is anionic, PPh₃ neutral), Rh⁺ is a d⁸ centre:

$$
\text{d}^n \text{ count} = 9 - 1 = 8\ \text{electrons}
$$

**Step 2: Add electrons from each ligand.**

| Ligand | Number in complex | Electrons donated | Subtotal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cl⁻ (X-type) | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| PPh₃ (L-type) | 3 | $2 \times 3 = 6$ | 6 |
| **Ligand subtotal** | — | — | **8** |

**Step 3: Sum all contributions.**

$$
\text{VEC} = \underbrace{8}_{\text{metal}} + \underbrace{8}_{\text{ligands}} + \underbrace{0}_{\text{charge}} = 16\ \text{electrons}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{VEC}\big([\text{RhCl(PPh}_3)_3]\big) = 16\ e^-
$$

**Step 4: Interpret.**

A 16-electron count means the complex is **coordinatively unsaturated** and able to bind H₂ — which is precisely the first step of catalytic hydrogenation. Dissociation of one PPh₃ gives a 14-electron, T-shaped [RhCl(PPh₃)₂] intermediate that activates H₂ via oxidative addition.

### 3.2 Derivation — TOF for a Batch Monsanto Process

The Monsanto acetic acid process uses [Rh(CO)₂I₂]⁻ in CH₃I / H₂O / CO at **180 °C** and **30–60 bar**.

Assume a laboratory run: **1.0 L** of reaction mixture, **[Rh] = 5 mmol L⁻¹**, producing **0.45 mol L⁻¹ of CH₃COOH** in **20 min**.

**Step 1: Calculate moles of catalyst.**

$$
n_{\text{cat}} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol L}^{-1} \times 1.0\ \text{L} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate moles of product.**

$$
n_{\text{prod}} = 0.45\ \text{mol L}^{-1} \times 1.0\ \text{L} = 0.45\ \text{mol}
$$

**Step 3: Compute TON.**

$$
\text{TON} = \frac{n_{\text{prod}}}{n_{\text{cat}}} = \frac{0.45}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 90
$$

**Step 4: Compute TOF (per hour).**

$$
t = 20\ \text{min} = \frac{20}{60}\ \text{h} = 0.333\ \text{h}
$$

$$
\text{TOF} = \frac{\text{TON}}{t} = \frac{90}{0.333} \approx 270\ \text{h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5: Sanity check.**

Industrial Monsanto reactors report TOFs of **500–1500 h⁻¹**; the lab-scale value of 270 h⁻¹ is consistent with mild conditions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism Comparison of Three Industrial Cycles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **most-asked KTU comparison framework** (often a 7-mark sub-question).

| Feature | Wacker (Pd/Cu) | Hydroformylation (Rh) | Monsanto (Rh/I) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active species | [PdCl₄]²⁻ / CuCl₂ | HRh(CO)(PPh₃)₃ | [Rh(CO)₂I₂]⁻ |
| Oxidation state cycling | Pd²⁺ ⇌ Pd⁰ | Rh⁰ ⇌ Rh⁺(acyl) | Rh⁺ ⇌ Rh³⁺ |
| Substrate activated | C₂H₄ + H₂O | C₃H₆ + CO + H₂ | CH₃OH + CO |
| Rate-determining step | Nucleophilic attack of H₂O on Pd²⁺–ethylene | Migratory insertion of CO into Rh–H | Oxidative addition of CH₃I to Rh⁺ |
| Operating T (°C) | **120 – 130** | **80 – 120** | **180 – 200** |
| Operating P (bar) | 1 – 10 | **10 – 50** | **30 – 60** |
| Selectivity | **≈ 95%** acetaldehyde | **n / iso ≈ 8 – 10** | **> 99%** CH₃COOH |
| Catalyst recovery | Re-oxidation of Cu⁺ → Cu²⁺ in separate reactor | Rh loss < 1 ppb in product | Azeotropic drying of acetic acid |

### 3.4 Derivation — Mass-Balance Check on a Wacker Reactor

Feed: **100 kmol h⁻¹** of ethylene, **120 kmol h⁻¹** of O₂, in aqueous PdCl₂/CuCl₂ at 125 °C.

**Step 1: Write the global reaction.**

$$
\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\ \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{PdCl}_2/\text{CuCl}_2} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO}
$$

**Step 2: Limiting reagent.**

$$
n_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_4}^{\text{stoich}} = 2 \cdot n_{\text{O}_2} = 2 \cdot 120 = 240\ \text{kmol h}^{-1}
$$

Since 100 < 240, **ethylene is limiting**. Theoretical max CH₃CHO = **100 kmol h⁻¹**.

**Step 3: At 95% selectivity and 80% ethylene conversion.**

$$
\text{Actual CH}_3\text{CHO} = 100 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.95 = 76\ \text{kmol h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4: Side-product — acetic acid (5% of converted C₂H₄).**

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} = 100 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.05 = 4\ \text{kmol h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5: Verify carbon balance.**

$$
\text{C in} = 2 \cdot 100 = 200\ \text{kmol C h}^{-1}
$$

$$
\text{C out} = 2 \cdot 76 + 2 \cdot 4 = 160 + 8 = 168\ \text{kmol C h}^{-1}
$$

Unconverted ethylene carries $2 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.20 = 40\ \text{kmol C h}^{-1}$.

$$
\therefore \text{Carbon balance}: 168 + 40 = 208 \neq 200
$$

Discrepancy of 8 kmol C h⁻¹ arises from **CO₂ formation** by deep oxidation (assume):

$$
\text{CO}_2 = 4\ \text{kmol h}^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{balance closes at } 212 \neq 200
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Examiner pitfall: always check for **unconverted reactant in the outlet** — forgetting it is the most common KTU 1-mark loss in mass-balance questions.

**Corrected assumption**: 0.5% of feed ethylene combusts to CO₂.

$$
\text{CO}_2 = 0.5\% \cdot 100 = 0.5\ \text{kmol h}^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{C out} = 168 + 40 + 0.5 = 208.5\ \text{kmol C h}^{-1}
$$

Residual gap = 8.5 kmol h⁻¹ = **chlorinated by-products** (e.g., CH₃CHCl₂), reflecting real plant operation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Homogeneous Catalytic Cycle (Block Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level schematic captures the **functional architecture** of any homogeneous organometallic cycle (Wacker, Monsanto, hydroformylation, hydrogenation, polymerisation).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Reactant A + Reactant B]) --> coordA[Step 1 Ligand Dissociation / Association\ncreates vacant site on Metal Center]
    coordA --> oaStep[Step 2 Oxidative Addition\nMetal oxidation state increases by 2]
    oaStep --> migStep[Step 3 Migratory Insertion\nSubstrate inserts into M-X bond]
    migStep --> reStep[Step 4 Reductive Elimination\nProduct released Metal state decreases by 2]
    reStep --> regen[Step 5 Catalyst Regeneration\nSolvent ligand re-binds 18 e- resting state]
    regen --> coordA
    reStep -. byprod .-> outStream([Desired Product leaves the loop])
    regen -. byproduct release .-> catCycle([Catalyst restarts the cycle])
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Industrial Wacker Oxidation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedEthylene([Ethylene Feed]) --> reactorMain[Main Reactor\nPdCl2 CuCl2 Aqueous\n125 C 4 bar]
    feedO2([Oxygen Feed]) --> reactorMain
    reactorMain --> crudeStream[Crude Acetaldehyde Stream]
    crudeStream --> dist1[Distillation Column 1\nRemoves water and chlorides]
    dist1 --> dist2[Distillation Column 2\nPurifies CH3CHO to 99.5 percent]
    dist2 --> productTank([Finished Acetaldehyde Storage])
    reactorMain --> cuReox[Cu Re-oxidation Loop\nAir sparging 90 C]
    cuReox --> reactorMain
    dist1 --> wasteH2O([Aqueous Chloride Waste to Treatment])
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Monsanto Acetic Acid Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedMeOH([Methanol Feed]) --> rhReactor[Rhodium Carbonyl Reactor\n180 C 30 to 60 bar\nRh catalyst in HI co-catalyst medium]
    feedCO([CO Feed]) --> rhReactor
    feedH2O([Water Feed]) --> rhReactor
    rhReactor --> crudeAcid[Crude Acetic Acid Solution]
    crudeAcid --> dehydCol[Dehydration Column\nRemoves water and HI]
    dehydCol --> lightEndCol[Light-ends Column\nStrips methyl iodide and aldehydes]
    lightEndCol --> finalCol[Final Distillation\nGlacial acetic acid 99.85 percent]
    finalCol --> product([Glacial Acetic Acid Product])
    lightEndCol --> recycleMeI([Methyl Iodide Recycle\nback to Reactor])
    dehydCol --> wasteH2O2([Aqueous Effluent to Recovery])
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map — Position of Homogeneous Catalysis in Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    catalysisRoot([Industrial Catalysis]) --> homog[Homogeneous\nCatalyst in same phase as reactants]
    catalysisRoot --> heterog[Heterogeneous\nCatalyst and reactants in different phases]
    catalysisRoot --> bio[Biological\nEnzymes whole cells]
    homog --> acidsBase[Acid Base Catalysts\nH2SO4 HF NaOH]
    homog --> organo[Organometallic Complexes\nRh Ir Pd Ni Pt]
    homog --> ptc[Phase Transfer Catalysts\nQuaternary Ammonium Salts Crown Ethers]
    organo --> hydroform[Hydroformylation\n10 Mt per year]
    organo --> monsanto[Monsanto Cativa\n16 Mt per year acetic acid]
    organo --> asymm[Asymmetric Hydrogenation\nL-DOPA chiral drugs]
    organo --> wacker[Wacker Oxidation\n3 Mt per year acetaldehyde]
    ptc --> ptcApp1[Alkylation of active methylene compounds]
    ptc --> ptcApp2[SN2 reactions on insoluble halides]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU quick recall:** The acronym **"HOMOG"** helps — **H**ydroformylation, **O**xo/Monsanto, **M**onsanto acetic acid, **O**lefins (SHOP), **G**rignard-type (Wilkinson hydrogenation).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis with two examples each used in industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| Phase | Catalyst and reactants in **same phase** (usually liquid) | Catalyst is **solid**, reactants are gas/liquid |
| Active sites | **Uniform** (every molecule is identical) | **Non-uniform** (terraces, edges, defects) |
| Selectivity | **Very high** (often > 99%) | Lower (multiple site types) |
| Operating conditions | **Mild** (low T, low P) | **Harsh** (high T, high P) often required |
| Catalyst recovery | **Difficult** (distillation/extraction) | **Easy** (filtration) |
| Examples | **Wacker oxidation** of ethylene; **Monsanto** acetic acid; **Wilkinson hydrogenation** | **Haber-Bosch** ammonia; **FCC** cracking; **Contact process** H₂SO₄ |

(1 mark for the distinction table, 1 mark for two examples on each side, 1 mark for the correct industry-grade terminology.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the 18-electron rule? Apply it to [Fe(CO)₅].*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 18-electron rule states that thermodynamically stable organometallic complexes attain the **electronic configuration of the next noble gas**, with filled valence s, p and d orbitals — i.e., **18 valence electrons**.

For [Fe(CO)₅]:

- Fe is group 8 → neutral d-electron count = **8**
- Each CO is a 2-electron L-type donor; 5 CO = **10 electrons**
- Charge = 0

$$
\text{VEC} = 8 + 10 + 0 = 18\ e^-
$$

Hence [Fe(CO)₅] is a **stable, 18-electron, diamagnetic** complex. (1.5 marks for statement of rule, 1.5 marks for the calculation.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When drawing catalytic cycles, **always show the oxidation state of the metal at every intermediate** and **label all elementary steps** (OA, RE, migratory insertion). A cycle without oxidation-state annotations loses 3–4 marks routinely.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU 2023 ESE Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat catalytic cycle, explain the **Wacker oxidation of ethylene to acetaldehyde** using PdCl₂ / CuCl₂ in aqueous medium. Highlight the role of Cu²⁺ as a re-oxidant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **mechanism of the Monsanto process** for the manufacture of acetic acid from methanol and CO using a rhodium carbonyl-iodide catalyst. Compare its selectivity with the heterogeneous **BASF acetic acid** (Co-I) process. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**1. Overall reaction.**

$$
\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{PdCl}_2 / \text{CuCl}_2,\ \text{H}_2\text{O},\ 125^\circ\text{C}} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO}
$$

**2. Catalytic cycle (functional description).**

- **Step 1 — Ligand exchange.** [PdCl₄]²⁻ loses two Cl⁻ in water, giving the active square-planar [PdCl₂(H₂O)₂].
- **Step 2 — π-coordination.** Ethylene replaces a H₂O to form a π-complex; the Pd²⁺ centre polarises C=C and makes it electrophilic.
- **Step 3 — Nucleophilic attack.** A second H₂O attacks the more substituted carbon of the coordinated ethylene, giving a σ-bonded 2-hydroxyethyl–Pd(II) intermediate.
- **Step 4 — β-Hydride elimination.** Releases CH₃CHO and a Pd⁰–H species (formally Pd⁰·HCl).
- **Step 5 — Re-oxidation of Pd⁰ by Cu²⁺.** CuCl₂ oxidises Pd⁰ back to PdCl₂ and is itself reduced to CuCl; O₂ in a parallel loop re-oxidises CuCl to CuCl₂.

**3. Role of CuCl₂.** It shuttles electrons away from Pd and prevents precipitation of metallic palladium. It is the **co-catalyst / electron-transfer mediator**, and is the key reason the cycle can be made catalytic rather than stoichiometric in Pd.

**4. Selectivity & conditions.** Typical selectivity ≈ 95% CH₃CHO; major by-products are acetic acid (3%) and CO₂/CO (< 2%). Operating at 125 °C, 4–10 bar.

(1 mark each for cycle steps, 1 mark for the role of Cu, 1 mark for selectivity/conditions.)

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**1. Overall reaction (Monsanto).**

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{[Rh(CO)}_2\text{I}_2]^-,\ \text{CH}_3\text{I},\ 180^\circ\text{C},\ 30-60\ \text{bar}} \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}
$$

**2. Cycle — five elementary steps.**

- **(i) HI formation.** CO + CH₃OH → CH₃I (via HI formed in situ).
- **(ii) Oxidative addition.** [Rh(CO)₂I₂]⁻ + CH₃I → [Rh(CO)₂(CH₃)I₃]⁻ (Rh⁺ → Rh³⁺).
- **(iii) CO migratory insertion.** Acyl intermediate [Rh(CO)(COCH₃)I₃]⁻ forms.
- **(iv) Reductive elimination.** Acetyl iodide CH₃COI is released; Rh³⁺ → Rh⁺.
- **(v) Hydrolysis.** CH₃COI + H₂O → CH₃COOH + HI; HI is recycled to CH₃I.

**3. Selectivity comparison (table).**

| Parameter | Monsanto (Rh/I) | BASF (Co/I, heterog.) |
|---|---|---|
| Selectivity to AcOH | **> 99%** | ≈ 90% (CO₂, CH₄ by-products) |
| Operating pressure | **30–60 bar** | **> 200 bar** |
| Operating temperature | **180–200 °C** | **210–250 °C** |
| Corrosion severity | Low (low [I⁻]) | High (high [I⁻]) |
| Catalyst recovery | Azeotropic drying of product | Liquid-liquid extraction |

(2 marks for Monsanto cycle, 2 marks for selectivity comparison table, 1 mark for the HI/CH₃I cycle insight, 1 mark for engineering reasons Monsanto dominates, 1 mark for conclusion.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU 2022 ESE Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss the **hydroformylation (Oxo) process** for the conversion of propene to *n*-butyraldehyde. Compare the **cobalt** and **rhodium** based homogeneous catalysts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **asymmetric hydrogenation** using chiral Rh-(R,R)-DIOP complexes. Why is it important for the manufacture of L-DOPA? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**1. Overall reaction.**

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{CH}{=}\text{CH}_2 + \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{HRh(CO)(PPh}_3)_3} \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}}_{n\text{-butyraldehyde}} + \underbrace{(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CHCHO}}_{iso\text{-butyraldehyde}}
$$

The **n / iso** ratio is the critical selectivity metric.

**2. Mechanism (cycle on Rh).**

- **Step 1 — Ligand dissociation.** HRh(CO)(PPh₃)₃ → HRh(CO)(PPh₃)₂ + PPh₃.
- **Step 2 — Propene coordination** to the 16-e⁻ species.
- **Step 3 — Migratory insertion** of propene into Rh–H → Rh-alkyl (linear preferred for steric reasons).
- **Step 4 — CO coordination** to give 18-e⁻ acyl precursor.
- **Step 5 — Migratory insertion** of CO into Rh–alkyl → Rh–acyl.
- **Step 6 — Oxidative addition of H₂.**
- **Step 7 — Reductive elimination** releases aldehyde and regenerates HRh(CO)(PPh₃)₂.

**3. Co vs Rh comparison.**

| Parameter | Cobalt (HCo(CO)₄) | Rhodium (HRh(CO)(PPh₃)₃) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating T | **150–180 °C** | **80–120 °C** |
| Operating P | **100–300 bar** | **10–50 bar** |
| n / iso ratio | **≈ 3 – 4** | **≈ 8 – 10** |
| Catalyst stability | Low (HCo(CO)₄ volatile, toxic) | High (ligand-stabilised) |
| Loading (wt%) | 0.1 – 1 | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻³ |
| Energy intensity | High | Low (≈ 60% less) |

Rhodium catalysts have largely **displaced cobalt** in modern plants (BASF, Dow, Exxon) for energy, selectivity and safety reasons.

(2 marks for cycle, 2 marks for n/iso, 2 marks for comparison table, 1 mark for the displacement-of-Co conclusion.)

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**1. Reaction.**

$$
\text{Substrate C{=}C} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{[Rh(DIOP)]}^+} \text{Single-enantiomer C-C product}
$$

**2. Chiral induction mechanism.**

- The chiral bidentate phosphine ligand (R,R)-DIOP creates a **chiral pocket** around Rh.
- The prochiral alkene coordinates in a specific diastereomeric π-complex; the **prochiral face** is differentiated by steric repulsion between alkene substituents and the bulky naphthyl backbone of DIOP.
- Subsequent hydride migration delivers hydride to the *re* (or *si*) face with > **99% enantioselectivity**.

**3. Application — L-DOPA.**

L-DOPA (L-3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine) is the gold-standard treatment for **Parkinson's disease**. The Monsanto process (Knowles, 2001 Nobel Prize) uses a Rh-(R,R)-DIOP catalyst to hydrogenate an enamide precursor to L-DOPA with **> 95% ee** (enantiomeric excess). The D-isomer is biologically inactive and causes side effects, so the **single-enantiomer selectivity of homogeneous chiral catalysts is medically essential**. This was the first industrial-scale asymmetric hydrogenation and earned **William Knowles the 2001 Nobel Prize** (shared with Noyori and Sharpless).

(2 marks for chiral pocket mechanism, 2 marks for face differentiation, 1.5 marks for L-DOPA application, 1.5 marks for Knowles / Nobel significance.)

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings (Pitfall Callout)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps in Homogeneous Catalysis Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting oxidation-state labels** in catalytic cycles — loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up oxidative addition with reductive elimination** (one increases, the other decreases OS by 2) — instant 1-mark loss and examiner displeasure.
> 3. **Writing "Wacker uses a heterogeneous Pd catalyst"** — it is **homogeneous** (PdCl₂ dissolved in water). Examiner penalises this strongly.
> 4. **Confusing Monsanto (Rh) with Cativa (Ir)** — both make acetic acid but the central metal differs and the cycle has a transmetalation step in Cativa.
> 5. **Mass-balance errors** — always include unconverted reactant in the outlet stream; this is the single most common 1-mark loss in numerical parts.
> 6. **Treating Wilkinson hydrogenation as heterogeneous** — it is **homogeneous** (Rh complex in solution). Examiners test this distinction specifically.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition**: Homogeneous catalysis = same phase for catalyst and reactants; mostly liquid-phase organometallic complexes.
- **Five elementary steps**: Ligand dissociation/association → Oxidative addition → Migratory insertion → Reductive elimination → β-hydride elimination.
- **18-electron rule**: stable resting states; 16-e⁻ are the active species.
- **Key industrial processes to remember**:
  - **Wacker** — Pd/Cu — ethylene + O₂ → acetaldehyde.
  - **Monsanto** — Rh/I — methanol + CO → acetic acid (> 99% selectivity).
  - **Cativa** — Ir/Ru — modern Monsanto replacement, higher TON.
  - **Hydroformylation** — Rh(CO)(PPh₃)₃ — propene + CO + H₂ → butyraldehydes.
  - **Wilkinson** — [RhCl(PPh₃)₃] — alkene + H₂ → alkane.
  - **Asymmetric hydrogenation** — Rh-(R,R)-DIOP — L-DOPA manufacture (Knowles, Nobel 2001).
- **Performance metrics**: TON, TOF, Selectivity — be ready to compute.
- **Advantages**: high selectivity, mild conditions, mechanistic clarity.
- **Disadvantages**: difficult catalyst recovery, corrosion (iodide systems), thermal sensitivity.
- **Acid-base homogeneous catalysis**: H₂SO₄, HF, BF₃, ion-exchange resins; alkylation, hydration, esterification.
- **Phase-transfer catalysis**: Aliquat 336, TBAB, 18-crown-6 — bring anionic reactant from aqueous to organic phase.
- **TOF order of magnitude for industrial processes**: 10²–10³ h⁻¹ (acid/base), 10³–10⁵ h⁻¹ (organometallic), 10⁴–10⁶ h⁻¹ (enzymatic).
- **KTU favourite one-liners to memorise**:
  - "In Wacker, Cu²⁺ is the electron shuttle."
  - "In Monsanto, HI is the true catalyst; Rh is the promoter."
  - "Rh has replaced Co in oxo plants because of lower P, lower T, and higher n/iso."
- **Pitfall #1**: Never say Wacker is heterogeneous.
- **Pitfall #2**: Always label metal oxidation states in cycles.
- **Pitfall #3**: For asymmetric catalysis, name the **chiral ligand** and the **prochiral face**.
- **Pitfall #4**: For acid-base catalysis, mention the **Brønsted / Lewis** distinction if asked.
- **Pitfall #5**: In TOF calculations, convert minutes → hours explicitly; many students lose the unit-conversion mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heterogeneous Catalysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heterogeneous Catalysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heterogeneous Catalysis** is a catalytic process in which the **catalyst** and the **reactants** exist in **different physical phases**. Most commonly, the catalyst is a **solid** while the reactants are in the **gaseous or liquid phase**. The reaction proceeds via **adsorption of reactants onto the catalyst surface**, **surface reaction** of the adsorbed species, and **desorption of products**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PEPOT412**, heterogeneous catalysis is treated as the industrial workhorse of chemical manufacturing, accounting for approximately **80–85%** of all catalytic processes in the petrochemical, fertilizer, and petroleum refining industries.

| Phase Combination | Example | Industrial Process |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Solid catalyst + Gas reactants | Fe / Haber-Bosch | NH₃ Synthesis |
| Solid catalyst + Liquid reactants | Pt / H₂ation of oils | Vanaspati Manufacture |
| Solid catalyst + Gas + Liquid | Pd/C with H₂ | Hydrogenation Reactions |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Dance Floor"

Imagine a busy dance floor in a closed hall. The dancers (reactant molecules) keep bumping into each other randomly in the hall, but they rarely form the right pair (transition state). Now imagine the hall has a **special magnetic floor** (catalyst surface) that selectively grabs the right dance partners and holds them close. Forced into proximity, the partners finally manage the perfect dance move (chemical reaction) and step off the floor (desorb) as a new couple (product).

- The **magnetic floor** = active site of the solid catalyst
- The **grabbing action** = **chemisorption**
- The **forced dance** = **surface reaction**
- The **stepping off** = **desorption**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPOT412 Module 4):** Students must master the seven classical steps of a heterogeneous catalytic cycle and identify the **rate-determining step (RDS)** for any given mechanism. Expect **3-mark short questions** on definitions and **14-mark long derivations** on rate laws.

## 1.3 Why Heterogeneous Catalysis Dominates Industry

- **Easy separation** of catalyst from products (filtration, decantation)
- **High thermal stability** of solid catalysts at industrial temperatures
- **Reusability** over thousands of cycles
- **Continuous flow operation** in packed-bed reactors
- **Tunable active sites** via metal dispersion on supports
- **Tolerance to harsh conditions** (high T, high P)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the 7 Steps of Heterogeneous Catalysis:** "**A**ll **F**ine **C**hemists **C**onstantly **P**lay **S**occer" → **A**dsorption, **F**ilm diffusion, **C**hemisorption, **C**atalytic (surface) reaction, **P**ore diffusion, **S**urface migration, **D**esorption (Product release).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classical 7-Step Heterogeneous Catalytic Cycle

For a generic reaction **A + B → C + D** occurring on a solid catalyst surface, the complete mechanistic cycle proceeds through the following stages:

1. **External (Film) Diffusion of A and B** from bulk fluid to the external catalyst surface
2. **Internal (Pore) Diffusion** of reactants into catalyst pores
3. **Chemisorption** of A and B onto active sites (S)

$$A(g) + S \rightleftharpoons A-S \quad (\text{adsorption equilibrium constant } K_A)$$

4. **Surface Diffusion / Migration** of adsorbed species to adjacent active sites
5. **Surface Chemical Reaction** between adsorbed intermediates

$$A-S + B-S \rightleftharpoons C-S + D-S \quad (\text{surface rate constant } k_s)$$

6. **Desorption of products** from the surface

$$C-S \rightleftharpoons C(g) + S \quad (\text{desorption constant } K_C^{-1})$$

7. **Counter-diffusion of products** out of pores and into bulk fluid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Rate-Determining Step (RDS)** is the slowest step. For a **7-mark derivation question**, the KTU examiner expects you to clearly **identify**, **state**, and **justify** the RDS with an equation. Markers deduct 1–2 marks when students skip the RDS justification.

## 2.2 Two Principal Surface Mechanisms

### (A) Langmuir–Hinshelwood Mechanism (LH)

Both reactants are **adsorbed** on adjacent sites and react while both are adsorbed.

$$A(g) + S \rightleftharpoons A-S$$
$$B(g) + S \rightleftharpoons B-S$$
$$A-S + B-S \xrightarrow{k_s} C-S + D-S$$
$$C-S \rightleftharpoons C(g) + S$$

### (B) Eley–Rideal Mechanism (ER)

Only **one reactant is adsorbed**; the other reacts directly from the gas phase with the adsorbed species.

$$A(g) + S \rightleftharpoons A-S$$
$$A-S + B(g) \xrightarrow{k_s} C-S$$
$$C-S \rightleftharpoons C(g) + S$$

> [!NOTE]
> **LH** dominates at **high pressures and low temperatures**; **ER** dominates at **low pressures and high temperatures** when one reactant is weakly adsorbing (e.g., H₂ on certain metal oxides).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Validity / Assumption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Langmuir Isotherm (single gas)** | $\theta = \dfrac{KP}{1+KP}$ | $\theta$ = fractional coverage, $K$ = adsorption constant (atm⁻¹), $P$ = pressure (atm) | Monolayer, uniform sites, no interaction |
| 2 | **Dual-site Langmuir (competitive)** | $\theta_A = \dfrac{K_A P_A}{1+K_A P_A + K_B P_B}$ | $K_A, K_B$ = adsorption constants of A, B | LH mechanism, dual sites |
| 3 | **Freundlich Isotherm** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = k P^{1/n}$ | $x/m$ = amount adsorbed per gram, $k,n$ = empirical constants | Heterogeneous surfaces, multilayer |
| 4 | **Temkin Isotherm** | $\theta = C_1 \ln(C_2 P)$ | $C_1, C_2$ = constants | Adsorbate–adsorbate interactions |
| 5 | **BET Isotherm (multilayer)** | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{(C-1)P}{V_m C P_0}$ | $V$ = volume adsorbed, $V_m$ = monolayer volume, $P_0$ = saturation pressure, $C$ = BET constant | Multilayer physical adsorption |
| 6 | **LH Rate (RDS = surface rxn)** | $r = \dfrac{k_s K_A P_A K_B P_B}{(1+K_A P_A + K_B P_B)^2}$ | $r$ = rate (mol g⁻¹ s⁻¹) | Both A & B weakly adsorbed |
| 7 | **LH Rate (RDS = adsorption of A)** | $r = \dfrac{k_a P_A}{1 + (K_B P_B / K_A P_A)}$ | $k_a$ = adsorption rate constant | Strong B adsorption, weak A |
| 8 | **ER Rate** | $r = \dfrac{k_s K_A P_A P_B}{1 + K_A P_A}$ | $k_s$ = surface rate constant | Only A adsorbs on surface |
| 9 | **Arrhenius Temperature Dependence** | $k = A \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol) | All elementary steps |
| 10 | **Turnover Frequency (TOF)** | $\text{TOF} = \dfrac{\text{moles of product}}{\text{moles of active site} \times \text{time}}$ | s⁻¹ | Catalyst activity metric |
| 11 | **Site Balance (single site type)** | $\theta_* + \theta_A + \theta_B + \theta_C = 1$ | $\theta_*$ = vacant site fraction | Conservation of surface sites |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU LaTeX Pitfall:** In KTU answer sheets, examiners often require the **derivation path** to be visible. Writing only the final rate equation without showing the **site balance substitution** and the **RDS approximation** typically loses **4–5 marks out of 14**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ammonia Synthesis (Haber-Bosch):** Fe-K₂O-Al₂O₃ catalyst; LH mechanism with N₂ dissociation as RDS
- **Petroleum Cracking:** Zeolite Y (FAU) catalyst; Bronsted acid sites
- **Three-Way Catalytic Converter:** Pt-Pd-Rh on Al₂O₃; simultaneous CO oxidation, NOₓ reduction, hydrocarbon oxidation
- **Methanol Synthesis:** Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃; ER-type mechanism with CO₂
- **Sulfuric Acid (Contact Process):** V₂O₅ on SiO₂; SO₂ → SO₃ oxidation
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

> **Statement:** Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm for the adsorption of a gas A on a uniform solid surface, assuming monolayer coverage and no lateral interactions.

**Assumptions:**
1. The surface has a fixed number of equivalent adsorption sites.
2. Each site can hold **only one** adsorbate molecule (monolayer).
3. Energetics of adsorption are identical at all sites.
4. No interaction between adsorbed molecules.

**Step 1 — Define fractional coverage.**

Let $\theta$ = fraction of surface sites occupied by A molecules at equilibrium.
Then $(1 - \theta)$ = fraction of vacant sites.

**Step 2 — Write the elementary adsorption and desorption rates.**

The rate of adsorption is proportional to the partial pressure of A and the fraction of vacant sites:

$$r_{ads} = k_a P_A (1 - \theta)$$

The rate of desorption is proportional to the fraction of occupied sites:

$$r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium condition (no net adsorption at equilibrium).**

At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a P_A (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$.**

Rearranging to isolate $\theta$:

$$k_a P_A - k_a P_A \theta = k_d \theta$$
$$k_a P_A = k_d \theta + k_a P_A \theta$$
$$k_a P_A = \theta (k_d + k_a P_A)$$
$$\theta = \frac{k_a P_A}{k_d + k_a P_A}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the equilibrium adsorption constant.**

Define $K = \dfrac{k_a}{k_d}$ (units: atm⁻¹). Substituting:

$$\theta = \frac{K P_A}{1 + K P_A}$$

**Step 6 — Examine limiting behavior.**

- At **low pressure** ($K P_A \ll 1$): $\theta \approx K P_A$ → Henry's Law (linear regime)
- At **high pressure** ($K P_A \gg 1$): $\theta \to 1$ → Complete monolayer coverage

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{K P_A}{1 + K P_A}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Langmuir–Hinshelwood Rate Law (Surface Reaction as RDS)

> **Statement:** For the reaction A + B → C + D proceeding by the Langmuir–Hinshelwood mechanism on a dual-site catalyst with the **surface reaction as the rate-determining step**, derive the rate law.

**Mechanism:**
- Step 1: $A(g) + S \rightleftharpoons A\cdot S$ with equilibrium constant $K_A$
- Step 2: $B(g) + S \rightleftharpoons B\cdot S$ with equilibrium constant $K_B$
- Step 3: $A\cdot S + B\cdot S \xrightarrow{k_s} C\cdot S + D\cdot S$ **(RDS)**
- Step 4: $C\cdot S \rightleftharpoons C(g) + S$ (fast, not affecting rate)

**Step 1 — Write the rate expression using surface concentrations.**

$$r = k_s \theta_A \theta_B$$

**Step 2 — Apply the site balance.**

The total site fraction must equal unity:

$$\theta_* + \theta_A + \theta_B + \theta_C = 1$$

Assuming the product C is weakly adsorbed (desorbs rapidly, $\theta_C \approx 0$):

$$\theta_* + \theta_A + \theta_B = 1$$

**Step 3 — Express $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$ using Langmuir isotherms.**

$$\theta_A = K_A P_A \, \theta_* \quad \text{and} \quad \theta_B = K_B P_B \, \theta_*$$

**Step 4 — Solve the site balance for the vacant site fraction $\theta_*$.**

Substituting the isotherms:

$$\theta_* + K_A P_A \theta_* + K_B P_B \theta_* = 1$$
$$\theta_* (1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B) = 1$$
$$\theta_* = \frac{1}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$.**

$$\theta_A = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B}$$
$$\theta_B = \frac{K_B P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the rate expression.**

$$r = k_s \cdot \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B} \cdot \frac{K_B P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B}$$

$$\boxed{r = \frac{k_s K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B)^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The denominator squared is the **signature of LH kinetics** with surface reaction as RDS. Memorize this — losing the square on the denominator is the single most common error.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Eley–Rideal Rate Law (Single Adsorbate)

> **Statement:** For the reaction A + B → C proceeding by the Eley–Rideal mechanism where **only A adsorbs** and B reacts from the gas phase, derive the rate law.

**Mechanism:**
- Step 1: $A(g) + S \rightleftharpoons A\cdot S$ with equilibrium constant $K_A$
- Step 2: $A\cdot S + B(g) \xrightarrow{k_s} C\cdot S$ **(RDS)**
- Step 3: $C\cdot S \rightleftharpoons C(g) + S$ (fast)

**Step 1 — Write the rate law.**

$$r = k_s \theta_A P_B$$

**Step 2 — Apply site balance with only A adsorbing (C desorbs fast).**

$$\theta_* + \theta_A = 1$$
$$\theta_* + K_A P_A \theta_* = 1$$
$$\theta_* = \frac{1}{1 + K_A P_A}$$

**Step 3 — Express $\theta_A$.**

$$\theta_A = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the rate expression.**

$$r = k_s P_B \cdot \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$$

$$\boxed{r = \frac{k_s K_A P_A P_B}{1 + K_A P_A}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Limiting Cases of LH Kinetics

Using the boxed LH rate law $r = \dfrac{k_s K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B)^2}$, we analyze physically meaningful limits:

| Limit | Condition | Simplified Rate | Apparent Order in A | Apparent Order in B |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Both weakly adsorbed | $K_A P_A, K_B P_B \ll 1$ | $r \approx k_s K_A K_B P_A P_B$ | 1 | 1 |
| A strongly adsorbed | $K_A P_A \gg 1 + K_B P_B$ | $r \approx \dfrac{k_s K_B P_B}{K_A P_A}$ | −1 | 1 |
| B strongly adsorbed | $K_B P_B \gg 1 + K_A P_A$ | $r \approx \dfrac{k_s K_A P_A}{K_B P_B}$ | 1 | −1 |
| Both strongly adsorbed | $K_A P_A, K_B P_B \gg 1$ | $r \approx \dfrac{k_s K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(K_A P_A + K_B P_B)^2}$ | 0 | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a strongly adsorbed species give a NEGATIVE order?** Because adding more of that species **blocks vacant sites**, leaving no room for the other reactant to adsorb. The rate therefore **decreases** with increasing partial pressure. KTU questions often test this counterintuitive behavior — a 3-mark question on "Why is the rate order in NH₃ negative in ammonia synthesis?" maps directly to this row.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete Heterogeneous Catalytic Cycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactant A in Bulk Fluid Phase] --> B[Step 1 External Film Diffusion]
    B --> C[Step 2 Internal Pore Diffusion]
    C --> D[Step 3 Chemisorption on Active Site S]
    D --> E[Step 4 Surface Migration]
    E --> F[Step 5 Surface Reaction A-S + B-S to C-S + D-S]
    F --> G[Step 6 Desorption of C and D]
    G --> H[Step 7 Counter Diffusion of Products to Bulk]
    H --> I[Products C and D Leave Catalyst Pellet]
    D --> D1{Adsorption RDS?}
    F --> F1{Surface Reaction RDS?}
    G --> G1{Desorption RDS?}
    F1 -->|Yes| R1[Rate r = ks times thetaA thetaB]
    D1 -->|Yes| R2[Rate r = ka times PA times 1 minus thetaA]
    G1 -->|Yes| R3[Rate r = kd times thetaC]
    R1 --> OUT[Overall LH Rate Law with Site Balance]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
```

## 4.2 LH vs ER Mechanism Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LH[Langmuir-Hinshelwood Pathway]
        A1[Gas A] --> A2[A adsorbed on site SA]
        B1[Gas B] --> B2[B adsorbed on site SB]
        A2 --> A3[A-S and B-S react on surface]
        B2 --> A3
        A3 --> A4[C-S and D-S desorb]
        A4 --> A5[Products C and D released]
    end
    subgraph ER[Eley-Rideal Pathway]
        C1[Gas A] --> C2[A adsorbed on site S]
        C2 --> C3[Gas B collides with A-S]
        C3 --> C4[C-S formed directly]
        C4 --> C5[C desorbs to gas phase]
    end
    LH -.High pressure preferred.-> NOTE1[Both species adsorb and react on surface]
    ER -.Low pressure preferred.-> NOTE2[Only one species adsorbs; other reacts from gas]
```

## 4.3 Catalyst Deactivation Pathways (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Active Fresh Catalyst] --> P1{Deactivation Mechanism}
    P1 -->|Poisoning| P1A[Strong chemisorption of impurity S on active site]
    P1A --> P1B[Permanent or temporary loss of activity]
    P1 -->|Sintering| P2A[Thermal agglomeration of metal nanoparticles]
    P2A --> P2B[Reduced active surface area]
    P1 -->|Coking| P3A[Carbon deposition from hydrocarbon side reactions]
    P3A --> P3B[Pore blockage and site coverage]
    P1 -->|Sulfur or metal deposition| P4A[Irreversible fouling]
    P4A --> P4B[Permanent deactivation]
    P1B --> REG[Regeneration by oxidation or reduction]
    P2B --> REG
    P3B --> REG
    P4B --> REP[Replacement required]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node ID Convention Used:** All node IDs above are pure alphanumeric strings prefixed with letters (e.g., `P1A`, `A2`, `START`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names, and all double-quoted labels contain clean alphanumeric text without markdown formatting. This ensures successful Mermaid rendering in GitHub, VS Code, and Obsidian.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define heterogeneous catalysis. Distinguish it from homogeneous catalysis with two key differences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Heterogeneous catalysis is a catalytic process in which the catalyst and the reactants exist in **different physical phases**, most commonly a **solid catalyst** with **gaseous or liquid reactants**.

> **Differences (2 marks):**
> 1. **Phase:** In heterogeneous catalysis, phases are different (e.g., solid + gas); in homogeneous catalysis, all species are in the **same phase** (typically liquid).
> 2. **Separation:** Heterogeneous catalysts are easily separated by **filtration/decantation**; homogeneous catalysts require **distillation or extraction**, making heterogeneous systems more industrially preferred.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]`
**What is the rate-determining step (RDS) in a heterogeneous catalytic cycle? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The **rate-determining step (RDS)** is the **slowest step** in a multi-step heterogeneous catalytic cycle that controls the **overall rate** of the reaction. All preceding steps are assumed to be in quasi-equilibrium, and all subsequent steps are considered fast.
>
> **Example (1 mark):** In the **Haber–Bosch synthesis of ammonia** over an Fe catalyst, the **dissociative chemisorption of N₂** ($N_2 + 2S \rightarrow 2N-S$) is the **RDS** due to the very strong triple bond of N₂ (bond dissociation energy = **945 kJ/mol**).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main QP]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** for a single gas adsorbing on a uniform solid surface, clearly stating all assumptions. Discuss the limiting cases of low and high pressure.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the reaction A + B → products occurring by the **Langmuir–Hinshelwood mechanism** with surface reaction as the RDS, derive the complete rate law. State the apparent reaction orders in A and B in the limit where **B is strongly adsorbed** and A is weakly adsorbed.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Assumptions of Langmuir Isotherm (2 marks):**
1. The surface contains a **fixed number of equivalent adsorption sites**.
2. Each site can hold **only one** adsorbate molecule (**monolayer**).
3. The **heat of adsorption is independent** of coverage (no lateral interactions).
4. Adsorption is **localized**; no surface migration of adsorbed molecules during equilibrium.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $\theta$ = fractional surface coverage. The rate of adsorption is $r_{ads} = k_a P (1-\theta)$ and the rate of desorption is $r_{des} = k_d \theta$. At equilibrium:

$$k_a P (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

Solving:

$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P} = \frac{K P}{1 + K P}, \quad \text{where } K = \frac{k_a}{k_d}$$

**Limiting Cases (1 mark):**
- **Low pressure** ($KP \ll 1$): $\theta \approx KP$ → **Henry's Law regime** (linear, first-order)
- **High pressure** ($KP \gg 1$): $\theta \to 1$ → **Complete monolayer** (zero-order)

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{KP}{1+KP}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Mechanism (1 mark):**
- $A + S \rightleftharpoons A\cdot S \quad (K_A)$
- $B + S \rightleftharpoons B\cdot S \quad (K_B)$
- $A\cdot S + B\cdot S \xrightarrow{k_s} \text{Products} \quad \text{(RDS)}$

**Rate expression (1 mark):** $r = k_s \theta_A \theta_B$

**Site balance (2 marks):** Assuming products desorb rapidly and do not occupy sites:

$$\theta_* + \theta_A + \theta_B = 1$$
$$\theta_* + K_A P_A \theta_* + K_B P_B \theta_* = 1$$
$$\theta_* = \frac{1}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B}$$

**Final rate law (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{r = \frac{k_s K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B)^2}}$$

**Limiting case — B strongly adsorbed, A weakly (1 mark):**

If $K_B P_B \gg 1 + K_A P_A$, then $1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B \approx K_B P_B$:

$$r \approx \frac{k_s K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(K_B P_B)^2} = \frac{k_s K_A P_A}{K_B P_B}$$

- **Apparent order in A** = **+1**
- **Apparent order in B** = **−1** (poisoning effect)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Main QP]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **Langmuir–Hinshelwood** and **Eley–Rideal** mechanisms for a heterogeneous catalytic reaction. Derive the rate law for the Eley–Rideal mechanism when only A adsorbs and B reacts from the gas phase.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss any **four** mechanisms of **catalyst deactivation** in heterogeneous catalysis. Explain how each can be **regenerated** (or why it cannot).

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Langmuir–Hinshelwood | Eley–Rideal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Adsorption | Both A & B adsorbed | Only A adsorbed; B from gas |
| Surface reaction | Between two adsorbed species | Between adsorbed A and gas-phase B |
| Pressure regime | Favors high pressure | Favors low pressure |
| Typical example | CO oxidation on Pt | CO + O → CO₂ on Pt (low P) |

**Derivation of ER Rate Law (4 marks):**

Step 1: $A + S \rightleftharpoons A\cdot S$ with $K_A$, Step 2 (RDS): $A\cdot S + B \xrightarrow{k_s} C\cdot S$, Step 3: $C\cdot S \rightleftharpoons C + S$.

Rate expression: $r = k_s \theta_A P_B$

Site balance: $\theta_* + \theta_A = 1 \Rightarrow \theta_* = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_A P_A}$, so $\theta_A = \dfrac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$.

$$\boxed{r = \frac{k_s K_A P_A P_B}{1 + K_A P_A}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**(i) Poisoning (2 marks):** Strong chemisorption of impurities (e.g., S, P, As) on active sites, blocking them. **Regeneration:** Possible for temporary poisons by oxidative treatment; permanent poisons (e.g., metal-metal bonding with Pt) require **catalyst replacement**.

**(ii) Sintering (1.5 marks):** At high temperatures, metal nanoparticles migrate and coalesce, reducing active surface area. **Regeneration:** Difficult; sometimes reversed by **redispersion in oxidizing atmosphere** but often irreversible.

**(iii) Coking (2 marks):** Carbonaceous deposits from hydrocarbon side reactions block pores and active sites (common in FCC units). **Regeneration:** Performed in industry by **controlled oxidative burn-off** in a regenerator (e.g., FCC catalyst regenerated at **700 °C** in air).

**(iv) Solid-State Transformation (1.5 marks):** Active phase converts to inactive phase (e.g., $\gamma$-Al₂O₃ → α-Al₂O₃ at high T, or metal sulfide → metal sulfate in H₂S). **Regeneration:** Generally **irreversible**; prevention via temperature control is preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the squared denominator** in the LH rate law (−2 marks)
> 2. **Not specifying the RDS** before writing the rate expression (−1 mark)
> 3. **Missing units** in the final boxed answer (−0.5 mark)
> 4. **Confusing $\theta$ and $\theta_*$** in the site balance (−1 mark)
> 5. **Writing "regeneration" without a specific method** (e.g., not saying "oxidative burn-off at 700 °C") (−0.5 mark)
> 6. **Drawing a Fischer-Tropsch analogy without justifying the RDS** in example questions (−1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT412 Module 4 — Heterogeneous Catalysis**

- ✅ **Definition:** Catalyst and reactants in **different phases** (solid catalyst + gas/liquid reactants)
- ✅ **Seven Steps of the Cycle:** Film diffusion → Pore diffusion → Chemisorption → Surface migration → Surface reaction → Desorption → Counter-diffusion
- ✅ **Langmuir Isotherm:** $\theta = \dfrac{KP}{1+KP}$ — monolayer, uniform sites, no interaction
- ✅ **Freundlich Isotherm:** $\dfrac{x}{m} = kP^{1/n}$ — empirical, heterogeneous surfaces
- ✅ **BET Isotherm:** multilayer adsorption, used for **surface area measurement**
- ✅ **LH Mechanism:** Both species adsorbed; rate has **squared denominator**; dominant at **high pressure**
- ✅ **ER Mechanism:** Only one species adsorbed; rate has **linear denominator**; dominant at **low pressure**
- ✅ **RDS Identification:** Must be stated **explicitly** before writing any rate law
- ✅ **Negative Reaction Order:** Arises when a species is **strongly adsorbed** (site blocking)
- ✅ **Site Balance:** $\theta_* + \sum \theta_i = 1$ is the **anchor** of every derivation
- ✅ **Deactivation:** Poisoning, Sintering, Coking, Solid-state transformation — each with a distinct regeneration strategy
- ✅ **Industrial Examples to Remember:**
  - **Haber-Bosch:** Fe catalyst, N₂ dissociation as RDS
  - **Contact Process:** V₂O₅ catalyst, SO₂ → SO₃
  - **FCC:** Zeolite Y, coking regenerated by oxidative burn-off
  - **Three-Way Converter:** Pt-Pd-Rh, simultaneous CO/HC/NOₓ conversion
- ✅ **Turnover Frequency (TOF):** s⁻¹ units, the key metric of **catalyst activity**
- ✅ **Arrhenius Behavior:** $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ for all elementary constants $k_a, k_d, k_s$
- ✅ **Signature Trick:** The **square in the denominator** = LH; **no square** = ER
- ✅ **Exam Strategy:** Always draw a **labeled 7-step block diagram** (worth 1–2 marks) before starting any derivation
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biocatalysts and their Typical Industrial Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biocatalysts and their Typical Industrial Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **biocatalyst** is a substance of biological origin — most commonly a protein (enzyme), a catalytic RNA (ribozyme), or a whole/immobilised microbial cell — that accelerates a specific chemical reaction under mild physiological conditions without being consumed in the overall stoichiometry. In modern process engineering, the term has been broadened to include *engineered enzymes*, *enzyme mimetics*, and *catalytic antibodies (abzymes)* that operate in non-aqueous media, ionic liquids, supercritical CO₂, and biphasic solvent systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT412, Module 4):**
> "Biocatalysts are natural macromolecular catalysts (predominantly proteins, occasionally ribonucleic acids) produced by living organisms. They exhibit extraordinary **chemo-, regio-, and stereoselectivity**, function under ambient temperature (≈ **20–40 °C**), atmospheric pressure (≈ **1 bar**), and near-neutral pH (≈ **6–8**), and are characterised by high *turnover numbers* (often 10³–10⁷ s⁻¹) and remarkable substrate specificity."

The most industrially significant class of biocatalysts is the **enzymes** — globular proteins with a precisely folded tertiary structure that creates an *active site* complementary in shape, polarity, and charge distribution to the transition state of the substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biocatalyst vs. Conventional Heterogeneous Catalyst — Key Distinctions**
> | Feature | Biocatalyst (Enzyme) | Inorganic Catalyst |
> |---|---|---|
> | Operating T | **20–40 °C** | **200–800 °C** |
> | Operating P | **≈ 1 bar** | **10–200 bar** |
> | Selectivity | Very high (single enantiomer) | Moderate |
> | Turnover No. (TON) | **10³–10⁷** | **10⁶–10⁹** |
> | Sensitivity | pH, T, [salt], heavy metals | Poisoning, sintering |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Lock-and-Key Workshop"

Imagine a **specialised locksmith's workshop** inside every living cell. Each workbench (the *active site*) is custom-built to fit *exactly one shape of key* (the *substrate*). When the key slides in, the workbench's tools (amino acid side chains such as –COO⁻, –NH₃⁺, –OH, –SH) bend, cut, or join the key with surgical precision. The locksmith (enzyme) is **not consumed** — it releases the modified key (product), resets its tools, and is immediately ready for the next customer. The locksmith works fastest when there are *just enough* keys to keep him busy but not so many that they queue up and jam the workshop — this saturation behaviour gives the classic **Michaelis–Menten** kinetic profile.

### 1.3 Classification of Biocatalysts

According to the **International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology (IUBMB)**, enzymes are classified into **six** major classes (EC 1 – EC 6):

1. **EC 1 — Oxidoreductases** — catalyse redox reactions (e.g., glucose oxidase, alcohol dehydrogenase).
2. **EC 2 — Transferases** — transfer functional groups between molecules (e.g., transaminases, kinases).
3. **EC 3 — Hydrolases** — cleave bonds with addition of water (e.g., lipases, proteases, esterases, amylases).
4. **EC 4 — Lyases** — cleave bonds by elimination (e.g., decarboxylases, aldolases, pectate lyases).
5. **EC 5 — Isomerases** — catalyse geometric/structural rearrangements (e.g., glucose isomerase, xylose isomerase).
6. **EC 6 — Ligases** — join two molecules with ATP consumption (e.g., DNA ligase, glutamine synthetase).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Workhorses (must remember):**
> Hydrolases and oxidoreductases together account for **>70 %** of all industrial enzyme sales. The single largest-selling industrial enzyme is **subtilisin** (a serine protease used in laundry detergents), followed by **glucose isomerase** (HFCS production) and **lipases** (biodiesel, cheese).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: How a Biocatalyst Works

The catalytic cycle of an enzyme proceeds through **six** discrete steps:

1. **Substrate Diffusion (k₁)** — the substrate (S) migrates from bulk solution to the enzyme's active site through a *diffusion layer* (often modelled by Fick's law).
2. **Michaelis Complex Formation (k₁)** — S binds non-covalently (H-bonds, van der Waals, hydrophobic, ionic) to form the enzyme–substrate complex **ES**. The equilibrium constant is $K_s = k_{-1}/k_1$.
3. **Induced Fit / Conformational Change** — the enzyme reshapes around the substrate (Koshland's *induced-fit* hypothesis, 1958), straining bonds and lowering the activation energy.
4. **Catalytic Chemical Transformation (k_cat)** — the bound substrate is converted to product through transition-state stabilisation.
5. **Product Release (k₃)** — the product (P) dissociates from the active site.
6. **Enzyme Regeneration** — the free enzyme (E) is restored and re-enters the cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transition-State Stabilisation** is the *quantum-mechanical heart* of biocatalysis. The active site binds the **transition state** (TS) more tightly than the substrate by a factor of **≈ 10⁸–10¹²**, which translates directly into the rate enhancement. Pauling's dictum: *"Enzymes are molecules whose structure is complementary to the activated complex of the reaction they catalyse."*

### 2.2 Enzyme Kinetics — The Quantitative Framework

The **Michaelis–Menten** model (1913) is the single most important equation in enzyme kinetics and a guaranteed high-yield topic in KTU examinations. It assumes:

- **Steady-state approximation:** $\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} \approx 0$
- **Single-substrate reaction:** $S \xrightarrow{E} P$
- **Rapid equilibrium** between E + S ⇌ ES, followed by slow irreversible conversion to P.

The fundamental rate law is:

$$v = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

Where:

- **v** = initial reaction velocity (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹)
- **V_max** = maximum velocity when all enzyme is in the ES form (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹)
- **[S]** = substrate concentration (mol L⁻¹)
- **K_m** = Michaelis constant (mol L⁻¹) = substrate concentration at which **v = ½ V_max**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation Block — Memorise for KTU Exams**

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Michaelis–Menten | $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Velocity vs. [S] hyperbolic curve |
| 2 | Definition of $K_m$ | $K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1}+k_{cat}}{k_1}$ | mol L⁻¹; substrate affinity indicator |
| 3 | Definition of $V_{max}$ | $V_{max} = k_{cat}[E]_T$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 4 | Turnover Number | $k_{cat} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{[E]_T}$ | s⁻¹; molecules converted per enzyme per second |
| 5 | Catalytic Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{k_{cat}}{K_m}$ | L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹; diffusion-limited ceiling $\approx 10^9$ |
| 6 | Lineweaver–Burk | $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$ | Double-reciprocal plot; y-int $= 1/V_{max}$, x-int $= -1/K_m$ |
| 7 | Eadie–Hofstee | $v = -K_m\cdot\dfrac{v}{[S]} + V_{max}$ | Slope $= -K_m$, y-int $= V_{max}$ |
| 8 | Hanes–Woolf | $\dfrac{[S]}{v} = \dfrac{[S]}{V_{max}} + \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}}$ | Slope $= 1/V_{max}$, y-int $= K_m/V_{max}$ |
| 9 | Competitive Inhibition | $K_m^{app} = K_m\left(1+\dfrac{[I]}{K_i}\right)$; $V_{max}$ unchanged | Inhibitor resembles substrate |
| 10 | Non-competitive Inhibition | $V_{max}^{app} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{1+[I]/K_i}$; $K_m$ unchanged | Binds only to ES |
| 11 | Uncompetitive Inhibition | Both $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ decrease by factor $(1+[I]/K_i)$ | Binds only to ES |
| 12 | Immobilisation Efficiency | $\eta_{imm} = \dfrac{\text{Activity of immobilised enzyme}}{\text{Activity of free enzyme}}\times 100$ | % retained activity |
| 13 | Arrhenius (T-dep.) | $k_{cat} = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Note: enzymes **denature** at high T → bell-shaped curve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked for *catalytic efficiency*, write **$\eta = k_{cat}/K_m$** and state its diffusion-controlled upper limit. Always specify **units** in derivations — losing 1 mark for missing units is the most common error.

### 2.4 Factors Affecting Biocatalyst Performance

1. **Temperature** — activity rises with T (Arrhenius) up to the *optimum* (typically **30–50 °C** for mesophilic enzymes); above it, the protein **denatures** (irreversible unfolding of tertiary structure, loss of active-site geometry).
2. **pH** — affects ionisation of active-site residues (Asp, Glu, His, Cys, Lys); most enzymes exhibit a *bell-shaped* pH-activity profile with optimum at the enzyme's natural physiological pH.
3. **Substrate Concentration** — hyperbolic saturation kinetics (Michaelis–Menten).
4. **Cofactors / Coenzymes** — many enzymes require metal ions (Zn²⁺, Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺/³⁺) or organic cofactors (NAD⁺/NADH, ATP, PLP, FAD).
5. **Immobilisation & Microenvironment** — partitioning effects, diffusional limitations.
6. **Solvent / Medium Engineering** — non-aqueous media (e.g., lipases in organic solvents for esterification).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industrial Sector | Role of Biocatalyst |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Single-enantiomer drug synthesis (e.g., (S)-naproxen via lipase) |
| **Detergents** | Proteases, lipases, amylases, cellulases in laundry powders |
| **Food & Beverage** | Glucose isomerase (HFCS), pectinases (juice clarification), rennet (cheese) |
| **Biofuels** | Lipases for biodiesel (FAME from triglycerides) |
| **Textile** | Amylases for desizing, cellulases for bio-polishing |
| **Pulp & Paper** | Xylanases for bio-bleaching |
| **Bioremediation** | Laccases, peroxidases for phenol degradation |
| **Asymmetric Synthesis** | Reductive aminases, transaminases (Sitagliptin process) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Michaelis–Menten Equation (Full, Step-by-Step)

**Reaction Scheme (Henri–Michaelis–Menten, 1913):**

$$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \underset{k_{-2}}{\overset{k_{cat}}{\rightarrow}} E + P$$

**Step 1 — Write the rate of product formation.**

The product P is formed only in the irreversible catalytic step:

$$v = \frac{d[P]}{dt} = k_{cat}[ES]$$

**Step 2 — Write the differential equation for the ES complex.**

The concentration [ES] changes due to formation from E + S and depletion by dissociation back to E + S and forward to E + P:

$$\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1[E][S] - k_{-1}[ES] - k_{cat}[ES]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-state approximation.**

Briggs & Haldane (1925) generalised the original Henri assumption by setting $\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} \approx 0$ (justified because [ES] is small and nearly constant after a brief pre-steady-state period):

$$0 = k_1[E][S] - (k_{-1} + k_{cat})[ES]$$

**Step 4 — Express free enzyme concentration using the mass balance.**

Total enzyme is conserved (free + bound):

$$[E]_T = [E] + [ES]$$

Substitute $[E] = [E]_T - [ES]$ into the steady-state equation:

$$0 = k_1([E]_T - [ES])[S] - (k_{-1} + k_{cat})[ES]$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect terms in [ES].**

$$0 = k_1[E]_T[S] - k_1[ES][S] - (k_{-1} + k_{cat})[ES]$$

$$k_1[E]_T[S] = [ES]\bigl(k_1[S] + k_{-1} + k_{cat}\bigr)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for [ES].**

$$[ES] = \frac{k_1[E]_T[S]}{k_1[S] + (k_{-1} + k_{cat})}$$

**Step 7 — Define the Michaelis constant.**

$$K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_{cat}}{k_1}$$

Divide numerator and denominator of the previous expression by $k_1$:

$$[ES] = \frac{[E]_T[S]}{[S] + K_m}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute [ES] into the rate equation from Step 1.**

Recall $V_{max} = k_{cat}[E]_T$ and $v = k_{cat}[ES]$:

$$v = k_{cat}[ES] = k_{cat}\cdot\frac{[E]_T[S]}{[S] + K_m}$$

$$\boxed{\;v = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}\;}$$

This is the **Michaelis–Menten equation** — the *master kinetic equation* of biocatalysis. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Cases (must be able to state in KTU viva/exam):**
> - When $[S] \ll K_m$:  $v \approx \dfrac{V_{max}}{K_m}[S]$ — **first-order** in S.
> - When $[S] \gg K_m$:  $v \approx V_{max}$ — **zero-order** in S; enzyme **saturated**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Lineweaver–Burk Double-Reciprocal Form

Starting from $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$, take the reciprocal of both sides:

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m + [S]}{V_{max}[S]}$$

Split the right-hand side into two terms:

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}}\cdot\frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{[S]}{V_{max}[S]}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}}\cdot\frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}\;}$$

This is the equation of a straight line $y = mx + c$, with:

- $y = 1/v$,  $x = 1/[S]$
- **Slope** $m = K_m / V_{max}$
- **y-intercept** $c = 1 / V_{max}$
- **x-intercept** $= -1 / K_m$

### 3.3 Derivation of Catalytic Efficiency $\eta$

At very low substrate concentration ($[S] \ll K_m$), the Michaelis–Menten equation simplifies to:

$$v \approx \frac{V_{max}}{K_m}[S] = \frac{k_{cat}[E]_T}{K_m}[S]$$

The second-order rate constant (with respect to S) is therefore:

$$\eta = \frac{k_{cat}}{K_m} \quad \left[\text{L mol}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-1}\right]$$

This ratio is called the **catalytic efficiency** or **specificity constant**. Its theoretical upper limit is set by the rate at which S and E can diffuse together in solution:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{k_{cat}}{K_m} \leq k_{diffusion} \approx 10^{8} - 10^{9}\;\text{L mol}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

Enzymes such as **catalase**, **acetylcholinesterase**, and **fumarase** operate at this diffusion-controlled ceiling.

### 3.4 Effect of Competitive Inhibition — Quantitative Derivation

In competitive inhibition, the inhibitor **I** binds only to free enzyme E (not to ES):

$$E + I \rightleftharpoons EI \quad\quad K_i = \frac{[E][I]}{[EI]}$$

Following the same Briggs–Haldane steady-state treatment but with the additional equilibrium for I, the apparent Michaelis constant becomes:

$$K_m^{app} = K_m\left(1 + \frac{[I]}{K_i}\right)$$

while $V_{max}$ is **unchanged** because, at sufficiently high [S], substrate outcompetes the inhibitor. The modified Michaelis–Menten equation is:

$$\boxed{\;v = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m^{app} + [S]} = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m\bigl(1+[I]/K_i\bigr) + [S]}\;}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Inhibitor Type | Binds To | Effect on $K_m^{app}$ | Effect on $V_{max}^{app}$ | Lineweaver–Burk Plot |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Competitive** | E only | ↑ Increases | **No change** | Lines intersect on **y-axis** |
| **Non-competitive (pure)** | E and ES equally | **No change** | ↓ Decreases | Lines intersect on **x-axis** |
| **Uncompetitive** | ES only | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | Parallel lines |
| **Mixed** | E and ES (different affinities) | Changes | Changes | Intersect *neither* axis |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The Biocatalytic Reaction Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate S in Bulk Solution] --> B[Diffusion through Boundary Layer]
    B --> C[Active Site Encounter]
    C --> D[ES Complex Formation - Michaelis Complex]
    D --> E[Induced Fit Conformational Change]
    E --> F[Catalytic Transformation - Transition State Stabilisation]
    F --> G[EP Complex Enzyme-Product]
    G --> H[Product Release - Diffusion Out]
    H --> I[Free Enzyme E Regenerated]
    I --> C

    J[Cofactor Mg2+ NAD+ PLP FAD] -.->|Required by| F
    K[Inhibitor I] -.->|Blocks step| D
    L[Temperature pH] -.->|Modulates activity of| F
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree: The Six EC Enzyme Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EC0[Biocatalysts] --> EC1[EC1 Oxidoreductases]
    EC0 --> EC2[EC2 Transferases]
    EC0 --> EC3[EC3 Hydrolases]
    EC0 --> EC4[EC4 Lyases]
    EC0 --> EC5[EC5 Isomerases]
    EC0 --> EC6[EC6 Ligases]

    EC1 --> EC1ex[Examples Glucose oxidase Alcohol dehydrogenase Catalase]
    EC2 --> EC2ex[Examples Transaminases Kinases Glycosyltransferases]
    EC3 --> EC3ex[Examples Lipases Proteases Amylases Esterases]
    EC4 --> EC4ex[Examples Decarboxylases Aldolases Pectate lyases]
    EC5 --> EC5ex[Examples Glucose isomerase Xylose isomerase]
    EC6 --> EC6ex[Examples DNA ligase Glutamine synthetase]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Industrial Immobilised Biocatalyst Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Feed[Substrate Feed Tank] --> Pump[Peristaltic Pump]
    Pump --> Reactor[Stirred Tank Reactor with Immobilised Enzyme Beads]
    Reactor --> Sep[Filter / Sieve to Retain Beads]
    Sep --> Product[Product Stream to Downstream Processing]
    Reactor --> Buffer[Buffer Reservoir pH 7.0 T 37C]
    Buffer --> Reactor
    Reactor --> Eff[Effluent Treatment]
```

### 4.4 Effect of Substrate Concentration on Reaction Velocity (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowS[Low Substrate Region]
        A1[First order regime] --> A2[v increases linearly with S]
    end
    subgraph HighS[High Substrate Region]
        B1[Zero order regime] --> B2[v approaches Vmax plateau]
    end
    LowS --> HighS
    HighS --> C[Saturation: All enzyme in ES form]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the *first-order* region, doubling [S] doubles v; in the *zero-order* region, increasing [S] has **no effect** on v because every active site is already occupied. This transition occurs around $[S] \approx K_m$.
