<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, PEPOT412]
**"What is the 18-electron rule? Why are d⁸ square-planar complexes often 16-electron and yet stable?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **18-electron rule** states that thermodynamically stable organometallic complexes of transition metals tend to have a total of **18 valence electrons** at the metal, corresponding to a filled valence shell of 5 d-orbitals, 1 s-orbital, and 3 p-orbitals. The rule is the analogue of the octet rule for main-group elements and is justified by the maximum occupancy of the 9 valence orbitals: 9 × 2 = 18.

**d⁸ square-planar** complexes of Rh(I), Pd(II), Pt(II), and Ni(II) are stable at **16 electrons** because the large d-orbital splitting in a square-planar ligand field leaves the high-lying $d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital *empty*, while the 8 d-electrons occupy the four lower d-orbitals completely. The system is therefore at a local energy minimum without needing to fill the antibonding orbital. Geometrically, the four ligands lie in a single plane (D₄ₕ symmetry), and the system is diamagnetic (all electrons paired).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "16-electron complexes are unstable." This is *wrong*. The 16-electron count is *normal* and preferred for d⁸ square-planar systems — the higher energy of the antibonding d-orbital acts as a "natural electron reservoir," preventing over-population.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, PEPOT412]
**"Distinguish between L-type, X-type, and Z-type ligands as per the CBC method. Give one example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Covalent Bond Classification (CBC)** method, proposed by M.L.H. Green (1995), classifies ligands by the number of electrons they contribute to the metal in a covalent (neutral) bonding scheme:

| Type | Electron Contribution | Bonding Mode | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L-type** | 2 electrons (lone-pair donor) | Dative σ-bond, M ← L | CO, PR₃, NH₃, H₂O, alkene |
| **X-type** | 1 electron (covalent) | Covalent σ-bond, M – X | H, Cl, Br, CH₃, Ph |
| **Z-type** | 0 electrons (Lewis-acid acceptor) | Dative π-bond, M → Z | BR₃, AlCl₃, HgCl₂ |

A given ligand may be classified differently depending on binding mode. For example, an η⁵-cyclopentadienyl (C₅H₅) ring is L₂X (contributes 5 e⁻), while a methyl group is X (1 e⁻).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse *donation direction*. L-type = metal-receives electrons. Z-type = metal-donates electrons (Lewis-acid partner). Many students swap these, losing a full mark.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model QP, PEPOT412]

**(a)** *Define organometallic compounds. With the help of suitable examples, explain the terms L-type and X-type ligands as per the CBC method. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

Organometallic compounds are those that contain at least one direct metal–carbon (M–C) σ-bond or a π-bonded carbon moiety such as an alkene, alkyne, arene, or carbonyl. They occupy a unique niche between inorganic and organic chemistry and are central to homogeneous catalysis.

**L-type ligands (2 e⁻ donors):** These are neutral species that donate a lone pair of electrons to the metal, forming a dative σ-bond. The metal→ligand back-donation is also possible when the ligand has low-lying π* orbitals (e.g., CO, alkenes). Examples: CO, PR₃, NH₃, H₂O, C₂H₄, pyridine.

**X-type ligands (1 e⁻ donors, covalent):** These are neutral radicals (H, CH₃, Cl) that form a *normal* 2-electron covalent σ-bond with the metal by sharing one electron from each partner. In the ionic counting method, they are treated as anions (X⁻) donating 2 electrons. Examples: H, Cl, Br, I, CH₃, C₆H₅.

**Bonding illustration with metal:**
- *Cr(CO)₆*: Cr is group 6; six CO ligands (L-type) each donate 2 e⁻ → $N_v = 6 + 12 = 18$ ✓
- *HCo(CO)₄*: Co group 9; 1 H (X-type, 1 e⁻) + 4 CO (L-type, 8 e⁻) → $N_v = 9 + 1 + 8 = 18$ ✓

Both examples satisfy the 18-electron rule, confirming the predictive power of CBC.

---

**(b)** *Explain oxidative addition and reductive elimination with a suitable example. Discuss their role in catalytic cycles. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Oxidative Addition (OA):** In this step, a low-valent, electron-rich metal centre inserts into a covalent A–B bond of a substrate (e.g., H₂, CH₃Cl, HX), simultaneously raising both the **oxidation state by 2** and the **coordination number by 2**.

**Canonical example:** Vaska's complex *trans*-[IrCl(CO)(PPh₃)₂] reacts with H₂:

$$
\text{Ir(I)Cl(CO)(PPh}_3)_2 + \text{H}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ir(III)(H)}_2\text{Cl(CO)(PPh}_3)_2
$$

Mechanistic description: The electron-rich Ir(I) (d⁸, 16 e⁻) uses its filled d-orbitals to *donate* into the σ* orbital of H₂, weakening the H–H bond, while the σ-bond of H₂ *donates* into the empty d-orbital of Ir. Concerted *3-centre-2-electron* transition state leads to two new Ir–H σ-bonds, octahedral Ir(III) (d⁶, 18 e⁻).

**Reductive Elimination (RE):** This is the *microscopic reverse* of OA. Two *cis* ligands on a high-valent metal couple and leave as a new A–B bond. The oxidation state decreases by 2 and the coordination number by 2.

**Example:** *cis*-[Rh(H)₂Cl(PPh₃)₃] eliminates HCl to give [RhCl(PPh₃)₃]:

$$
\text{Rh(III)(H)}_2\text{Cl(PPh}_3)_3 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Rh(I)Cl(PPh}_3)_3 + \text{HCl}
$$

For RE to occur, the two leaving groups must be in *cis* positions. *trans* isomers are kinetically inert and rarely undergo RE.

**Role in catalytic cycles:** OA "switches on" a substrate (cleaves a strong A–B bond), and RE "switches off" the product (releases a new bond). Together, they form the *bookends* of a catalytic cycle. The classic **Wilkinson's hydrogenation** proceeds by OA(H₂) → MI(alkene) → RE(alkane), three of the seven elementary steps described in Section 2.5.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common mark-loss in part (b):**
> 1. Failing to mention that **OA increases both OS and CN by 2** — full 2-mark deduction.
> 2. Saying OA and RE occur on *any* metal; they require a *square-planar d⁸* or *16-electron* precursor.
> 3. Confusing *β-hydride elimination* with RE. β-H elim does *not* change OS; RE does.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model QP, PEPOT412]

**(a)** *Using Wilkinson's catalyst RhCl(PPh₃)₃ as an example, derive the electron count by both ionic and covalent methods. Show that the 16-electron, square-planar geometry is consistent with the 18-electron rule framework. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the complex.** Wilkinson's catalyst is **RhCl(PPh₃)₃**, an orange-red solid soluble in benzene, used for homogeneous hydrogenation of alkenes.

**Step 2 — Ionic (oxidation-state) method.**

Identify the oxidation state of Rh:
- Rh group number: 9
- Charge contribution: Cl⁻ = −1, PPh₃ = 0
- Sum of ligand charges = −1

$$
\text{OS} = 9 - 1 - 0 - 0 - 0 = +1
$$

Therefore, Rh is in the +1 state → **d⁸** (since Rh is group 9 and 9 − 1 = 8 d-electrons).

Ligand contributions in the ionic method:
- 1 Cl⁻ (X-type, ionic): 2 e⁻
- 3 PPh₃ (L-type, neutral): 3 × 2 = 6 e⁻

Total valence electrons:

$$
N_v = d^n + \text{Cl contribution} + 3 \times \text{PPh}_3 \text{ contribution} = 8 + 2 + 6 = 16 \text{ e}^{-}
$$

**Step 3 — Covalent (CBC) method.**

Metal: Rh as neutral atom → 9 valence electrons.
Ligand contributions in CBC:
- 1 Cl (X-type, covalent): 1 e⁻
- 3 PPh₃ (L-type): 3 × 2 = 6 e⁻

Total:

$$
N_v = 9 + 1 + 6 = 16 \text{ e}^{-}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification of consistency.** Both methods give **16 e⁻**, confirming the result is method-independent.

**Step 5 — Connection to the 18-electron framework.** Rh(I) is a d⁸ metal. In a square-planar ligand field (D₄ₕ), the large d-orbital splitting means the $d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital is very high in energy. Eight d-electrons occupy the four lower d-orbitals fully, and four ligand electron pairs occupy the hybrid sp²d orbitals. The system is at a local energy minimum, *analogous* to an 18-electron complex but with 16 electrons because the 17th and 18th electrons would have to enter the high-energy $d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital, which is energetically unfavourable.

**Step 6 — Catalytic activation.** To enter the cycle, one PPh₃ dissociates, producing a 14-electron T-shaped active species. The 14 e⁻ is *highly* coordinatively unsaturated and is the active form that undergoes oxidative addition of H₂.

---

**(b)** *Draw a complete catalytic cycle for the hydrogenation of an alkene using Wilkinson's catalyst. Identify each elementary step and the electron count at every intermediate. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Overall Reaction:**
$$
\text{R-CH=CH-R} + \text{H}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{RhCl(PPh}_3)_3}\; \text{R-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-R}
$$

**Step-by-step cycle (in the Mermaid block above — see Section 4.3).**

| Step | Transformation | OS | CN | e⁻ count |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RhCl(PPh₃)₃ (16 e⁻, sq-pl) | +1 | 4 | 16 |
| 2 | − PPh₃ → RhCl(PPh₃)₂ (14 e⁻, T-shape) | +1 | 3 | 14 |
| 3 | + H₂ → OA → Rh(H)₂Cl(PPh₃)₂ (18 e⁻, oct) | +3 | 6 | 18 |
| 4 | + alkene → Rh(H)₂Cl(PPh₃)₂(alkene) (18 e⁻, oct) | +3 | 6 | 18 |
| 5 | MI of alkene into Rh–H → Rh(alkyl)(H)Cl(PPh₃)₂ (16 e⁻, oct) | +3 | 6 | 16 |
| 6 | RE of alkane → RhCl(PPh₃)₂ + alkane (14 e⁻, T-shape) | +1 | 3 | 14 |
| 7 | + PPh₃ → RhCl(PPh₃)₃ (16 e⁻, regenerated) | +1 | 4 | 16 |

**Elementary steps identified:**
1. **Ligand dissociation** (PPh₃ leaves, 16 → 14 e⁻).
2. **Oxidative addition** of H₂ (OS +1 → +3, CN +1 → +2, 14 → 18 e⁻).
3. **Substrate coordination** (alkene binds, 18 e⁻).
4. **Migratory insertion** of alkene into Rh–H bond (16 e⁻).
5. **Reductive elimination** of alkane (OS +3 → +1, CN 6 → 3, 16 → 14 e⁻).
6. **Ligand re-association** of PPh₃ to regenerate the resting state.

**Key features:** The cycle oscillates between 14, 16, and 18 e⁻, never exceeding 18. The 14 e⁻ T-shaped intermediate is the *active form*. The 16 e⁻ RhCl(PPh₃)₃ is the *resting state*, present in 95–99 % of the catalyst population — a *kinetically* stable, easy-to-handle precursor that releases the active species slowly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Forgetting to track the **electron count at every step** — full 2-mark deduction.
> 2. Drawing the *final* alkene-product bond incorrectly (should be sp³ C–C after hydrogenation, not C=C).
> 3. Confusing *cis/trans* isomerism — only *cis-dihydride* undergoes migratory insertion; *trans* is catalytically inactive.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Organo-Metallic Catalyst (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Catalyst containing a *direct M–C bond* (σ or π), with variable OS, CN, and geometry.
- **18-Electron Rule:** $N_v = 9 \text{ orbitals} \times 2 = 18$. Filled-valence-shell stability.
- **16-Electron Exception:** d⁸ square-planar (Rh(I), Pd(II), Pt(II), Ni(II)) — stable despite 16 e⁻.
- **CBC method (Green 1995):** L-type = 2 e⁻ donor; X-type = 1 e⁻ covalent; Z-type = 0 e⁻ acceptor.
- **Ionic method:** Assign OS first; d^n + 2(L) + 2(X⁻) = 18.
- **7 Elementary Steps:** dissociation, association, OA, RE, MI, β-H elim, transmetallation.
- **Curtis–Eisenberg Principle:** Catalyst oscillates **16 e⁻ ⇌ 18 e⁻**; resting state is 16 e⁻.
- **OA:** ΔOS = +2, ΔCN = +2; example: Ir(I) + H₂ → Ir(III)(H)₂.
- **RE:** ΔOS = −2, ΔCN = −2; *cis*-geometry required.
- **Wilkinson's catalyst RhCl(PPh₃)₃:** 16 e⁻, d⁸, square-planar; active form is 14 e⁻ T-shaped.
- **Monsanto acetic acid [Rh(CO)₂I₂]⁻:** Rh(I)/Rh(III) cycle; CH₃I oxidative addition.
- **Ziegler–Natta:** TiCl₄/MgCl₂ + AlR₃; isotactic polypropylene.
- **Grubbs 1st gen:** Ru=CHPh(PCy₃)₂Cl₂; olefin metathesis via [2+2] cycloaddition.
- **Industrial KPIs:** **TON > 10³**, **TOF > 1 s⁻¹** for economic viability.
- **Back-donation (Dewar–Chatt–Duncanson):** Metal → π* of substrate activates CO, alkenes, arenes.
- **Donation direction (do not mix up):** L-type = *ligand→metal*; Z-type = *metal→ligand*.
- **Wilkinson's cycle:** Dissociation → OA(H₂) → MI(alkene) → RE(alkane) → Re-association.
- **μ-bridging ligands:** Each metal counts only the electrons it receives; μ-Cl gives 1 e⁻ to *each* side (CBC) or 2 e⁻ to *one* side (ionic).
- **η⁵-Cp** = 5 e⁻ (L₂X); **η³-allyl** = 3 e⁻ (LX); **η⁶-benzene** = 6 e⁻ (L₃); **η²-alkene** = 2 e⁻ (L).
- **Memorise these for KTU:** Rh(9), Pd(10), Pt(10), Ni(10), Fe(8), Co(9), Ir(9), Ru(8), Mo(6), Ti(4).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dual Function Catalyst
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Dual Function Catalyst # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Dual Function Catalyst** (also known as a **Bifunctional Catalyst**) is a heterogeneous catalytic system that contains **two distinct types of catalytically active sites** on the same catalyst particle, each performing a **different and complementary chemical function** during a single overall reaction network. The two functions operate **synergistically in tandem**, where the chemisorbed reaction intermediate formed on Site A migrates (often via surface diffusion or "spillover") to Site B to undergo a transformation that Site A alone cannot perform. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Mills / Sinfelt Convention):** > *"A dual function catalyst is a multi-component catalytic system in which one type of site activates the molecule through a hydrogenation–dehydrogenation function (metallic function) while a second type of site, typically an acidic function, performs a carbon–carbon bond rearrangement (isomerization/cracking function). The two functions are coupled through a common surface intermediate."* The classical and most industrially important example is the **Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃** (Platinum on chlorided alumina) catalyst used in: - **Naphtha catalytic reforming** - **n-Paraffin hydroisomerization** - **Hydrocracking of heavy feedstocks** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **car repair workshop with two specialized mechanics**: - **Mechanic A (Metal sites – Pt, Pd, Ni):** Expert in *electrical work* (hydrogenation / dehydrogenation). He can strip or attach hydrogen atoms instantly. - **Mechanic B (Acid sites – Cl-Al₂O₃, zeolites):** Expert in *structural reshaping* (skeletal isomerization, cracking). He rearranges the carbon skeleton but cannot touch hydrogen. A normal-paraffin molecule enters the workshop. Mechanic A removes a pair of H atoms (creating an olefin) and passes the intermediate down the bench. Mechanic B bends the carbon chain into a branched shape (iso-olefin). Mechanic A then puts the H atoms back. **The car is delivered as a high-octane iso-paraffin.** Neither mechanic could do the full job alone. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The catalyst is *one physical particle* but *two chemical personalities*. The geometry of the metal–acid site proximity (typically 1–10 nm apart) is critical — too far, and the intermediate decays before reaching Site B. ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | |---|---|---| | **Metal Function Dispersion** | $D_m$ | **0.5 – 1.0** (fraction exposed) | | **Metal Loading** | $w_{Pt}$ | **0.3 – 0.6 wt %** | | **Chloride Loading (Acid Function)** | $w_{Cl}$ | **0.8 – 1.2 wt %** | | **Bifunctional Distance** | $d_{M-A}$ | **< 10 nm** (Sinfelt criterion) | | **Operating Temperature** | $T$ | **420 – 510 °C** (reforming) | | **H₂ / HC Molar Ratio** | $R_{H_2/HC}$ | **4 – 6** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Two-Function" Logic — Structured Steps The dual function paradigm follows a strict three-stepping-stone mechanism. Consider the model reaction: **n-Paraffin (P_n) → Iso-Paraffin (P_i)** ### **Step 1 — Dehydrogenation on Metal Site (M)** The saturated paraffin is adsorbed on a metallic site (Pt⁰, Pd⁰, Ni⁰) and undergoes C–H bond scission to release H₂ and form a chemisorbed **n-olefin intermediate**. $$P_n \; + \; 2M \; \rightleftharpoons \; P_n\text{-}M \; \rightleftharpoons \; O_n\text{-}M \; + \; H_2$$ This is a **fast, near-equilibrium** step because metal-catalyzed H-addition/removal has very low activation energy (~30–50 kJ/mol). ### **Step 2 — Skeletal Isomerization on Acid Site (A)** The n-olefin migrates via surface diffusion (or "olefin spillover") to an acidic Brønsted/Lewis site. It is protonated to a **carbenium ion (sec-alkyl cation)**, which then rearranges via a methyl/hydride shift to an **iso-carbenium ion**, and deprotonates to yield an **iso-olefin**. $$O_n\text{-}M \; \xrightarrow{\text{diffusion}} \; O_n\text{-}A \; \rightarrow \; [C_n H_{2n+1}]^+ \text{-}A \; \rightarrow \; O_i\text{-}A$$ This is the **rate-determining step (RDS)** in most bifunctional systems. Activation energy: **120 – 180 kJ/mol**. ### **Step 3 — Hydrogenation on Metal Site (M)** The iso-olefin diffuses back to a metal site, picks up the previously released H₂, and desorbs as a saturated iso-paraffin product. $$O_i\text{-}A \; \xrightarrow{\text{diffusion}} \; O_i\text{-}M \; + \; H_2 \; \rightarrow \; P_i \; + \; 2M$$ ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Bifunctionality > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Two Sites Are Necessary (KTU High-Yield Point):** > - A **pure metal** (Pt/SiO₂) cannot break the C–C skeleton — it only does H-chemistry. > - A **pure acid** (Cl-Al₂O₃ alone) cannot activate a saturated paraffin — alkanes do not protonate easily. > - **The olefin intermediate is the chemical "bridge"** — only olefins are reactive enough on acid sites to form carbenium ions. > - The metal's job is to *generate* this bridge; the acid's job is to *use* it. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Relationship | Symbols & Units | Application / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $r = \dfrac{k_a \, K_m \, P_{P_n}}{1 + K_m \, P_{P_n} + K_i \, P_{P_i}}$ | $k_a$ = acid site rate const; $K_m$, $K_i$ = adsorption eq. consts (Pa⁻¹) | Overall rate when **acid step is RDS** | | 2 | $K_{eq,m} = \dfrac{P_{O_n} \, P_{H_2}}{P_{P_n}}$ | Equilibrium constant of metal dehydrogenation | Step 1 (assumed at equilibrium) | | 3 | $r = k_a \, P_{O_n,eq} = k_a \, K_{eq,m} \, \dfrac{P_{P_n}}{P_{H_2}}$ | Substituting Eq.2 into Eq.1 at low coverage | **Sinfelt Bifunctional Rate Law** | | 4 | $E_{a,obs} = E_{a,acid} + \Delta H_{dehydrogenation}$ | $E_a$ in kJ/mol | Apparent activation energy of bifunctional rxn | | 5 | $\theta_{vacancy} = 1 - \theta_{P_n} - \theta_{P_i}$ | Fraction of vacant acid sites | Site balance on acid function | | 6 | $\text{COKE} = f(d_{M-A}, \; T, \; P_{H_2})$ | Empirical | Too far $d_{M-A}$ → olefin polymerizes → coke | | 7 | $\text{RON}_{product} = 92 \, \text{(n-C}_7\text{)} \rightarrow 45 \, \text{(i-C}_7\text{)}$ | Research Octane Number | Net quality lift from bifunctional rxn | **Note on Eq. 3 — Sinfelt's Law:** This is the most-tested KTU formula. It shows that *increasing H₂ partial pressure retards the rate* because the metal dehydrogenation equilibrium (Step 1) is shifted backward. This is why reformers operate at moderate, not ultra-high, H₂ pressures. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility | Industrial Application | Reaction Performed | Bifunctional Pair | |---|---|---| | **Naphtha Reforming** | n-Paraffin → iso-Paraffin, cycloalkanes → aromatics | Pt / Cl-Al₂O₃ | | **Hydroisomerization** | n-C₄ / n-C₅ → iso-C₄ / iso-C₅ | Pt / H-MOR zeolite | | **Hydrocracking** | Heavy VGO → diesel + jet | Ni-Mo / Ni-W on Y-zeolite + Al₂O₃ | | **Luboil Dewaxing** | n-Paraffin removal from waxy distillates | Pt / ZSM-5 (shape-selective acid) | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Sinfelt Bifunctional Rate Law This is the **single most important derivation** in this module. We assume a Langmuir–Hinshelwood model with the **acid isomerization step (Step 2) as the rate-determining step**, and the **metal dehydrogenation step (Step 1) at quasi-equilibrium**. ### **Step A — Identify the Rate-Determining Step (RDS)** The metal-catalyzed dehydrogenation is fast and reversible. The acid-catalyzed skeletal isomerization requires the formation of a high-energy carbenium ion transition state. Therefore: $$r_{overall} \; = \; r_{acid} \; = \; k_a \, \theta_{O_n, A}$$ where $\theta_{O_n, A}$ is the surface coverage of the *n-olefin* on the acid site, and $k_a$ is the intrinsic rate constant of the acid function (units: s⁻¹). ### **Step B — Express the Acid Site Coverage** Assume that the acid site has only three possible occupants: vacant ($\theta_v$), covered by n-olefin ($\theta_{O_n}$), and covered by iso-olefin ($\theta_{O_i}$). At steady state: $$\theta_v + \theta_{O_n} + \theta_{O_i} \; = \; 1$$ Assuming the desorption of iso-olefin is fast and readsorption negligible compared to the RDS, we approximate $\theta_{O_i} \approx 0$, so: $$\theta_{O_n, A} \;\approx\; 1 - \theta_{O_n, A} \quad \text{(only valid for low } \theta_{O_n} \text{)}$$ For a more general Langmuir treatment, we retain $\theta_{O_n, A}$ as a variable to be eliminated next. ### **Step C — Couple the Acid Coverage to the Metal Equilibrium** The flux of n-olefin to the acid site is determined by the **surface concentration of n-olefin in equilibrium with the metal site**. At the metal site: $$P_n \; + \; 2M \;\rightleftharpoons\; O_n\text{-}M \; + \; H_2$$ The equilibrium constant is: $$K_{eq,m} \;=\; \dfrac{a_{O_n, M} \, P_{H_2}}{P_{P_n} \, a_{M}^2}$$ Assuming the metal site activity is unity ($a_M \approx 1$ in the limit of low coverage), and the olefin partial pressure in the gas phase $P_{O_n}$ is in equilibrium with the surface coverage $a_{O_n, M}$ via a desorption constant $K_d$: $$a_{O_n, M} \;=\; K_d \, P_{O_n}$$ The olefin then "spillsover" to the acid site, and we assume a quasi-equilibrium distribution such that: $$\theta_{O_n, A} \;=\; K_{sp} \, P_{O_n, ss}$$ where $P_{O_n, ss}$ is the steady-state gas-phase partial pressure of the n-olefin intermediate and $K_{sp}$ is a spillover adsorption constant. ### **Step D — Solve the Mass Balance for the Intermediate Olefin** At steady state, the rate of olefin formation on metal = rate of consumption on acid: $$r_{formation} \;=\; k_m \, \theta_{P_n, M} \quad \text{(fast)}$$ $$r_{consumption} \;=\; k_a \, \theta_{O_n, A} \quad \text{(RDS)}$$ The total olefin leaving the metal must be consumed by the acid: $$k_m \, P_{P_n} / P_{H_2} \;=\; k_a \, \theta_{O_n, A}$$ Therefore the surface coverage of n-olefin on the acid site is: $$\theta_{O_n, A} \;=\; \dfrac{k_m}{k_a} \, \dfrac{P_{P_n}}{P_{H_2}}$$ ### **Step E — Substitute Back into the Rate Equation** Inserting the result of Step D into the rate expression of Step A: $$ \begin{aligned} r_{overall} \; &= \; k_a \, \theta_{O_n, A} \\ &= \; k_a \cdot \left( \dfrac{k_m}{k_a} \cdot \dfrac{P_{P_n}}{P_{H_2}} \right) \\ &= \; k_m \, \dfrac{P_{P_n}}{P_{H_2}} \end{aligned} $$ If we generalize $k_m$ as an effective lumped constant $k_{eff}$ that bundles the spillover, adsorption, and dehydrogenation kinetics, we obtain the **canonical Sinfelt Bifunctional Rate Law**: $$\boxed{\; r \;=\; k_{eff} \, \dfrac{P_{P_n}}{P_{H_2}} \;}$$ ### **Step F — Physical Interpretation of the Final Law** The boxed equation tells us three engineering-critical facts: 1. **Rate is first-order in n-paraffin partial pressure** → faster with richer feed. 2. **Rate is *inverse* first-order in H₂ partial pressure** → H₂ is a *retardant*, not a driver. Higher H₂ suppresses dehydrogenation equilibrium. 3. **Apparent activation energy** = $E_{a,acid} + \Delta H_{dehydrogenation}$ (≈ 200 kJ/mol typical) — *higher* than either step alone, because the two barriers add. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step-D Algebra Check:** Setting $P_{H_2} = 10$ bar and $P_{P_n} = 1$ bar with $k_{eff} = 0.5$ mol/(kg·h) gives $r = 0.05$ mol/(kg·h). Increasing $P_{H_2}$ to 20 bar halves the rate. This is the key H₂-pressure sensitivity students must remember. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Metal:Acid Ratio A crucial KTU concept is how the **balance of the two functions** affects selectivity. | Metal Function Strength | Acid Function Strength | Net Behavior | Industrial Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | **Strong (high Pt)** | **Weak (low Cl)** | Dehydrogenation dominates, little isomerization | Mainly aromatics (good for RON) | | **Weak (low Pt)** | **Strong (high Cl)** | Excessive cracking on acid, no H₂ supply | Light gas make, low liquid yield | | **Balanced (≈ 0.5 wt% Pt, 1 wt% Cl)** | **Balanced** | Optimal isomerization, minimum coking | **Target design for reformers** | | **Negligible (no metal)** | **Strong** | Catalyst deactivates in minutes (coke) | Not viable — verifies need for metal | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Bifunctional Reaction Pathway on Pt / Cl-Al₂O₃ The figure below shows the **spillover-driven tandem mechanism** on a single catalyst pellet. The metal site and acid site are physically separated on the same particle, and the olefin intermediate migrates between them. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[("n-Paraffin
n-C7H16
gas phase")] -->|Step 1
Dehydrogenation| B["Metal Site Pt0
n-Olefin intermediate
adsorbed"] B -->|"+H2 released
to gas phase"| C(("H2")) B -->|Olefin surface
diffusion spillover| D["Acid Site
Cl-Al2O3
carbenium ion
rearrangement"] D -->|Step 2 Skeletal
isomerization
RDS| E["Acid Site
i-Olefin
intermediate"] E -->|Diffusion back
to metal| F["Metal Site Pt0
i-Olefin adsorbed"] C -->|H2 readsorbs| F F -->|Step 3
Hydrogenation| G[("i-Paraffin
i-C7H16
RON = 45 to 92")] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Reforming Catalyst Pellet This block diagram maps the **physical structure of the bifunctional Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃ pellet**, showing how the two functions are co-located in a single porous particle. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph pellet["Bifunctional Catalyst Pellet (~1.5 mm dia)"] direction TB subgraph gamma["gamma-Al2O3 Support (high SA ~200 m2/g)"] direction LR ptA["Pt Nanocrystallite
d ~ 1-5 nm

Balanced bifunctional

Always label the **intermediate species** explicitly. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define a dual function (bifunctional) catalyst. Give one industrial example and state the role of each function.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A dual function catalyst is a heterogeneous catalyst possessing **two distinct types of active sites** on the same catalyst particle, each performing a different catalytic function in a coupled reaction sequence. The two functions communicate through a **common surface intermediate** that migrates between sites. **Industrial Example:** Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃ used in catalytic reforming of naphtha. **Roles:** - **Metal function (Pt⁰ sites):** Performs dehydrogenation of n-paraffins to n-olefins and the final hydrogenation of iso-olefins to iso-paraffins (hydrogen transfer). - **Acid function (Cl-Al₂O₃):** Performs skeletal isomerization of the n-olefin via carbenium ion rearrangement to give the iso-olefin. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark example + 1 mark roles = 3 marks)* --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Why is a pure acid catalyst (e.g., Cl-Al₂O₃ alone) ineffective for n-paraffin isomerization? How does the metal function solve this problem?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A pure acid catalyst is ineffective for n-paraffin isomerization because **saturated alkanes are not sufficiently basic/nucleophilic to be protonated** by Brønsted acid sites to form the carbenium ion required for C–C rearrangement. The activation energy for direct protonation of an sp³ C–H bond is prohibitively high. The **metal function solves this by first dehydrogenating the n-paraffin to an n-olefin** on Pt sites, producing an intermediate with a C=C double bond. Olefins are ~10⁶ times more reactive on acid sites and are easily protonated to form the required **secondary carbenium ion**, which then rearranges to the iso-carbenium and deprotonates to the iso-olefin. The iso-olefin is finally hydrogenated back to the iso-paraffin on the metal site. *(1.5 marks problem identification + 1.5 marks metal-function solution = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each carries 14 marks split as 7 + 7.* --- ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **mechanism of n-pentane isomerization over a Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃ dual function catalyst**. Identify the rate-determining step and justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Sinfelt bifunctional rate law** for the above reaction and discuss how the rate depends on (i) n-pentane partial pressure, (ii) hydrogen partial pressure, and (iii) temperature. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks** **Sketch (2 marks):** Two-box diagram — Metal Box (Pt) and Acid Box (Cl-Al₂O₃) connected by an arrow labeled "n-olefin intermediate (spillover)." **Mechanism Steps (4 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Step 1 (Metal site, fast equilibrium):} \quad & n\text{-}C_5H_{12} \;\rightleftharpoons\; n\text{-}C_5H_{10} \;+\; H_2 \\ \textbf{Step 2 (Acid site, RDS):} \quad & n\text{-}C_5H_{10} \;\xrightarrow{H^+}\; [\text{sec-}C_5H_{11}]^+ \;\rightarrow\; [\text{iso-}C_5H_{11}]^+ \;\rightarrow\; i\text{-}C_5H_{10} \\ \textbf{Step 3 (Metal site, fast):} \quad & i\text{-}C_5H_{10} \;+\; H_2 \;\rightarrow\; i\text{-}C_5H_{12} \end{aligned} $$ **RDS Justification (1 mark):** The acid-catalyzed skeletal isomerization is the **slowest step** because it requires formation of a high-energy carbenium ion (E_a ≈ 150 kJ/mol), whereas metal-catalyzed H-transfer has E_a ≈ 40 kJ/mol and is at quasi-equilibrium. #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks** **Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1 above, assuming the metal dehydrogenation is at equilibrium and the acid isomerization is RDS: $$ \begin{aligned} K_{eq,m} &= \dfrac{P_{n\text{-}olefin} \cdot P_{H_2}}{P_{n\text{-}C_5}} \quad \text{(Step 1 equilibrium)} \\ \Rightarrow P_{n\text{-}olefin} &= K_{eq,m} \cdot \dfrac{P_{n\text{-}C_5}}{P_{H_2}} \\ r_{overall} &= r_{acid} = k_a \cdot P_{n\text{-}olefin} \\ \Rightarrow \quad r_{overall} &= k_a \, K_{eq,m} \, \dfrac{P_{n\text{-}C_5}}{P_{H_2}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad r_{overall} &= k_{eff} \, \dfrac{P_{n\text{-}C_5}}{P_{H_2}} \end{aligned} $$ where $k_{eff} = k_a \cdot K_{eq,m}$. **Discussions (3 marks — 1 each):** - **(i) n-Pentane pressure:** Rate is **first-order** in $P_{n\text{-}C_5}$. Higher paraffin partial pressure → higher rate (linear dependence). - **(ii) Hydrogen pressure:** Rate is **inverse first-order** in $P_{H_2}$. Higher H₂ shifts the dehydrogenation equilibrium *backward*, starving the acid site of olefin intermediate. This is the well-known **hydrogen inhibition** in reforming. - **(iii) Temperature:** Rate increases with T following Arrhenius, but with the **combined activation energy** $E_{a,app} = E_{a,acid} + \Delta H_{dehydrogenation} \approx 200$ kJ/mol, which is *higher* than either step alone. --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the concept of **"olefin spillover"** in bifunctional catalysis. How does the distance between metal and acid sites affect catalyst performance and selectivity? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃ catalyst is used for n-hexane isomerization at 450 °C, 15 bar total pressure, and H₂/hydrocarbon molar ratio of 5. The feed contains 80 mol% n-hexane and 20 mol% H₂ at the inlet. If the equilibrium constant of dehydrogenation at 450 °C is $K_{eq,m} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ bar and the acid-site rate constant is $k_a = 0.8$ s⁻¹, calculate the **initial rate of isomerization** using the Sinfelt bifunctional rate law. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks** **Definition of Olefin Spillover (3 marks):** Olefin spillover is the **surface migration of an olefin intermediate** from the metal site, where it is generated by dehydrogenation, to the acid site, where it undergoes isomerization. The migration occurs via: - Surface diffusion across the support (γ-Al₂O₃) over nanometer distances. - Desorption–readsorption in the gas phase (less common at high pressure). - Direct surface "hopping" through adsorbed layers. The intermediate exists transiently — its **surface lifetime (~10⁻³–10⁻¹ s)** must exceed the **diffusion time** between the two site types, otherwise it desorbs or polymerizes. **Effect of Distance (4 marks):** - **Distance < 5 nm (ideal):** Fast spillover, high isomerization selectivity, low coke. Both functions effectively coupled. - **Distance 5–50 nm (acceptable):** Moderate spillover, some loss of selectivity, slight coke formation begins. - **Distance > 100 nm (poor):** Spillover too slow; olefins desorb, polymerize, or coke the acid site. Catalyst deactivates in hours. - **Physical mixture of Pt/Al₂O₃ + Cl-Al₂O₃ (macroscopic):** Behaves as if two separate catalysts; the dual function is essentially lost. This is why **co-impregnated or co-precipitated** Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃ outperforms physical mixtures of the two components. #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks** **Given Data (1 mark):** - $T = 450$ °C, $P_{total} = 15$ bar, H₂/HC = 5 - Feed: 80 mol% n-C₆, 20 mol% H₂ - $K_{eq,m} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ bar, $k_a = 0.8$ s⁻¹ **Step 1: Compute Partial Pressures (2 marks)** With a H₂/HC ratio of 5, on a basis of 1 mol n-hexane, there are 5 mol H₂. Total moles = 6. $$P_{n\text{-}C_6} = \left(\dfrac{1}{6}\right) \times 15 = 2.5 \text{ bar}$$ $$P_{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{5}{6}\right) \times 15 = 12.5 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 2: Apply Sinfelt Law (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} r &= k_a \cdot K_{eq,m} \cdot \dfrac{P_{n\text{-}C_6}}{P_{H_2}} \\ &= (0.8 \; \text{s}^{-1}) \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{bar}) \times \dfrac{2.5 \; \text{bar}}{12.5 \; \text{bar}} \\ &= (0.8) \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.2) \\ &= 1.92 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{(mol/L·s, with appropriate units)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Final Answer with Units (1 mark)** $$\boxed{\; r_{initial} = 1.92 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1} \;}$$ **Comment:** Doubling the H₂ pressure (e.g., changing ratio to 10:1) would halve the rate to $9.6 \times 10^{-5}$, confirming strong H₂ inhibition. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to label the intermediate species** in the sketch → loses 1 mark in Q-B-A(a). > 2. **Wrong sign of H₂ dependence** — students often write "rate increases with H₂ pressure" because H₂ is a reactant in Step 3. **Wrong!** The rate law is *inverse* in H₂ because Step 1 (dehydrogenation) is at equilibrium and is the *source* of the intermediate. H₂ pushes that equilibrium backward. > 3. **Confusing Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃ with Pt/SiO₂** — the chloride is the acid site; SiO₂ is essentially non-acidic. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion. > 4. **Skipping units in the final numerical answer** → loses 0.5–1 mark in Q-B-B(b). > 5. **Drawing two separate catalysts instead of one pellet with two site types** → loses the "bifunctional" essence marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Dual Function Catalyst):** - ☐ **Definition:** A single catalyst with **two distinct active site types** performing complementary functions through a **common surface intermediate**. - ☐ **Industrial prototype:** **Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃** in catalytic reforming and hydroisomerization. - ☐ **Function 1 — Metal (Pt):** Dehydrogenation of n-paraffin → n-olefin; final hydrogenation of iso-olefin → iso-paraffin. - ☐ **Function 2 — Acid (Cl⁻ on Al₂O₃):** Protonation of olefin → carbenium ion → methyl/hydride shift → iso-olefin (RDS). - ☐ **The bridging intermediate:** **n-Olefin** — the chemical "messenger" between the two sites. - ☐ **Spillover:** Surface migration of the olefin from metal to acid; distance must be **< 10 nm** (Sinfelt criterion). - ☐ **Sinfelt Bifunctional Rate Law:** $\;r = k_{eff} \, P_{P_n} / P_{H_2}\;$ — inverse first-order in H₂. - ☐ **Hydrogen is a *retardant***, not a promoter (counter-intuitive but true). - ☐ **Apparent E_a** = $E_{a,acid} + \Delta H_{dehydrogenation} \approx 200$ kJ/mol. - ☐ **Chloride role:** Generates Brønsted acid sites on γ-Al₂O₃; loading 0.8–1.2 wt%. - ☐ **Balanced design:** ~0.5 wt% Pt + ~1 wt% Cl gives optimal isomerization selectivity. - ☐ **Unbalanced risks:** Acid-only → coking; metal-only → no skeletal rearrangement; thermal only → low conversion. - ☐ **Modern variants:** Pt/H-MOR (n-C₄ to iso-C₄), Pt/ZSM-5 (luboil dewaxing, shape-selective). - ☐ **Comparison anchor:** Pure metal = H-chemistry only; pure acid = needs olefin to start; **dual function = works on saturated feedstocks**. - ☐ **Master equation to memorize:** $\; \boxed{r = k_{eff} \, \dfrac{P_{n\text{-paraffin}}}{P_{H_2}}}\;$

topics.title: Zeolite and their Typical Industrial Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT412 - CATALYST SCIENCE AND CATALYTIC PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Zeolite and their Typical Industrial Applications # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Zeolites > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > **Zeolites** are crystalline, hydrated aluminosilicate minerals (or their synthetic analogues) belonging to the family of **microporous tectoaluminosilicates**. Chemically, they are represented by the empirical formula: > > $$\text{M}_{x/n}^{n+} \left[(\text{AlO}_2)_x (\text{SiO}_2)_y \right] \cdot w\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > > where: > - $\text{M}^{n+}$ = exchangeable extra-framework cation (Na$^+$, K$^+$, Ca$^{2+}$, H$^+$, NH$_4^+$, rare earth) > - $y/x$ = **silica-to-alumina ratio (SAR / SiO$_2$/Al$_2$O$_3$)**, the most critical descriptor of zeolite acidity and thermal stability > - $w$ = moles of intracrystalline (zeolitic) water trapped inside the cages > - $y/x \geq 1$ is mandatory (**Loewenstein's rule**: no Al–O–Al linkages are permitted) The word "zeolite" was coined by Swedish mineralogist **Axel Fredrik Cronstedt (1756)** from the Greek words *zeo* ("to boil") and *lithos* ("stone") — because the material visibly releases water vapour when heated in a flame, appearing to "boil". > [!NOTE] > **Modern definition adopted by IZA (International Zeolite Association):** > A zeolite is a porous aluminosilicate with a **three-dimensional, 4-connected tetrahedral framework** (each T-atom = Si or Al is bonded to 4 oxygens), enclosing cavities (cages) and channels of molecular dimensions (**2–20 Å**), populated by exchangeable cations and removable guest water molecules. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Apartment Building" Imagine a high-rise apartment building built entirely of glass and concrete bricks. The building is: | Building Feature | Zeolite Equivalent | |------------------|--------------------| | Bricks (load-bearing) | SiO$_4^{4-}$ and AlO$_4^{5-}$ tetrahedra (TO$_4$ units) | | Mortar between bricks | Bridging oxygen atoms (–O–) | | Apartment doors (corridors) | Pore openings / windows (8-, 10-, 12-membered rings) | | Staircase lifts | Channels running through 1D, 2D, or 3D pore networks | | Tenants | Charge-balancing cations (Na$^+$, H$^+$, Ca$^{2+}$) and water | | Building manager (security) | Only certain "tenants" (molecules) can pass through "doors" of fixed size | **Intuition for a first-time reader:** - Just as a building's corridor width determines what furniture can pass, a zeolite's pore aperture (ring size) determines which reactant/product molecules can diffuse in and out. - The "tenant" cations (Na$^+$, H$^+$) sit at specific sites — these are the **active sites** where catalytic reactions occur. - Replacing Na$^+$ with H$^+$ converts a non-acidic zeolite into a **powerful solid Brønsted acid**, capable of cracking heavy hydrocarbons. --- ## 1.3 Key Structural Terminology (Syllabus Anchors) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Terminology — Must Know:** > - **T-atom** = tetrahedrally coordinated framework atom (Si or Al). > - **Primary Building Unit (PBU)** = the TO$_4$ tetrahedron. > - **Secondary Building Unit (SBU)** = geometric arrangements of 4 to 16 T-atoms (e.g., S4R = single 4-ring, S6R, S8R, D4R, D6R, D8R). > - **Composite Building Unit (CBU)** = larger sub-units forming the framework (e.g., the *pentasil unit* in ZSM-5). > - **Framework Type Code (FTC)** = three-letter IZA code (e.g., MFI, FAU, MOR, BEA). > - **Supercage** = a large internal cavity (e.g., the 12.5 Å supercage of Faujasite). > - **Window / Pore aperture** = the free diameter of the smallest ring in the channel system. **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (bolded for exam recall):** - **SiO$_4$ tetrahedron ionic radius of Si$^{4+}$ ≈ 0.26 Å** - **Al$^{3+}$ ionic radius ≈ 0.39 Å** (larger → more distortion when Al replaces Si) - **T–O bond length ≈ 1.60–1.65 Å** - **T–O–T bond angle ≈ 130°–180°** (controls pore shape) - **Typical pore apertures: 8-MR = ~4.1 Å, 10-MR = ~5.5 Å, 12-MR = ~7.4 Å** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a Zeolite The framework of a zeolite is built in a stepwise, modular fashion. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for board questions on "zeolite structure" worth 7–14 marks. ### Step 1 — Primary Building Unit (PBU) The basic unit is the **TO$_4$ tetrahedron** (T = Si or Al): - Central T-atom (Si$^{4+}$ or Al$^{3+}$) is $sp^3$ hybridized. - 4 oxygen atoms are arranged tetrahedrally at the corners. - Each oxygen is shared with an adjacent tetrahedron → T–O–T linkage (bridging oxygen). ### Step 2 — Secondary Building Units (SBUs) Tetrahedra link to form simple rings and prisms: | SBU | Description | Common in | |-----|-------------|-----------| | S4R | Single 4-ring (4 TO$_4$ units) | LTA, GIS | | S6R | Single 6-ring (hexagonal) | FAU, EMT, CHA | | S8R | Single 8-ring | MOR (8-MR channels) | | D4R | Double 4-ring (cube of 8 T-atoms) | LTA (α-cage) | | D6R | Double 6-ring (hexagonal prism, 12 T-atoms) | FAU, EMT, CHA | | D8R | Double 8-ring | — | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Insight:** When asked to "draw the SBU of Faujasite", the answer is **D6R (Double 6-Ring / Hexagonal Prism)**. The sodalite cage itself is a *composite* of 8 S6R units. ### Step 3 — Composite Building Units (CBUs) Larger structural motifs: - **Sodalite cage (β-cage):** 24 T-atoms, 6 S6R faces, internal free diameter ≈ 6.6 Å. Occurs in FAU, SOD, EMT, LTA. - **Pentasil unit:** 5-membered ring chain motif; characteristic of MFI (ZSM-5) and MEL (ZSM-11) frameworks. - **Gmelinite cage:** 24 T-atoms with two S6R + six S4R. ### Step 4 — Pore System - **1D channels:** MOR, LTL, EUO - **2D channels:** MFI (ZSM-5 has straight + sinusoidal), FER, HEU - **3D channels:** FAU, LTA, BEA, CHA, AEI --- ## 2.2 The Role of Aluminum — Origin of Acidity and Ion Exchange > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Central Idea (Heart of Zeolite Catalysis):** > Each substitution of Si$^{4+}$ by Al$^{3+}$ in the framework introduces a **formal negative charge of –1** per substitution, because the framework gains one extra electron (Al has one less +ve charge). This charge MUST be neutralized by a cation (e.g., Na$^+$, H$^+$, NH$_4^+$, Ca$^{2+}$, La$^{3+}$). The consequences are: 1. **Ion exchange capability:** The charge-balancing cation is mobile and can be exchanged with others. 2. **Brønsted acidity:** When the cation is H$^+$, the bridging Si–O(H)–Al unit is a strong Brønsted acid site (proton donor). 3. **Lewis acidity:** When dehydration is severe, the Al site becomes a Lewis acid (electron acceptor). 4. **Hydrophilicity:** Higher Al content → more hydrophilic; lower Al → hydrophobic (siliceous zeolites). --- ## 2.3 Shape Selectivity — The Most Asked KTU Concept Shape selectivity arises from the geometric constraint imposed by the pore aperture on reacting molecules. There are **three classical types** (Csicsery, 1984): | Type | Mechanism | Classic Example | |------|-----------|-----------------| | **Reactant shape selectivity** | Only molecules smaller than the pore can enter; bulky ones are rejected. | n-Paraffin cracking on Ca-exchanged 5A zeolite (linear paraffins enter, branched/isoparaffins are excluded). | | **Product shape selectivity** | All reactants enter, but only those that yield products slim enough to diffuse out are observed. | Methanol-to-gasoline (MTG) on H-ZSM-5: only C$_{1}$–C$_{10}$ hydrocarbons escape; heavy aromatics are trapped and coke. | | **Transition-state (restricted transition-state) selectivity** | A bulky transition state cannot fit inside the pore, even though reactant and product both can. | Transalkylation of dialkylbenzenes on H-Mordenite: the *trans*-isomer transition state is sterically forbidden → para-isomer is favoured kinetically. | > [!NOTE] > **Additional 4th type (later, Derouane):** *Molecular traffic control* — two non-intersecting channels each guide a specific molecule. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cheat Sheet — Memorize Before the Exam:** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use / Exam Tip | |---|-------------------|------------|----------------| | 1 | **Empirical formula** | $\text{M}_{x/n}^{n+}[(\text{AlO}_2)_x(\text{SiO}_2)_y]\cdot w\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Standard descriptor | | 2 | **Silica-to-alumina ratio (SAR)** | $\text{SAR} = \frac{\text{Si atoms}}{\text{Al atoms}} = \frac{y}{x}$ | Higher SAR → more thermally stable, less acidic | | 3 | **SiO$_2$ / Al$_2$O$_3$ mass ratio** | $\frac{\text{SiO}_2\% \times 60}{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3\% \times 102} = \frac{y}{2x}$ | Common in industrial data sheets | | 4 | **Maximum Al by Loewenstein's rule** | $y/x \geq 1$ (lowest SAR = 1) | No Al–O–Al allowed | | 5 | **Theoretical cation count per unit cell** | $N_{\text{cation}} = x$ (or $x/n$ for M$^{n+}$) | Direct from formula | | 6 | **Brønsted acid sites per gram (theoretical max)** | $N_{\text{acid}} = \frac{x \cdot N_A}{M_{\text{UC}}}$ | $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$ | | 7 | **Framework density (FD)** | $\text{FD} = \frac{\text{T-atoms per unit cell} \times 10^3}{V_{\text{UC}}\ (\text{\AA}^3)}$ | Lower FD → larger pore volume | | 8 | **Pore aperture vs ring size (approx.)** | $d_{\text{pore}} \approx 2.5 \times (n - 1)^{0.5}\ \text{\AA}$, $n$ = ring members | 8-MR ≈ 4.1 Å, 10-MR ≈ 5.5 Å, 12-MR ≈ 7.4 Å | | 9 | **Constraint Index (CI)** | $\text{CI} = \frac{k_{\text{trans-2-butene isomer.}}}{k_{\text{3-methylpentane cracking}}}$ | Used to rank medium-pore zeolites | | 10 | **Strong acid site concentration** | $\propto \frac{1}{\text{SAR}}$ for low SAR; saturation at SAR > 25 | Quantitative acidity rule | > **Real-world utility:** These equations appear verbatim in industrial FCC catalyst design, FCC unit monitoring, and refinery feedstock selection. Engineers use SAR to choose the right zeolite (USY for FCC, ZSM-5 for octane boosting, MOR for hydroisomerization, etc.). --- ## 2.5 Classification of Zeolites by Pore Size (Board Favourite) | Class | Ring size | Pore diameter | Examples (IZA code) | Key reactions | |-------|-----------|----------------|----------------------|----------------| | **Small-pore** | 8-MR | ~4.1 Å | LTA (Type A), CHA (SAPO-34), ERI | Selective for CH$_3$OH → C$_2$H$_4$ (MTO) | | **Medium-pore** | 10-MR | ~5.5 Å | MFI (ZSM-5), MEL, FER, TON | MTG, MTO, MFI-based dewaxing, FCC additive | | **Large-pore** | 12-MR | ~7.4 Å | FAU (X, Y), MOR, BEA (Beta), ECR-1 | FCC main catalyst, hydrocracking, alkylation | | **Extra-large pore** | >14-MR | >8.0 Å | VFI, CLO, UTL | Limited industrial use | > [!NOTE] > **Memory aid (KTU trick):** *S-M-L-X* = *Small / Medium / Large / Extra-large* = 8 / 10 / 12 / 14+ ring members. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Si/Al Ratio and Acidity from Unit Cell Formula **Problem:** A synthetic faujasite zeolite has the unit cell composition: $$\text{Na}_{56}\left[(\text{AlO}_2)_{56}(\text{SiO}_2)_{136}\right] \cdot 264\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Calculate (a) the Si/Al atomic ratio, (b) SiO$_2$/Al$_2$O$_3$ mass ratio, (c) number of Brønsted acid sites per gram after converting to H-form, and (d) percentage weight loss on complete dehydration. **Step (a) — Si/Al atomic ratio (SAR):** From the empirical formula, $x = 56$ (Al atoms) and $y = 136$ (Si atoms). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SAR} &= \frac{y}{x} \\[4pt] \text{SAR} &= \frac{136}{56} \\[4pt] \text{SAR} &= 2.4286 \end{aligned} $$ Rounded: **SAR = 2.43** (typical for "low-silica" Y zeolite; commercial USY has SAR ≈ 6–30). **Step (b) — SiO$_2$/Al$_2$O$_3$ mass ratio:** The formula in mass basis uses M(SiO$_2$) = 60.08 g/mol, M(Al$_2$O$_3$) = 101.96 g/mol. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\text{SiO}_2}{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} &= \frac{2 \cdot y \cdot 60.08}{2 \cdot x \cdot 50.98} \quad \text{(mole of SiO}_2 \text{ is } y\text{, mole of Al}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ is } x/2\text{)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2 \cdot 136 \cdot 60.08}{2 \cdot 56 \cdot 50.98} \\[4pt] &= \frac{16{,}341.8}{5{,}709.8} \\[4pt] &= 2.862 \end{aligned} $$ Alternatively, using the shortcut: (SiO$_2$/Al$_2$O$_3$) = 2 × (Si/Al) × (28.09/26.98) ≈ 2 × SAR × 1.041 = **2.08 × SAR**. Plugging in: 2.08 × 2.43 = **5.05**... wait, let me recheck. The mass ratio in the *framework* is (2 × Si atoms × 60.08) / (2 × Al atoms × 50.98) where the denominator is one Al$_2$O$_3$ per 2 Al atoms, hence: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\text{SiO}_2}{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} &= \frac{y \cdot 60.08}{(x/2) \cdot 101.96} = \frac{2y \cdot 60.08}{x \cdot 101.96} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2 \times 136 \times 60.08}{56 \times 101.96} \\[4pt] &= \frac{16{,}341.8}{5{,}709.8} \\[4pt] &= 2.862 \end{aligned} $$ **SiO$_2$/Al$_2$O$_3$ = 2.86 (mass basis).** **Step (c) — Acid sites per gram (H-form, assuming all 56 Na$^+$ replaced by H$^+$):** Molecular weight of unit cell: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{UC}} &= 56(23) + 56(26.98 + 2 \times 16) + 136(28.09 + 2 \times 16) + 264(18) \\[4pt] M_{\text{Na}} &= 56 \times 23 = 1288 \\[4pt] M_{\text{AlO}_2} &= 56 \times 59.98 = 3358.9 \\[4pt] M_{\text{SiO}_2} &= 136 \times 60.09 = 8172.2 \\[4pt] M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} &= 264 \times 18.02 = 4757.3 \\[4pt] M_{\text{UC}} &= 1288 + 3358.9 + 8172.2 + 4757.3 \\[4pt] M_{\text{UC}} &= 17{,}576.4\ \text{g/mol} \end{aligned} $$ > Note: After H-exchange, replace 56 Na (mass 56 × 23) with 56 H (mass 56 × 1), the dehydrated H-form M ≈ 17,576.4 − 4757.3 − 1288 + 56 = **11,587 g/mol**. Acid sites per gram: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{acid}} &= \frac{56 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{11{,}587} \\[4pt] &= \frac{3.37 \times 10^{25}}{11{,}587} \\[4pt] &= 2.91 \times 10^{21}\ \text{sites/g} \end{aligned} $$ **≈ 4.83 mmol H$^+$/g** (typical of USY H-form). **Step (d) — Weight loss on dehydration:** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\,\text{loss} &= \frac{264 \times 18.02}{17{,}576.4} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= \frac{4757.3}{17{,}576.4} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= 27.1\% \end{aligned} $$ **27.1% weight loss on full dehydration** — typical for Y zeolite. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Theoretical Ion-Exchange Capacity (TIEC) **Problem:** For a zeolite with SAR = 5, calculate the **Theoretical Ion-Exchange Capacity (TIEC)** in meq/g. **Derivation:** Per mole of unit cell (assuming 1 Al per "elementary cell" of SAR = 5): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mass of one AlO}_2\text{Si}_5\text{O}_{10}\text{ unit} &= 5 \times 60.09 + 1 \times 59.98 + \text{O for charge balance correction} \\[4pt] &\approx 5 \times 60.09 + 59.98 = 360.43\ \text{g/mol of "framework T-atoms"} \end{aligned} $$ Charge per Al = 1 equivalent. So, $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TIEC} &= \frac{1000\ \text{meq/mol}}{360.43\ \text{g/mol}} \\[4pt] &= 2.77\ \text{meq/g} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification with general formula** — for any SAR (= n), the equivalent mass (g per equivalent of Al) is: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{mass}} &= n \cdot M(\text{SiO}_2) + M(\text{AlO}_2) = n \cdot 60.09 + 59.98 \\[4pt] \text{TIEC (meq/g)} &= \frac{1000}{n \cdot 60.09 + 59.98} \end{aligned} $$ For SAR = 2.43 (our earlier example): $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{mass}} &= 2.43 \times 60.09 + 59.98 = 145.99 + 59.98 = 205.97 \\[4pt] \text{TIEC} &= \frac{1000}{205.97} = 4.86\ \text{meq/g} \end{aligned} $$ This matches the 4.83 mmol/g obtained earlier (1 meq = 1 mmol for monovalent H$^+$). ✅ > [!NOTE] > **Board Examiner Tip:** A common mistake is forgetting that 1 Al produces **1 equivalent** (not 0.5). Each framework Al carries a single negative charge regardless of the counter-ion valence. Always use 1 eq per Al. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Constraint Index (CI) Interpretation **Problem:** Two zeolites A and B give the following first-order rate constants at 773 K for n-hexane cracking: - Zeolite A: $k_{n-\text{C6}} = 2.0\ \text{h}^{-1}$ - Zeolite B: $k_{n-\text{C6}} = 0.6\ \text{h}^{-1}$ The rate of iso-butane cracking is 0.4 h$^{-1}$ and 0.5 h$^{-1}$ respectively. Compare pore sizes qualitatively. **Solution approach:** The **Constraint Index** is defined as: $$ \text{CI} = \frac{\log(\text{fraction of } n\text{-hexane remaining at } 10\% \text{ cracking of 3-MCP})}{\text{similar ratio for 3-methylpentane}} \quad \text{(per Chu)} $$ A simpler working definition used at KTU level: $$ \text{CI} = \frac{k_{n\text{-hexane cracking}}}{k_{3\text{-methylpentane cracking}}} $$ - For Zeolite A: $\text{CI} = 2.0/0.4 = 5.0$ → medium pore (10-MR), e.g., **ZSM-5**. - For Zeolite B: $\text{CI} = 0.6/0.5 = 1.2$ → large pore (12-MR), e.g., **Faujasite (Y)**. **Interpretation:** - High CI (>5) → strong steric hindrance → small-medium pores reject 3-methylpentane. - Low CI (~1) → both molecules enter freely → large pores. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Framework Density and Pore Volume **Problem:** Zeolite Beta (BEA) has 64 T-atoms per unit cell and unit cell volume 4050 Å$^3$. Calculate framework density and comment on pore openness. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FD} &= \frac{64 \times 10^3}{4050} \\[4pt] &= \frac{64{,}000}{4050} \\[4pt] &= 15.8\ \text{T-atoms}/1000\ \text{Å}^3 \end{aligned} $$ For reference: - FAU: FD ≈ 12.7 T/1000 Å$^3$ (very open) - MFI: FD ≈ 17.9 T/1000 Å$^3$ (moderately open) - SOD: FD ≈ 17.2 T/1000 Å$^3$ (dense) **BEA at 15.8 T/1000 Å$^3$ is highly open**, consistent with its large 12-MR pore system. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Building-Unit Architecture (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Primary Building Unit PBU
